Document 17 of 500

'Disservice to democracy' - Windsor-Essex PC candidates dodge Ontario election debates, interviews

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/disservice-democracy-windsor-essex-pc-candidates/docview/3169465096/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Disservice+to+democracy%27+-+Windsor-Essex+PC+candidates+dodge+Ontario+election+debates%2C+interviews&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Essex County's Tory candidates are under fire for refusing to face their Ontario election rivals in public debate during the current campaign - and they and their party appear unwilling to say why.
Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario headquarters will not acknowledge if it's an official policy or edict from PC Leader Doug Ford, and local candidates haven't responded to the Star's calls or messages.
But the local practice of dodging debates and media questions appears to be a common theme for Tory candidates across Ontario.
"It is inherently less democratic," Jeffrey Dvorkin, a senior fellow at Massey College and former University of Toronto journalism program director, told the Star.
"This is about micromanaging a campaign, which Ford has done in the past, which is to say there'll be one message and it should always be from either the premier or a senior cabinet minister."
The Windsor-Essex Regional Chamber of Commerce was forced to cancel its traditional all-candidates election debate this week for the ridings of Windsor West, Windsor-Tecumseh, and Essex due to lack of participation.
Chamber president Ryan Donally said some parties had yet to choose candidates, while all candidates for one party declined to participate. A source with knowledge of the debates told the Star the party refusing to take part was the Progressive Conservatives.
Parties eligible for the debate included the Liberals, NDP, Green Party, and PCs, who all have representation at Queen's Park.
Incumbent PC candidates Anthony Leardi in Essex and Andrew Dowie in Windsor-Tecumseh, along with Windsor West candidate Tony Francis, did not respond to the Star's repeated requests for comments about why they refused to join the debate.
Leardi and Francis also refused to talk to reporters following PC party Leader Doug Ford's campaign kickoff in Windsor. Dowie did talk to reporters that day, but has since ignored multiple interview requests from the Star, including for a phone interview or emailed responses to questions for an election riding profile
Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario Leader Doug Ford greets local workers near the Ambassador Bridge in Windsor on Jan. 29, 2025, where he launched his re-election campaign.
Dowie and Francis were also no-shows at a Windsor-Tecumseh and Windsor West candidates forum hosted Wednesday night by Emmanuel United Church. Other candidates running in those ridings showed up.
Windsor's Ward 9 Coun. Kieran McKenzie, who watched that debate, took to social media to congratulate the candidates "who had enough courage and respect for basic principles of democracy to show up." He also wrote "shame on you" to those who didn't appear.
They're not engaging, and it's sad
McKenzie told the Star that it's "incumbent" on candidates to take all steps possible to communicate what they will do if elected. That includes attending debates and responding to media questions, he said.
"They need to talk," said McKenzie. "That's what an election campaign is about, having that conversation with the public. They're not engaging, and it's sad.
"It's undemocratic, and hopefully they'll reconsider."
Tory candidates in several other cities have also refused media interview requests or debate invitations, including those in Timmins, Sudbury, Halton, Walkerton, Owen Sound, Durham, Sarnia-Lambton, and Brampton, among others.
In response to the Star's request for comment, a PC party staffer identified only as Caitlin repeated verbatim a terse message sent to media in other cities who had similar questions about the lack of engagement.
"Ontario PC candidates will spend every day of the campaign at people's doors, speaking to voters about our plan to protect Ontario."
Caitlin did not respond to a follow-up email asking for more explanation, including how attending a debate would negate the ability to knock on doors, as candidates from other parties are doing.
It's a similar strategy to one the Ontario Tories employed during the 2022 provincial election when they won a majority of seats at Queen's Park.
It's a disservice to democracy
"Parties have always wanted more control over the message," said Dvorkin. "The internet has made that more complicated. So in order to manage the message, political parties have limited who can speak on behalf of anybody.
"I think that's the downside of the digital culture. Even though it promises to let 1,000 opinions bloom, the effort to control the message is greater than ever."
During the current election campaign, Doug Ford has also started limiting his exposure to media scrutiny, allowing questions from only six reporters per press conference.
Related
All-candidates debates for provincial election cancelled in Windsor
Doug Ford uses Windsor's Ambassador Bridge as backdrop for campaign launch
Ontario election 2025: What are key issues for Windsor area voters headed to polls?
"It used to be that when you were on the campaign trail, it meant that you got a lot of information out of people, and then you were able to write a report around that," said Dvorkin, a former journalist and journalism instructor.
"Now you're not able to do that because nobody's talking to you. I think that is a disservice to elections, and it's a disservice to democracy."
twilhelm@postmedia.com

Ontario election 2025: Who are the 8 candidates on Essex ballot?

Author: Holmes-Hill, Millar

Publication info: Holmes-Hill, Millar. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-who-are-8-candidates-on/docview/3169459875/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+2025%3A+Who+are+the+8+candidates+on+Essex+ballot%3F&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Holmes-Hill%2C+Millar&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here's what you need to know about Essex for the Feb. 27 Ontario election:
The riding has a population of over 134,000 and covers 2,272 square kilometres, according to Elections Ontario 2021 census data.
The riding encompasses the bulk of Essex County, including five municipalities - Amherstburg, LaSalle, Essex, Kingsville and most of Lakeshore (everything west of Rochester Townline Road). Tecumseh and Leamington are part of other provincial electoral ridings.
Electoral boundary map for the Ontario riding of Essex.
Since reconfiguration 23 years ago, the riding was held by Liberal Bruce Crozier from 1999 to 2011, and then Taras Natyshak of the NDP from 2011 to 2022. Progressive Conservative Anthony Leardi won the last election in 2022 with 51 per cent of the vote, delivering Essex to the Tories for the first time in 60 years. The NDP's Ron LeClair was runner-up with 28 per cent of the vote, followed by Liberal Manpreet Brar with nine per cent.
In the 2022 election, 49,025 people out of 101,723 eligible voters cast ballots in Essex, a turnout of 47 per cent.
There are eight names on the Essex ballot in this year's election:
Note: Ontario Party's Travis Jacques did not respond to multiple requests for comment ahead of the Star's print deadline.
STEPHEN HIGGINS, Green Party of Ontario
Occupation: Owner, PURE Mobile Wash
Stephen Higgins, Green Party of Ontario candidate for Essex.
Reason for running:
"I was tired of the political discourse and rhetoric coming out of the three major parties and of voting for an MPP who is not empowered to do anything other than vote down the party lines regardless of its impact on constituents in the riding."
Top priorities:
"Working with the people of the riding and municipalities to solve the housing affordability crisis; reinvesting in our education system; and restoring the health care we deserve including mental health support.
"The Green Party of Ontario wants to do politics differently and allow for the people of Essex to truly have a voice."
ANTHONY LEARDI, Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario
Occupation: Incumbent MPP; lawyer; former Amherstburg deputy mayor
Anthony Leardi, Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario candidate for Essex.
Reason for running:
"We need to give Doug Ford a strong mandate to stand up for Ontario against Donald Trump's tariffs."
Top priorities:
Building Ontario's economy with infrastructure investments; protecting workers from the "threat" of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs; get more people working in the skilled trades; keep Ontario competitive by fighting the "job-killing" federal carbon tax; banning drug consumption sites near schools and daycares; building a new Windsor hospital.
KEVIN LINFIELD, None of the Above Direct Democracy Party
Occupation: Engineer
Kevin Linfield, None of the Above Direct Democracy Party candidate for Essex.
Reason for running:
"MPPs and MPs should not be whipped into following party policy ahead of what is best for people in their riding, their province, and their country."
Top Priorities:
"All political decisions will be made based on what is good for the community, the province, and the country."
RACHAEL MILLS, Ontario New Democratic Party
Occupation: Talent acquisition consultant
Rachael Mills, Ontario New Democratic Party candidate for Essex.
Reason for running:
"The more I got involved in my community the more concerned I became. I realized how deeply interconnected our challenges are and how urgently we need real balanced solutions. Essex deserves a government that supports and prioritizes us when we're in need, not just before an election.
"My passion for community, sustainability, and advocacy has led me to this opportunity to be a strong voice for our region."
Top priorities:
"Effective, well-funded universal health care so that no one is left behind. Our education system must be properly resourced to provide safe, high-quality learning environments for all.
"Protect the jobs and livelihoods that sustain our community. Affordability is a growing crisis - housing, groceries, and child care should not be out of reach for working families. Protecting our environment and farmland is critical for climate resilience, safeguarding our homes, and ensuring food sovereignty."
TAMARA STOMP, Ontario Liberal Party
Occupation: Lawyer, former deputy mayor of Kingsville
Tamara Stomp, Ontario Liberal Party candidate for Essex.
Reason for running:
"The way Doug Ford has favoured backroom deals with friends, spent millions on a spa on public land, and the millions he is spending on this election for a tariff fight mandate, leaves me with little confidence that he can or will do the right thing.
"I am very worried for the future with Doug Ford at the helm."
Top priorities:
"All key portfolios of the provincial government have gotten worse over the last seven years under Doug Ford. We need a new path and Bonnie Crombie's platform appealed to me most. Bonnie's plan is to build more homes you can afford. Education has been gutted and that is not acceptable.
"I will be right there with Bonnie to realize her goal that everyone in Ontario has a doctor. Facing our health care crisis will take hard work and stamina. I have always worked hard and am ready for the challenge."
WILLIAM SZABO VERZOC, Independent
Occupation: Medical anthropologist
William Szabo Verzoc, Independent candidate for Essex.
Reason for running:
"We need an MPP that actually listens to voters and answers phone calls and emails. Someone that is willing to offer the kind of help that the most vulnerable amongst us deserve.
"That's why I'm running as the Independent Candidate for Essex."
Top priorities:
To increase funding to Ontario colleges; break ground on the new "county" hospital; increased funding for Ontario Disability Support Program recipients; and to "actually transfer money" to Essex County for capital projects and other maintenance needs.
BRIGITTE BELTON, New Blue Party
Occupation: OTR (long-haul) truck driver
(NO PHOTO AVAILABLE)
Reason for running:
"To protect our services, our culture, our farmers, and our future. I believe Ontario needs positive change in government - a government run by people like you and me. We, the people, know what we need."
Priorities:
Improved accessibility to medical care; affordable housing; creating safer neighbourhoods; reducing HST and scrapping the carbon tax; easing tension with "our southern neighbour."
mholmeshill@postmedia.com

Document 19 of 500

Ontario election 2025: What are key issues for Windsor area voters headed to polls?

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-what-are-key-issues-windsor/docview/3169434821/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+2025%3A+What+are+key+issues+for+Windsor+area+voters+headed+to+polls%3F&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario voters head to the polls in a week. Here is a look at some of the top issues for Windsor-Essex voters:
Trump and Tariffs
With tariffs threatened on all Canadian goods, and U.S. President Donald Trump doubling down on 25 per cent tariffs levied on steel and aluminum imports to the U.S., trade uncertainty has become a defining issue for Windsor-Essex voters in this election.
Windsor is projected to be among the hardest-hit Canadian cities by U.S. tariffs, which could climb to 50 per cent, and are set to take effect on March 12.
Meanwhile, Trump has also floated the idea of 100 per cent tariffs on the Canadian auto sector, putting Windsor-Essex's manufacturing directly in the crosshairs.
The uncertainty has already cost local manufacturers millions as they attempt to react to the moving target that is Trump's threats.
"Everyone knows how the supply chain works here," said University of Windsor political science professor Lydia Miljan.
"We understand that a car isn't built solely in Detroit or solely in Canada, that those parts and vehicles go across that border multiple times. So we have a much better understanding of just how devastating it would be if the major auto manufacturers said, 'Well, we're going to shutter our Canadian factories and just produce in the States.'
"It could become insurmountable with that kind of trade dispute. So I think people here are very sensitive to this issue."
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford has made tariffs the cornerstone of his campaign, even kicking off the election in Windsor West, with the Ambassador Bridge - North America's busiest trade corridor - as his backdrop.
While other major parties continued campaigning, Ford visited Washington, D.C., last week, joining other provincial leaders to push back against U.S. tariffs and make a case for Ontario's economic interests.
Opposition party leaders have argued Ford's justification for calling a snap election more than a year early to strengthen his mandate to steer Ontario through uncertain times is misleading.
They claim Ford was eyeing an early election long before the tariff threats and is seizing the moment to capitalize on his party's popularity in the polls.
Health Care
Health care is always a central issue in provincial elections, and this campaign is no exception - but this time, the conversation has been dominated by the shortage of family doctors.
An estimated 2.5 million Ontarians lack access to a family doctor, according to the Ontario College of Family Physicians.
In Windsor alone, the Ontario Medical Association estimates 27 additional physicians are needed to serve 34,000 residents currently without a family doctor.
Opposition leaders have slammed the Ford government's pre-election $200 rebate cheque giveaway, which cost the province $3 billion, arguing the funds should have been invested in health care and other public services rather than a one-time payout.
Bonnie Crombie's Liberals have pledged to ensure every Ontarian has access to a family doctor within four years by recruiting thousands to the workforce. Another Liberal promise is to end "hallway health care" by increasing wages for nurses and personal support workers.
The New Democrats, led by Marit Stiles, have promised to recruit 3,500 new doctors, as well as hire 15,000 nurses over the next three years, to strengthen front-line care.
The Tories have committed to covering 90 per cent of Windsor's acute care hospital's $2-billion price tag. The project timeline is ahead of schedule, with Phase 1 opening for proposals last December.
The new facility is expected to streamline health care in Windsor-Essex, centralizing services that are currently scattered across multiple locations.
Housing and Cost of Living
Tackling Ontario's housing supply crisis was a key issue in the 2022 election campaign, with Ford's PC party promising to build 1.5 million homes over the next decade.
The key concern for Windsor-Essex voters heading to the polls is "how quickly we can get affordable housing," said Miljan. Windsor appeared on track last September to meet its 2024 housing targets in order to secure a $3-million boost from Ontario's Building Faster Fund.
Opposition leaders, however, argue that the Progressive Conservatives have not delivered on their housing promises and not yet met any of their annual targets, especially as the cost of living continues to balloon.
In January, Windsor's average rent across all housing types was $1,644 per month.
"People are feeling the pinch," said Miljan.
"Even though our housing prices probably aren't as high as they are in other regions, they're still high. Rent is still really difficult for a lot of people to afford."
The NDP has promised to assist with affordability by introducing a monthly grocery rebate for lower- and middle-income families. It also plans to establish as consumer watchdog to crack down on grocery price gouging.
The Liberals have pledged to offer relief to Ontarians with a 22 per cent cut to the middle-income tax bracket, scrapping HST on home heating and hydro, and doubling Ontario Disability Support Program benefits.
Although Windsor has pushed for greater provincial support to tackle the homelessness and addiction crises, with city council urging the Ford government to create a dedicated Ministry of Homelessness, Miljan does not foresee this issue shaping the outcome of the election.
Related
Ontario election: Windsor West boasted one of province's poorest 2022 voter turnouts
Ontario election: What you need to know to vote
Ontario election: Windsor-Tecumseh candidates prioritize health care, housing, affordability
Advanced Voting:
Ontarians have multiple opportunities to cast their ballots before election day on Feb. 27, including advance voting that opens Thursday.
For information about advance voting locations in Windsor-Essex, visit voterinformationservice.elections.on.ca
mmazak@postmedia.com

Document 20 of 500

Health care, housing key priorities for candidates in Windsor-Tecumseh; Ontario Election Incumbent Dowie captured riding for PC party in 2022 with nearly half the total vote

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/health-care-housing-key-priorities-candidates/docview/3169286441/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Health+care%2C+housing+key+priorities+for+candidates+in+Windsor-Tecumseh%3B+Ontario+Election+Incumbent+Dowie+captured+riding+for+PC+party+in+2022+with+nearly+half+the+total+vote&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The riding of Windsor-Tecumseh can trace its roots back to a 1996 merger of pieces of the former ridings of Windsor-Riverside and Windsor-Walkerville to create Windsor-St. Clair.
In 2007, Windsor-St. Clair became Windsor-Tecumseh and contains all of Tecumseh and the Windsor neighbourhoods of Riverside, portions of Walkerville and Sandwich South.
The riding has 122,798 residents based on the 2021 Census count.
Windsor-Tecumseh also contains much of the area's emerging economic might, being home to a large collection of tool and mould-making companies and the new $6-billion NextStar Energy plant.
It hosts Windsor Airport, which will see significant industrial development on the airport lands and Sandwich South. The region's new acute-care hospital will also be located in the riding. The riding's voting preferences have evolved from being overwhelming supportive of the Liberals' Dwight Duncan (1995 to 2013), followed by even more staunch support for the NDP's Percy Hatfield (2013 to 2022), and then Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario MPP Andrew Dowie, who won the seat with nearly half the total vote (46 per cent) in 2022.
The NDP's Gemma Grey-Hall was the 2022 runner-up (30 percent), followed by Liberal Gary Kaschak (14 per cent).
Turnout in 2022 was 38,779 out of a possible 94,976 voters, a decline of 7.2 per cent over the 2018 election.
Dowie and Grey-Hall, both running again in the riding, will be joined in this year's race by Liberal Connor Logan, the Green Party's Roxanne Tellier, Communist Party's Kyle Ford and New Blue's Sophia Sevo, who also ran in 2022. The following is a synopsis of each candidate's reasons for running and their priorities for the riding.
Note: PC candidate Andrew Dowie and the Greens' Roxanne Tellier didn't return multiple requests from the Windsor Star for a phone interview or emailed responses to questions. The New Blue Party's Sophia Sevo and Ontario Party's Steven Gifford entered the race too late to be included here.
GEMMA GREY-HALL, Ontario New Democratic Party Occupation: Senior development officer, University of Windsor Faculty of Science Reason for running: "I think it's imperative we have a government fighting for what people need and what matters most to them. I'm not seeing that with our current Doug Ford government.
"I've been speaking to a growing body of people who are also deeply unhappy with the state of our health care, education. And we're still waiting for our hospital."
Priorities: "Health care is at top of the list. We need to ensure we're using our dollars to support our health care system by funding it appropriately.
"Next, it's fighting the tariffs. You need to elect a government that's focused on providing people with what they need when times are dire. We know we're facing a current and ongoing threat from the American government regarding tariffs."
CONNOR LOGAN, Ontario Liberal Party Occupation: Constituency assistant for member of Parliament Irek Kusmierczyk (L Windsor Tecumseh) Reason for running: "I've seen the issues with health care. I don't have a family doctor.
"Housing, I can't afford to buy a home and there's many people my age (23) and even in their 30s who are struggling to find a home.
"I also see the issues of education and the cuts to programs that are important to students. Our education system is seemingly breaking at the seams."
Priorities: "We need to get the mega hospital built. There are people sitting for hours in emergency rooms and hallways of hospitals.
"There are also people waiting at walk-in clinics for hours. We need more investment there. We need more family doctors.
"We also need to build affordable homes. To do this we're going to scrap some of the taxes (development charges) that have been put up by previous governments that will end up saving $170,000 on a home.
"With education, we'll cut interest rates on OSAP (Ontario Student Assistance Program), so that will help students as well."
KYLE FORD, Communist Party of Canada (Ontario) Occupation: Mould maker Reason for running: "I feel the time is getting right for people to reconsider the economic system we're living in. Running is the way to raise the party's profile and get a chance to get our voice in government and combat the Conservative and Liberal talking points raised, the anti-worker legislation that has been passed and put a voice for the working people in Parliament." Priorities: "Number one is addressing the cost-of-living crisis. It has made life difficult for everyone.
"In addition to that is supporting workers' rights. There are a lot of working-class people in the riding.
"If they're living paycheque to paycheque, the risk from corporations pulling out of the area or layoffs and restructuring in economic times like this is a real risk for workers. They need support and help from the government that really hasn't been given lately."

Document 21 of 500

Rumoured Costco in east end gets green light from committee

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rumoured-costco-east-end-gets-green-light/docview/3169259739/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rumoured+Costco+in+east+end+gets+green+light+from+committee&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=SR.8&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Windsor may be one more step closer to having a second Costco location.
City council's development committee this week backed plans for a massive commercial build in east Windsor on land rumoured to have been selected for a Costco big-box store.
Plans for a one-storey building with a single horizontal stripe labelled "COSTCO RED" were included in documents presented to the committee. One page of the plans had the words "Costco prototype" at the bottom in a font that was the same colour as the document's background, making it visible only via search.
The proposed development, which is still subject to approval from council as a whole, would see two large commercial buildings and 1,385 parking spaces cover nearly 20 acres (eight hectares) of land immediately north of the Home Depot on Tecumseh Road East.
Mayor Drew Dilkens would not confirm whether Windsor is getting another Costco but said the city and the company have been in communication about a second store location in Windsor.
"We've worked very hard in a very competitive environment to, uh, work with Costco as they contemplate a second location in our area," Dilkens told reporters during an unrelated media event at city hall on Monday.
"The land has been identified. A lot of work has been done behind the scenes. This will really fill in a piece of land in the city that is right for commercial and retail development."
Dilkens added that Costco is his "favourite place to go," and that Windsor's sole location on Walker Road is always very busy.
"They know they need another store, and we're hopeful that they choose Windsor, and that they get the right site, and that our investments and our commitment to them lead to some really great things happening in that part of our city on all fronts, including residential."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford raised the topic of a second Costco coming to Windsor during a visit to the city in September.
"I know it may not seem big, but rumour has it, a Costco is coming to town. That's two to 300 more jobs. That's important, good-paying jobs," Ford said.
A story that followed was one of the Windsor Star's top-read online news articles of 2024, indicating strong community interest.
The development committee on Monday approved official plan amendments and rezoning to allow for commercial use on the land, which is made up of three lots and is abutted by Walmart, Home Depot, the Serbian Centre, and train tracks.
The approval also permits am- bulance and food catering services on the site, as well as facilities for manufacturing, warehouses, food packaging, medical appliances, research and development, and stormwater management. It prohibits child care centres.
Rock Developments East Windsor Inc., proposes to develop the land for commercial purposes, with more than 24,000 square metres of ground floor area over multiple commercial pads.
The first phase of the project consists of a 15,071-square-metre, single-storey building with a gas bar and 967 parking spaces, the report said. Phase 2 includes a separate one-storey building that covers 9,537 square metres and 418 more parking spaces.
A stormwater facility will be built at the north end of the land, the report said. "It's great to see the area being developed," said Ward 10 Coun. Jim Morrison, who chairs the development committee.
"There's been a lot of guesses about what is going in there, and you can see the infrastructure that we (the city) are going to do - we're doing a pretty significant development out this way."
The city's proposed 2025 capital budget includes nearly $20 million for road and stormwater infrastructure in the area, which is referred to as the Forest Glade North Secondary Plan and is generally bound by Jefferson Boulevard to the west, Tecumseh Road East to the south, Lauzon Parkway to the east, and the Via Rail corridor to the north.
A budget document says plans include extending Catherine Street west and south from Walmart to Tecumseh Road East; extending Roseville Gardens Drive from Tecumseh Road East north to Catherine Street; and constructing a regional stormwater management facility to service the area.
Dilkens said other development is expected to happen in the area, including multi-residential developments that will depend on the new stormwater facility.
"The prospect of what's happening there is really, really positive on a number of fronts and will certainly increase the property tax base in the City of Windsor quite handsomely," the mayor said.
tcampbell@postmedia.com

Subject: Committees; Stormwater management

Ontario election: Windsor-Tecumseh candidates prioritize health care, housing, affordability

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-windsor-tecumseh-candidates/docview/3169095649/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+Windsor-Tecumseh+candidates+prioritize+health+care%2C+housing%2C+affordability&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The riding of Windsor-Tecumseh can trace its roots back to a 1996 merger of pieces of the former ridings of Windsor-Riverside and Windsor-Walkerville to create Windsor-St. Clair.
In 2007, Windsor-St. Clair became Windsor-Tecumseh and contains all of Tecumseh and the Windsor neighbourhoods of Riverside, portions of Walkerville and Sandwich South.
The riding has 122,798 residents based on the 2021 Census count.
Windsor-Tecumseh also contains much of the area's emerging economic might, being home to a large collection of tool and mould-making companies and the new $6-billion NextStar Energy plant.
It hosts Windsor Airport, which will see significant industrial development on the airport lands and Sandwich South. The region's new acute-care hospital will also be located in the riding.
Electoral boundary map for the Ontario riding of Windsor-Tecumseh.
The riding's voting preferences have evolved from being overwhelming supportive of the Liberals' Dwight Duncan (1995 to 2013), followed by even more staunch support for the NDP's Percy Hatfield (2013 to 2022), and then Progressive Conservative of Ontario MPP Andrew Dowie, who won the seat with nearly half the total vote (46 per cent) in 2022.
The NDP's Gemma Grey-Hall was the 2022 runner-up (30 per cent), followed by Liberal Gary Kaschak (14 per cent).
Turnout in 2022 was 38,779 out of a possible 94,976 voters, a decline of 7.2 per cent over the 2018 election.
Dowie and Grey-Hall, both running again in the riding, will be joined in this year's race by Liberal Connor Logan, the Green Party's Roxanne Tellier, Communist Party's Kyle Ford and New Blue's Sophia Sevo, who also ran in 2022.
The following is a synopsis of each candidate's reasons for running and their priorities for the riding.
Note: PC candidate Andrew Dowie and the Greens' Roxanne Tellier didn't return multiple requests from the Windsor Star for a phone interview or emailed responses to questions. The New Blue Party's Sophia Sevo and Ontario Party's Steven Gifford entered the race too late to be included here.
GEMMA GREY-HALL, Ontario New Democratic Party
Occupation: Senior development officer, University of Windsor Faculty of Science
Gemma Grey-Hall, Ontario New Democratic Party candidate in the Ontario 2025 election for the riding of Windsor-Tecumseh.
Reason for running: "I think it's imperative we have a government fighting for what people need and what matters most to them. I'm not seeing that with our current Doug Ford government.
"I've been speaking to a growing body of people who are also deeply unhappy with the state of our health care, education and we're still waiting for our hospital."
Priorities: "Health care is at top of the list. We need to ensure we're using our dollars to support our health care system by funding it appropriately.
"Next, it's fighting the tariffs. You need to elect a government that's focused on providing people with what they need when times are dire. We know we're facing a current and ongoing threat from the American government regarding tariffs."
CONNOR LOGAN, Ontario Liberal Party
Occupation: Constituency assistant for member of Parliament Irek Kusmierczyk (L - Windsor-Tecumseh)
Connor Logan, Ontario Liberal Party candidate in the Ontario 2025 election for the riding of Windsor-Tecumseh.
Reason for running: "I've seen the issues with health care. I don't have a family doctor.
"Housing, I can't afford to buy a home and there's many people my age (23) and even in their 30s who are struggling to find a home.
"I also see the issues of education and the cuts to programs that are important to students. Our education system is seemingly breaking at the seams."
Priorities: "We need to get the mega hospital built. There are people sitting for hours in emergency rooms and hallways of hospitals.
"There are also people waiting at walk-in clinics for hours. We need more investment there. We need more family doctors.
"We also need to build affordable homes. To do this we're going to scrap some of the taxes (development charges) that have been put up by previous governments that will end up saving $170,000 on a home.
"With education, we'll cut interest rates on OSAP (Ontario Student Assistance Program), so that will help students as well."
KYLE FORD, Communist Party of Ontario
Occupation: Mould maker
Kyle Ford, Communist Party of Canada-Ontario candidate in the 2025 election for the riding of Windsor-Tecumseh.
Reason for running: "I feel the time is getting right for people to reconsider the economic system we're living in. Running is the way to raise the party's profile and get a chance to get our voice in government and combat the Conservative and Liberal talking points raised, the anti-worker legislation that has been passed and put a voice for the working people in Parliament."
Priorities: "Number one is addressing the cost-of-living crisis. It has made life difficult for everyone.
"In addition to that is supporting workers' rights. There are a lot of working-class people in the riding.
"If they're living paycheque to paycheque, the risk from corporations pulling out of the area or layoffs and restructuring in economic times like this is a real risk for workers. They need support and help from the government that really hasn't been given lately."

Document 23 of 500

Poll shows Ford in lead as voting day nears

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poll-shows-ford-lead-as-voting-day-nears/docview/3168913071/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poll+shows+Ford+in+lead+as+voting+day+nears&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a little more than a week to go until election day, the Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario holds a commanding 20-point lead over its nearest rival party.
A new Postmedia-Leger poll shows 48 per cent of Ontarians would cast a ballot for Doug Ford's PCs, while 28 per cent would vote for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals. Just 16 per cent say they will vote for the New Democrats led by Marit Stiles.
The original election date was to be in June 2026.
Eighteen per cent of Ontarians say their impressions of Ford have improved since the start of the election and 16 per cent of Ontarians say the same thing about Crombie.
"In the snap election, in these unprecedented times, we've seen Ford's lead already," said Jennifer McLeod Macey, senior vice-president, public affairs, with Leger. "This really cements it."
While 36 per cent say their impression of Ford has worsened over the course of the election campaign, he remains the leader that large minorities of Ontarians trust most to handle the issues facing the province. Ford's significant presence when it comes to dealing with the United States may have changed the trajectory of this election. More than three times as many Ontarians (45 per cent) say they trust Ford to protect against U.S. aggression compared to the 13 per cent who say the same of Crombie.
"His response to that has really made him stand out, and maybe what would have happened in a snap election in other times would have been a different story," said McLeod Macey.
Thirty-seven per cent of Ontarians identify Ford as the politician they trust most to grow the economy, compared to 16 per cent who say they most trust Crombie, and nine per cent who chose Stiles.
While Ford struggles on other fronts - he barely has the edge on Crombie on health care and education - he also enjoys significant leads on criminal justice and dealing with gridlock on Ontario's streets.
For Ontario voters, the single biggest issue going into the election is the cost of living, at 22 per cent, followed by trade with the United States at 11 per cent, which is tied with housing affordability. Other classic campaign issues, such as health care, education, public safety and climate change are priority issues for single-digit percentages of Ontarians, the polling shows.
"We do see that in tough economic times that social issues do decline. That particularly tends to be the case with things like climate change, the environment," said McLeod Macey.
Overall, 33 per cent of Ontarians say they are satisfied with Ford's tenure as premier and will vote for him again. He was first elected in 2018. Thirty-six per cent, however, say they are dissatisfied and will vote for another party this time around.
There is one other wild card at play: those who are unsure. Twelve per cent of those polled say they aren't sure who they'll vote for.
"So one thing I'm curious to see is what voter turnout will be on the day," said McLeod Macey.
The PC's popularity is strongest in the Greater Toronto Area, with 58 per cent of Ontarians in that region saying they are leaning toward voting for Ford's party. At 40 per cent, the party's support is lowest in the northern part of the province. Crombie's Liberals are at their most popular in Metro Toronto, with 32-per-cent support, although this is still dwarfed by the 42 per cent in that area who say they'll be voting PC.
The NDP does best in the province's north, where 26 per cent say they'll cast a ballot for the party.
Ford's PCs are most popular, at 53 per cent, with those aged 55 and older. The party struggles comparatively among the younger cohort: 38 per cent of those between the ages of 18 and 34 say they will be voting PC.
That said, the PCs still have a fairly commanding lead across all age demographics. The Ontario Liberals have the support of 32 per cent of 18-to-34-year-olds, but that's still six points behind the PCs.
The polling was done between Feb. 14 and 16 using an online survey of 1,002 Ontario adults. The results have been weighted to ensure a representative sample of Ontario households. For comparison purposes, a probability sample of this size yields a margin of error no greater than plus or minus 3.09 per cent, 19 times out of 20.

Location: United States--US

Document 24 of 500

What are Windsor area's key issues in this election?; Trade and tariffs, health care and cost of living emerge as concerns

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-are-windsor-areas-key-issues-this-election/docview/3168913000/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+are+Windsor+area%27s+key+issues+in+this+election%3F%3B+Trade+and+tariffs%2C+health+care+and+cost+of+living+emerge+as+concerns&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario voters head to the polls in a week. Here is a look at some of the top issues for Windsor-Essex voters:
TRUMP AND TARIFFS With tariffs threatened on all Canadian goods, and U.S. President Donald Trump doubling down on 25 per cent tariffs levied on steel and aluminum imports to the U.S., trade uncertainty has become a defining issue for Windsor-Essex voters in this election.
Windsor is projected to be among the hardest-hit Canadian cities by U.S. tariffs, which could climb to 50 per cent, and are set to take effect on March 12.
Meanwhile, Trump has also floated the idea of 100 per cent tariffs on the Canadian auto sector, putting Windsor-Essex's manufacturing directly in the crosshairs.
The uncertainty has already cost local manufacturers millions as they attempt to react to the moving target that is Trump's threats.
"Everyone knows how the supply chain works here," said University of Windsor political science professor Lydia Miljan. "We understand that a car isn't built solely in Detroit or solely in Canada, that those parts and vehicles go across that border multiple times. So we have a much better understanding of just how devastating it would be if the major auto manufacturers said, 'Well, we're going to shutter our Canadian factories and just produce in the States.'
"It could become insurmountable with that kind of trade dispute. So I think people here are very sensitive to this issue."
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford has made tariffs the cornerstone of his campaign, even kicking off the election in Windsor West, with the Ambassador Bridge - North America's busiest trade corridor - as his backdrop. While other major parties continued campaigning, Ford visited Washington, D.C., last week, joining other provincial leaders to push back against U.S. tariffs and make a case for Ontario's economic interests.
Opposition party leaders have argued Ford's justification for calling a snap election more than a year early to strengthen his mandate to steer Ontario through uncertain times is misleading.
They claim Ford was eyeing an early election long before the tariff threats and is seizing the moment to capitalize on his party's popularity in the polls. HEALTH CARE Health care is always a central issue, and this campaign is no exception - but this time, the conversation has been dominated by the shortage of family doctors.
An estimated 2.5 million Ontarians lack access to a family doctor, according to the Ontario College of Family Physicians.
In Windsor alone, the Ontario Medical Association estimates 27 additional physicians are needed to serve 34,000 residents currently without a family doctor, The Opposition has slammed the Ford government's pre-election $200 rebate cheque giveaway, arguing the $3 billion it cost should have been invested in health care and other public services rather than a one-time payout.
Bonnie Crombie's Liberals have pledged to ensure every Ontarian has access to a family doctor within four years by recruiting thousands to the workforce. Another Liberal promise is to end "hallway health care" by increasing wages for nurses and personal support workers.
The New Democrats, led by Marit Stiles, have promised to recruit 3,500 new doctors, as well as hire 15,000 nurses over the next three years, to strengthen front-line care.
The Tories have committed to covering 90 per cent of Windsor's acute care hospital's $2-billion price tag. The project timeline is ahead of schedule, with Phase 1 opening for proposals last December. The new facility is expected to streamline health care in Windsor-Essex, centralizing services that are currently scattered across multiple locations. HOUSING AND COST OF LIVING Tackling Ontario's housing supply crisis was a key issue in the 2022 election campaign, with Ford's PC party promising to build 1.5 million homes over the next decade. The key concern for Windsor-Essex voters heading to the polls is "how quickly we can get affordable housing," said Miljan. Windsor appeared on track last September to meet its 2024 housing targets in order to secure a $3-million boost from Ontario's Building Faster Fund.
Opposition leaders, however, argue that the Progressive Conserva- tives have not delivered on their housing promises and not yet met any of their annual targets, especially as the cost of living continues to balloon. In January, Windsor's average rent across all housing types was $1,644 per month.
"People are feeling the pinch," said Miljan.
"Even though our housing prices probably aren't as high as they are in other regions, they're still high. Rent is still really difficult for a lot of people to afford."
The NDP has promised to assist with affordability by introducing a monthly grocery rebate for lower- and middle-income families. It also plans to establish a consumer watchdog to crack down on grocery price gouging.
The Liberals have pledged relief with a 22 per cent cut to the middle-income tax bracket, scrapping HST on home heating and hydro, and doubling Ontario Disability Support Program benefits.
Although Windsor has pushed for greater provincial support to tackle the homelessness and addiction crises, with city council urging the Ford government to create a dedicated Ministry of Homelessness, Miljan does not foresee this issue shaping the outcome of the election. ADVAN CED VOTING: Ontarians have multiple opportunities to cast their ballots before election day on Feb. 27, including advance voting that opens Thursday. For information about advance voting locations in Windsor-Essex, visit voterinformationservice.elections.on.ca. mmazak@postmedia.com

Subject: Political campaigns; Manufacturers; Local elections; Cost of living; Manufacturing; Voters; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Manufacturers Cost of living Manufacturing Tariffs

Location: United States--US

Identifier / keyword: ontario; voters; polls; issues; trump; tariffs

Document 25 of 500

Smith to skip U.S. meeting amid work on budget; Premier pushes for cross-border talks to continue

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/smith-skip-u-s-meeting-amid-work-on-budget/docview/3168912956/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Smith+to+skip+U.S.+meeting+amid+work+on+budget%3B+Premier+pushes+for+cross-border+talks+to+continue&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith is cancelling a trip to Washington, D.C., planned for this week, but says she's not giving up on her strategy of face-to-face engagement with U.S. lawmakers to head off the threat of tariffs.
Smith says she's skipping the 2025 Winter Meeting of the National Governors Association, set to get underway on Thursday, to put the finishing touches on Alberta's forthcoming budget.
"We've decided to remain in Alberta this week to prepare for the budget that will be released next week," Smith said in a statement to National Post.
Smith's cancellation comes one week after she joined her fellow premiers for a diplomatic mission to Washington that garnered middling reviews.
The 13 premiers managed to score a White House meeting with two mid-level Trump officials, but the visit ended on a sour note when one of the meeting's hosts poked fun at the delegation on social media.
"To be clear, we never agreed that Canada would not be the 51st state," Trump aide James Blair said in response to a recap of the meeting attributed to B.C. Premier David Eby.
The premiers' charm offensive also didn't stop President Donald Trump from singling out Canada repeatedly last week when announcing his plan for reciprocal tariffs on U.S. trade partners.
Despite last week's mishaps, Smith is still pushing for cross-border dialogue.
"Our diplomacy in the U.S. has been working and we' ll continue to do that work," Smith said on Tuesday.
Smith travelled to Las Vegas in December to attend the Western Governors' Association Winter Meeting and signed an energy pact with 14 U.S. governors one month earlier.
Her cancellation means that just two premiers, Ontario's Doug Ford and Nova Scotia's Tim Houston, will be in Washington for the governors' meeting.
Jamie Tronnes, executive director of the Center for North America Prosperity and Security in Washington, said several governors will be disappointed by the low Canadian turnout.
"Governors are worried about how retaliatory tariffs on things like, for example, Kentucky bourbon, will affect their states and want to be seen as doing something about the issue," said Tronnes.
Tronnes added that some U.S. holiday destinations are already seeing a drop-off in tourism dollars from Canadian travellers.
"Just a 10 per cent drop in Canadian tourism would be a $2.1 billion (USD) loss to the American economy," he said. "Right now, what we're looking at is a 25 per cent drop, depending on how things go and how long it will be sustained for."
National Post rmohamed@postmedia.com

Subject: Liquor; Governors; Tourism; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

Company / organization: Name: National Post; NAICS: 513110

Document 26 of 500

Ontario election 2025: Can New Democrats hold on in Windsor West?

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-can-new-democrats-hold-on/docview/3168661033/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election+2025%3A+Can+New+Democrats+hold+on+in+Windsor+West%3F&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's New Democrats have kept Windsor West in its corner for a decade, but, with voting day just a week away, how secure is the party's grip on the only riding west of London that isn't Tory blue?
The riding's strong NDP loyalty was put to the test in the last two provincial elections when the winning party's lead over the Progressive Conservatives narrowed significantly. The Liberal Party, which dominated the Windsor West polls for years until Lisa Gretzky's 2014 win for the NDP, isn't even on the ballot this time.
PC Leader Doug Ford chose Windsor West to launch his re-election campaign in January. He used the Ambassador Bridge as a backdrop to illustrate the threat of tariffs from across the border, but the location also signalled the Tories see a strong opportunity to flip the riding and unseat the NDP incumbent.
"It was no coincidence that they launched their campaign in that riding to really boost their candidate's appeal," University of Windsor political science professor Lydia Miljan told the Star. "I think the (PC) party certainly had considered Windsor West being in play for them."
"They want sort of a fortress in Southern Ontario. It just looks nice on a map. There's part of that. But I think, just in general, political parties go where they think they can win and where they see the opposition being vulnerable.
"And because the results of the last election were so close, they probably feel they are in striking distance of flipping it toward their column."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford visited the riding of Windsor West and posed in the shadow of the Ambassador Bridge in Windsor on Wednesday, Jan.29, 2025, to launch his re-election campaign focused on threatened U.S tariffs.
The city's deep labour roots, strong union presence, and large industrial workforce had helped make Windsor West a safe NDP bet for over a decade. For this election, the Windsor & District Labour Council, representing more than 31,000 workers in the region, has thrown its support behind all four area NDP candidates.
PC candidate Tony Francis could make Windsor West a riding to watch when Ontarians head to the polls Feb. 27.
Doug Ford has leveraged the U.S. tariffs threat - with the potential impact most deeply felt by Windsor-Essex's automotive and manufacturing sectors - to position himself as the candidate best equipped to protect jobs and Ontario's economy.
Ontario's PC leader said he called the election more than a year early to secure a stronger mandate to navigate the expected turmoil of President Donald Trump's policies over the next four year.
Opposition politicians, including Windsor West incumbent MPP Gretzky - a longtime critic of Ford's handling of health care, housing, and the rising cost of living - dismissed his snap election call as a self-serving political move.
The ballot in Windsor West includes the Green Party's Nick Kowalski, New Blue's Joshua Griffin, Ontario Party's Matthew Giancola, and the None of the Above Direct Democracy Party's Mark Dewdney.
Adding another twist to the race, the Liberals - who held the riding years before Gretzky's first win in 2014 - are sitting this one out, leaving the riding without a candidate representing the party that governed Ontario until Ford's first majority victory in 2018.
"If you consider that there's no Liberal candidate, that does make it more interesting," said Miljan.
"Presumably, it might actually help Lisa Gretzky hold on to the riding, just because she might be able to pick up some of those Liberal voters.
"If the Liberals had fielded a candidate, it would have been a little bit more difficult for Lisa to hold on. But having no other left-of-centre major party run could be enough to keep Lisa Gretzky in that seat, because she can hold on to all of the progressive vote."
The past two provincial elections have seen the Liberals slip to third place in Windsor West, and the PCs advance as the NDP's leading competitor.
In 2022, Gretzky captured 42 per cent of the vote, but she saw a big dip in total votes (to 13,395 from 20,276 in 2018). The second-place PC candidates garnered over 11,000 votes in each of the previous two elections, but John Leontowicz squeezed the gap with 35 per cent of the total in 2022. Gretzky won in 2018 with 52 per cent of the vote, followed by the PC's Adam Ibrahim, with 28.5 per cent.
In 2014, the PCs were a distant third, trailing both the NDP and Liberals with just 5,225 votes - or 14 per cent.
"I think there could be a number of different factors," Miljan said.
"There's demographic changes. There's also organizational changes on the ground. Then there's the whole thing that is lower voter turnout. So to what extent the people who stayed home could have been disaffected Liberals, that could have had an impact."
Voter turnout in Windsor West dipped in 2022, with only 33.6 per cent of eligible voters showing up at the polls - down from 43 per cent in 2018, and among the worst turnouts across Ontario.
Also this election, there are no candidates' debates in Windsor West due to a lack of interest among those vying against the incumbent. Still, Miljan believes Windsor West's PC candidate won't have trouble reaching voters.
"The Francis name is certainly well-known in the Windsor region, just because of our history with the former mayor," Miljan said, referring to Eddie Francis, who is related.
Related
Labour council endorses all local NDP candidates as PCs get Windsor union support
Analysis: Doug Ford touts Tories as party for workers in Windsor - not everyone buying it
Ontario election: Windsor West boasted one of province's poorest 2022 voter turnouts
Both the local ridings of Essex and Windsor-Tecumseh - historically, contests between the NDP and Liberals - turned Tory blue in the 2022 election, marking a conservative shift in the region's political landscape.
With Windsor West the only patch of orange in a Southwest Ontario political map painted blue - and Ford's PCs dominating the opinion polls ahead of advanced voting that starts Thursday - the question now is whether Windsor West remains NDP, or joins a surrounding sea of blue.
mmazak@postmedia.com

Subject: Provincial elections; Election results; Candidates; Voters; Politics; Voter behavior; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Document 27 of 500

Ontario election: Windsor West boasted one of province's poorest 2022 voter turnouts

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-windsor-west-boasted-one/docview/3168633452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+Windsor+West+boasted+one+of+province%27s+poorest+2022+voter+turnouts&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here's what you need to know about Windsor West for the Feb. 27 Ontario election (advance voting Feb. 20-22):
Windsor West has a population of more than 130,000 and covers 83 square kilometres, according to Elections Ontario, which uses 2021 census data.
The riding is bound to the south by the city's border with LaSalle and Tecumseh and to the north and west by the Detroit River. To the east, Windsor West is generally bound by Langlois Avenue but juts out to Pillette Road between Tecumseh Road East and the airport.
It encompasses both Windsor hospitals, the Ambassador Bridge, and most of what should become Ojibway National Urban Park.
The riding was created in 1996 when the Mike Harris PC government changed provincial riding boundaries to match federal ones. The new Windsor West included parts of former Windsor-Sandwich and Windsor-Walkerville ridings.
Elections Ontario map showing the electoral boundaries for the provincial riding of Windsor West
The NDP has held the riding since Lisa Gretzky's election in 2014. Before her, Windsor West was Liberal red for 15 years, with Sandra Pupatello (1999-2011) and Teresa Piruzza (2011-2014).
In the 2022 provincial election, 32,039 of 95,295 registered voters (33.6 per cent) cast ballots, one of the worst turnouts across Ontario. Gretzky won with 42 per cent of the vote, followed by PC candidate John Leontowicz (35 per cent) and Liberal Linda McCurdy (13 per cent).
Here are the 2025 candidates. Note: Matthew Giancola (Ontario Party) is on the ballot but entered the race too late to be included here; the Liberals briefly had a candidate who subsequently withdrew.
MARK DEWDNEY, None of the Above Direct Democracy Party
Age: 51
"A big part of why I'm running is because party politicians don't know what to think - they're mouthpieces for their top donors." Also running to send the message that people are "tired of government making things more expensive and giving us less every year for it."
Top priorities: Diversifying Windsor's economy by attracting medical research and education; reducing red tape and government bureaucracy with automation; amplifying community paramedicine; and "being able to give the disenfranchised a true voice."
TONY FRANCIS, Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario
Age: 61
Running to "continue to build on the progress" the Doug Ford PCs have "achieved to grow our economy, create new jobs, protect livelihoods and keep (us) competitive, not just a week, or a month but for years to come."
Tony Francis, Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario candidate for Windsor West.
Top priorities: Building Ontario's economy with infrastructure investments; protecting workers in the face of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats "by doing whatever is necessary to protect people's livelihoods in our community," including "investing tens of billions of dollars to keep people working;" fighting the federal carbon tax and urging Ottawa to cut red tape; banning drug consumption sites near schools and daycares and clearing encampments from public parks; and building a new Windsor hospital.
LISA GRETZKY, Ontario New Democratic Party
Age: 54
"I am proud to represent Windsor West both here at home and at Queen's Park. I have been a fighter for this community by speaking truth to power and bringing important pieces of legislation forward that have directly impacted this region," including introducing anti-scab labour legislation, the Intimate Partner Violence Epidemic Act, the More Than a Visitor Act for seniors in long-term care; banning iGaming ads, Noah and Gregory's law, Homelessness Task Force Act, and a motion to help create the Ojibway National Urban Park.
Lisa Gretzky, Ontario New Democratic Party candidate for Windsor West.
Top priorities: "As the member of provincial Parliament for the last decade, I am hearing more about the cost of living, lack of affordable housing, our decimated public healthcare system, lack of access to family doctors and crumbling school conditions for our kids. We need investments in mental health and addictions supports. The record number of food bank users including seniors and children living in poverty, especially in Windsor, is alarming and a clear failure of government."
JOSHUA GRIFFIN, New Blue Party
Age: 44
"I am running because I believe the current fiscal situation in Ontario is dire. The current government's spending and healthcare policies, and cuts to education programs are unacceptable. The Ontario Conservatives have not provided the change they promised, and the NDP's track record on fiscal responsibility is concerning. The rising cost of living, increased use of food banks, and growth of tent cities demonstrate the urgent need for change. Additionally, the high salaries of hospital CEOs amidst a shortage of frontline healthcare staff are deeply troubling."
Joshua Griffin, New Blue Party of Ontario candidate for Windsor West.
Top priorities: Fight for increased staffing and bed space at local hospitals; implement a "fair, across-the-board tax cut;" ensure proper educational assistant staffing at area schools; attract more mental health professionals to Windsor West through a targeted ad campaign; and advocate for a standalone medical school in Windsor and encourage people to enter family and emergency medicine with a loan forgiveness program.
NICK KOLASKY, Green Party of Ontario
Kolasky could not be reached for comment but did send the Star a news release about his candidacy in which he states that, after a successful career in manufacturing, he chose to relocate to Windsor and has pursued his passion for photography and protecting the environment.
Nick Kolasky, Green Party of Ontario candidate for Windsor West.
"We need more strong, independent voices we can trust to protect Ontario's economy and our environment," Kolasky said. "With the Ontario Greens, I'm committed to protecting the people and places we love from corporate greed and Doug Ford's short-sighted policies."
tcampbell@postmedia.com

Subject: Workforce planning; Provincial elections; Cost of living; Mental health; Health care policy; Elections; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Workforce planning Cost of living

Company / organization: Name: Democratic Party; NAICS: 813940; Name: Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario; NAICS: 813940

Identifier / keyword: windsor; ontario; election; voting; population; covers

Document 28 of 500

Can New Democrats hold on in Windsor West?; Ontario Election 2025 Progressive Conservatives see strong opportunity to flip riding

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/can-new-democrats-hold-on-windsor-west-ontario/docview/3168494770/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+New+Democrats+hold+on+in+Windsor+West%3F%3B+Ontario+Election+2025+Progressive+Conservatives+see+strong+opportunity+to+flip+riding&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 29 of 500

Riding had poor turnout in 2022's provincial vote; Ontario Election NDP hopes to hang onto Windsor West with withdrawal of Liberal candidate

Author: Tayl Or Campbell

Publication info: Tayl Or Campbell. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/riding-had-poor-turnout-2022s-provincial-vote/docview/3168493561/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Riding+had+poor+turnout+in+2022%27s+provincial+vote%3B+Ontario+Election+NDP+hopes+to+hang+onto+Windsor+West+with+withdrawal+of+Liberal+candidate&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Tayl+Or+Campbell&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here's what you need to know about Windsor West for the Feb. 27 Ontario election (advance voting Feb. 20-22):
Windsor West has a population of more than 130,000 and covers 83 square kilometres, according to Elections Ontario, which uses 2021 census data.

Here are the 2025 candidates. Matthew Giancola (Ontario Party) is on the ballot but entered the race too late to be included here; the Liberals briefly had a candidate who subsequently withdrew.
MARK DEWDNEY, NONE OF THE ABOVE DIRECT DEMOCRACY PARTY Age: 51
"A big part of why I'm running is because party politicians don't know what to think - they're mouthpieces for their top donors." Also running to send the message that people are "tired of government making things more expensive and giving us less every year for it."
Top priorities: Diversifying Windsor's economy by attracting medical research and education; reducing red tape and government bureaucracy with automation; amplifying community paramedicine; and "being able to give the disenfranchised a true voice."
TONY FRANCIS, PROGRESSIVE CON SERVATIVE PARTY OF ONTARIO Age: 61
Running to "continue to build on the progress" the Doug Ford PCs have "achieved to grow our economy, create new jobs, protect livelihoods and keep (us) competitive, not just a week, or a month but for years to come."
Top priorities: Building Ontario's economy with infrastructure investments; protecting workers in the face of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats "by doing whatever is necessary to protect people's livelihoods in our community," including "investing tens of billions of dollars to keep people working;" fighting the federal carbon tax and urging Ottawa to cut red tape; banning drug consumption sites near schools and daycares and clearing encampments from public parks; and building a new LISA GRET ZKY, ONTARIO NEW DEMOCRATIC PARTY Age: 54
"I am proud to represent Windsor West both here at home and at Queen's Park. I have been a fighter for this community by speaking truth to power and bringing important pieces of legislation forward that have directly impacted this region," including introducing anti-scab labour legislation, the Intimate Partner Violence Epidemic Act, the More Than a Visitor Act for seniors in long-term care; banning iGaming ads, Noah and Gregory's law, Homelessness Task Force Act, and a motion to help create the Ojibway National Urban Park.
Top priorities: "As the member of provincial Parliament for the last decade, I am hearing more about the cost of living, lack of affordable housing, our decimated public healthcare system, lack of access to family doctors and crumbling school conditions for our kids. We need investments in mental health and addictions supports. The record number of food bank users including seniors and children living in poverty, especially in Windsor, is alarming and a clear failure of government."
JOSHUA GRIFFIN, NEW BLUE PARTY Age: 44
"I am running because I believe the current fiscal situation in Ontario is dire. The current government's spending and healthcare policies, and cuts to education programs are unacceptable. The Ontario Conservatives have not provided the change they promised, and the NDP's track record on fiscal responsibility is concerning. The rising cost of living, increased use of food banks, and growth of tent cities demonstrate the urgent need for change. Addition- ally, the high salaries of hospital CEOs amidst a shortage of front line healthcare staff are deeply troubling."
Top priorities: Fight for increased staffing and bed space at local hospitals; implement a "fair, across-the-board tax cut;" ensure proper educational assistant staffing at area schools; attract more mental health professionals to Windsor West through a targeted ad campaign; and advocate for a stand-alone medical school in Windsor and encourage people to enter family and emergency medicine with a loan forgiveness program.
NICK KOLASKY, GREEN PARTY OF ONTARIO
Kolasky could not be reached for comment but did send the Star a news release about his candidacy in which he states that, after a successful career in manufacturing, he chose to relocate to Windsor and has pursued his passion for photography and protecting the environment.
"We need more strong, independent voices we can trust to protect Ontario's economy and our environment," Kolasky said. "With the Ontario Greens, I'm committed to protecting the people and places we love from corporate greed and Doug Ford's short-sighted policies."
tcampbell@postmedia.com HERE'S HOW TO CAST YOUR VOTE ON OR BEFORE FEB. 27
Ontario's snap election is set for Feb. 27. Here's what you need to know about casting a ballot. WHO IS ELIGIBLE TO VOTE? Residents of Ontario who are Canadian citizens and 18 or older can vote.
WHAT DO I NEED TO VOTE?
Those who are not already registered must add or update their information at registertovoteon.ca to receive a voter information card before election day. Elections Ontario says the cards with information about your riding and assigned voting locations will be in the mail this week, from Monday to Saturday. You must bring your voter card and one piece of identification that shows your name when you go to vote. If you don't receive a voter card, you can still cast a ballot by bringing a piece of ID that shows both your name and home address to the polling station. Elections Ontario says photo ID is not required.
HOW DO I VOTE IN ADVANCE OR BY MAIL?
If you need to vote by mail, you must apply online before 6 p.m. ET on Friday to receive a voting kit. For your mail-in vote to be counted, Elections Ontario must receive a completed kit by 6 p.m. ET on Feb. 27. You can vote in person at your local election office up until 6 p.m. on Feb. 26. Voters can also head to advance polls in their electoral districts from Thursday to Saturday. Locations will be listed on the Elections Ontario website. The website also has information for those who may be eligible to vote from home or a hospital under certain circumstances. HOW DO I VOTE ON ELECTION DAY? You can cast a ballot at your assigned polling station from 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. on Feb. 27.

Subject: Workforce planning; Provincial elections; Local elections; Cost of living; Mental health; Health care policy; Voters; Voter behavior
