Document 65 of 500

Ontario Election 2025: Affordability the focus as tariffs take a pause

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-affordability-focus-as/docview/3164337071/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Election+2025%3A+Affordability+the+focus+as+tariffs+take+a+pause&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Homeless encampments, tax cuts, and highway tolls - the focus of Ontario's election campaign has suddenly shifted.
U.S. President Donald Trump was set to impose crippling 25 per cent tariffs against Canadian products on Tuesday. But that was staved off at the last minute following conversations between Trump and Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
With that threat on the backburner, Ontario election candidates are tackling other issues.
Tariffs knocked out of the spotlight
Ontario's main political parties pivoted to affordability measures Wednesday, as the threat of U.S. tariffs took a back seat on the campaign trail.
The NDP and Liberal leaders focused on housing and affordability issues, with promises to end homeless encampments and provide more financial support for people with disabilities.
The Progressive Conservatives pledged to make an ongoing tax cut for gasoline and diesel fuel permanent and also remove tolls from the publicly owned portion of Highway 407 east of Toronto.
The affordability crisis has dogged Doug Ford's government over the past several years and was set to be a major campaign issue before Donald Trump won the 2024 U.S. presidential election.
Homeless encampments sprouted up across Ontario during the pandemic and have increased in number since. There were some 1,400 encampments in the province in 2023, the Association of Municipalities of Ontario found.
Related
Analysis: Doug Ford touts Tories as party for workers in Windsor - not everyone buying it
Where are they? Liberals, Greens remain Ontario election no-shows in Windsor-Essex
More than 80,000 people were homeless in Ontario last year, the association said in a groundbreaking report based on provincial data released in January.
Stiles listed several steps she would take to end encampments in communities across the province, calling their presence Ford's "greatest failure." But she would not say how much her plan would cost, saying only "that will all come" later.
The association of municipalities has said $11 billion over 10 years would be required to end chronic homelessness.
Meanwhile, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie vowed to double payments under the Ontario Disability Support Program, which currently maxes out at $1,368 per month for a single person. She said the boost would be permanent, pegged to inflation and phased in over two years
The Canadian Press
Opposition leaders pounce on Ford
Opposition leaders accused Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford on Thursday of being partially responsible for the behaviour of two former staffers alleged to have used their contacts in a real-estate rezoning scheme.
Oakville, Ont., property owner Amanpreet Jakhar and two numbered companies have filed a $2.2-million lawsuit against former government staffers Ryan Amato and Shiv Raj, as well as developer Frontier Group.
Jakhar is suing for negligence, breach of contract, fraudulent misrepresentation and other alleged offences. Jakhar and the companies say they wanted to have three properties rezoned so they could be developed. They allege Amato, Raj and the Frontier Group pledged to rezone those properties "using their backchannel contacts and political connections."
Amato and Raj, through their lawyer, denied the allegations as "false and baseless accusations which do not reflect the high level of integrity and professionalism" to which the men hold themselves.
Both acted in "full compliance with the law," Joshua Henderson said.
Frontier Group's lawyer did not immediately respond to a request for comment, but all three defendants have filed notices in court of their intent to fight against the lawsuit.
The Canadian Press.
Where the leaders are
Here's where the leaders of Ontario's main political parties are on Thursday, Feb. 6:
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford
Waterloo: Ford is set to visit two manufacturing facilities in the region and hold a media availability.
-
NDP Leader Marit Stiles
Hamilton: Stiles is set to make a campaign stop at noon.
Sault Ste. Marie: She will then make another campaign stop at a café at 6:30 p.m.
-
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie
Toronto: Crombie is scheduled to make an announcement about health care in Scarborough at 9 a.m.
Thunder Bay: She will be campaigning there at 5:30 p.m.
-
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner
Guelph: Schreiner will make an announcement at 10:30 a.m., before campaigning in the city with volunteers.
Hamilton: He is set to kick off the party's campaign with local candidate Lucia Iannantuono at 2 p.m.
The Canadian Press

NDP candidate announced for Chatham-Kent-Leamington

Author: Terfloth, Trevor

Publication info: Terfloth, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-candidate-announced-chatham-kent-leamington/docview/3164336980/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+candidate+announced+for+Chatham-Kent-Leamington&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Terfloth%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario New Democrats announced their candidate for Chatham-Kent-Leamington for the provincial election.
Christian Sachs, a trustee with the Thames Valley District School Board, will run in the Southwestern Ontario riding.
She is a "dedicated mother of five, passionate community volunteer, and advocate for Ontario's children and families," the party stated on its website.
It added as a trustee she works to improve education, hold the system accountable, and ensure children have the support to thrive.
"I am honoured to be the NDP candidate for Chatham-Kent-Leamington," Sachs said in release Wednesday. "After seven years of Conservatives, life is harder for everyone and I am committed to continue serving communities across our region with everything I have.
"Under the leadership of Marit Stiles, the Ontario NDP will build housing, fix health care, invest in education and make life more affordable."
As a volunteer with the Home and School Association, Sachs helps raise funds for local schools.
"I am very excited to have Christian on the NDP team," Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles said in the release. "You deserve to have a government on your side and Christian is ready to be a strong voice at Queen's Park.
"Christian is a dedicated advocate for her community and is committed to sustainability and rural communities. Together we will create a better and brighter future for Ontarians."
PC incumbent Trevor Jones, elected to his first term in 2022, is the other declared candidate for the riding.
That year, he won with 17,522 votes, ahead of second-place New Democrat candidate Brock McGregor, who had 11,163 votes.
Rick Nicholls had 5,478 votes for the Ontario Party, while New Blue's Rhonda Jubenville had 1,463.
Green candidate Jennifer Surerus had 1,244 votes.
The Elections Ontario deadline to get on the ballot is Feb. 13. Election day is Feb. 27.
tterfloth@postmedia.com

Reader letters: Doug Ford - C'mon and let us know, should he stay or should he go?

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/reader-letters-doug-ford-cmon-let-us-know-should/docview/3164246791/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reader+letters%3A+Doug+Ford+-+C%27mon+and+let+us+know%2C+should+he+stay+or+should+he+go%3F&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Time to show exit door to Mr. Ford
How is it possible for Doug Ford to believe he is entitled to a third term as premier when he has failed to govern well with his existing mandate?
He commissioned reports on post-secondary education, safe treatment and injection sites, femicide and the epidemic of intimate partner violence (IPV). Once the reports were received, he promptly ignored the recommendations and in the case of IPV curtailed the committee work. The crisis continues in each of these vital areas.
He squandered tax dollars - up to $1.4 billion to expand the alcohol market, $3 billion in taxpayer payoffs and undetermined dollars for the Greenbelt fiasco which is still under RCMP investigation
This money could have been invested in children's mental health services and other provincial interventions like affordable housing. When the Children's Aid Societies raised the dire consequences of underfunding and the ensuing crisis in children's mental health services, Ford callously ignored their pleas and instead ordered an audit of their budgets.
School boards have faced similar threats and as a result we see a dismantling of key programs which should be enhanced.
The Ford government has increased income insecurity, undermined public services and left us ill-prepared to weather the future economic uncertainties.
It is time to show the exit door to Mr. Ford.
Marion Overholt
Windsor
Trump 'just what we needed' for Canada
Re: Brown-John: Return of the American bull in a china shop (Opinion, Jan. 17)
I can't tell you how much I enjoy reading Lloyd Brown-John, as he turns delirious with Trump Derangement Syndrome.
Yes, Trump can be bombastic and childish at times, but threatening Canada with 25% tariffs is just what we needed to get us out of our complacent comfort zone.
Cutting the apron strings, though difficult at first, will help us regain our status as world leaders in all areas of commerce, science, medicine and defence.
The upside is much greater than the downside.
Rick Mailloux
Windsor
Doug Ford should run on current record
I would like to remind Doug Ford that the reason we have fixed election dates is so politicians don't call early elections to take advantage of favourable polls, regardless of what kind of excuse he can make up.
If he wants to call an early election, though, I think he should run on
his current record of putting Ontario into "have-not" status.
If he wants to defend us from anyone, he can defend us from western premiers who incorrectly assume that we are getting money from their taxpayers. Apparently, they think the 40% of equalization payments that come from Ontario taxpayers when we are a "have" province magically manifests itself out of thin air when we are not.
Tim Bilida
Windsor
$200 cheque won't buy my vote
Regarding Doug Ford calling an early election - he needs to remember Ontario residents he ignored and undervalued in his time as premier.
Ontario students, teachers and special needs children. Nurses, lack of family doctors, serious wait times for health care. Closing Ontario Science Centre without consultation; backdoor deals like trying to overturn the Greenbelt lands; an obscene 95-year deal for a high-end spa.
Homelessness, food inequity, lack of affordable housing - a $200 cheque won't buy my vote.
MaryAnn Benoit
Windsor
Related
Doug Ford uses Windsor's Ambassador Bridge as backdrop for campaign launch
My $200 goes to Windsor's Downtown Mission
I tested Doug Ford's claim he is accessible to anyone and sent him an email. I never received a reply.
I wanted to know why he is wasting over $150 million on an election under the guise of clarifying his 'mandate.' He has been premier for years; I find it hard to believe he still doesn't know what his job is.
Now I read that he wasted $1.4 billion to expand liquor sales - I have yet to hear anyone complain about being unable to buy a six-pack, but I have heard stories about people sitting in the ER for hours.
If this money was available, why was he not using it to fix our health care system? Having alcohol available in big-box stores only lines the pockets of corporations already fleecing the public.
But rather than dealing with the issues, his government put a law in place forcing people into homes they do not want to go to under threat of financial ruin. He spent another $3 billion to send cheques to everybody rather than focusing on getting money to struggling families - his wealthy investment banker buddy does not need $200.
And then, of course, there was the attempted parceling out of the Greenbelt to his developer friends, which only warranted an "apology" from Ford when he got caught.
Ford and the Progressive Conservatives have shown time and time again that they do not stand "for the people," no matter how many shiny commercials they produce with our money to convince us otherwise.
Americans failed to pay attention during their last election. I hope Ontarians are not as complacent or easily duped.
I will be giving my cheque to the Downtown Mission.
My vote is not for sale.
Siggi Mosmann
Windsor

Document 68 of 500

Analysis: Doug Ford touts Tories as party for workers in Windsor - not everyone buying it

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/analysis-doug-ford-touts-tories-as-party-workers/docview/3164246789/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Analysis%3A+Doug+Ford+touts+Tories+as+party+for+workers+in+Windsor+-+not+everyone+buying+it&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Not all that long ago, it would have been a surprising sight - a provincial Tory leader donning union garb and courting autoworkers.
It's likely not the image most would conjure when thinking of the Progressive Conservatives, a party often seen as more aligned with big business interests and reducing the power of unions.
But there was Premier Doug Ford last week in Windsor, pulling a Unifor hoodie over his head, posing for photos with union members, and promising to support Ontario workers.
Unifor Local 200 president John D'Agnolo, left, chats with Ontario Premier Doug Ford on Wednesday, Jan. 29, 2025, at the Local 444 union hall.
"When it comes to investment for auto, he has supported us," Unifor Local 200 president John D'Agnolo, who represents Ford workers, told the Star.
"Historically, when we looked at Conservatives and support on future investments, it was a struggle. That's not the case with Doug. He has offered the money to get production in Windsor, in Brampton, in Oakville. That has not been the case traditionally.
"We have really struggled at times, both federally and provincially, when we're looking for future investment and money from governments."
The Unifor folks have been a popular bunch lately, with their Turner Road union hall playing host to several political big hitters over the last couple of weeks. On Jan. 16, it was outgoing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
Following Ford's campaign trail stopover last Wednesday, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, whose party has long held the image of a labour ally, dropped in on Friday.
Call it a recognition of the political heft of labour and its vital importance to the economy.
Flanked by NDP candidates Lisa Gretzky (Windsor West), left, and Rachael Mills (Essex), right, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles speaks on Friday, Jan. 31, 2025, at the Unifor Local 444 Union Hall in Windsor.
"We'll continue to drive that home to all three parties, how important this auto industry is to our communities," said D'Agnolo. "Not just Windsor, it's throughout Ontario and Canada. We can never have them forget that."
Ford called a snap election last week, stating he needs a new mandate to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian products.
He used the Ambassador Bridge, the country's most important trade crossing, as backdrop for his reelection campaign kickoff on Wednesday in Windsor.
"We will do whatever it takes to protect workers and their livelihoods," he said. "We will invest tens of billions of dollars in new spending to keep people working. We will work with unions and businesses to retain and re-skill our hardest-hit workers."
Ford followed that up on Thursday with a visit to London, where he promised big investments to protect Ontario autoworkers.
The Tories also projected a distinct pro-worker image leading up to the last election cycle, with Ford stating at one point in 2021, "I will break a brick wall down to support them."
Ford received endorsements from several unions in 2022.
In recent years, the Ford government pledged millions of dollars to train autoworkers. It also came to the table with incentives to keep the NextStar Energy battery plant in Windsor-Essex and thousands of jobs that come with it.
Ford recently said his government will maintain its share of EV battery production subsidies.
Last week, LiUNA (Labourers International Union of North America) announced it is endorsing Ford in the current election. On Monday, the Ontario Professional Fire Fighters Association (OPFFA) threw its support behind Ford, giving the Tories their first-ever endorsement from a public sector union.
But for many unions and worker advocates, the image of Doug Ford - friend of the worker - is hard to accept.
"It's a gimmick," said Laura Walton, president of the Ontario Federation of Labour (OFL). "In the last seven years, if we're talking about what Ford has done, let's talk about how he hasn't protected workers. People should not be fooled by gimmicky slogans or photo ops in our communities."
The Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE) Ontario has launched a "Fire the Boss" campaign aimed at its 290,000 members in the province. The satirical campaign encourages workers to "send packing the one boss they can fire: Premier Doug Ford."
"Ford might want to shift attention to Trump's threats, but CUPE members will go to the polls to vote for stronger public services and a new premier who will deliver honestly for working-class Ontarians," said CUPE Ontario president Fred Hahn.
"To do that, we're keeping the focus where it belongs - on out-of-touch multimillionaire Doug Ford and his nearly seven-year record of damage and deceit."
Ford first came to power in 2018 on a campaign to make Ontario "open for business."
Shortly after that, his government introduced a bill essentially undoing labour reforms brought in by the previous Liberal government.
It froze Ontario's minimum wage at $14 for two years. It repealed former premier Kathleen Wynne's labour legislation giving all Ontario workers a minimum of two sick days and requiring employers to pay part-time and casual staff the same rate as full-time workers.
The bill also repealed changes to the Labour Relations Act that made it easier for workers in some sectors to join a union.
In 2019, the Ford government introduced Bill 124, which froze public sector wages. The Court of Appeal for Ontario later ruled the legislation was unconstitutional.
"All the way along, there has been no sign of him working for workers," said Walton. "And now that we're on the campaign trail, we want workers to be a backdrop, but workers are not pawns for politics."
Ford and the Tories rode to a second majority in 2022 as the "Working for Workers" party, pulling down endorsements from several unions.
Unifor Local 200 president John D'Agnolo is shown at the Unifor Local 444 union hall on Friday, Jan. 31, 2025.
But that year he also drew the ire of many unions, including some who had endorsed him, with his government's Keeping Students in Class Act. Also known as Bill 28, it imposed a contract on 55,000 CUPE education workers and made it illegal from them to go on strike.
D'Agnolo also had his issues with Ford. He was a signatory to a Unifor open letter in 2022 which criticized Bill 28, calling it "excessive, imprudent and unfair."
Yet when it comes to defending against U.S. tariffs, and protecting worker interests in general, D'Agnolo told the Star he'll talk to anyone willing to listen.
Unifor national president Lana Payne advised her members this week that Canada's largest private-sector union "is not endorsing a party in this Ontario election."
Related
Doug Ford uses Windsor's Ambassador Bridge as backdrop for campaign launch
Ontario Election 2025: Trump tariffs hitting as election picks up steam
D'Agnolo said he believes the Tories have "realized how important auto is to Ontario."
"Every party, bring them in, because this is so important," he said. "We have enough battles as is, and we have another one with tariffs.
"So we have to make sure that we bring everybody in to make sure what's important, especially with the tariffs, is that they're all on the same page."
twilhelm@postmedia.com

Subject: Political campaigns; Workers; Presidents; Strikes; Endorsements; Public sector; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Workers Strikes Public sector Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada

Identifier / keyword: surprising; sight; provincial; leader; donning; union

Document 69 of 500

Rumoured east Windsor Costco gets green light from Windsor committee

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/rumoured-east-windsor-costco-gets-green-light/docview/3164241752/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rumoured+east+Windsor+Costco+gets+green+light+from+Windsor+committee&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Windsor may be one more step closer to having a second Costco location.
City council's development committee this week backed plans for a massive commercial build in east Windsor on land rumoured to have been selected for a Costco big-box store.
Plans for a one-storey building with a single horizontal stripe labelled "COSTCO RED" were included in documents presented to the committee. One page of the plans had the words "Costco prototype" at the bottom in a font that was the same colour as the document's background, making it visible only via search.
The proposed development, which is still subject to approval from council as a whole, would see two large commercial buildings and 1,385 parking spaces cover nearly 20 acres of land immediately north of the Home Depot on Tecumseh Road East.
Mayor Drew Dilkens would not confirm whether Windsor is getting another Costco but said the city and the company have been in communication about a second store location in Windsor.
"We've worked very hard in a very competitive environment to, uh, work with Costco as they contemplate a second location in our area," Dilkens told reporters during an unrelated media event at city hall on Monday.
"The land has been identified. A lot of work has been done behind the scenes. This will really fill in a piece of land in the city that is right for commercial and retail development."
Dilkens added that Costco is his "favourite place to go," and that Windsor's sole location on Walker Road is always very busy.
"They know they need another store, and we're hopeful that they choose Windsor, and that they get the right site, and that our investments and our commitment to them lead to some really great things happening in that part of our city on all fronts, including residential."
Another view of the east Windsor land targeted for a major commercial development is shown near Tecumseh Road East between Jefferson Boulevard and Lauzon Parkway on Tuesday, Feb.4, 2025.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford raised the topic of a second Costco coming to Windsor during a visit to the city in September.
"I know it may not seem big, but rumour has it, a Costco is coming to town. That's two to 300 more jobs. That's important, good-paying jobs," Ford said.
A story that followed was one of the Windsor Star's top-read online news articles of 2024, indicating strong community interest.
The development committee on Monday approved official plan amendments and rezoning to allow for commercial use on the land, which is made up of three lots and is abutted by Walmart, Home Depot, the Serbian Centre, and train tracks.
The approval also permits ambulance and food catering services on the site, as well as facilities for manufacturing, warehouses, food packaging, medical appliances, research and development, and stormwater management. It prohibits child care centres.
Rock Developments East Windsor Inc., proposes to develop the land for commercial purposes, with more than 24,000 square metres of ground floor area over multiple commercial pads.
The first phase of the project consists of a 15,071-square-metre, single-storey building with a gas bar and 967 parking spaces, the report said. Phase 2 includes a separate one-storey building that covers 9,537 square metres and 418 more parking spaces.
A stormwater facility will be built at the north end of the land, the report said.
"It's great to see the area being developed," said Ward 10 Coun. Jim Morrison, who chairs the development committee.
"There's been a lot of guesses about what is going in there, and you can see the infrastructure that we (the city) are going to do - we're doing a pretty significant development out this way."
Related
Second Windsor Costco big-box store heading to city's east side - source
Windsor looking at 'staggering' fee hikes for home builds, new development
The city's proposed 2025 capital budget includes nearly $20 million for road and stormwater infrastructure in the area, which is referred to as the Forest Glade North Secondary Plan and is generally bound by Jefferson Boulevard to the west, Tecumseh Road East to the south, Lauzon Parkway to the east, and the VIA Rail corridor to the north.
A budget document says improvements include extending Catherine Street west and south from Walmart to Tecumseh Road East; extending Roseville Gardens Drive from Tecumseh Road East north to Catherine Street; and constructing a regional stormwater management facility to service the area.
Dilkens said other development is expected to happen in the area, including multi-residential developments that will depend on the new stormwater facility.
"The prospect of what's happening there is really, really positive on a number of fronts and will certainly increase the property tax base in the City of Windsor quite handsomely," the mayor said.
tcampbell@postmedia.com( tcampbell@postmedia.com )

Subject: Committees; Stormwater management

Company / organization: Name: Costco Wholesale Corp; NAICS: 455211; Name: Walmart Inc; NAICS: 454110, 455110, 455211

Rumoured Costco in east end gets green light from committee

Document 71 of 500

Ford touts his Tories as party for workers - some feel 'it's a gimmick'; Analysis

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-touts-his-tories-as-party-workers-some-feel/docview/3164117528/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+touts+his+Tories+as+party+for+workers+-+some+feel+%27it%27s+a+gimmick%27%3B+Analysis&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 72 of 500

Canadian pride takes a surge; Tariffs Threat has Canadians pulling together, finding ways to avoid U.S. goods

Author: Knight, Chris

Publication info: Knight, Chris. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadian-pride-takes-surge-tariffs-threat-has/docview/3164117423/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+pride+takes+a+surge%3B+Tariffs+Threat+has+Canadians+pulling+together%2C+finding+ways+to+avoid+U.S.+goods&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Knight%2C+Chris&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If there's a silver lining to be found in the recent threat of an all-out trade war between the United States and Canada, it would seem to be a resurgence of pride among Canadians. That has manifested itself in positive ways, including buy-Canadian campaigns, as well as negative acts, such as the booing of the U.S. national anthem at sporting events.
On Wednesday, Montreal's Fairmount Bagel started selling a red-and-white "Canada Strong" variety of the popular baked good. In Ottawa, the makers of the "Canada Is Not For Sale" hat worn by Ontario Premier Doug Ford during a meeting of Canada's premiers has seen a huge jump in sales, while the hat's motto has become a social media hashtag.
Meanwhile, a moribund website, madeinca.ca, has seen a spike in traffic as Canadian producers and consumers rush to advertise and locate locally manufactured products. The site now includes a grocery shopping guide.
Loblaw, Canada's largest grocery retailer, announced Sunday that it would try to leapfrog U.S. purchases and buy from Mexico instead.
"Where we have had to purchase products made in the U.S., we will look for alternatives in- cluding products from Mexico which has also been subjected to these unnecessary tariffs," Loblaw chief executive Per Bank said, adding the U.S. decision was "wrong-headed."
On the social media site Reddit, the BuyCanadian forum - introduced in 2018 during an - other round of Trump trade tariffs - is seeing more comments as well.
And there are polling numbers to prove it. A recent survey by the Angus Reid Institute found that, between last December and this month, the number of Canadians describing themselves as "very proud" rose to 44 per cent from 34 per cent.
In the same period, those who said they had a "deep emotional attachment" to Canada rose by a similar amount, to 59 per cent in February from 49 per cent in December. And the number of people who thought Canada should join the U.S. fell to just four per cent, from an already meagre six per cent in December. The swelling pride was not evenly distributed across the country, however. The poll found the biggest increases were in British Columbia, Quebec and the Maritimes, which all saw rises of between 12 per cent and 15 per cent on the "proud or very proud" and "deep emotional attachment" questions.
Smaller gains of between three and nine per cent were seen in Alberta, Saskatchewan and Ontario, while Manitoba alone saw a slight drop of about three per cent.
The pollster pointed out that the shift to greater pride in Canada also bucks a trend that has seen pride fall gradually over the past several decades. "Deep emotional attachment" was cited by 65 per cent of respondents in a 1991 Angus Reid poll, falling to 62 per cent by 2016 and just 49 per cent last year, before the recent rebound.
A variety of polls has shown the number of people who are "very proud" to be Canadian has fallen slowly but steadily since the 1980s.
While it's impossible to say for certain whether Donald Trump's threats of tariffs and continued talk of annexation are the reason for Canadians' suddenly glowing hearts, the poll did find that the tariff issue was the most closely followed major news event since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020. Fully 56 per cent of respondents said they were following news of tariffs very closely, while another 33 per cent reported following them closely, for a total of 89 per cent. This topped other recent events such as the most recent federal budget (62 per cent), the Israel-Hamas War (80 per cent), Russia's invasion of Ukraine (66 per cent) and the WE Charity scandal (73 per cent).
One has to go back to February 2020 to find higher numbers, when 90 per cent were following the emergence of COVID-19 closely or very closely.
There is fear as well as general interest in the news of possible tariffs, with three per cent of respondents saying they expect to lose their job if tariffs are implemented, and a further 30 per cent saying they're concerned about what it would mean for their jobs.
The new poll was conducted online from Sunday to Monday, and carries a margin of error of plus or minus two percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
National Post

Subject: Political campaigns; Polls & surveys; Canadians; Social networks; COVID-19; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks Tariffs

Document 73 of 500

Liberals, Greens largely quiet so far in region

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-greens-largely-quiet-so-far-region/docview/3164117302/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals%2C+Greens+largely+quiet+so+far+in+region&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A week into the province's snap election campaign, the Ontario Liberal Party has yet to officially nominate candidates in any of the Essex County ridings.
The Green Party of Ontario has also not announced candidates for Windsor West, Windsor-Tecumseh, or Essex.
With only three weeks left to go before election day, the lack of candidates so far puts those parties at a clear disadvantage, said University of Windsor political science professor Lydia Miljan.
"Whoever they do nominate is only going to have a very short runway to door knock and to make connections," said Miljan. "It also suggests that they don't have a lot of enthusiastic people who want to run for the party."
The Ontario Liberal Party's head office did respond to the Star's request for comment. The Green Party of Ontario also did not respond to a request for comment.
Connor Logan, a 23-year-old University of Windsor political science graduate, told the Star Tuesday he will be the Liberal candidate in Windsor-Tecumseh and hopes to be officially nominated by the end of the week.
Husain Wiquar, president of the Windsor West Provincial Liberal Association (PLA), said the party had made its choice for candidate but was waiting on the paperwork to come back from the party's head office. He said Tuesday he did not know when the candidate will be confirmed and announced.
"We have been working on the candidate," he told the Star. "The papers have gone in and have not yet come back for the announcement. We are in the process of putting up a candidate for the Windsor West riding." Darcie Renaud, president of the Essex PLA, said her association still did not have a potential candidate to run.
"We are working to nominate the Liberal candidate for Essex and hope to have more information for you soon," Renaud told the Star on Tuesday.
Ontario voters go to the polls Feb. 27 after a tight four-week election campaign.
Progressive Conservative Premier Doug Ford officially triggered an early election on Jan. 28. He says he needs a new mandate to deal with U.S. President Donald Trump and his threat of 25 per cent tariffs against Canada.
The next provincial election had been scheduled for June 2026.
But the premier had been suggesting long before tariffs took centre stage that he was itching for an election. Miljan said it was a probability that the other provincial parties should have been ready for.
"It is significant," said Miljan. "I mean, I think they should have gotten the memo that there was an election happening this spring. Doug Ford certainly had signalled that for the last six months."
When and if they do announce candidates, she said those people will be at a significant disadvantage in an already short election campaign period in ridings against some tough competition.
Incumbents Lisa Gretzky (NDP - Windsor West), Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh) and Anthony Leardi (PC __ Essex) would likely be tough to topple under any circumstances.
But faced with a short runway and potentially limited name recognition, opponents will have an even tougher uphill climb so late in the game.
According to Elections Ontario, the confirmed candidates in Windsor West include Tony Francis for the Progressive Conservatives and Joshua Griffin for the New Blue Party.
The Windsor-Tecumseh candidates include the NDP's Gemma Grey-Hall and Sophia Sevo for the New Blue Party.
In Essex, the candidates include Racheal Mills for the NDP and Kevin Linfield for the None of the Above Direct Democracy Party.
Ifthe Liberals and Greens do not announce any candidates, Miljan said that also presents an obvious issue.
"If you don't have a candidate, you can't win the riding," she said. "Your party is not going to be on the ballot if there's no candidate. So it doesn't matter how well the leader does, you're basically giving up those seats."
The Elections Ontario deadline for filing candidate nomination papers is 2 p.m. on Feb. 13. twilhelm@postmedia.com

Subject: Provincial elections; Nominations; Candidates; Elections; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Document 74 of 500

Ontario Election 2025: What's the main campaign issue now that Trump tariffs are on hold?

Author: Star City Desk

Publication info: Star City Desk. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-whats-main-campaign-issue/docview/3163804731/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Election+2025%3A+What%27s+the+main+campaign+issue+now+that+Trump+tariffs+are+on+hold%3F&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Star+City+Desk&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trump tariffs were the main issue at the start of the Ontario election campaign - in fact, Doug Ford called a snap vote, claiming he needed a strong mandate specifically to stand up to American protectionism.
But then after two calls with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau on Monday, US President Donald Trump said he would put threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada on hold for at least 30 days. Trump wants to see how much Canada deals with clamping down the border further to prevent fentanyl and illegal aliens from entering the U.S.
So, in terms of the Ontario election, now what?
Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Ontario NDP leader Marit Stiles and Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner have all called out Ford, saying potential U.S. tariffs were never the reason for the election. They say Ford only wanted to boost his own fortunes - and they therefore want the election to be about other issues, such as health and education.
They'll all continue to make their pitch to voters on the campaign trail.
Threat to ban American booze affected Trump?
Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford had a fire in his belly and was talking bourbon Tuesday as he addressed U.S. President Donald Trump and his on-again, off-again promises to impose stiff tariffs on Canadian goods.
After Trump hit the pause button on planned 25-per-cent tariffs Monday, Ford reversed course on his plan to have U.S. alcohol removed from LCBO shelves. That would have included everything from California wine to Kentucky bourbon.
Ford wants to continue to negotiate with Trump, who says he will revisit the tariff question March 1. But Ford says the change in the president's tone early this week is at least partly the result of hearing loud complaints from those against his tax plan, including U.S. alcohol producers worried about lost sales in Canada.
"It's about working together and explaining to the American people," Ford said during a provincial election campaign stop at an east Ottawa window and door manufacturing company.
"That's where the pressure comes. The pressure comes from the senators, the governors, the congresswomen and men who are getting calls from their constituents, shouting up and down and screaming."
The LCBO, Ford says, carries 3,600 products from 35 U.S. states and 2,000 companies.
- Ken Warren, Ottawa Citizen
Stiles attacks Ford's record
At a rally in Kingston Tuesday afternoon, Ontario NDP leader Marit Stiles said a lot has happened this week with U.S. President Donald Trump's looming tariffs. She told supporters that after seven years, Ontario Premier Doug Ford has made the province weak and vulnerable.
"He needed to stick to a strong united plan with all of the other provinces and the federal government," Stiles said. "I think Doug Ford was coming out from day one with plans on the back of a napkin and that worries me because we need a strong united approach."
Maegen Kulchar, Kingston Whig-Standard
Where the leaders are
Here's where the leaders of Ontario's main political parties are on Wednesday, Feb. 5:
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford
Pickering: Ford will make an announcement at 1 p.m. in Pickering. He will also visit workers and tour a Kubota Canada facility. He will then go to Oshawa for an event with the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers.
-
NDP Leader Marit Stiles
Toronto: Stiles will make an announcement at 10 a.m. She will then speak at the TMU Democracy Forum at noon.
-
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie
Hamilton: Crombie will join local candidate Dawn Danko at a Hamilton coffee shop to speak with residents at 9 a.m. She will then make an announcement at 11:30 a.m.
-
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner
Guelph: Schreiner is set to make an announcement at Gorweir Farms at 1 p.m. with local candidate Bronwynne Wilton. He will then spend time canvassing in the community.
The Canadian Press

Where are they? Liberals, Greens remain Ontario election no-shows in Windsor-Essex

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/where-are-they-liberals-greens-remain-ontario/docview/3163786978/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+are+they%3F+Liberals%2C+Greens+remain+Ontario+election+no-shows+in+Windsor-Essex&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 76 of 500

A deepening doctor shortage is now 'more than a crisis'; Ontario Election

Author: Bieman, Jennifer

Publication info: Bieman, Jennifer. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/deepening-doctor-shortage-is-now-more-than-crisis/docview/3163599745/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+deepening+doctor+shortage+is+now+%27more+than+a+crisis%27%3B+Ontario+Election&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Bieman%2C+Jennifer&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: They lined up in the middle of the night, braving snow squalls and frigid temperatures, not for sought-after concert tickets or a Boxing Day doorcrasher deal, but just for a chance to get a family doctor.
That was the scene in Walkerton weeks ago, where the imminent arrival of a new doctor in the midwestern Ontario community attracted a crowd of more than 1,000 people; the first 500 getting a doctor, the remainder put on a wait list for another expected to arrive later this year.
"What we're seeing is more than a crisis, it's a catastrophe in many communities," said Dr. Dominik Nowak, president of the Ontario Medical Association, a group that represents the province's 43,000 physicians.
"It's not the first time something like this has happened in Ontario. Across the province nearly one in four people are without a family doctor."
In a provincial election whose early days were typified by toughtalk about U.S. President Donald Trump's avowed tariffs on Canadian goods and general cost-ofliving concerns, Nowak and others are urging voters to make the family doctor crisis a ballot-box issue.
More than 2.5 million Ontarians are without a family doctor, the all-important entry-point for patients into the publicly funded health care system. That number is expected to balloon to 4.4 million by 2026, as family doctors retire or leave the field in the coming years, the OMA estimates.
In Windsor, the OMA says 27 doctors are needed for 34,000 unattached patients.
"We need this to be an election issue," said Dr. Jobin Varughese, president of the Ontario College of Family Physicians, a non-profit group that represents more than 18,000 family physicians and medical students in the province.
"Ontarians want family doctors. We don't want what happened in Walkerton, with people lining up for hours for a family doctor, to happen in other communities. But it really could in the current situation."
Whichever party forms the government on Feb. 27 should revisit the fees Ontario's health insurance plan pays family doctors, which has been stagnant for more than a decade, Nowak said.
"That's one of the main reasons we see family practices closing up shop or doctors retiring with no one to replace them," Nowak said, adding the fees paid to doctors sometimes aren't enough to run a practice.
"Our funding models are decades out of date and they haven't kept up with what patients need."
But funding is only one factor.
The OMA wants to see the provincial government establish more team-based clinics where family doctors and other healthcare providers including nurses, physiotherapists, social workers and occupational therapists can work together.
"Only around 30 per cent of practices have that kind of support," Nowak said, adding this comprehensive, collaborative model also attracts new graduates to work in family medicine.
"There's been a stagnation of not just modernizing funding models, but building and expanding these teams over the last decade or so."
A provincial government that is serious about solving the family doctor crisis would also reduce the administrative burden on physicians, who are spending approximately 19 hours a week on paperwork, on top of the time they spend seeing patients, Varughese said.
Access to artificial intelligence note-taking software and the development of a centralized referral system, which would cut down on the time family doctors spend chasing down specialist appointments for their patients, would help, he said. Fixing the system family doctors work within will pay off, Varughese said.
"Family doctors find joy in their work, when they're not burdened by the system or having administrative hurdles that hold them up or devalue the work that they do," Varughese said. "The Ontario College of Family Physicians has solutions and people who are willing to help. We want to get to the point where every Ontarian has access to a family physician." WHERE THE BIG THREE PARTIES STAND LIBERALS
The Ontario Liberals have pledged to educate, attract and retain 3,100 family doctors in the province if elected, ensuring everyone in Ontario has a primary care physician within four years.
Calling fixing family medicine the "single most important priority" if it forms government, the Liberals are pledging to bring in new domestic and internationally trained family doctors.
The Liberals are also promising to improve and expand access to team-based clinics and modernize family medicine by eliminating the use of fax machines and making appointments available on evenings and weekends.
The Liberals' plan also includes ending the practice of penalizing patients and doctors when patients seek care at walk-in clinics.
NEW DEMOCRATS
The Ontario NDP said Sunday it will be releasing more details in the coming days, but have advocated for increased funding for primary health care teams, home care and hospital care.
Among other pledges, the party is promising to connect every Ontarian to a family doctor or nurse practitioner by recruiting more health care professionals into primary care teams and increasing residency seats and licensing opportunities for internationally educated doctors.
"Under Doug Ford's Conservatives, people are left waiting for primary care, emergency rooms have been shuttered, and for-profit health care has exploded," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said in a statement last month, adding "improving access will take political leadership that has so far been absent in our province."
PROG. CON SERVATIVES
Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government, on the eve of the election call, announced $1.4 billion in new funding to connect two million more people to family doctors and primary care teams within four years. The amount is in addition to a previous $400 million earmarked for primary care by the Ford government.
The funding will be used to implement the plan created by Ontario's Primary Care Action Team, a panel led by former federal health minister Jane Philpott.
As part of that pre-election announcement, the PC government said they planned to introduce legislation to outline the standards people in Ontario can expect when accessing primary care.
jbeiman@postmedia.com HOW MANY FAMILY DOCTORS DO WE NEED? ESTIMATED FAMILY DOCTOR SHORTAGE AS OF 2022, BY COMMUNITY

Document 77 of 500

NDP wants Canada-first procurement policies; Gretzky, Masse say country needs to go on the offensive after Trump's threats

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-wants-canada-first-procurement-policies/docview/3163598782/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+wants+Canada-first+procurement+policies%3B+Gretzky%2C+Masse+say+country+needs+to+go+on+the+offensive+after+Trump%27s+threats&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a potential trade war threatening to plunge the economy into chaos, federal and provincial NDP members in Windsor want Canada to immediately ban U.S. companies from government contracts.
Windsor West MP Brian Masse and Windsor West MPP Lisa Gretzky, who is on the campaign trail, held a media conference on Monday calling for Canada "to go on the ofef nsive" with policies that favour domestic companies.
Late Monday U.S. President Donald Trump agreed to pause his tariffs for 30 days.
But before the reprieve, Masse said that even if tariffs are avoided this time, the long-standing dependency on American trade and goodwill will continue to make Canada vulnerable.
"We're going to continue to have Trump threaten these things," he said.
"So we're saying these are the types of things, the tools that we have access to right away, that we should be exercising." "And banning Americans from participating in the procurement should be the first thing, because they've actually done it to us with 'Buy American.'" U.S. President Donald Trump declared a trade war on his closest allies over the weekend, signing his threats of 25 per cent tariffs against Canada and Mexico into reality.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau immediately hit back, imposing similar tariffs on American products.
The tariffs were scheduled to take effect on Tuesday, though Trump agreed to hold off for at least 30 days after Canada and Mexico made several commitments to improve border security.
MP Irek Kusmierczyk (L Windsor-Tecumseh) could not be reached for comment on Monday.
But Gretzky and Masse said efforts to protect the Canadian economy must go beyond tit-fortat tariffs.
They also want provincial and federal governments to favour Canadian companies in bid selection processes, increase Canadian content requirements in contracts and prioritize the use of Canadian materials such as steel and lumber.
Gretzky said it's not feasible for Canada to eliminate all reliance on other countries, but increasing self-reliance is vital to be ready for turbulent times.
Government procurement should be, "first and foremost, made in Windsor, made Ontario, made in Canada," she said.
"You would have thought we would have learned that during the pandemic, but nothing has been done provincially to actively future proof our economy and protect jobs," she said.
"It shouldn't have taken the threat of the American president and the political whim of whoever happens to be in power over there. We should have already been future proofing. We should have made sure we were investing in local producers, local workers and local manufacturing to the maximum degree."
Premier Doug Ford did announce Monday in the midst of his election campaign that the Ontario government would ban U.S. companies from provincial contracts until the tariffs are removed. He also said Monday the province would rip up a $100-million contract with Starlink, the satellite internet company owned by tech billionaire Elon Musk, who is the head of Trump's new Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE).
Gretzky called Ford's announcements political opportunism.
"He has a history of subsidizing very wealthy companies, foreign companies, taking contracts away from Ontario or Canadian companies and giving them to American companies or to Austrian companies," she said.
"When there is a Canadian option or Ontario option that's what they should be doing. And he doesn't have a history of doing that."
If the tariffs materialize, Masse said there also needs to be more attention focused on helping border communities, "which are going to have increasing complications more than anywhere else."
In Windsor, he said that could mean providing support to operate the Windsor-Detroit tunnel if revenues drop due to a prolonged trade war and declining Canadian dollar.
"There's where federal support would be appropriate to make sure that infrastructure is preserved for the long term and the city's residents aren't punished, making sure the infrastructure is at its highest level of accountability and continues to be the link that we need it for the future," said Masse.
"We need to preserve our infrastructure."
twilhelm@postmedia.com

Ontario Election 2025: A temporary tariff reprieve, but is it for candidates?

Author: Star City Desk

Publication info: Star City Desk. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-temporary-tariff-reprieve/docview/3163465354/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Election+2025%3A+A+temporary+tariff+reprieve%2C+but+is+it+for+candidates%3F&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Star+City+Desk&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: US President Donald Trump on Monday halted his threatened 25 per cent tariffs against Canada and Mexico, after calls with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum - but only for a month.
Nevertheless, the threat of tariffs, which could be implemented in 30 days, still loomed large for the Ontario party leaders, who once again fanned out across the province spreading their message to voters.
The Ontario election has taken on more significance in the face of American protectionism, which the candidates hope to exploit.
Forget the tariffs?
OTTAWA - The leaders of Ontario's NDP and Liberal party say now that the threat of tariffs from the U.S. is on hold for at least a month, it's time for the election campaign to focus on provincial policy.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles is in Ottawa today and is promising to spend more money in the education system, hiring more staff, supporting students with disabilities, adding $830 million per year for school repairs, and creating a universal school food program.
She says Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford is trying to "con" voters into thinking the snap election is all about tariffs, but it should be about Ford's record since he was first elected in 2018.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie says now that U.S. President Donald Trump has agreed to pause his threat to impose 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian goods for at least 30 days, Ford has lost his excuse for this election.
She says the Feb. 27 election should be about both protecting the province and fixing the public health-care system.
Ford has said he called the election because he needs a new mandate to deal with not just the immediate threat of tariffs, but to handle four years of relations with the U.S. under Trump.
The Canadian Press
Where the leaders are
Here's where the leaders of Ontario's main political parties are on Tuesday, Feb. 4:
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford
Ottawa: Ford will make an announcement at 11:45 a.m. in Ottawa. He will also visit workers at SMART Local 47.
-
NDP Leader Marit Stiles
Ottawa: Stiles will make an announcement at Maison de la francophonie d'Ottawa at 10 a.m.
Kingston: Stiles will then make a campaign stop in Kingston at 3:30 p.m.
-
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie
Brampton: Crombie will make an announcement in Brampton about tax cuts at 10:30 a.m.
Toronto: Crombie will hold an event at a think tank at Toronto Metropolitan University at 1:30 p.m. She will then speak to transit riders at 6 p.m.
-
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner
Bracebridge: Schreiner is set to canvass in Bracebridge at 10 a.m. before joining local candidate Matt Richter at his campaign office at 1:30 p.m.
Huntsville: Schreiner will then go to a local campaign launch at Canvas Brewery in Huntsville at 7 p.m.
This report by The Canadian Press was first published Feb. 4, 2025.
The Canadian Press
New poll: Doug Ford in the lead after rolling the dice with early election call
Doug Ford's early election gamble seems to be paying off: With more than three weeks to go, polling shows that almost half of Ontario voters are seriously considering a vote for the Progressive Conservatives.
The new poll from Leger shows that 47 per cent of Ontarians say they've decided to vote - or are leaning towards voting - for Ford's incumbent PCs. The Ontario Liberals, led by Bonnie Crombie, the former mayor of Mississauga, trail with the support of 23 per cent of Ontarians. The New Democrats, led by Merit Stiles, have just 17 per cent.
"While there's some disagreement whether or not the tariffs were a good reason for a call, there doesn't seem to be any particular political fallout for Premier Ford and the Progressive Conservatives for going ahead with the election now," said Andrew Enns, executive vice-president of Leger's Central Canada operations, in an interview.
National Post

Ontario Election 2025: A temporary tariff reprieve, but not for candidates

Author: Star City Desk

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-2025-temporary-tariff-reprieve/docview/3163438093/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Election+2025%3A+A+temporary+tariff+reprieve%2C+but+not+for+candidates&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Star+City+Desk&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 81 of 500

Ontario election: A deepening doctor shortage is now 'more than a crisis'

Author: MacLeod, Brian

Publication info: MacLeod, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-election-deepening-doctor-shortage-is-now/docview/3163429692/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+election%3A+A+deepening+doctor+shortage+is+now+%27more+than+a+crisis%27&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=MacLeod%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Jennifer Bieman
They lined up in the middle of the night, braving snow squalls and frigid temperatures, not for sought-after concert tickets or a Boxing Day deal, but just for a chance to get a family doctor.
That was the scene in Walkerton weeks ago, where the imminent arrival of a new doctor in the midwestern Ontario community attracted a crowd of more than 1,000 people; the first 500 getting a doctor, the remainder put on a wait list for another expected to arrive later this year.
"What we're seeing is more than a crisis, it's a catastrophe in many communities," said Dr. Dominik Nowak, president of the Ontario Medical Association, a group that represents the province's 43,000 physicians.
"It's not the first time something like this has happened in Ontario. Across the province nearly one in four people are without a family doctor."
In a provincial election whose early days were typified by tough-talk about U.S. President Donald Trump's avowed tariffs on Canadian goods and general cost-of-living concerns, Nowak and others are urging voters to make the family doctor crisis a ballot-box issue.
More than 2.5 million Ontarians are without a family doctor, the all-important entry-point for patients into the publicly funded health care system. That number is expected to balloon to 4.4 million by 2026, as family doctors retire or leave the field in the coming years, the OMA estimates.
In Windsor, the OMA says 27 doctors are needed for 34,000 unattached patients.
"We need this to be an election issue," said Dr. Jobin Varughese, president of the Ontario College of Family Physicians, a non-profit group that represents more than 18,000 family physicians and medical students in the province.
"Ontarians want family doctors. We don't want what happened in Walkerton, with people lining up for hours for a family doctor, to happen in other communities. But it really could in the current situation."
Whichever party forms the government on Feb. 27 should revisit the fees Ontario's health insurance plan pays family doctors, which has been stagnant for more than a decade, Nowak said.
"That's one of the main reasons we see family practices closing up shop or doctors retiring with no one to replace them," Nowak said, adding the fees paid to doctors sometimes aren't enough to run a practice.
"Our funding models are decades out of date and they haven't kept up with what patients need."
But funding is only one factor.
Related
$3.1B agreement promises more Windsor area doctors, shorter ER waits
Solutions needed as local family physician crisis growing worse
The OMA wants to see the provincial government establish more team-based clinics where family doctors and other health-care providers - including nurses, physiotherapists, social workers and occupational therapists - can work together.
"Only around 30 per cent of practices have that kind of support," Nowak said, adding this comprehensive, collaborative model also attracts new graduates to work in family medicine.
"There's been a stagnation of not just modernizing funding models, but building and expanding these teams over the last decade or so."
A provincial government that is serious about solving the family doctor crisis would also reduce the administrative burden on physicians, who are spending approximately 19 hours a week on paperwork, on top of the time they spend seeing patients, Varughese said.
Access to artificial intelligence note-taking software and the development of a centralized referral system, which would cut down on the time family doctors spend chasing down specialist appointments for their patients, would help, he said.
Fixing the system family doctors work within will pay off, Varughese said.
"Family doctors find joy in their work, when they're not burdened by the system or having administrative hurdles that hold them up or devalue the work that they do," Varughese said. "The Ontario College of Family Physicians has solutions and people who are willing to help. We want to get to the point where every Ontarian has access to a family physician."
Where the big three parties stand Liberals
The Ontario Liberals have pledged to educate, attract and retain 3,100 family doctors in the province if elected, ensuring everyone in Ontario has a primary care physician within four years.
Calling fixing family medicine the "single most important priority" if it forms government, the Liberals are pledging to bring in new domestic and internationally trained family doctors.
The Liberals are also promising to improve and expand access to team-based clinics and modernize family medicine by eliminating the use of fax machines and making appointments available on evenings and weekends.
The Liberals' plan also includes ending the practice of penalizing patients and doctors when patients seek care at walk-in clinics.
New Democrats
The Ontario NDP said Sunday it will be releasing more details in the coming days, but have advocated for increased funding for primary health care teams, home care and hospital care.
Among other pledges, the party is promising to connect every Ontarian to a family doctor or nurse practitioner by recruiting more health care professionals into primary care teams and increasing residency seats and licensing opportunities for internationally educated doctors.
"Under Doug Ford's Conservatives, people are left waiting for primary care, emergency rooms have been shuttered, and for-profit health care has exploded," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said in a statement last month, adding "improving access will take political leadership that has so far been absent in our province."
Progressive Conservatives
Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government, on the eve of the election call, announced $1.4 billion in new funding to connect two million more people to family doctors and primary care teams within four years.
The amount is in addition to a previous $400 million earmarked for primary care by the Ford government.
The funding will be used to implement the plan created by Ontario's Primary Care Action Team, a panel led by former federal health minister Jane Philpott.
As part of that pre-election announcement, the PC government said they planned to introduce legislation to outline the standards people in Ontario can expect when accessing primary care.
jbeiman@postmedia.com
How many family doctors do we need?
Estimated family doctor shortage as of 2022, by community
London: 56 doctors, 71,000 unattached patients
Chatham-Kent: 16 doctors, 20,000 unattached patients
Sarnia: 8 doctors, 10,000 unattached patients
Windsor: 27 doctors, 34,000 unattached patients
Stratford: 2 doctors, 3,000 unattached patients
Woodstock: 2 doctors, 2,100 unattached patients
Source: Ontario Medical Association

Trump trade war doubles Ford PC lead in Ontario election race: Poll; Trump trade war doubles Ford PC lead in Ontario election race: Poll

Author: Bryan Pas Sifiume

Publication info: Bryan Pas Sifiume. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trump-trade-war-doubles-ford-pc-lead-ontario/docview/3163298067/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump+trade+war+doubles+Ford+PC+lead+in+Ontario+election+race%3A+Poll%3B+Trump+trade+war+doubles+Ford+PC+lead+in+Ontario+election+race%3A+Poll&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Bryan+Pas+Sifiume&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Impending American tariffs have given the Doug Ford PCs their highest approval ratings in nearly a year, new polling suggests.
The Liaison Strategies poll, commissioned by the National Ethnic Press and Media Council of Canada (NEPMCC,) found the Ontario Progressive Conservatives doubled their lead over the previous week, pulling a 43 per cent approval rating among decided and leaning voters compared to the Bonnie Crombie Liberals at 31 per cent between Jan. 31 and Feb. 1.
The PCs were polled 39 per cent against a Liberal 33 per cent the week previous, the closest the two parties have come in a year.
"Ford focused his campaign on tariffs and the trade war from the very start and, for now, that is paying dividends with Ontarians," said David Valentin, principal at Liaison Strategies.
Valentin said the Ontario Liberals have two bright spots - their lead in the City of Toronto and their dead heat in the province's north.
Indeed, the 416 remains the Liberals' trusty stronghold, where they're pulling 33 per cent support compared to the PC's 27 per cent. Toronto's undecided vote, currently sitting at a quarter of respondents, could still play a factor.
Likewise, the province's undecided vote dwells around the same range across all locations and demographics - PC support for the ever-important 18-34 age range currently sits at 29 per cent, just two points ahead of that cohort's undecided vote.
Fortunes for the Marit Stiles NDP, however, continue to slide.
"There is no good news in this report for the Ontario NDP, who continue to slide and are now down to 16 per cent," Valentin said.
"In south-central Ontario, Hamilton and Niagara, they are trailing the PCs by 13 points, despite the fact they have been first or a close second all throughout the year."
NDP support has seen a precipitous decline since their late-summer high of 23 per cent, with this week's 16 per cent representing their lowest ratings in a year.
The poll was conducted between Jan. 31 and Feb. 1 with 1,274 Ontario voters. The poll's margin of error is ± 2.74 per cent, 19 times out of 20. bpassifiume@postmedia.com

Subject: Trade disputes; Voters; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Trade disputes Tariffs
