Document 141 of 500

Tariffs imperil 23K greenhouse jobs; Most of the county's vegetable crops are shipped across the American border

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tariffs-imperil-23k-greenhouse-jobs-most-countys/docview/3156854818/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tariffs+imperil+23K+greenhouse+jobs%3B+Most+of+the+county%27s+vegetable+crops+are+shipped+across+the+American+border&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the bulk of Essex County's greenhouse crops shipped across the American border, a spokesman for the region's economically important agricultural sector warns there could be major job losses if president-elect Donald Trump's threatened tariffs take effect.
Richard Lee, executive director of Ontario Greenhouse Vegetable Growers (OGVG), told the Star as many as 23,000 local jobs are at stake.
"We're very concerned. We can't take these threats lightly based on our trade dependency with the U.S.," Richard Lee, executive director of Ontario Greenhouse Vegetable Growers (OGVG), told the Star.
The incoming president has threatened a 25-per cent tariff on all goods exported to the U.S. from Canada and Mexico, possibly as soon as he's sworn in on Monday.
Those threats should not be underestimated, said Lee, whose organization represents 170 vegetable growers across the province.
But Essex County, as home to 75 per cent of the province's greenhouse farming acreage - and host to the largest cluster of greenhouses in North America - could absorb the brunt of the impact of any U.S. tariffs.
"We've been exploring options on the provincial and federal levels to identify the potential risks and impacts to our sector," said Lee.
Though it remains unclear which sectors might be targeted by the tariffs - or if they will even be imposed at all - Lee said he plans to "prepare for the worst and expect the best."
Last year, OGVG growers exported $1.6 billion worth of fresh farm produce to American markets, according to Lee. Essex County greenhouses, mainly growing tomatoes, cucumbers and peppers, accounted for about $1.36 billion - or 85 per cent - of that value.
The Ontario Greenhouse Vegetable Growers supports roughly 35,000 agricultural jobs. That includes positions in greenhouse operations, packaging, trucking, brokering and marketing.
Lee warns Trump's proposed tariffs could put as many as 29,000 of those jobs at risk, especially if the levied taxes remain in place for an extended period without government subsidies to offset the impact.
Of those jobs, he estimates 23,000 would be on the line in Essex County, making the region particularly vulnerable.
"I grew up in the Windsor-Essex County area," said Lee. "It's hard to not know someone who is somehow tied or connected to what's grown here to support food security across North America. It will impact a lot of people."
Among those threatened local farm jobs are 8,000 to 10,000 positions filled by seasonal and temporary foreign workers employed at Essex County farms and greenhouses during peak growing season.
"If there's no production and there's no farming, those workers will be sent home," said Lee.
"Temporary foreign workers coming in from source countries usually have a fixed contract with the employer here in Canada," he explained.
"It's not like we can shift them to another farm or create jobs for them because of the restrictions they've entered the country under."
At a press conference earlier this week, Ontario Premier Doug Ford warned U.S. tariffs could cost up to 450,000 to 500,000 jobs in his province, depending on the sectors targeted.
Beyond that, tariffs could strain the long-standing bilateral trade partnership between the bordering countries, governed by the Canada-United States-Mexico Trade Agreement (CUSMA), which was brokered during Trump's first term.
In 2023, Canada exported $40.5 billion worth of agri-food products to its American neighbour and largest trading partner. That same year, Canada imported $32 billion worth of agri-food products from the U.S.
It's not the retailer or the shipper but consumers who will be most impacted by tariffs, said Lee, with price markups on American grocery store shelves.
"So, they're unfairly taxing the consumer at the worst possible time, when affordability is one of the major crises that we're facing as Canadians and Americans," said Lee.
With higher costs for most consumers, Lee said demand would plummet and fewer Ontario greenhouse vegetables would get shipped across the border.
But Lee said it's difficult to predict exactly what will happen once Trump steps into his second term Jan. 20. The current political climate, he added, is similar to 2016 when Trump brazenly threatened tariffs on Canadian goods before his first stint as president.
"His threats made back in his first term were illogical, and ultimately did cause ripple effects," said Lee.
"The tariffs were aimed mainly at steel and aluminum, but it was short-lived. That's why it's important that the federal government has this level of preparedness, and I understand they've identified a list of retaliatory tariffs they can impose on products coming into Canada.
"Hopefully, Trump is going to take a similar approach and exempt food from the list of goods he's targeting."
What is different this time around, said Lee, is the growing political uncertainty within Canada.
Amid waning popularity in the polls, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced last week he was stepping down as party leader and prorogued Parliament until March. His announcement followed shortly after the resignation of his longtime deputy prime minister and minister of finance, Chrystia Freeland.
During a Windsor visit on Thursday, the outgoing prime minister once again condemned Trump's tariff threats and warned of trade retaliation by Canada in response.
"There will be other consequences felt throughout the value chain domestically that will have detrimental impacts people probably won't recover from," said Lee.
"I hope they (senior governments) have a strategic plan well laid out and ready to execute." mmazak@postmedia.com

Subject: Greenhouses; Food products; Consumers; Trade agreements; Prime ministers; Farmworkers; Farms; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Consumers Trade agreements Tariffs

Location: Mexico; United States--US; Canada; North America

Identifier / keyword: essex; greenhouse; crops; shipped; american; border

Title: Tariffs imperil 23K greenhouse jobs; Most of the county's vegetable crops are shipped across the American border

Publication title: The Windsor Star; Windsor, Ont.

First page: A.2

Publication date: Jan 18, 2025

Section: City+Region

Document 142 of 500

23,000 jobs: Essex County farmers warn of huge job losses under Trump tariffs

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/23-000-jobs-essex-county-farmers-warn-huge-job/docview/3156794450/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=23%2C000+jobs%3A+Essex+County+farmers+warn+of+huge+job+losses+under+Trump+tariffs&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the bulk of Essex County's greenhouse crops shipped across the American border, a spokesman for the region's economically important agricultural sector warns there could be major job losses if President-elect Donald Trump's threatened tariffs take effect.
Richard Lee, executive director of Ontario Greenhouse Vegetable Growers (OGVG), told the Star as many as 23,000 local jobs are at stake.

Cocktail tomatoes are shown at the Via Verde operation in Leamington on Friday, Jan. 17, 2025.
The Ontario Greenhouse Vegetable Growers supports roughly 35,000 agricultural jobs. That includes positions in greenhouse operations, packaging, trucking, brokering, and marketing.
Lee warns Trump's proposed tariffs could put as many as 29,000 of those jobs at risk, especially if the levied taxes remain in place for an extended period without government subsidies to offset the impact.
Of those jobs, he estimates 23,000 would be on the line in Essex County, making the region particularly vulnerable.
"I grew up in the Windsor-Essex County area," said Lee. "It's hard to not know someone who is somehow tied or connected to what's grown here to support food security across North America. It will impact a lot of people."
Among those threatened local farm jobs are 8,000 to 10,000 positions filled by seasonal and temporary foreign workers employed at Essex County farms and greenhouses during peak growing season.
"If there's no production and there's no farming, those workers will be sent home," said Lee.
"Temporary foreign workers coming in from source countries usually have a fixed contract with the employer here in Canada," he explained.
"It's not like we can shift them to another farm or create jobs for them because of the restrictions they've entered the country under."
At a press conference earlier this week, Ontario Premier Doug Ford warned U.S. tariffs could cost up to 450,000 to 500,000 jobs in his province, depending on the sectors targeted.
Beyond that, tariffs could strain the longstanding bilateral trade partnership between the bordering countries, governed by the Canada-United States-Mexico Trade Agreement (CUSMA), which was brokered during Trump's first term.
In 2023, Canada exported $40.5 billion worth of agri-food products to its American neighbour and largest trading partner. That same year, Canada imported $32 billion worth of agri-food products from the U.S.
Workers are shown at the Via Verde greenhouse operation in Leamington on Friday, Jan. 17, 2025.
It's not the retailer or the shipper but consumers who will be most impacted by tariffs, said Lee, with price markups on American grocery store shelves.
"So, they're unfairly taxing the consumer at the worst possible time, when affordability is one of the major crises that we're facing as Canadians and Americans," said Lee.
With higher costs for most consumers, Lee said demand would plummet and fewer Ontario greenhouse vegetables would get shipped across the border.
But Lee said it's difficult to predict exactly what will happen once Trump steps into his second term Jan. 20. The current political climate, he added, is similar to 2016 when Trump brazenly threatened tariffs on Canadian goods before his first stint as president.
"His threats made back in his first term were illogical, and ultimately did cause ripple effects," said Lee.
"The tariffs were aimed mainly at steel and aluminum, but it was short-lived. That's why it's important that the federal government has this level of preparedness, and I understand they've identified a list of retaliatory tariffs they can impose on products coming into Canada.
"Hopefully, Trump is going to take a similar approach and exempt food from the list of goods he's targeting."
What is different this time around, said Lee, is the growing political uncertainty within Canada.
Amid waning popularity in the polls, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced last week he was stepping down as party leader and prorogued Parliament until March. His announcement followed shortly after the resignation of his long-time deputy prime minister and minister of finance, Chrystia Freeland.
Related
Trump tariffs an 'existential threat' to Ontario, NDP leader warns in Windsor
Prepare for 'pain' with Trump's return, Windsor leaders warn
'Time to show up and fight' - Trudeau warns of 'robust' retaliation against Trump tariffs on Windsor trip
During a Windsor visit on Thursday, the outgoing prime minister once again condemned Trump's tariff threats and warned of trade retaliation by Canada in response.
"There will be other consequences felt throughout the value chain domestically that will have detrimental impacts people probably won't recover from," said Lee.
"I hope they (senior governments) have a strategic plan well laid out and ready to execute."
mmazak@postmedia.com

Document 143 of 500

'Time to show up and fight' - Trudeau warns of 'robust' retaliation against Trump tariffs on Windsor trip

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/time-show-up-fight-trudeau-warns-robust/docview/3156784566/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Time+to+show+up+and+fight%27+-+Trudeau+warns+of+%27robust%27+retaliation+against+Trump+tariffs+on+Windsor+trip&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With his decade as prime minister ending before this spring, outgoing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau hasn't stopped yet to reflect on his legacy - he came out swinging on Thursday in Windsor against the incoming American president's tariff threats.
"This is the time for a prime minister to show up and fight, and this is exactly what I am doing," Trudeau said during one stop at a plant owned by manufacturer Anchor Danly.
Speaking to reporters from the factory floor, Trudeau said Donald Trump's proposed 25 per cent tariff on Canadian goods will raise costs for American consumers and put American jobs at risk - "not something anyone wants because our economies are so intertwined."
Trudeau said he will continue to explain to Americans that the proposed tariff would harm "one of the strongest economic relationships in the world at a time when our continent should be taking on the world." He again warned of Canadian trade retaliation during his trip to the city with Canada's busiest border crossing for commercial goods.
If the incoming U.S. administration does move forward with "unfair and unjust tariffs," he said, Canada will "respond in measured ways, but in robust ways, demonstrating once again Canadians know how to stand up for ourselves."
As justification for the tariffs, Trump has pointed to an "invasion" of migrants and fentanyl entering the United States from Canada.
Trudeau said Canada has demonstrated that its border "is not the source of challenges with the United States," adding that half of a per cent of the fentanyl that enters the U.S. comes from Canada, as do half of a per cent of the migrants.
"Borders are not the issue, but still we put in over $1 billion to upgrade and strengthen our border processes to prevent the flow of illegal goods and people in both directions," Trudeau said.
Trudeau said he came to Windsor-Essex - where Trump's proposed tariffs could have devastating economic effects - to fight for workers who are "more exposed to trade with the United States than anyone else." He said he reassured union representatives and workers in the manufacturing sector that the federal government "continues to be there for them" during an "incredibly crucial time."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks with labour leaders and others at the Unifor Local 444 union hall in Windsor on Thursday, Jan. 16, 2025.
The Ambassador Bridge between Windsor and Detroit is the busiest commercial crossing along the Canada-U.S. border. According to the federal government, it handles more than $390 million in trade each day.
"I'm glad to be in Windsor and I'm going to continue doing my job as prime minister every single day, which is defending Canadian interests, standing up for Canada, and actually defending the most successful trading relationship in the world between Canada and Americans," the prime minister told reporters.
On what might be his last visit to Windsor as prime minister, Justin Trudeau is shown at the Anchor Danly plant in Windsor on Thursday, Jan. 16, 2025.
On what felt like a campaign-style visit with multiple stops in the region, the prime minister visited l'Essor Secondary School in Tecumseh, the Unifor Local 444 headquarters on Turner Road in Windsor, and the corporate headquarters of Windsor-based Anchor Danly, one of the largest tool and die manufacturers in North America.
Related
Prime Minister Trudeau goes to school during Thursday visit to Windsor
Prepare for 'pain' with Trump's return, Windsor leaders warn
Trump tariffs an 'existential threat' to Ontario, NDP leader warns in Windsor
Trudeau announced last week he would be stepping down as Liberal party leader and prime minister as soon as a successor is chosen. The decision followed a growing revolt among party ranks and plummeting polling numbers.
Trudeau became leader of the Liberal party in 2013 and prime minister in 2015.
tcampbell@postmedia.com( tcampbell@postmedia.com )

Document 144 of 500

Brown-John: Return of the American bull in a china shop

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/brown-john-return-american-bull-china-shop/docview/3156784080/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Brown-John%3A+Return+of+the+American+bull+in+a+china+shop&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Lloyd Brown-John
My late mother had a penchant for addressing boors and clumsy oafs as "behaving like bulls in a china shop." A person lacking social skills, manners or a person simply impolite, uneducable, unenterprising could fetch down her wrath.
On Monday, Donald Trump will be inaugurated again as POTUS (President of the United States). This event will be historic because the new president has a criminal record, is a known misogynist, and has a capacity to express random thoughts without any moral or ethical understanding of prospective consequences.
Trump's niece Mary Trump, in her 2020 book, "Too Much and Never Enough: How My Family Created the World's Most Dangerous Man," described as characteristic Donald Trump's persistent expression of unsubstantiated "facts" and his total disregard for the consequences of his mindless mutterings.
I am reminded of mathematician, satirist and musician Tom Lehrer's 1965 anti-Nazi collaborator song about Wernher von Braun. Somewhat like Trump's rambling comments, Lehrer includes in his song about von Braun's involvement in American missile development: "Once the rockets are up, who cares where they come down? That's not my department," says Wernher von Braun.
For many Americans, Trump is most assuredly a national embarrassment. But for the Trumpies, as one updated MAGA hat proclaims: "Jesus is My Savior, Trump is My President."
Neither Trump nor his puppet master - Elon Musk, who injected an estimated US$270 million or more into Trump's re-election campaign - seem to be able to constrain their tongues.
So far, the disastrous duo have upset and insulted the people of Panama, Greenland and Denmark, Britain's prime minister and, of course, Canada and Canadians. And all this before they are formally in exercise of the enormous power of the U.S. presidency.
Alberta's Trump boot-licking premier may be the only responsible Canadian politician ready to sell her soul and oil regardless of consequences for Canadians to America's energy-gulping economy.
To his credit, Ontario Premier Doug Ford has made it clear that threats of tariffs by Trump may have serious consequences for Americans. The latter depend heavily upon hydro generated in Ontario to keep the drivel of FOX TV news on the air for many Americans.
I have no idea how automobiles are made as I've never been inside any auto assembly plant. However, auto components move back and forth across the border several times in the manufacturing process. Trump has indicated he wants to start a tariff war, so how would multiple tariffs on component parts affect the price to American consumers of automobiles?
Trump's threat of a tariff war can have little positive consequence either for Americans or Canadians. Tariffs are tax walls designed to protect inefficient industries. The costs of all tariffs will always emerge as higher prices for consumers. Once again, the ramblings of an uninformed mind that seems incapable of understanding that threats have consequences.
According to Trump, America is subsidizing Canada for hundreds of billions of dollars annually. He is actually talking about a modest U.S. trade deficit with Canada because there is not a single subsidy coming to Canada from the U.S.
He has also noted that Canada is protected by U.S. military might. The often forgotten North American Aerospace Defense (NORAD) agreement was an American idea. The idea being to ensure that potential Russian nuclear missiles were destroyed over Canada rather than the U.S.
Like Wernher von Braun, "who cares where the missiles come down" as long as it was Canada?
Monday will witness the inauguration of probably the most dangerous person to ever occupy America's White House.
Related
Brown-John: Mr. Trump, how about we turn U.S. states into Canadian provinces?
Brown-John: Trudeau not alone as PM missing 'that graceful exit'
As California burns, Trumps' fundamental immaturity has surfaced when he refers to California Gov. Gavin Newsom pejoratively and spreads his usual misinformation about natural disasters. A real president would be extending hands of assistance instead of invective.
Canadians, and especially those of us close to America's border, need to exercise caution and cold calculation in dealing with an unpredictable American president.
The next four years could be very bumpy.
Lloyd Brown-John is a University of Windsor professor emeritus of political science and director of Canterbury ElderCollege. He can be reached at lbj@uwindsor.ca( mailto:lbj@uwindsor.ca ).

Document 145 of 500

Alberta's oil now the real threat to Canadian unity

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/albertas-oil-now-real-threat-canadian-unity/docview/3156680027/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta%27s+oil+now+the+real+threat+to+Canadian+unity&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For years, it's been assumed that the biggest threat to Canadian unity comes from the Province of Quebec. It hosted a separatist terrorist movement in the 1960s and is home to the sovereigntist Parti Québécois. It held two referendums on separation, the last of which saw the country hang together by less than one per cent of the vote. Today, the party tops the polls and is threatening to hold another referendum if it takes power next year.
But the game has changed, thanks to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and U.S. president-elect Donald Trump. The biggest threat to the integrity of Canada no longer lies in the east, but in the west - namely, the Province of Alberta. A showdown is looming between Edmonton and Ottawa over Canada's upcoming tariff war with the United States - one that will start this week, when provincial premiers meet with the federal government to hash out a "Team Canada" response to Trump's threats.
The battle lines are clear. Over the weekend, Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly said that "everything is on the table" when it comes to countering Trump's tariffs, including withholding the four-million barrels of oil Alberta ships south every day.
This caused a backlash from Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, who spent part of the weekend meeting with Trump at his Mar-a-Lago resort. "We won't stand for that and you should never, ever threaten something you cannot do," she said. Smith reminded Joly that western Canadian crude flows to refineries in Ontario and Quebec through Enbridge's Line 5 pipeline, which runs through the U.S. Threats to cut off oil are thus "empty," she added, and would fuel a national unity crisis.
Smith is not wrong. If the federal government isn't careful, it could inflame the "Wexit" movement, which, for the first time, would be feasible, because it could find a willing partner in Washington. Trump thinks it would be great if Canada became America's 51st state. But what parts would he want? Downtown Toronto, with its core of NDP and Liberal voters? The Province of Quebec, which speaks a different language? Nope. The prize would be Alberta, with its oil and conservative mindset. And its border with the Northwest Territories. If Trump got Alberta, his next stop would be striking a deal with the N.W.T., then on to neighbouring Yukon, to increase the U.S.'s access to the Arctic and its undersea oil reserves.
And how would Albertans feel about joining the U.S.? In a December poll, 30 per cent said the province would benefit, the highest percentage in the country. Thirty-eight per cent of Albertans also thought the province would be better off as its own country, while only 35 per cent of Quebecers felt the same about their province. A separate poll, released Tuesday, found that when asked if Canada as a whole should join the U.S., 18 per cent of Albertans agree - far from a majority, but the highest percentage in the country.
For this, we can thank Trudeau and Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault. Over the past decade, they've been determined to put Alberta's oil industry out of business. Between their federal carbon tax, incoming emissions cap and general denigration of the resource as dirty and shameful, it's no wonder that the West feels disrespected.
It feels like a rerun of Pierre Trudeau's infamous National Energy Program, which caused thousands of Albertans to lose their livelihoods and spawned the bumper sticker, "Let the Eastern Bastards Freeze in the Dark."
But Trudeau père was also concerned about national unity and would roll over in his grave if his son's legacy was the breakup of Canada. As Trudeau prepares to exit stage left, our leadership cannot allow that to happen. Canada needs to present a tough, united front that respects all its provinces. If we don't hang together, there is a real risk we will break apart. Postmedia News Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Decades

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

Top dog: OPP's newest canine member Vinny is on the job in Essex County

Author: Kotsis, Julie

Publication info: Kotsis, Julie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/top-dog-opps-newest-canine-member-vinny-is-on-job/docview/3156570308/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Top+dog%3A+OPP%27s+newest+canine+member+Vinny+is+on+the+job+in+Essex+County&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Kotsis%2C+Julie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's a new sheriff in town and his bite is worse than his bark.
Actually, he's not a traditional sheriff but a canine and his name is Vinny.
K9 Vinny and his partner OPP Const. Brett Holland arrived in December and are helping to keep local communities and surrounding areas safe as the newest addition to the Essex County OPP detachment.
Holland, who transferred from the Upper Ottawa Valley detachment, and Vinny - a two-year-old Belgian Malinois - recently completed a 21-week K9 general service course.
This is the first police dog Holland has had as a partner. They have been together since July.
"Like the dogs, a lot of consideration is placed into handler selection as well," Holland said.
He had to work with an experienced dog handler and go through an application process. That was followed by a seven-day, 24-hour intensive course.
Once he passed that, Holland and Vinny began the 21-week general service dog training, which they completed on Dec. 6.
OPP Const. Brett Holland and his canine partner Vinny, pictured outside the Manning Road detachment on Thursday, Jan. 16, 2025, completed a 21-week general service dog training program.
Vinny is a general service dog with training qualifications that include tracking for lost or missing and wanted people, criminal apprehension and tactical obedience.
Holland said his partner will participate in a five-week firearms and drug detection training course in February.
"Vinnie and I are both extremely excited to be in Essex County, and we're excited to be assisting the frontline officers with their investigations and contributing to public safety," Holland told the Star Thursday.
OPP officials say the specialized K9 unit's "timeliness to a scene is crucial."
"Essex County OPP welcomes PC Brett Holland and his new partner Vinny and congratulates them on the completion of a very rigorous and demanding K9 Training Course," said detachment commander Mark Loucas in a statement.
"As the OPP's largest detachment in the province, having the capacity to provide K9 Services in support of our communities is invaluable, and you can expect to see Vinny on patrol with his partner throughout Essex County."
Holland and Vinny replace Const. Milan Matovski and his dog Maximus. Maximus retired in 2023 and Matovski, who is now a staff sergeant, recently transferred to OPP headquarters in Orillia.
Vinny lives with Holland but he doesn't snuggle at night on the sofa with him.
A climate-controlled kennel was built in his backyard and the dog has access to fresh water at all times.
"The OPP makes sure that he's well taken care of," Holland said. "This is a dream job for me and he's doing very, very well. He's an exceptional tracking dog."
Related
Windsor police dog Hasko remembered as 'most excellent, most fearless'
Retiring Maximus tracked more suspects than any other OPP police dog
Holland said his partner has a lot of drive and doesn't have a lot of downtime, by his own choice.
"So he doesn't come inside my house or anything like that," Holland said. "He doesn't really want to. He wants to be outside and he wants to be working as much as he possibly can."
jkotsis@postmedia.com
twitter.com/KotsisStar

Document 147 of 500

Prime Minister Trudeau goes to school during Thursday visit to Windsor

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/prime-minister-trudeau-goes-school-during/docview/3156521831/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Prime+Minister+Trudeau+goes+to+school+during+Thursday+visit+to+Windsor&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Outgoing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau visited Windsor on Thursday to tour an industrial parts plant, talk to union members, and take a local wrestler up on a special invitation.
Trudeau's daylong trip to the Windsor area began with a stop at L'Essor high school in Tecumseh. He visited a classroom, talked to students and took questions.
He was there at the invitation of local Olympic wrestler Linda Morais, who teaches at the school. When Canada's Olympians visited Ottawa in December, Morais invited Trudeau to visit her class.
Morais, who won a bronze medal at the 2016 World Wrestling Championships and gold at the 2019 championships, also competed at the 2024 Summer Olympics in Paris.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks to students in a portable classroom at L'Essor Secondary School in Tecumseh on Thursday, Jan. 16, 2025.
After stopping by the high school, Trudeau headed with Liberal Windsor-Tecumseh MP Irek Kusmierczyk, who arranged the meetings, to the Unifor Local 444 headquarters on Turner Road for a meet-and-greet with union members.
The third stop on his itinerary is a manufacturing plant in the city, where he was scheduled to tour the plant and take questions from reporters.
The visit is maybe one of Trudeau's last trips to Windsor as Canada's prime minister.
Faced with a growing revolt among Liberal ranks and dismal polling numbers, Trudeau ended weeks of speculation last week when he announced he was stepping down as party leader and prime minister as soon as a successor is chosen.
Trudeau, who became leader of the Liberal party in 2013 and prime minister in 2015, made the announcement Jan. 6 outside Rideau Cottage in Ottawa.
The Windsor visit also comes ahead of anticipated tariffs threatened by U.S. president-elect Donald Trump, which could have devastating effects on the Windsor region and its manufacturing and agricultural sectors.
Related
Trump tariffs an 'existential threat' to Ontario, NDP leader warns in Windsor
'Mixed emotions' - Windsor-Essex MPs react to Prime Minister Trudeau resignation
Trump continues to threaten imposing a universal 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian and Mexican imports when he takes office on Monday, Jan. 20.
He has also repeatedly taunted Trudeau by calling him the "governor" of Canada and stating Canada should become the 51st U.S. state.
Following the announcement that he is stepping down, Trudeau wrote on social media that "there isn't a snowball's chance in hell that Canada would become part of the United States."

Document 148 of 500

Ottawa looking at options after auto sector says killing EV incentives a 'disaster' for industry

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-looking-at-options-after-auto-sector-says/docview/3156394821/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+looking+at+options+after+auto+sector+says+killing+EV+incentives+a+%27disaster%27+for+industry&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Despite announcing an end to a $5,000 federal incentive for the purchase or lease of electric vehicles, the minister responsible for FedDev Ontario insists Ottawa has no intention of abandoning support for the automotive industry during the transition to electric vehicles.
"We're looking at many things. We will not undercut our own commitments," Ruby Sahota told the Star on Tuesday during a visit to Windsor.
The move announced by the Liberals this week to cut the iZEV grants brought angry protests from representatives of the Canadian automotive industry. They accuse the federal government of "hypocrisy" and creating "a disaster" for the industry and dealer networks if it doesn't also scrap its zero-emission vehicle sales mandates.
"It was a very successful fund," said Sahota. "We went from EVs being three per cent of the sector to now being over 16 per cent. We want to continue to see that grow.
"The conversations are happening and, before March 31 or after, these (eliminating sales mandates, replenishing the zero emissions fund) are things that can be considered."
The zero-emissions fund was scheduled to run out of money on March 31, but auto industry representatives expected the program would continue to help support it reach Ottawa's EV sales mandates or face significant fines.
"(Monday's) announcement by the federal government to halt all consumer incentives for zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs) comes at the same time as a slowdown in the buildout of public charging infrastructure," Brian Kingston, president/CEO of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturers' Association, said in a statement.
"As a result of these developments, the federal government's mandated ZEV sales targets are increasingly unrealistic and must end. Mandating Canadians to buy ZEVs without providing them the supports needed to switch to electric is a made-in-Canada policy failure."
Those federal mandates require 20 per cent of all new vehicle sales in Canada to be classified as zero emission vehicles by 2026, 60 per cent by 2030 and 100 per cent by 2035.
Sahota wouldn't say what will happen with the EV sales mandates or whether the timelines could be pushed back.
"Of course, we're considering any requests that are made by the auto sector, they're an important sector in Ontario," Sahota said.
"The door is open to have those conversations to make sure we can still continue to bring down emissions, continue down the path we committed to as a government when it comes to EVs.
"I know there has been some challenges and we're open to making sure that it works for all our goals."
Transport Canada reported electric vehicles represented 16.5 per cent of total sales in Canada in the third quarter of 2024 and 14.2 per cent for the first nine months of last year.
'The door is open.' MP Ruby Sahota, minister of democratic institutions and minister responsible for the Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario, speaks during a press conference in Windsor on Tuesday, Jan. 14, 2025.
The pause on EV incentives by the federal government represents the third jurisdiction in the country to scrap such programs. Ontario ended its program in 2018 and Quebec announced last year it will phase out its incentives in 2026.
"The Liberal federal government has backed away from supporting the transition to electric vehicles and now we are left with a completely unrealistic plan at the federal level," said Tim Reuss, president/CEO of the Canadian Automobile Dealers Association.
"There is hypocrisy in imposing ambitious ZEV mandates and penalties on consumers when the government is showing a clear lack of motivation and support for their own policy goals.
"The end of the federal consumer EV purchase incentive program while maintaining electric vehicle mandates will be a disaster for consumers, dealers, manufacturers and the Canadian economy."
Auto industry officials also noted the slowdown in the building of charging infrastructure hampers sales. There were 4,717 chargers added in Canada in 2024 compared to 7,412 in 2023.
Related
Feds remain confident in their bet on auto industry's electrification despite slowing sales
Going electric is auto industry's biggest challenge in over a century
Global Automakers of Canada CEO/president David Adams said incentives and supports don't need to be permanent fixtures, but are important to help the industry through the transformation to electrification until price parity is achieved with gas-powered products.
"If the government is going to mandate manufacturers to put ZEVs into the marketplace and pay severe penalties for not doing so, then government needs to ensure that it is doing its part to address key barriers to EV uptake - price and infrastructure," said Adams, whose organization represents 15 automotive companies based outside of Canada.
"If they are not prepared to do that, then the federal government (and Quebec and BC) must consider revising or eliminating their mandates as the fundamental assumption and EV adoption rates on which the mandates were based, has changed."
Dwaddell@postmedia.com
Twitter.com/winstarwaddell

Subject: Fines & penalties; Automobile sales; Electric vehicles; Automobile dealers

Federal government looking at options after ending EV incentives

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/federal-government-looking-at-options-after/docview/3156269176/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 150 of 500

Premiers, PM hash it out over U.S. tariff threats; Mull Response

Author: Taylor, Stephanie; Trépanier, Antoine; Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie; Trépanier, Antoine; Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premiers-pm-hash-out-over-u-s-tariff-threats-mull/docview/3156269019/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premiers%2C+PM+hash+it+out+over+U.S.+tariff+threats%3B+Mull+Response&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie%3BTr%C3%A9panier%2C+Antoine%3BNardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The chair of Canada's premiers had a message for Americans walking into Wednesday's meeting with the prime minister, called to discuss the country's plan should U.S. president-elect Donald Trump make good on his threat to slap a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian goods.
"Canada is not for sale," were the words emblazoned on the hat worn by Ontario Premier Doug Ford - a nod to Trump's frequent comments of Canada being a "51st state."
Canadian officials initially brushed the statement off as a joke. But with Trump's swearing-in only four days away and the threat of his incoming administration applying across-the-board tariffs on Canadian products becoming a worrying inevitably, it's clear leaders are increasingly losing their sense of humour.
"President Trump tries to divide us," Ford told reporters. "The country comes first and we will continue to fight these tariffs and we will use every tool at our disposal."
Agreeing on what exactly those tools might be topped the discussion Wednesday when the premiers sat down with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, the first faceto-face meeting the group has had since Trudeau announced his resignation on Jan. 6.
With talk of Canada preparing a package of retaliatory tariffs and other counter measures, western premiers say they want the idea floated by some, including Ford, of blocking energy exports declared off the table, same with applying any export tariffs.
"It would be the most divisive conversation that this nation would ever have," Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe warned.
On the weekend, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith made that case publicly after meeting with Trump at his Mar-a-Lago estate in Florida, which raised eyebrows among Canadian officials questioning how unified a front premiers were presenting.
Speaking before Wednesday's meeting, Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew emphasized the importance of maintaining unity, when asked about the idea of blocking energy exports.
"The role that I want to have is to urge unity and that we have a comprehensive approach."
Canada's energy sector is the queen in the game of chess, Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey said.
"We don't need to expose our queen too early. The opposition needs to know that our queen exists, but they don't need to know what we are going to do with the queen.
"Let's see what the tariffs are first and have a proportional, but a strong response to the United States," Furey added.
Speaking at the start of the meeting, Trudeau said there were two main themes on the agenda. First, opportunities to expand and deepen partnerships with the Americans, and then Canada's response to potential tariffs.
"It's an important moment for Canadians to see us coming together to work to deal with the most important relationship."
Participating on the federal side was Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc, Foreign Affairs Mélanie Joly and Kristen Hillman, ambassador to the United States. Smith and B.C. Premier David Eby were joining virtually.
For weeks, federal and provincial leaders have been making the case to governors and everyday Americans, including through TV appearances, that slapping tariffs on Canadian goods would be bad for the U.S.
In an op-ed published Wednesday in The Hill, Quebec Premier François Legault argued "that tariffs of 25 per cent on exports from Canada and Mexico would reduce American GDP by at least 1 per cent over one year, not to mention inflation."
"Tariffs of 25 per cent would therefore only result in increasing the prices of beer cans, car parts, airplane parts and a host of other products for American consumers and businesses," he wrote.
He pointed out Quebec is a major supplier to the U.S. military aviation industry, such as engines, landing gear and flight simulators.
Legault also emphasized one of Quebec's greatest assets: electricity. The province exports its electricity to the U.S., including via a transmission line to New York state.
"This clean, dependable and cheap electricity is an extraordinary advantage for New Yorkers and their businesses. It lowers the cost of energy for Trump Tower, to give just one example."
Getting everyone to agree on the best approach is easier said than done, according to P.E.I. Premier Dennis King.
"It's hard to bring 13 premiers and the prime minister together and agree to the colour of Cheezies," he said. "But I do think at our root, we are about trying to do what's best for Canada, and I think we're going to try to find a very unique way to get to that today, or at least, I hope so."
As premiers assembled in Ottawa, federal Public Safety Minister David McGuinty and Immigration Minister Marc Miller told reporters the RCMP and Canada Border Services Agency were deploying additional drones, two leased Apache helicopters and surveillance towers at the Canada-U.S. border.
"These will help us to further enhance our border surveillance and our intelligence operations, again with our American partners, as further proof of our strong relationship with the United States," McGuinty told reporters.
Trump made his tariff threat after warning Canada and Mexico to take steps stop the flow of migrants into the U.S., and halt the flow of the deadly drug fentanyl, which has fuelled an overdose crisis across North America. Canadian officials say while they take Trump's concerns seriously, they reject the notion that Canada's border is where the problem lies.
National Post

Document 151 of 500

Liberals in the West see chance for revival; Leadership Race

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-west-see-chance-revival-leadership-race/docview/3156267684/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+in+the+West+see+chance+for+revival%3B+Leadership+Race&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The past several years have been lean times for Liberals west of Ontario, but insiders are hoping a spirited leadership race that happens to overweight western Liberal constituencies could help the party rebuild bridges in the region, after nearly a decade of frosty relations under Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's leadership.
"I'm already seeing tons of excitement for the upcoming leadership campaign," said Kent Hehr, a lifelong Alberta Liberal and ex-Calgary MP.
"Liberals around here have been shell-shocked for the last little while, but they're looking rejuvenated of late," Hehr said. "It's been nice to see." (Hehr resigned from Trudeau's cabinet in 2018 amid sexual harassment allegations.)
Vancouver-based Liberal strategist Mark Marissen noted the party's leadership vote rules, which give each electoral district 100 points, regardless of the number of members, tilt the system in favour of western ridings with smaller membership bases.
"The one thing that does happen is that you hear from everybody across the country ... every riding has the same weight in this leadership race," said Marissen.
Hehr estimates there are 1,000 Liberal members in his downtown Calgary riding. He expects this number to grow.
Core y Hogan, a Calgary-based strategist and former executive director of the Alberta Liberal Party, noted the leadership race's biggest names so far all have roots in Western Canada.
"Mark Carney, Chrystia Freeland and Christy Clark - all three of them grew up out West," Hogan said.
Carney, a former central banker turned Wall Street heavyweight, is set to launch his campaign in his hometown of Edmonton on Thursday. Clark, after organizing a planned leadership campaign, announced Tuesday she had changed her mind, saying her French was too weak. Freeland, whose resignation from cabinet in December preceded the prime minister's decision to quit, was raised in Peace River, Alta., although she now lives in Toronto. She is reportedly planning to enter the race in the coming days.
North Vancouver, B.C., MP Jonathan Wilkinson is also rumoured to be weighing a leadership run.
Hehr said the rules give an edge to better-organized campaigns with stronger ties to local riding associations.
"You have to have people on your team who can pick up the phone and ask, 'Hey, can you search your database for a guy I met at the gas station in Zama City, (Alta.)?'" Hehr said.
Ex-Vancouver Liberal MP and former federal cabinet minister Ujjal Dosanjh said much of the blame for the party's collapse in Western Canada leads to Trudeau.
"(Trudeau's) really severed all of his relationships with traditional Liberals before he came in," Dosanjh said. "He had new people that he recruited and put into those positions."
National Post rmohamed@postmedia.com

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada; Western Canada

Identifier / keyword: several; years; times; liberals; ontario; insiders

Document 152 of 500

Municipalities warn Ontario at 'tipping point' as homeless numbers grow

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/municipalities-warn-ontario-at-tipping-point-as/docview/3156054823/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Municipalities+warn+Ontario+at+%27tipping+point%27+as+homeless+numbers+grow&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Brian Williams, Local Journalism Initiative reporter
An in-depth study released Thursday by the Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) - a non-profit organization representing 444 municipalities in the province, including Windsor and Essex County municipalities - details the growing toll of homelessness on individuals, families, communities and governments. Here are some things to know:
ONTARIO'S HOMELESSNESS CRISIS
The study, called Municipalities Under Pressure: The Human and Financial Cost of Ontario's Homelessness Crisis, contains stark figures about the homeless population and encampments across the province. AMO did the study in partnership with Calgary-based software company HelpSeeker Technologies, the Ontario Municipal Social Services Association and the Northern Ontario Service Deliverers Association.
AMO is calling on provincial and federal governments to aid municipalities by taking "significant, long-term action" on affordable housing, mental health, addiction services and income supports to address homelessness.
"We need other orders of government to recognize the scale and the urgency of this crisis," AMO president Robin Jones said at a news conference Thursday.
An email statement from the office of Paul Calandra - Ontario's minister of municipal affairs and housing - said the provincial government has made "the largest investment in affordable housing and anti-homelessness programs in Ontario's history," though the figure wasn't stated in the email.
The federal government didn't respond to a request for comment.
WHY PROVINCE IS AT A 'TIPPING POINT' ON HOMELESSNESS
The study says about 80,000 Ontarians - more than the city of Sarnia, the population of which is about 73,000 - were homeless in 2024, a figure that has jumped by 25 per cent since 2022.
"We are at a real tipping point without concrete action," Jones said. "It is only going to get worse."
The study suggests without significant intervention, homelessness could double in a decade, and more than triple to nearly 300,000 people in an economic downturn, the report said.
In London, 1,935 people were homeless in September 2024, the city says on its website.
Oxford County Warden Marcus Ryan cited substance abuse, mental illness and domestic violence as contributors to homelessness in his jurisdiction that includes Woodstock.
"These are issues that municipalities are not really equipped to deal with," he said.
HOW MANY ENCAMPMENTS DOES ONTARIO HAVE?
Karen Redman, chairperson of Mayors and Regional Chairs of Ontario, said at the news conference there are roughly 1,400 homeless encampments across the province in both small and large communities.
In September 2024, there were 102 encampments in London, the city's website states.
In nearby Woodstock, a city of about 47,000 people, there were 44 encampments in September 2024, a city staff report says.
Redman continued: "Municipal staff and our first responders are seeing first-hand how our current responses are not working."
Tents are shown at a homeless encampment in Sarnia's Rainbow Park on May 12, 2024.
The email statement from Calandra's office said the province "has taken decisive action to support municipalities in dealing with homeless encampments across the province," noting the government of Doug Ford believes those experiencing homelessness should have "access to proper support and stable housing."
Calandra's office stated that, last month, the provincial government announced it was investing $75 million to "help connect those living in encampments with needed housing.
"We have heard loud and clear from the people of Ontario that they want their parks and public spaces back," the minister's office said. "Encampments are a public safety concern and not a solution to homelessness."
WHAT IS THE ESTIMATED COST OF ENDING HOMELESSNESS, ELIMINATING ENCAMPMENTS IN ONTARIO?
Ontario is the only province where the responsibility for social housing falls on municipalities, AMO says.
The study indicates municipal funding for housing and homelessness programs has grown significantly in recent years, totalling more than $2.1 billion in 2024.
Recent provincial investments represent a fraction of what's required for already over-extended shelter and housing programs, the study says.
"Mayors have asked us for help in managing this complex issue and that is why, over the next three years, we're investing a record $3 billion in affordable housing, anti-homelessness and emergency shelter supports," Calandra's office said.
Related
'Eye-opening' - A night of counting homelessness in Windsor
Windsor students pitch ideas to combat homelessness
Fight for Home: 'We're not bad people' - residents of Windsor's homeless encampments stuck in limbo
The 136-page AMO report estimates about $11 billion during 10 years could end chronic homelessness by adding affordable housing, improving transitional and supportive services and enhancing prevention programs, while an additional $2 billion during eight years could largely eliminate encampments.
A BETTER APPROACH TO ENDING HOMELESSNESS?
The study proposes a new approach focusing on long-term housing solutions instead of temporary emergency measures and enforcement.
Ryan shared the sentiment that emergency shelters "should be viewed as just that: providing emergency shelter.
"We cannot allow our emergency shelter systems to become a housing solution, because it's not," he said.
Oxford County recently announced its intention to use $2 million acquired by Woodstock for surpassing its housing goal on a new homelessness service centre.
"This research outlines what is needed and AMO is ready, willing and able to work with the province to chart a path forward to end homelessness in Ontario," Jones said.
bwilliams@postmedia.com
The Local Journalism Initiative is funded by the Government of Canada

Subject: Press conferences; Homeless encampments; Shelters; Affordable housing; Homeless people

Document 153 of 500

Chatham-Lakeshore hydro line comes in under budget; Project finished a year ahead of schedule, adding capacity for industry, agri-food

Author: Terfloth, Trevor

Publication info: Terfloth, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chatham-lakeshore-hydro-line-comes-under-budget/docview/3155882711/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chatham-Lakeshore+hydro+line+comes+in+under+budget%3B+Project+finished+a+year+ahead+of+schedule%2C+adding+capacity+for+industry%2C+agri-food&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Terfloth%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new Chatham to Lakeshore transmission line - carrying enough energy to power the entire city of Windsor - has been completed a year ahead of schedule, Hydro One and the province announced Monday.
The power line was energized Dec. 17, with its early completion reducing the estimated project cost approved by the Ontario Energy Board by about $30 million.
The new investment is valued at an estimated $237 million. It adds about 400 megawatts to the electricity network to support the local agri-food and manufacturing supply chain in southwest Ontario.
Stephen Lecce, Ontario's minister of energy and electrification, was on hand for Monday's announcement at Hydro One's Chatham switching station, along with officials from the utility and representatives from several municipalities.
"In this region, we are seeing explosive growth," Lecce said. "There's a massive demand for energy, in fact, at a rate twice the rate of the provincial average.
"Through greenhouses and industrial growth, and population growth, we are keeping up with that growth by delivering transmission infrastructure."
Lecce said the focus on reliable and affordable energy is key to ensuring businesses, including the new NextStar Energy battery plant in Windsor and the growing greenhouse sector concentrated in the Leamington-Kingsville area, remain competitive in the face of potential U.S. tariffs.
Building public confidence also is important, he said, with respect to the timeline and cost efficiencies. He credited the partnership between the province, Hydro One and host communities.
David Lebeter, Hydro One president and chief executive, said the project is a "shining example of what we can achieve when we collaborate and work together with Indigenous communities, the province, municipalities, residents and landowners" to deliver critical electricity infrastructure projects.
"Electricity is the backbone of our economy. This line is the first of five new transmission lines in southwest Ontario that will support growth and security in our local supply chains and continue to make Ontario an attractive place to live and invest," he stated in a news release.
According to the Independent Electricity System Operator, electricity demand is predicted to increase approximately 75 per cent across Ontario by 2050.
The 49-kilometre, double-circuit, 230-kilovolt Chatham-to-Lakeshore transmission line is part of a network of Hydro One projects across the region. Construction began in the spring of 2023 by Aecon Power Services.
"You're looking at the future of Chatham-Kent and the whole region," Chatham-Kent Mayor Darrin Canniff said. "If we didn't have this, we'd have a closed-forbusiness sign up."
MPP Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex) said the Ford government is "committed to securing the reliable and affordable energy essential for rebuilding Ontario's economy, keeping costs down for families, and supporting new home construction across the province."
"This project will not only deliver that dependable energy supply but also create good-paying jobs, bolster our world-class greenhouse sector, and fuel Ontario's economic growth for years to come," he said.
Lakeshore Mayor Tracey Bailey said the line will provide the needed capacity to attract and expand local businesses and meet the evolving needs of residents.
"We are proud to work with Hydro One and our neighbouring communities to deliver benefits to our region for years to come," she said.
Hilda MacDonald, Leamington mayor and Essex County warden, noted that "the food produced in Essex County creates jobs for local people and feeds Ontarians provincewide.
"A clean, reliable source of energy that powers our agri-food sector benefits us all." tterfloth@postmedia.com

Subject: Energy; Food; Electricity

Identifier / keyword: chatham; lakeshore; transmission; carrying; enough; energy

'Massive demand' - Hydro One completes major new $237M power line to Essex County

Document 155 of 500

Guest column: For better democracy, how about 'randomly chosen' politicians?

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-better-democracy-how-about-randomly/docview/3155575904/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+For+better+democracy%2C+how+about+%27randomly+chosen%27+politicians%3F&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: James Winter
In 2025, we face a federal election, and quite possibly an Ontario election.
More than a half-billion dollars will be spent on the next federal election, on the election apparatus. Additionally, $80 million will be spent on marketing and advertising individuals and parties. The Liberal leadership race now underway requires a $350,000 entrance fee.
Increasingly, we hear slogans and sound bites, devoid of policy.
It seems to many of us that elections are remote and elected politicians are from - or quickly absorbed into - the status quo. Political decisions, consequently, do not reflect the views of average citizens.
Some people who run are power-hungry. Their intention is not to serve the best interests of the public. Their decisions are self-serving.
We feel the system is not democratic - and it is not. Democracy means majority rule.
But let's use the example of Ontario. Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives received 40.8% of the vote in 2022. In other words, almost 60% of those who voted, chose another party. Yet, the PCs won 67% of the seats - a majority government. This is owing to our first-past-the-post system.
Federally, we elect a party, and after successive victories we opt for seemingly the only other choice, the other party. Currently, we're switching the Liberals for the Conservatives. Federally, that's all we've ever done: Liberals, Conservatives, Liberals again ...
A system using proportional representation, which most of the world uses, would make things more democratic. But what else could we do?
In ancient Athenian democracy, 'sortition' - or simple random selection - was the primary method of choosing politicians, as it was thought to be more democratic than elections, which tend to favour the well-to-do. Sortition is how we choose our jurors, who can rule on people's lives.
All Canadians over 18 would be eligible, subject to a civics test such as we give new citizens. Everyone has an equal chance. Those chosen would come from all walks of life. In 2019, 11 of 37 in the Trudeau cabinet were lawyers.
Randomly chosen politicians lead more democratically, as they feel more commitment to the social good.
And without election campaigns - no opportunities for special interests, such as big business, to run things.
James Winter is a professor emeritus at the University of Windsor

Subject: Democracy; Politicians; Political advertising; Elections

Identifier / keyword: james; winter; federal; election; quite; possibly

Document 156 of 500

Smith delivers a warning to feds; Ban on Alberta oil exports to U.S. would lead to 'National Unity crisis,' pRemier says

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/smith-delivers-warning-feds-ban-on-alberta-oil/docview/3155461135/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Smith+delivers+a+warning+to+feds%3B+Ban+on+Alberta+oil+exports+to+U.S.+would+lead+to+%27National+Unity+crisis%2C%27+pRemier+says&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith says that if the federal government imposes an export ban on Alberta oil going to the United States as a retaliatory measure against expected U.S. tariffs it would precipitate a "national unity crisis."
"Oil and gas is owned by the provinces, principally Alberta, and we won't stand for that," Smith said Monday. "I can't predict what Albertans would do."
She warned that Ottawa would have a "national unity crisis on (its) hands at the same time as having a crisis with our U.S. partners."
Smith made the remarks during a virtual press conference with reporters, which followed her meetings with U.S. president-elect Donald Trump at his West Palm Beach Mar-a-Lago resort and his golf club over the weekend.
Mélanie Joly, Canada's foreign affairs minister, was asked on CTV's Question Period talk show on Sunday if Canada would consider cutting off energy to the United States in the event of a tariff war, and responded, "Everything is on the table."
Canada is preparing a list of U.S. products against which surtaxes could be levied, should Trump bring in his promised 25-per-cent tariff on Canadian goods after he's inaugurated next week.
Canada made similar moves during negotiations in 2019 with the first Trump administration, adding more than $16 billion in surtaxes to a variety of American imports, including steel, aluminum, yogurt, toilet paper, dishwashers and various other items.
Joly's response echoes a pitch made by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who threatened in mid-December to cut off energy supply to the northeastern United States should the Trump tariffs become a reality. Smith has staunchly opposed such a measure, but said Monday morning that if Trump imposes tariffs, there will need to be a Canadian response.
"We have to wait and see what he does, and then we have to have a measured and mature response to it that hopefully does not damage the relationship or damage Canadian consumers and Canadian businesses too much," Smith said.
Smith, who said she attended Trump's Saturday soirée as a guest of celebrity investor and former Conservative party leadership candidate Kevin O'Leary, described her conversations with Trump as "constructive," but also seemed resigned that, come Jan. 20, Trump will be going ahead with tariffs, regardless of what Canadian politicians do.
She said that she is "not expecting" Alberta oil to be exempt from the Trump administration's tariffs, although she remains hopeful that the United States will want to purchase more oil from Canada for its refineries in the Midwest.
"I haven't seen any indication in any of the president's public commentary, or even in the comments that he had with me, that he's inclined to change his approach," Smith said.
The Alberta premier has been on an extensive heartsand-minds campaign in the United States, meeting with governors and appearing on conservative-leaning news programs. She has been making the case directly that U.S. tariffs would increase energy costs to American consumers. She has, however, faced criticism that by boosting Alberta she may be undermining the broader Canadian response to tariffs.
"I'm hoping that by talking about the importance of energy, that we'll be able to avoid tariffs on every Canadian product," said Smith.
Since Trump first made threats of tariffs on Canadian exports, Canadian provinces and the federal government have scrambled to respond.
In recent weeks, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has met with Trump at Mar-a-Lago, and a deputation of cabinet ministers has also travelled to Florida to meet with the incoming president's transition team. So far, there has been little indication that Trump has been persuaded to abandon his plan for tariffs, and it's all occurring against the backdrop of a domestic political crisis in Ottawa.
Last Monday, Trudeau announced that he would be resigning as prime minister, kicking off a race for the leadership of the Liberal party and proroguing Parliament until March 24. Smith has called for an immediate election to avoid what she called the "leadership vacuum" in Ottawa.
"We need somebody at the table who is able to engage with the (Trump) administration saying, 'You got a four year mandate. I got a four year mandate, let's deal.'And we don't have that right now," Smith said.
Canada's premiers and representatives from the federal government are meeting on Wednesday to discuss the tariff response. As well, a joint mission of provincial premiers will travel to Washington, D.C., on Feb. 12 to meet with Trump administration officials and talk trade. The Canadian economy, being orders of magnitude smaller than the United States', will be far more harmed by a prolonged trade war than the American economy will be, Smith said.
National Post

Subject: International trade; Political campaigns; Surtax; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Document 157 of 500

Point Pelee National Park to close for second deer cull in two months

Author: Kotsis, Julie

Publication info: Kotsis, Julie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/point-pelee-national-park-close-second-deer-cull/docview/3155461030/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Point+Pelee+National+Park+to+close+for+second+deer+cull+in+two+months&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Kotsis%2C+Julie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Point Pelee National Park will be closed temporarily later this month to allow Parks Canada and Caldwell First Nation members to conduct a deer cull.
Based on current data, it is estimated that the current deer herd population remains higher than what the ecosystem can support in the Leamington park, Parks Canada officials said in a news release.
It is the second deer cull being undertaken at Point Pelee, one of Canada's smallest national parks, this winter.
The first cull was held in early November, when officials estimated the deer herd population was twice as large as what the park's ecosystem can support.
Park officials did not say how many deer were culled in November nor how overpopulated the park is currently.
A high population of white-tailed deer in the park creates a serious threat to forest and savannah health and the species that depend on these habitats, officials said.
Parks Canada is responsible for maintaining and restoring ecological health in national parks, while Caldwell First Nation's traditional territory encompasses the park.
The deer cull is part of Parks Canada's hyperabundant deer management program, which also includes ecosystem monitoring, deer population monitoring, species at risk protection, ongoing research and collaboration.
It also provides opportunities for Caldwell First Nation to mentor youth and strengthen traditional connections to the land, in addition to sharing knowledge and expertise with Parks Canada.
The temporary closure begins Jan. 24 and continues through Jan. 31. The park will reopen to visitors on Feb. 1.
For further information, including up-to-date notices on park closures, visit the park's website at www.parkscanada.gc.ca/pelee or contact Point Pelee National Park at pelee.info@pc.gc.ca or 519-322-2365. jkotsis@postmedia.com twitter.com/KotsisStar

Subject: Animal populations; Deer; Parks & recreation areas; National parks
