"American companies ... would seek emergency injunctions in court, and if I was running those companies, that's where we would end up rather quickly," he said.
"If somebody used a legal instrument... to stop your goods from being sold and gave preference and precedence to your competition, you have a fiduciary obligation to go to court or go to Washington, or both," Volpe said.
"If I'm a shareholder, and that happens and I don't hear that, I push for change."
Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre's reaction to Trump on Tuesday, in which he said "Canada will never be the 51st state of the U.S.," was effective, said Volpe.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who announced this week he plans to resign when a new leader is chosen for the Liberal Party, posted on social media there is "not a snowball's chance in hell" that Canada will join the U.S.
"We know well from Trump 1.0 (his method) is to seek out weakness and pick the scab," said Volpe. Uncertainty in the Liberal government's leadership is a weakness, he said.
WINDSOR, ONT: JANUARY 8, 2025 - The Windsor riverfront along the Detroit River is shown on Wednesday, January 8, 2025 with downtown Detroit in the background.
Still, Trump's comments, whether or not he follows through, will likely have an effect, Volpe said.
"I think his comments are going to affect the markets, and that's important for auto companies, because they're publicly trading their share values.
"(Markets are) an important tool for shepherding the type of capital they need for new investment, or mergers and acquisitions, or to stave off tough times. So you're going to bet that any of those companies are going to be absolutely watching the markets very, very tightly, and they'll have a short- and long-term plan on Trump."
Can companies be browbeaten by Trump into making decisions favouring investment in the U.S. during the president's term? "I don't think there's a clear answer to that," said Volpe.
Trump has promised to end environmental incentives, including those aimed at electric vehicle batteries. Vehicle production slowed to 1.3 million cars last year, down from two million in 2018, largely due to the slowdown in demand for EV vehicles.
But the auto sector also stands poised to provide EV technology for the U.S.'s military. "So if you're hurting the EV transition, you're hurting your strategic capability to be flexible in the new world," Volpe said, noting that China is moving fast on EV technology.
MP Brian Masse (NDP - Windsor West), said Canadians must prepare for Trump's actions.
"There is no doubt that he's going to use every opportunity to ... use bully tactics to get manufacturers and investors to only consider the United States," Masse said.
"I strongly believe, for the auto sector, we should be examining right now domestic procurement policies for fleet purchases for municipalities, provinces and federal government, to see what products that are made in Canada in our auto sector fit the needs of addressing our goals of increasing, not only just our production, but also stabilizing," in the face of tariffs, he said.
"Because clearly there's going to be some pain."
Related
Trump's huge tariff threats not about trade but border security, Windsor observers warn
Brown-John: Mr. Trump, how about we turn U.S. states into Canadian provinces?
Guest column: Trump will be bad, but likely survivable
Canada provides an advantage for the auto industry because of the lower Canadian dollar and government health-care benefits, rather than benefits provided by auto companies, which leads to a healthier workforce, Masse said.
"We want more strength and response with regards to planning, organizing, how we're going to ... get prepared to do some amelioration for industries that are affected."
Asked if there was potential for Canada to lose some investment decisions to the U.S. during Trump's term, Masse replied: "That's a very real possibility."
He reiterated his call for a national auto strategy to set measurable targets and goals for investments.
bamacleod@postmedia.com

Subject: Aluminum; Automobiles; Supply chains; Leadership; Presidents; False information; Electric vehicles; Tariffs

Document 169 of 500

What finally forced PM to quit; From Byelection losses to cratering polls, 8 key events in the lead-up

Author: Trépanier, Antoine

Publication info: Trépanier, Antoine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-finally-forced-pm-quit-byelection-losses/docview/3152789468/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+finally+forced+PM+to+quit%3B+From+Byelection+losses+to+cratering+polls%2C+8+key+events+in+the+lead-up&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Tr%C3%A9panier%2C+Antoine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The question "Will he stay?" must have been asked a thousand times in the last couple of years. Until now the answer was always yes. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau was going to lead the Liberals into the next general election. He said so. His shrinking band of supporters were certain of it.
On Monday, that changed. Trudeau said he would be proroguing Parliament, calling a Liberal leadership race, and would resign after a new leader is chosen.
How did it come to this? These are the eight key events that explain what changed the prime minister's mind from being sure he'd lead the Liberals into the next election to announcing his resignation on Monday.
JUNE 2024: THE TORONTO- ST. PAUL'S BYELECTION
Though the polls haven't been kind to the Liberals since 2022, they could still eke out the occasional byelection win: in Notre-Dame-de-Grâce-Westmount in June 2023, and Mississauga-Lakeshore the previous December.
Then came Toronto-St. Paul's. You could call it the first big iceberg to hit the Liberal ship. It was one of their safest seats in the country, held by Liberals since 1993. But in a shocking upset, they lost the seat to the Conservatives on June 24. The message to the Liberals was clear: there is no such thing as safe Liberal seats anymore with Trudeau at the helm of the party.
JULY 2024: SUMMER OF DISCONTENT
After that stunning loss, more and more MPs within the Liberal caucus began questioning Trudeau's leadership. Three days after the results came in, New Brunswick Liberal MP Wayne Long sent a letter to his colleagues demanding Justin Trudeau's resignation.
"The voters have spoken loud and clear.
They want change. I agree," he wrote.
The Ontario Liberal caucus, which relies on urban Toronto support, had been shaken to its core. Trudeau met with his caucus in Sudbury, Ont., and apparently bought himself more time. He promised the party would bounce back.
In September, he appointed former bank governor (and now rumoured leadership wannabe) Mark Carney as his special economic adviser, in an apparent gesture to demonstrate he was willing to adapt to new realities.
SEPTEMBER 2024: BREAKUP WITH THE NDP
As the Liberals headed toward another key byelection in Quebec, the NDP surprised the prime minister by announcing on Sept. 4 that it was leaving a deal in place for more than two years. The supply and confidence agreement could have kept the minority Liberals in power until June 2025, with the NDP's support, in exchange for policy favours.
The dental-care program, for instance, was the result of that deal. Abruptly, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh tore it up.
"The fact is the Liberals are too weak, too selfish and too beholden to corporate interests to fight for people. They can't change things, they can't restore hope, they can't stop the Conservatives," he said at the time.
Although Singh would continue to prop up the Liberals in several subsequent confidence votes, the loss of the deal meant Trudeau was no longer safe in Parliament.
SEPTEMBER 2024: DISASTER IN MONTREAL
Despite the party's shaken confidence, Liberals in Quebec were still confident of winning a byelection in their stronghold of LaSalle- Émard-Verdun on Sept. 16. The seat, once held by former prime minister Paul Martin, was vacant after former attorney general David Lametti resigned following his ejection by Trudeau from cabinet.
But an underperforming Liberal candidate, a strong Parti Québécois that helped the Bloc Québécois, and a weak prime minister led to another major upset for the Liberals. They lost the byelection to the Bloc Québécois in a nail-biter, and suddenly Quebec, the party's last provincial stronghold, was now up for grabs.
OCTOBER 2024: MINISTERS FLEE CABINET
On Oct. 17, four senior cabinet ministers announced they would not seek re-election in the next election. National Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau, Sports Minister Carla Qualtrough, Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario Minister Filomena Tassi, and Northern Affairs Minister Dan Vandal joined two other cabinet ministers who had decided not to run.
Pablo Rodriguez and Seamus O'Regan had already resigned from cabinet because they did not plan to run again. For many, the next election was looking like a death march: the Conservatives had now increased their lead to 20 points in the polls over the Liberals.
OCTOBER 2024: A REVOLT BEGINS
Though the summer of discontent had cooled, it reignited when Parliament returned in the fall, and eventually became an autumn revolt. It was a very polite revolt: a delicate and courteous letter signed by two dozen Liberal MPs was presented to the prime minister on Oct. 24, asking him to resign. The MPs were mere backbenchers, and no cabinet minister openly supported them.
Trudeau said he was staying.
DECEMBER 2024: FREELAND'S COUP DE GRCE
Sensing he needed to shore up his popularity, the prime minister turned populist in November by announcing a hastily designed GST holiday for two months beginning mid-December and promising a $250 cheque to 18 million Canadians who earned employment income the previous year.
Then-finance minister Chrystia Freeland dutifully defended the giveaway in public, even as polls showed that voters saw it as a vote-buying gimmick. Behind the scenes, however, the relationship between the prime minister Freeland soured over the plan.
Trudeau then reportedly decided to fire her, and appoint her to a new job - without a ministerial portfolio - to oversee U.S.-Canada relations given the incoming presidency of Donald Trump.
He told her of his decision just days before she was due to present a $62-billion deficit in the fall economic statement. (She had promised it would be no more than $40 billion.)
Instead, the morning she was to table her promise-breaking mini-budget, she resigned, with an acid letter saying she had lost confidence in Trudeau.
First a few, then a flood of Liberals began calling for Trudeau to quit. This time, he had gone too far.
END OF 2024: THE DEADLIEST POLL
Trudeau still didn't plan to give up. He frequently insisted that he could beat Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre in a general election. Many Trudeau loyalists argued that "campaigns matter." The polls may be down for the Liberals, they admitted, but there was still time for Trudeau to turn it around.
Then, in the dying days of 2024, the Angus Reid Institute released a poll showing the Liberals had fallen to 16 per cent of voter support. The Conservatives had soared to 45 per cent. Only 16 per cent of respondents also said they wanted Trudeau to cling to power, and nearly half of them wanted the prime minister to resign immediately.
The numbers spelled possible obliteration for the Liberals in the next election: they might lose official party status.
Trudeau finally listened.
On Monday, he said he would quit.
National Post atrepanier@postmedia.com

Subject: Resignations; Leadership; Confidence; Scandals; Voters; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Resignations Leadership

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: question; asked; thousand; times; couple; years

Document 170 of 500

Guest column: Opposition leaders could benefit if Premier Ford doesn't call early vote

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-opposition-leaders-could-benefit-if/docview/3152496016/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+Opposition+leaders+could+benefit+if+Premier+Ford+doesn%27t+call+early+vote&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Brian Lilley
Doug Ford's decision to shelve the idea of taking Ontario into an early election doesn't come without risk for the province's 26th premier. The main risk is that one of his opponents, specifically Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, might get her act together and become a real threat if an election happens later.
Ontario's next election is scheduled for June 2026, but since last spring, there has been speculation that Ford could call an early election. That idea, supported by some of his closest political advisors, appears to be off the table for now.
Ford has never been fully sold on the idea of an early election, neither has he ruled it out, though. Instead, he has toyed with an early vote in public to keep the opposition New Democrats and Liberals on their toes.
Since last summer, both parties have been putting election preparations into high gear.
Both parties have sped up candidate recruitment and many candidates are already nominated. Fundraising attempts have kicked into high gear as well, with both opposition parties trying to catch up, in vain, with the well-financed PC Party.
The NDP was so sure of an early election that they cancelled their party convention, which had been scheduled for March.
With all of the uncertainty in Ottawa these days - including a leadership crisis in the governing Liberal Party and Trump's threats of tariffs on all Canadian exports to the United States - Ford has really cooled on the idea of an early election. At a time when he is publicly calling for stability and putting forth the premiers as the leadership the country needs at the moment, Ford now views the idea of going early as a threat.
According to those with inside knowledge, the premier is concerned about looking opportunistic if he calls a campaign when there is such a leadership vacuum at the federal level.
The threat of a federal vote also complicates things.
By putting off the election call, Ford may be giving NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Crombie a huge gift - time. Both parties and leaders are already much further along in their election preparedness than they otherwise would be, and having more time to prepare will only make them stronger.
Crombie is already out testing policy ideas that are designed to tap into voters who may have voted Liberal in the past but have sided with Ford and the PCs in the last two elections.
In November, Crombie announced that a Liberal government under her leadership would remove the HST from home heating and hydro bills. She also promised to drop the middle-class provincial income tax rate from 9.15% to 7.15%, a move that would save someone earning $75,000 annually $1,500 in tax savings.
Later, she announced a plan to expand access to family doctors and to make buying a home more affordable. The merits of these programs and policies can be debated, but right now, they show a shift away from Ontario's Liberal Party trying to win power by becoming more like the NDP.
"I'm a different kind of Liberal," Crombie said recently during an informal chat at a downtown Toronto cafe.
Perhaps, and if she is, then she could pose a threat to Ford down the road, but not yet. A year after winning the Liberal leadership, her party remains stuck with voter support in the 24-27% range while Ford and his PC Party are regularly polling above 40% voter support, higher than what they took in the 2022 election.
Related
LILLEY: Don't expect Doug Ford to call an early election this spring
LILLEY: Doug Ford's election speculation gets opposition ready
Analysis: Who pulls election plug first - Trudeau or Ford?
If an election were held today in Ontario, there is no doubt that Doug Ford would be elected to a third healthy majority government. That was part of the appeal of going early, taking advantage of the opposition not being ready.
Until the federal political scene and the threats from Washington are dealt with, the idea that Ford will go early is off the table. That gives Crombie, and Stiles - if the NDP can get organized - time to improve their chances.
Brian Lilley is a political columnist with the Toronto Sun.

Subject: Tax rates; Leadership; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax rates Leadership

Document 171 of 500

Opposition leaders could benefit if Ford doesn't call early election

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/opposition-leaders-could-benefit-if-ford-doesnt/docview/3151475565/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opposition+leaders+could+benefit+if+Ford+doesn%27t+call+early+election&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 172 of 500

'The silence is deafening'; Liberal MPS say time's up for trudeau to state his intentions

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/silence-is-deafening-liberal-mps-say-times-up/docview/3151475282/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+silence+is+deafening%27%3B+Liberal+MPS+say+time%27s+up+for+trudeau+to+state+his+intentions&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Prime Minister Justin Trudeau returning from vacation on Friday, some Liberal MPs say the time has arrived for him to face Canadians and say whether or not he intends to resign.
Trudeau has spent the past two weeks since former finance minister Chrystia Freeland's stunning resignation largely out of the public eye, at first staying behind closed doors and then setting off on his annual Christmas vacation, this time to B.C. Trudeau had said he would use the time to reflect on his future.
In speaking to fellow Liberals since Parliament broke for its holiday recess, Helena Jaczek, an Ontario MP who has called for Trudeau to quit, said what has become noticeable now is his silence.
"We want to keep our party together, and at this point, we're not hearing from the prime minister," she told National Post in an interview.
"I think everyone understood the need for some reflection, but it is what, more than two weeks now since Chrystia Freeland resigned. And so what I'm hearing that's a little different is, 'well the silence is deafening. Where's the leadership in a time of crisis?'" Another Ontario Liberal MP, who spoke on the condition of anonymity, said the calls for Trudeau to resign are coming from every corner, whether it's from residents who are not usually close followers of politics or "dyed-in-the-wool Liberals."
That MP said time is running out before others in caucus get prepared to add their name to the roster of roughly 20 Liberal MPs who have gone public with their desire for Trudeau to resign, adding the fact that the prime minister has yet to make a decision "is almost as bad" as the blow Freeland dealt by resigning on the day she was set to deliver the government's fiscal update, announcing her move publicly in a scathing letter.
"This isn't just about Liberal political intrigue," the MP said. "Canadians are looking at a situation where they genuinely have a concern about who will be leading the country and what the plan is going forward on confronting the Trump threat."
Regional caucus chairs were set to meet on Friday to discuss the requests to hold a national caucus meeting.
Of the 51 out of 75 Ontario Liberal MPs who met after the House of Commons broke for the holidays, most said that Trudeau should resign - a message that Jaczek says has been communicated to the Prime Minister's Office, citing information from the group's chair.
Ontario Liberal caucus chair Michael Coteau has yet to respond to a request for comment from National Post.
"As has already been widely reported, there was a consensus. Obviously, that's not unanimity, but there was a consensus that the prime minister should resign," said Jaczek.
A Quebec Liberal MP confirmed the sentiment that Trudeau must go is also shared by members within its provincial caucus, although its chair, Stéphane Lauzon, clarified in a statement posted to X that no letter has been signed or circulated by its caucus since they last met on Dec. 11, which was several days before Freeland resigned.
New Brunswick MP Wayne Long shared the letter sent by the Atlantic Liberal caucus chair, Nova Scotia MP Kody Blois, asking that Trudeau step aside, telling National Post that caucus members feel "disillusioned" that the prime minister has not yet responded to the growing calls.
"People are beyond mad now," Long says, "I think they're just really sad that the prime minister and in particular (the Prime Minister's Office) and those around him have allowed this to happen."
"Good leadership, of course, should have a lot of reflection, but good leadership also acknowledges when his caucus and, more importantly, Canadians, want to hear from him."
Long, who was one of the earliest to publicly call for Trudeau to quit following the party's surprise byelection loss in the former Liberal stronghold of Toronto-St. Paul's last June, said he expects Trudeau ultimately will resign.
"There is no other option," he said. "It's time to get out of fantasyland and get back to reality and reality clearly states that the prime minister's time is up."
Other Liberal MPs believe he will do the same, including B.C. MP Ken Hardie who said he believes Trudeau will do so "for the good of the country," as the Liberals enter an election year where they are set to face Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who has been riding high in public opinion polls since summer 2023, while Trudeau has been unsuccessful in his attempts to gain back any favour among Canadians.
Hardie pointed to a retreat the Liberal caucus had that year in London, Ont., where MPs "were assured at that time that there was a path forward."
"The leader and the Prime Minister's Office, they're pretty smart people so (we) said, 'OK, fine, let's see what that path forward is.'Well, so far we haven't seen it."
While Hardie said he believes Trudeau deserves to take the time he needs to reflect on his next steps, "the sooner the better" it would be to present a plan to Canadians.
Taleeb Noormohamed, who chairs the B.C. caucus, confirmed it met on Dec. 23 and "had a very healthy discussion about the future of the party," which remains confidential, but added those views have been communicated to the Prime Minister's Office.
On Thursday, Toronto MP and former cabinet minister Marco Mendicino added his name to the list of MPs who are not seeking re-election. Others include Nova Scotia MP Sean Fraser, who previously served as housing minister.
In a statement shared on the platform X, Mendicino did not address Trudeau's leadership, but noted how he disagreed with the Liberal government's foreign policy direction, particularly when it comes to its relationship to Israel, which he said had "deteriorated."
"In political parties, there must be room for different views."
National Post staylor@postmedia.com

Document 173 of 500

The plight of the 'NIMBY'; Part 1 Of 2; Windsor neighbourhoods fighting what seems to be a losing battle to stop change

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/plight-nimby-part-1-2-windsor-neighbourhoods/docview/3151315479/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+plight+of+the+%27NIMBY%27%3B+Part+1+Of+2%3B+Windsor+neighbourhoods+fighting+what+seems+to+be+a+losing+battle+to+stop+change&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Windsor's neighbourhoods are being destroyed - or so some would have us think.
Streets lined with single-family homes and left unchanged for decades are suddenly rife with proposals for multi-unit housing projects - developments that existing residents fear will obliterate the quiet, close-knit communities they enjoy, threatening homes in which they've invested hardearned dollars to maintain.
Residents'worries about one development often mirror the next: lost parking, lost privacy, more traffic, more noise, increased flooding, decreased property values, and the destruction of a neighbourhood's character.
It all comes down to change, change residents who are speaking out are convinced would be for the worse.
In conversations with the Star, and during city council and development committee meetings, those homeowners say they don't oppose development.
They would support different builds, ones they consider more "appropriate" for their neighbourhoods. They even support housing density - but not on their own suburban streets.
Some would consider these folks NIMBYs, which stands for not-in-my-backyard. But these so-called Windsor NIMBYs reject the term. They consider it dismissive, a convenient label for politicians to use instead of addressing concerns residents deem so legitimate, they've been turning out in droves at city hall to make themselves heard.
Alas, often to no avail. With all levels of government pushing for more housing, and new provincial legislation severely limiting Windsor's power to quash residential development, local residents are starting to feel their voices don't matter.
And yet it's not stopping them from banding together to attempt to thwart change.
"There is a wave of frustration," Catherine Archer told the Star.
The South Windsor resident, now in her 60s, has lived in the same neighbourhood around Roseland Golf and Curling Club for more than four decades. As the needs of her family evolved, she and her husband moved from one house to another, always in the same area, and retired to Cabana Road, where developers now want to erect multiple apartment buildings with dozens of units in a denser configuration to what's there now.
Archer hopes to unite her neighbours against those developments and others, including a city-led plan for luxury condos on municipal land occupied by Roseland Golf and Curling Club's oversized clubhouse, now without curling. She launched petitions against the Cabana developments and the city's Roseland plan.
"People care about what happens in their neighbourhood," Archer said.
"People have a right to say, 'I don't like this. This is what would fit better.'" Although city council has already voted to pursue housing on the Roseland property, Archer hopes she and her neighbours can still sway elected officials against the Cabana Road developments. Those proposals have yet to go before Windsor's development committee or council for consideration, but developers have held virtual public open houses to gather area residents' feedback.
What's wrong with development along Cabana Road in South Windsor? Some don't see an issue.
Windsor's mayor has said the major east-west artery with transit routes is an ideal place for more densely packed housing. Over the last nine years, the city has spent tens of millions of dollars upgrading it from what had been a county road with gravel shoulders.
But Archer, who appreciates the roadwork, has a list of reasons why she believes residential densification would better fit somewhere else. That list includes flooding, traffic, a lack of parking, and the removal of mature trees.
"NIMBY - I don't believe that's what I am," Archer said. "I think it's just a passion, it's a care for a neighbourhood.
"You want the essence of the neighbourhood to remain. There's all of the technical issues that are involved, but there's also a human component that I think city hall is missing altogether."
Elsewhere in South Windsor, a group of residents has banded together to oppose a townhouse project by Sixth Concession. The developer proposes 27 residential units on a new road that empties onto Spago Crescent, a low-traffic street with a tight curve.
Residents told the Star they anticipate traffic chaos at the proposed intersection where close calls are already a regular occurrence.
They also think the added density will worsen existing traffic problems along Sixth Concession, a former county road without sidewalks or bike lanes lined by deep ditches. They question city hall experts and the traffic impact studies that state the development would make a minimal difference.
"The people in this neighbourhood and community have lived experience," said Daniela Fraley, a resident of Spago Crescent since 2007. "We know exactly what happens when it happens. It's already an issue.
"When you add all this extra burden onto it, to me, it's just a disaster waiting to happen."
Fraley and her neighbours have attended a public open house held by the developer, a forum for gathering resident feedback. They've also spoken out against the plans at city hall during lengthy meetings of council's development and heritage committee.
Now, they're waiting for the matter to come back to city council for consideration. Ward 9 Coun. Kieran McKenzie, who represents Spago residents, recently asked that council's decision be deferred, giving the city and developer more time to assess an alternate access point.
City council now finds itself "in the line of fire" when residents don't like a development proposal, Mayor Drew Dilkens said during a public meeting this summer. He said that's because council is bound by new provincial housing legislation - the same legislation that city planners rely on when making recommendations about new development.
Among other things, that legislation pushes for more housing types at various levels of affordability. It also limits who can appeal council's land use decisions at the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT).
"We're trying to educate the residents so they understand what we can do and what we can't do to help them," Dilkens said.
If city planners deem a proposal appropriate, council's development committee - and eventually city council - usually approves it, regardless of how many residents express opposition.
That's because developers whose applications are denied can appeal council's decision to the OLT. The lengthy and costly appeal process forces the city to defend its position and explain why council decided against its own planners' recommendations.
Dilkens has said that, in his 18 years on council, he cannot recall a time when the city won an appeal in a case where council went against city planners' guidance.
At a meeting in July, when council approved steps to streamline the development process for residential growth, Dilkens said, "The change is going to be difficult for some." He acknowledged there would be "points of friction" because of the amount of growth underway.
"There needs to be a wide recognition, not just at this table, but in the community, that neighbourhoods are going to change," Ward 2 Coun. Fabio Costante said during the same meeting.
In April, the province introduced Bill 185, the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act, which, in part, limits third-party appeals of municipal planning decisions. That new law makes it much harder for individuals and resident groups to fight developments outside city hall.
Residents fought and lost an appeal over a four-storey condo on former church property at Devonshire Court and Kildare Road. Under Bill 185, the same appeal would not be accepted today. That also goes for an unsuccessful years-long appeal to the Local Planning Appeal Tribunal (OLT's predecessor) by Citizens for an Accountable Megahospital Planning Process (CAMPP) over land chosen for a new regional acute care hospital.
During a visit to Windsor on Sept. 16, Ontario Premier Doug Ford suggested Ontarians can no longer be picky about what kinds of housing is built and where.
"People need homes. They need the hope of owning a home one time. And this is no different than anywhere in the province," Ford said.
"People know we're short homes.
You can't say we need more housing: 'Oh, by the way, not in my backyard.'It doesn't cut it anymore. At one time, the people in that community walked into a new home, just like anyone else, so they have to be flexible."
That messaging appears to have failed to sway all Windsor homeowners, some of whom remain vocally opposed to the onslaught of change planned for their neighbourhoods.
On Sept. 23, city council approved an official plan amendment for lands on Sixth Concession on the north side of Ducharme Street - a different development from the one Spago residents oppose - to permit three-storey townhouses with 24 units on property previously occupied by one single-family home.
Several people spoke out in opposition, including Suzanne De-Froy, who's lived on Ducharme Street for more than 25 years. Like the nearby Spago residents, she argued the city should upgrade Sixth Concession before approving denser housing development in the area.
She brought up a previous media report in which Dilkens said it's "almost impossible" to deny new development applications that reach city council.
"I appreciate that, but I'm left wondering why we even have elected officials who make promises to protect the citizens who voted for them," DeFroy said.
With other higher-density developments completed in the broader area - she characterized them as "sacrifices" made in the Sixth Concession/North Talbot corridor - DeFroy said she and fellow residents cannot be accused of NIMBYism.
Dan Bussey, who lives on nearby Morand Street, told council anything other than single-detached houses won't "blend in with the continuity of existing homes" and expressed concern about privacy.
"Just imagine living in a house and being very comfortable there. Now a multiplex is built next door," Bussey said. "How horrible it would be to live there and be robbed of the privacy of your own yard."
Marc Masotti of the Masotti Group, the developer behind that project and several others in the region, told council he was born and raised in the home he plans to demolish to make way for townhouses. When his mother moved out of the 48-year-old house only a few years ago, they tried to sell it, but interested buyers weren't willing to pay the asking price for a home in need of "a lot of upgrading."
"The interest we were getting, ironically, on the property was from other developers," he said.
"I understand peoples' hesitation. I understand the residents' concerns. I understand the stereotype that sometimes developers get: the greedy developers looking out for their best interest.
"I'm a very transparent person.
I'm a passionate person. I care about Windsor-Essex, and I care about South Windsor.
"I have yet to do one development where I haven't sat here with opposition and taken on questions."
Masotti acknowledged that Sixth Concession needs upgrading, but added, "My contention is it's projects like this that perpetuate infrastructure advancement" through the development charges the city collects.
"We always take into consideration the residents when we do our development projects," he said.
Mike Davis with Siv-ik Planning and Design Inc., who is working with Masotti, told council his people undertook a "significant public outreach program," with a project website - a "home base for sharing information" with the neighbourhood. To tell folks about the site, they did a postcard drop to all properties within 120 metres of the land. They spoke with residents at a community information session before they created a concept for the development.
"We heard a lot about parking.
We heard a lot about privacy and setbacks. We also heard about an aversion specifically to an apartment form of development," Davis said.
As a result, they went with townhouses with garages and 40 parking stalls in total. They also shifted the buildings to the western part of the property to give "as much separation as possible" from existing yards, Davis said.
Ultimately, council gave Masotti and his team the green light to build the proposed 24 units in four townhouse buildings on a single lot.
Developer-led public open houses are a requirement of the city's development review process, which has three stages. The first two are considered "pre-consultation" and involve disseminating studies and info to city staff for scrutiny and feedback.
In the final stage, a developer submits a formal development application and all required supporting documents for further review.
City staff then identify the conditions and requirements that will form a final recommendation to council, which could include stormwater management, vehicle access points, and more.
Vladimir Drobngakovic, who has lived on Spago Crescent in South Windsor for 11 years, told the Star that when he and his neighbours spoke out at a developer's open house and at city hall, he felt townhouse development plans were already set in stone, no matter what input residents gave.
"I believe my voice should count as a taxpaying citizen," Drobngakovic said.
Drobngakovic and his neighbours took issue with what they felt was a lack of data on how new townhouses would affect traffic on their abutting street. They were told it would have a "negligible impact," he said, but he didn't believe city staff and wanted a more fulsome study included in the report to city council.
"Do we trust the experts? No, we don't. At least, I don't," Drobngakovic said.
A lack of trust in city staff - planners, engineers, and others - appears to be growing. Residents who've lived through basement flooding and witnessed car accidents on their streets doubt accredited professionals who study those issues and conclude developments won't significantly change things.
It's also not a developer's job to calm traffic, or to fix any other pre-existing issues, said Neil Robertson, the city's executive director of planning and development.
"That shouldn't be the expectation of the development," Robertson told the Star. "We do the review to ensure that they're not going to make those conditions worse or contribute to them in any negative way."
As for residents of Spago still waiting to find out the fate of the lot adjacent to their street, Fraley said, "We don't oppose something going in there. We just want something that's going to be safe and something that's reasonable."
She hopes city council sides with existing property owners. But if the townhouse development she and her neighbours dread is eventually approved, she said she'll probably move.
"I don't want to be burdened with all of that," she said. "I don't want to be scared every time I go out of my driveway."
Neighbour Drobngakovic said he felt the same.
"I bought the home so I can enjoy it, so I can enjoy the area, the residents, my fellow neighbours," he said.
"If I can't enjoy it, then what's the point of staying in this area?" EDITOR'S NOTE: This is first in twopart series. Next: How does Windsor city hall decide what gets built? tcampbell@postmedia.com twitter.com/wstarcampbell

Subject: Legislation; Councils; Appeals; Neighborhoods; Planning; Housing developments; Public officials; Cities; Homeowners; Condominiums

Document 174 of 500

Essex County seeks to map its transportation future

Author: MacLeod, Brian

Publication info: MacLeod, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/essex-county-seeks-map-transportation-future/docview/3151227210/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Essex+County+seeks+to+map+its+transportation+future&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=MacLeod%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How does one make a roadmap of a roadmap?
If you're Jerry Behl, Essex County's manager of transportation planning and development, you get cracking on a master plan for the region's transportation in the region for the next 30 years.
It's not simply about widening roads or building new ones. It's tying together economic growth, social opportunities, micro-transportation - such as ebikes - and a predicted substantial increase in the county's population.
The 2021 Census had about 193,000 people living in Essex County, among its seven municipalities and the various rural areas.
But that population is expected to grow from 30 to 50 per cent in the next 30 years, said Alvaro Almuina, vice-president of municipal transportation for R.J. Burnside and Associates, an engineering and environmental consulting firm, who is guiding the development of the county's transportation master plan.
That means, said Behl, the county is looking at a population of somewhere between 268,000 and 315,000 people.
The county can expect to add from 2,300 to 3,900 new residents each year. For perspective, for the last 15 years, the county has grown at a rate of about 1,000 new residents per year.
TECUMSEH, ONT: OCTOBER 17, 2024. The newly completed roundabout at the intersection of County Road 42 and County Road 43/Banwell Road is shown on Thursday, October 17, 2024. The Transportation Master Plan is looking at what the road system must look like in 30 years.
Add to that expected growth in Windsor, which is expected to hit 2.9 per cent a year until at least 2028. Economic growth in the region - with the NextStar EV battery plant, and its many suppliers, means robust growth is expected well into the foreseeable future.
That makes transportation master plan - a guiding document that will help future decisionmakers - an important tool not only to ensure county residents can travel to and from work, but also to ensure an efficient movement of goods.
It must also take into account the possible effects of climate change as environmental pressures such as flooding affect the county's roads.
"In future years, if fuel prices were to rise to an extent where people are thinking much more about saving money, perhaps we would see more smaller vehicles or ebikes," said Behl in an interview with the Windsor Star.
OLDCASTLE, THURSDAY, DECEMBER 5, 2024 - lvaro Almuina, vice-president of municipal transportation for R.J. Burnside and Associates, an engineering and environmental consulting firm, is guiding the development of the county's transportation master plan. He presented at a public meeting at the Ciociaro Club Thursday, December 5, 2024.
That is one of the many considerations the master plan must entertain in putting together a transportation master plan over the next few months. The process started last June, with a couple of public meetings so far. More are expected in February with the process scheduled to go well into the summer.
The first public meeting took place at the Essex Civic Centre earlier this fall, and the second took place at the Ciociaro Club on Dec. 5.
So far, the process hasn't grabbed residents' imagination. While employees of the various counties and the Essex Region Conservation Authority attended the most recent meeting, only a handful of interested residents took part.
Almuina, who is guiding the process, said the process of getting people from A to B is complicated.
He cited key themes: safe and "equitable" travel - meaning transportation opportunities exist for "multi-model" transportation - cars, public transit, bicycles, ebikes, and it must work for everyone - families, seniors, those without cars and those who need accessible transportation.
It must also make the most efficient use of energy and not just fossil fuels.
Nadine Ibrahim, an assistant professor urban engineering at the University of Waterloo, told the audience at the public meeting that congestion isn't solved by bigger roads or new technology.
"If everybody was to drive an electric vehicle, we still have the exact same congestion we have today," she said, in the wake of an expected increase in population.
Many residents in Windsor-Essex commute outside their own region to work. So the master plan must consider not just where people live, but where they're going to work.
Justin Jones, who leads the Active Transportation Section at HDR, an engineering firm that focuses on "smart" transportation said about 30 per cent of the population does not drive due to age, income or other factors, Jones said.
"I think it's really important for us to emphasize that we are failing to serve about 30 per cent of our population when we only design for plate people who can drive," he said.
The county has done some modelling already, mapping out the city's major road network that will help discover what is needed to address the growing population.
There is no public transit system serving the entire county but there are buses that operate from Windsor to LaSalle, Amherstburg, Kingsville, Essex and Leamington.
Is a county-wide public transit system needed?
"Feedback that I have so far is that there is an interest in providing more transit within the county," said Behl, saying timing of such a system is a chicken and egg scenario, depending on when ridership would be enough to support it.
Ibrahim notes municipalities such as Wellington County, Pembroke and the District Municipality of Muskoka are experimenting with on-demand public transportation.
If ebikes become popular modes of transportation, what trails are needed and where would charging stations be located?
And what of bike lanes? Premier Doug Ford has recently issued more stringent rules regarding where they can be placed, but if ebikes do become more popular, would more bike lanes be needed and where would they be best located? And would an electric charging infrastructure be needed?
All questions for the master plan.
The County Wide Active Transportation system, a non-motorized trail system that first developed a master plan in 2012, then updated it a few years ago, aims to have an 800-km network connecting all seven local municipalities and the municipality of Chatham-Kent.
LAKESHORE, ONT: DECEMBER 3, 2024 - A County Wide Active Transportation System Route sign is shown along Old Tecumseh Road in Lakeshore on Tuesday, December 3, 2024. How would trails tie in to the road system for transportation?
"It's getting this balance between all these things ... which road do we have? Cyclists and cycle facilities, on all roads and maybe county roads aren't the best place every time for some cyclists," said Behl. "In some places they're the only really good direct alternatives."
Lori Newton who spoke at the public meeting, lauded LaSalle, Tecumseh, Kingsville and Amherstburg for their systems of walking and biking trails that connect with roads, and with ERCA's trail system, as well as Windsor's waterfront trail system.
But she noted the region's transportation system was largely mapped out and built in after the Second World War and engaged only able-bodied men who needed to get to and from work as quickly as possible.
"It's not going to surprise anybody that (the design of transportation system) often leads to under serving vulnerable road users, pedestrians and cyclists, often as we widen car lanes to accommodate even ever widening and larger vehicles," Newton said.
Simply building roads to accommodate all modes of transportation isn't working, she said.
"I used to believe that share the road was the way to go. ... But I don't think it is fair and it isn't safe for people walking in cycling to have to share the road with a two-tonne truck who view vulnerable road users as an impediment, as an obstacle in their very important car journey."
There is also the question of resilience, a term that's prominent among policy makers and infrastructure planners in recent years.
Several roads have been shut down in the county this year due to flooding. Resilience in the transportation system means it can withstand the potential ravages of climate change, or that doesn't break down in the face of gasoline shortages.
Behl is seeking more "meaningful" engagement than he's getting from the public meetings.
He plans to reach out to migrant workers, students, the elderly population, Indigenous communities, greenhouse growers and any others who have a stake in the transportation of people and goods throughout the region.
Ibrahim says residents must participate to make sure the county's future is well planned, just as those in previous decades did.
"The decisions we make today will far outlive us ... We're at a stage where we can influence the decisions three decades from now, so it's a huge responsibility, but we have the ability to influence now."
The county has an online survey asking for public input. The survey can be found on the Essex County Master Transportation Plan website at: tinyurl.com/r329pd2c.
bamacleod@postmedia.com

Subject: Transportation planning; Automobiles; Public transportation; Older people; Consulting firms; Economic growth; Engineering firms; Municipalities; Roads & highways; Bicycling

Business indexing term: Subject: Consulting firms Economic growth Engineering firms

Title: Essex County seeks to map its transportation future

Document 175 of 500

'Wave of frustration' - Windsor neighbourhoods losing battle to stop change

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/wave-frustration-windsor-neighbourhoods-losing/docview/3151124767/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Wave+of+frustration%27+-+Windsor+neighbourhoods+losing+battle+to+stop+change&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Windsor's neighbourhoods are being destroyed - or so some would have us think.
Streets lined with single-family homes and left unchanged for decades are suddenly rife with proposals for multi-unit housing projects - developments that existing residents fear will obliterate the quiet, close-knit communities they enjoy, threatening homes in which they've invested hard-earned dollars to maintain.
Residents' worries about one development often mirror the next: lost parking, lost privacy, more traffic, more noise, increased flooding, decreased property values, and the destruction of a neighbourhood's character.
It all comes down to change, change residents who are speaking out are convinced would be for the worse.
In conversations with the Star, and during city council and development committee meetings, those homeowners say they don't oppose development. They would support different builds, ones they consider more "appropriate" for their neighbourhoods. They even support housing density - but not on their own suburban streets.
Some would consider these folks NIMBYs, which stands for not-in-my-back-yard. But these so-called Windsor NIMBYs reject the term. They consider it dismissive, a convenient label for politicians to use instead of addressing concerns residents deem so legitimate, they've been turning out in droves at city hall to make themselves heard.
Alas, often to no avail.
With all levels of government pushing for more housing, and new provincial legislation severely limiting Windsor's power to quash residential development, local residents are starting to feel their voices don't matter.
And yet it's not stopping them from banding together to attempt to thwart change.
"There is a wave of frustration," Catherine Archer told the Star.
The South Windsor resident, now in her 60s, has lived in the same neighbourhood around Roseland Golf and Curling Club for more than four decades. As the needs of her family evolved, she and her husband moved from one house to another, always in the same area, and retired to Cabana Road, where developers now want to erect multiple apartment buildings with dozens of units in a denser configuration to what's there now.
People care about what happens in their neighbourhood
Archer hopes to unite her neighbours against those developments and others, including a city-led plan for luxury condos on municipal land currently occupied by Roseland Golf and Curling Club's oversized clubhouse, now without curling. She launched petitions against the Cabana developments and the city's Roseland plan.
"People care about what happens in their neighbourhood," Archer said.
"People have a right to say, 'I don't like this. This is what would fit better.'"
'The change is going to be difficult for some.' Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens speaks at a press conference along Cabana Road on July 30, 2024.
Although city council has already voted to pursue housing on the Roseland property, Archer hopes she and her neighbours can still sway elected officials against the Cabana Road developments. Those proposals have yet to go before Windsor's development committee or council for consideration, but developers have held virtual public open houses to gather area residents' feedback.
What's wrong with development along Cabana Road in South Windsor? Some don't see an issue.
Windsor's mayor has said the major east-west artery with transit routes is an ideal place for more densely packed housing. Over the last nine years, the city has spent tens of millions of dollars upgrading it from what had been a county road with gravel shoulders.
But Archer, who appreciates the roadwork, has a list of reasons why she believes residential densification would better fit somewhere else. That list includes flooding, traffic, a lack of parking, and the removal of mature trees.
"NIMBY - I don't believe that's what I am," Archer said. "I think it's just a passion, it's a care for a neighbourhood.
"You want the essence of the neighbourhood to remain. There's all of the technical issues that are involved, but there's also a human component that I think city hall is missing altogether."
Elsewhere in South Windsor, a group of residents has banded together to oppose a townhouse project by Sixth Concession. The developer proposes 27 residential units on a new road that empties onto Spago Crescent, currently a low-traffic street with a tight curve.
Residents told the Star they anticipate traffic chaos at the proposed intersection where close calls are already a regular occurrence.
They also think the added density will worsen existing traffic problems along Sixth Concession, a former county road without sidewalks or bike lanes lined by deep ditches. They question city hall experts and the traffic impact studies that state the development would make a minimal difference.
Just a disaster waiting to happen
"The people in this neighbourhood and community have lived experience," said Daniela Fraley, a resident of Spago Crescent since 2007. "We know exactly what happens when it happens. It's already an issue.
"When you add all this extra burden onto it, to me, it's just a disaster waiting to happen."
Fraley and her neighbours have attended a public open house held by the developer, a forum for gathering resident feedback. They've also spoken out against the plans at city hall during lengthy meetings of council's development and heritage committee.
Now, they're waiting for the matter to come back to city council for consideration. Ward 9 Coun. Kieran McKenzie, who represents Spago residents, recently asked that council's decision be deferred, giving the city and developer more time to assess an alternate access point.
City council now finds itself "in the line of fire" when residents don't like a development proposal, Mayor Drew Dilkens said during a public meeting this summer. He said that's because council is bound by new provincial housing legislation - the same legislation that city planners rely on when making recommendations about new development.
Among other things, that legislation pushes for more housing types at various levels of affordability. It also limits who can appeal council's land use decisions at the Ontario Land Tribunal.
The construction site of a condominium complex on Lauzon Road in east Windsor is shown on June 27, 2024. As elsewhere across Ontario and Canada, Windsor is under pressure to do its bit to help solve the current housing crisis.
"We're trying to educate the residents so they understand what we can do and what we can't do to help them," Dilkens said.
If city planners deem a proposal appropriate, council's development committee - and eventually city council - usually approves it, regardless of how many residents express opposition.
That's because developers whose applications are denied can appeal council's decision to the Ontario Land Tribunal (OLT). The lengthy and costly appeal process forces the city to defend its position and explain why council decided against its own planners' recommendations.
Dilkens has said that, in his 18 years on council, he cannot recall a time when the city won an appeal in a case where council went against city planners' guidance.
Neighbourhoods are going to change
At a meeting in July, when council approved steps to streamline the development process for residential growth, Dilkens said, "The change is going to be difficult for some." He acknowledged there would be "points of friction" because of the amount of growth underway.
"There needs to be a wide recognition, not just at this table, but in the community, that neighbourhoods are going to change," Ward 2 Coun. Fabio Costante said during the same meeting.
In April, the province introduced Bill 185, the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act, which, in part, limits third-party appeals of municipal planning decisions. That new law makes it much harder for individuals and resident groups to fight developments outside city hall.
Residents fought and lost an appeal over a four-storey condo on former church property at Devonshire Court and Kildare Road. Under Bill 185, the same appeal would not be accepted today. That also goes for an unsuccessful years-long appeal to the Local Planning Appeal Tribunal (OLT's predecessor) by Citizens for an Accountable Megahospital Planning Process (CAMPP) over land chosen for a new regional acute care hospital.
During a visit to Windsor on Sept. 16, Ontario Premier Doug Ford suggested Ontarians can no longer be picky about what kinds of housing is built and where.
'People need homes.' Premier Doug Ford speaks during a press conference in Windsor on Sept. 16, 2024, at the future site of the new Windsor/Essex Acute Care Hospital.
"People need homes. They need the hope of owning a home one time. And this is no different than anywhere in the province," Ford said.
"People know we're short homes. You can't say we need more housing: 'Oh, by the way, not in my backyard.' It doesn't cut it anymore. At one time, the people in that community walked into a new home, just like anyone else, so they have to be flexible."
That messaging appears to have failed to sway all Windsor homeowners, some of whom remain vocally opposed to the onslaught of change planned for their neighbourhoods.
On Sept. 23, city council approved an official plan amendment for lands on Sixth Concession on the north side of Ducharme Street - a different development from the one Spago residents oppose - to permit three-storey townhouses with 24 units on property previously occupied by one single-family home.
Several people spoke out in opposition, including Suzanne DeFroy, who's lived on Ducharme Street for more than 25 years. Like the nearby Spago residents, she argued the city should upgrade Sixth Concession before approving denser housing development in the area.
She brought up a previous media report in which Dilkens said it's "almost impossible" to deny new development applications that reach city council.
"I appreciate that, but I'm left wondering why we even have elected officials who make promises to protect the citizens who voted for them," DeFroy said.
At a meeting of city council on Sept. 23, 2024, resident Suzanne DeFroy speaks out against a proposed higher-density townhouse development near her South Windsor home.
With other higher-density developments completed in the broader area - she characterized them as "sacrifices" made in the Sixth Concession/North Talbot corridor - DeFroy said she and fellow residents cannot be accused of NIMBYism.
Dan Bussey, who lives on nearby Morand Street, told council anything other than single-detached houses won't "blend in with the continuity of existing homes" and expressed concern about privacy.
"Just imagine living in a house and being very comfortable there. Now a multi-plex is built next door," Bussey said. "How horrible it would be to live there and be robbed of the privacy of your own yard."
Marc Masotti of the Masotti Group, the developer behind that project and several others in the region, told council he was born and raised in the home he plans to demolish to make way for townhouses. When his mother moved out of the 48-year-old house only a few years ago, they tried to sell it, but interested buyers weren't willing to pay the asking price for a home in need of "a lot of upgrading."
"The interest we were getting, ironically, on the property was from other developers," he said.
"I understand peoples' hesitation. I understand the residents' concerns. I understand the stereotype that sometimes developers get: the greedy developers looking out for their best interest.
"I'm a very transparent person. I'm a passionate person. I care about Windsor-Essex, and I care about South Windsor.
"I have yet to do one development where I haven't sat here with opposition and taken on questions."
Masotti acknowledged that Sixth Concession needs upgrading, but added, "My contention is it's projects like this that perpetuate infrastructure advancement" through the development charges the city collects.
"We always take into consideration the residents when we do our development projects," he said.
Marc Masotti (centre) of Masotti Group answers questions about a proposed townhouse development on Sixth Concession and Ducharme Street during a meeting of Windsor city council on Sept. 23, 2024.
Mike Davis with Siv-ik Planning and Design Inc., who is working with Masotti, told council his people undertook a "significant public outreach program," with a project website - a "home base for sharing information" with the neighbourhood. To tell folks about the site, they did a postcard drop to all properties within 120 metres of the land. They spoke with residents at a community information session before they created a concept for the development.
"We heard a lot about parking. We heard a lot about privacy and setbacks. We also heard about an aversion specifically to an apartment form of development," Davis said.
As a result, they went with townhouses with garages and 40 parking stalls in total. They also shifted the buildings to the western part of the property to give "as much separation as possible" from existing yards, Davis said.
Ultimately, council gave Masotti and his team the green light to build the proposed 24 units in four townhouse buildings on a single lot.
Developer-led public open houses are a requirement of the city's development review process, which has three stages. The first two are considered "pre-consultation" and involve disseminating studies and info to city staff for scrutiny and feedback.
In the final stage, a developer submits a formal development application and all required supporting documents for further review. City staff then identify the conditions and requirements that will form a final recommendation to council, which could include stormwater management, vehicle access, points, and more.
My voice should count
Vladimir Drobngakovic, who has lived on Spago Cresent in South Windsor for 11 years, told the Star that when he and his neighbours spoke out at a developer's open house and at city hall, he felt townhouse development plans were already set in stone, no matter what input residents gave.
"I believe my voice should count as a taxpaying citizen," Drobngakovic said.
Drobngakovic and his neighbours took issue with what they felt was a lack of data on how new townhouses would affect traffic on their abutting street. They were told it would have a "negligible impact," he said, but he didn't believe city staff and wanted a more fulsome study included in the report to city council.
"Do we trust the experts? No, we don't. At least, I don't," Drobngakovic said.
Bird's-eye view of a crane operator moving dirt at the foundation of a townhouse under construction on Barkley Avenue in Windsor on April 10, 2024.
A lack of trust in city staff - planners, engineers, and others - appears to be growing. Residents who've lived through basement flooding and witnessed car accidents on their streets doubt accredited professionals who study those issues and conclude developments won't significantly change things.
It's also not a developer's job to calm traffic, or to fix any other pre-existing issues, said Neil Robertson, the city's executive director of planning and development.
"That shouldn't be the expectation of the development," Robertson told the Star. "We do the review to ensure that they're not going to make those conditions worse or contribute to them in any negative way."
Related
'Going to be a different place:' Windsor-Essex builders scramble to solve affordable housing riddle Author of the article:
'Neighbourhoods are going to change' - Angry reactions as Windsor speeds up home builds
Windsor ranks near bottom in new report on Canadian homebuilding
As for residents of Spago still waiting to find out the fate of the lot adjacent to their street, Fraley said, "We don't oppose something going in there. We just want something that's going to be safe and something that's reasonable."
She hopes city council sides with existing property owners. But if the townhouse development she and her neighbours dread is eventually approved, she said she'll probably move.
"I don't want to be burdened with all of that," she said. "I don't want to be scared every time I go out of my driveway."
Neighbour Drobngakovic said he felt the same.
"I bought the home so I can enjoy it, so I can enjoy the area, the residents, my fellow neighbours.
"If I can't enjoy it, then what's the point of staying in this area?"
EDITOR'S NOTE: This is first in two-part series. Next: How does Windsor city hall decide what gets built?

Document 176 of 500

Liberals plunge to all-time low in polls; 16 Per Cent support could mean only six seats for party in next government

Author: Hopper, Tristin

Publication info: Hopper, Tristin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-plunge-all-time-low-polls-16-per-cent/docview/3150953120/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+plunge+to+all-time+low+in+polls%3B+16+Per+Cent+support+could+mean+only+six+seats+for+party+in+next+government&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2025-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Hopper%2C+Tristin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With dozens of MPs in the Liberal caucus now calling for the resignation of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, an Angus Reid Institute poll finds that the party's public support has dropped to a historic low.
Among decided voters, just 16 per cent expressed an intention to vote Liberal in the next election, according to a survey published Monday.
The figure "represents a low in vote intention for the Liberals since Trudeau became leader, but quite possibly the lowest support the party has received in modern times," read an accompanying analysis.
The usual benchmark for Liberal ignominy are the opinion polls preceding the 2011 federal election. Under the leadership of Michael Ignatieff, the party scored its worst electoral result, winning just 34 seats and becoming the thirdranked party in the House of Commons for the first time since Confederation.
Even then, Ignatieff never once suffered a poll that put him as low as 16 per cent. His worst recorded showing was an April 2011 COM-PAS poll that put the Liberals at 17 per cent.
Another Ipsos-Reid poll released that same day put the Liberals at 18 per cent. At the time, Ipsos-Reid called it "one of the worst showings in memory," but it would end up being one of the more accurate predictions of the 2011 election. The Liberals won over just 18.9 per cent of the electorate on election day.
If 16 per cent holds until election day, the Liberals could end up beating the usually cited record for catastrophic electoral defeat: the 1993 immolation of the Progressive Conservatives under Kim Campbell.
In that election, the PCs garnered just 16.04 per cent of the popular vote, a result that yielded just two seats in the House of Commons, and ultimately doomed the party as a viable electoral force.
A projection by election modeller Raymond Liu found that if the latest Angus Reid results hold up on election day, the Liberals could enter the next Parliament with just six seats. Five of those would be in Atlantic Canada, with only one - the Montreal riding of Saint-Léonard-Saint-Michel - remaining in Quebec.
The Liberals were already scraping new lows in popular support before the snap resignation of Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland triggered a crisis within the party.
Over the weekend, the party's Atlantic caucus released a letter publicly calling for Trudeau to step down - joining more than a dozen fellow Liberals who have already said the same.
Prior to the crisis, the Angus Reid Institute had the Liberals at 21 per cent in an October poll. The five points they've lost in the interim two months seems to have been shared evenly by the Conservatives, the NDP and the Bloc Québécois, who all saw slight upticks in their own poll numbers.
Nevertheless, the NDP is still in markedly poor shape given the collapse of the Liberals.
Historically, the New Democrats have been the direct beneficiaries of Liberal disillusionment; the Liberals'worst-ever showing in 2011 coincided with the best-ever showing for the NDP.
But even as Liberal support has entered steady decline since the 2021 federal election, the NDP has stubbornly hovered at around 20 per cent, hitting just 21 per cent in the most recent survey.
The Conservatives, meanwhile, are on track for a blowout victory. The new poll has them at 45 per cent. That's 24 points higher than the next closest contender (the NDP), and eight points higher than the combined vote share of both the NDP and the Liberals.
Liu's projection has them winning a supermajority of 241 seats - roughly 70 per cent of all the seats. It's a victory that would best anything the Liberals have ever done, but it would still fall in third place behind Progressive Conservative landslides won in both 1958 and 1984.
National Post

Subject: Prime ministers

Company / organization: Name: Ipsos-Reid; NAICS: 541910
