Document 247 of 500

'Spending fiasco' - Premier orders school board audits amid travel scandals: MPP

Author: Morden, Paul

Publication info: Morden, Paul. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/spending-fiasco-premier-orders-school-board/docview/3126407285/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Spending+fiasco%27+-+Premier+orders+school+board+audits+amid+travel+scandals%3A+MPP&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Morden%2C+Paul&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: SARNIA - An audit of Ontario school boards has been ordered by Premier Doug Ford following a report the Lambton Kent District school board spent more than $32,000 to send three employees to a conference in Hawaii, says the MPP for Sarnia-Lambton.
The trip - taken by staff from the Chatham-based board's Indigenous liaison committee - is just the latest of several recent travel scandals that have irked taxpayers in communities across Southwestern Ontario.
"I won't use the vernacular to describe what I think of this spending fiasco that took place," Sarnia-Lambton MPP Bob Bailey said of the Hawaii trip. "I'm very disappointed in the board, and the boards across the province."
CBC London reported it cost more than $32,000 for the trio to attend a conference in Hawaii from Jan. 3-6. Bailey, a member of the Progressive Conservative government, said it's gotten the attention of the premier.
"Premier Ford said we're calling for an audit of all the school boards, both public and Catholic, to see how they're spending their money," Bailey said. "If they're going to be asking for more money and more dollars, education is important, but the money's got to be spent on students and on program spending."
The Lambton Kent board has 66 schools, 55 elementary and 13 secondary, in Sarnia, Lambton County and Chatham-Kent. In a statement, officials defended the decision to send three employees to Hawaii.
"Attending this conference provided (board) Indigenous education staff with the invaluable, once-in-a-career opportunity to elevate their professional development and actively contribute to the ongoing growth and improvement of" the board, the statement said.
The board confirmed that three "front line" staff from its Indigenous education team attended the January conference in Waikoloa, Hawaii, but didn't confirm the cost.
Bailey questioned why the trip was needed at all.
"They could have done the same thing on a Zoom call ... for a heck of a lot less money," he said. "We expect, as the government, that the money that's going to these school boards will be spent on students, and teachers and programs. Not on expensive trips, getaways."
The board said the trip was approved by senior administration and supported by an Indigenous liaison committee made up of the four First Nations - Aamjiwnaang, Delaware, Kettle and Stony Point and Walpole Island - with which board works.
Expenses, including registration fees, travel expenses, accommodations and meals, were paid from the board's funds for Indigenous education and professional development.
There have been three other recent cases of school board travel spending across Southwestern Ontario that have garnered public attention:
The London-based Thames Valley District school board spent more than $38,000 on a three-day August retreat by 18 officials at the hotel inside the Toronto Blue Jays stadium. After it was uncovered by the London Free Press and Education Minister Jill Dunlop announced an operational audit of the board, the education director took a paid leave and several other senior staff exited.
This week, Postmedia News reported the London District Catholic school board spent $16,000 on a one-night, two-day August meeting for 26 senior staffers in St. Catharines.
The Brantford Expositor reported an art-buying trip to Italy by four trustees with the Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District school board cost more than $50,000. It was later announced the trustees would repay expenses incurred by the board. Ontario's education ministry has launched a governance review of the board.
pmorden@postmedia.com
Related

School board boss clamps down on executives' spending after $38K retreat

NDP Struggling Nationally Despite Provincial Wins, Poll Indicates; 'Concerning'drop in Singh support; BQ, Liberals gain

Author: Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-struggling-nationally-despite-provincial-wins/docview/3126262839/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+Struggling+Nationally+Despite+Provincial+Wins%2C+Poll+Indicates%3B+%27Concerning%27drop+in+Singh+support%3B+BQ%2C+Liberals+gain&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Support for federal New Democrats in B.C. and the Prairies is dropping at a "concerning" rate for leader Jagmeet Singh, a new poll suggests, despite recent gains by his provincial counterparts in Manitoba and Saskatchewan.
The Postmedia-Leger poll finds that the NDP's popularity has dropped two percentage points nationally, to 15 per cent, since the last poll on Sept. 30.
Conservatives remain at 42 per cent support, while the Liberals are at 26 per cent, a one-point increase. The Bloc Québécois's national support bumped up two points to nine per cent, translating to 36-per-cent popularity in Quebec.
But digging deeper into regional variations show a problematic trend for the federal NDP, said Leger vice-president Andrew Enns.
The poll suggests the federal NDP's support in British Columbia has fallen to 19 per cent, a 10-point drop since late September when it was at 29 per cent. It also shows the party losing four points in the Manitoba and Saskatchewan Prairie region, from 25 to 21 per cent.
"They're certainly not regions of strength for the NDP, and that's concerning" for the party, Enns said.
The NDP's support remains unchanged in Ontario, Quebec and the Atlantic provinces.
"They have to do well in British Columbia, quite frankly," Enns said.
"They have a lot of seats in that province and they have to be a bit worried that the federal Tories are going to start encroaching into some of their areas."
Enns said the drop is all the more remarkable given the recent boosts in popularity for provincial NDP parties in both Saskatchewan and Manitoba.
In October 2023, the Manitoba NDP won government after nearly 11 years of Progressive Conservative rule.
Last month, Saskatchewan NDP Leader Carla Beck led her party to its strongest showing in nearly two decades, winning 27 of the province's 61 ridings.
In B.C., the provincial NDP was able to win re-election with a thin majority last month, although it lost seats from the 2020 election.
Enns says that Singh is likely pondering why those provincial NDP brands did relatively well, while his federal brand is struggling.
The Saskatchewan NDP leader also distanced herself from the federal brand during the provincial election, which Enns said should trouble Singh.
"That disconnect is challenging for the federal NDP," Enns said.
"The NDP, and Jagmeet Singh, has had trouble connecting with Canadians for quite a while," he added.
The new poll also suggests the rise in the Bloc Québécois's popularity in Quebec suggests the party is eating into Liberal and Conservative support there.
"Conservatives can't ignore that because they have between 10 and 12 seats that they want to continue to hold in Quebec," Enns said. "And it obviously gets very difficult for them to grow that (number) if the Bloc Québécois keeps going up."
"But I also think the Liberals have to be a little nervous about that as well, because that probably starts to put some of those suburban Montreal seats that the Liberals hold ... in play," he added.
The Liberals'fear is likely compounded by their byelection loss in the Montreal riding of LaSalle-Émard- Verdun to the Bloc in September.
Still, over the last four months, polls are showing the Liberals'support slowly trending upward after roughly a year of stumbling, Enns said.
That uptick has come largely at the expense of the NDP, while strong levels of Conservative support have remained virtually untouched.
"They (Liberals) don't seem to be, at this stage of the game, chipping away at any sort of Conservative voters, which ... at some point has got to start to happen" if the Liberals want to stay in government, Enns said.
"If you're the prime minister, you're not out of the woods by any stretch, but if nothing else, it probably bolsters the prime minister's message to his caucus that, 'look, we're making progress,'" he added.
There remains "significant dissatisfaction" with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government, the poll shows.
Only 27 per cent of respondents said they were satisfied with the current Liberal government, while 68 per cent said they were dissatisfied.
The polling was done between Nov. 1 and 3 with a sample of 1,549 Canadians via an online panel. While margins of error can't be applied to online panels, a probability sample of this size would be plus or minus 2.44 per cent, 19 times out of 20.
National Post cnardi@postmedia.com

Document 249 of 500

Trump's win will bring boost to Trudeau

Author: Kinsella, Warren

Publication info: Kinsella, Warren. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trumps-win-will-bring-boost-trudeau/docview/3126262728/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trump%27s+win+will+bring+boost+to+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Kinsella%2C+Warren&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The happiest Canadian on Wednesday was Justin Trudeau.
Some conservatives will be happy, of course. There's always been some Trump fans on Canada's right.
But the happiest guy of all? It is the leader of the Liberal Party of Canada. Here's why: For months now, Trudeau has been very, very unpopular. The gap has been as much as 20 points, for more than a year.
As such, he has thrown everything at the wall to see what would stick. Abortion, hidden agenda, foreign interference - you name it. But none of it has worked.
Until now. Until Donald Trump came "roaring back," as The New York Times put it, with a big, big win in the electoral college. Trump becomes the first Republican presidential candidate to win the popular vote in 20 years.
But that's American politics, which the commentariat will be endlessly debating for the next two years, until the 2026 midterms. Or, at least until JD Vance figures out a way to drive an aging Trump out using the 25th amendment.
This writer helped win a few major majority governments up here in Canada. Along the way, I learned that Canadian voters have a very different set of priorities. And, with Wednesday's announcement of Trump's win, I guarantee you - absolutely guarantee you - that many, many of them are fullon freaking out.
Not Justin Trudeau. Trudeau has been thrown a lifeline by millions of American voters who grabbed the steering wheel and yanked it to the right. At some point, the prime minister will come out looking sombre and serious. He will stand before a gaggle of microphones.
He will say three things.
One, he will say that he has reached out to Donald Trump to offer his congratulations (I doubt he got through). Two, he will say that his government will continue to put the priorities of Canadians first, and continue to work closely with our most important ally and trading partner.
And then, third - in response to a question from somebody at CBC or the Toronto Star - he will say that it is now more important than ever before that Canada has a progressive team to protect Canada's interests. He will say that part with the appropriate level of drama and passion. He will say "progressive" 100 times, if he can.
And you know what? Many Canadians - who to this point have deeply disliked Trudeau - will agree with that. And, soon enough, the polls will reflect that.
Will it be enough to bridge a 20-point gap? Not right away.
But Trump's MAGA party now controls the U.S. Senate and soon will control the House of Representatives. He will have total dominance. In the coming months, Ukraine will slip under the waves, having been abandoned by the United States. Trump will look the other way as the Chinese Communist Party finally makes its move on Taiwan. In the coming months, Europe will turn inward and NATO will be on its way to becoming a Wikipedia entry, and not much else.
It's at that point that Canadians will really and truly start to freak out. And they will start considering who they should be voting for.
I do not believe, not for a moment, that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is indifferent to the fate of Ukraine, Taiwan, NATO or global stability. I think he has grown in his job. I think it would be unfair to call him a Trumper.
But politics isn't fair. And Justin Trudeau is going to be working very hard to give Pierre Poilievre a shiny new MAGA tattoo. It may even work.
Sometime during Tuesday night, everything changed. For the United States, for Europe and even for little old Canada. It's about to get really bouncy.
And, if you look closely enough, you will see Justin Trudeau suppressing a smile.

Subject: Voters

Location: United States--US; Canada; Ukraine; Taiwan; Europe

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: North Atlantic Treaty Organization--NATO; NAICS: 928120

Document 250 of 500

School board approves plan to make deep cuts; Province could take control if $6.4m deficit not eliminated

Author: Macleod, Brian

Publication info: Macleod, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/school-board-approves-plan-make-deep-cuts/docview/3125867731/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=School+board+approves+plan+to+make+deep+cuts%3B+Province+could+take+control+if+%246.4m+deficit+not+eliminated&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Macleod%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Citing fears the province could take over if painful budget cuts weren't made, Windsor-Essex public school board trustees approved a plan Tuesday night to cut almost 63 full-time positions, eliminate a popular special education program and cancel the international baccalaureate program.
The decision by Greater Essex County District School Board trustees to support the Multi-Year Financial Recovery Plan, which passed in a 4-2 vote, came after the provincial government instructed the board to eliminate its $6.4 million deficit by the 2026-27 school year.
Six people who spoke as delegations before the vote urged trustees to keep the RISE (Reaching Individual Success and Excellence) program, and two others spoke in favour of keeping the international baccalaureate program.
But rejecting the cuts in the financial plan risked having the province take over the school board's operations, which some trustees felt would put the board in a worse position.
An attempt by trustee Kim McKinley to save the international baccalaureate program long enough to allow current students enrolled in grades 9 and 10 to finish their diploma failed in a vote after trustees were told by director of education Vicki Houston the money would have to be cut somewhere else.
It could not be determined Tuesday night how much it would take to maintain the program temporarily, though board chair Gale Hatfield said it would be "a big chunk of money."
"If we don't do this now, the ministry will take over and the ministry is not going to be as kind as we are, knowing our community, knowing our board, knowing what we want to provide for our kids," she said.
Several of the delegations appealed to trustees to reject the budget and risk letting the province take over the board. Provincial supervisors did just that at another board for about two years, Hatfield responded.
"All the decisions were made by that single supervisor on behalf of the ministry, so they can make decisions on whatever they want," she said.
Joanna Conrad, the parent of a child with special needs and who is a member of the board's special education advisory committee, urged trustees to reject the financial plan and let the province take over the board and be accountable for cutting much-needed services for children.
"Do you realize what that means?" trustee Connie Buckler asked.
"Yeah, I do," Conrad replied.
"What I think is that the ministry is going to come in and they're probably going to look top down."
"They're not going to look at the most vulnerable students and take away from them. That's my hope anyway," Conrad said.
"I wouldn't bank on that hope," Buckler replied.
"From what I know from the ministry, what they will be looking at are unfunded spending expenses," Hatfield said. "That's the problem at our board."
Trustees blamed the deficit and unfunded positions on inadequate funding from the province.
A large portion of the board's deficit stems from its decision to carry about 800 positions - not directly funded by the province - to maintain special education programs because community programs for kids with special needs either have long wait-lists or are expensive. That support of about 6,000 students costs the local board more than $10 million annually.
Funding for those positions is pulled from elsewhere in the budget.
The financial plan calls for cuts in several areas, including: - 34.5 positions by restructuring supports for RISE students; - 15.5 central office positions, via attrition where possible; - Two non-bargaining employees, via attrition; - One international baccalaureate position at the elementary school level and 2.8 positions at the secondary school level; - Two social worker positions; - Five psychologists and speech language pathologists; - The board would also reduce the budget for occasional staff to cover absences.
RISE provides elementary school half-day instruction in English and math outside the regular classroom for students who are more than two years behind in those areas.
Students in the program will be integrated into regular classrooms, which is the case at other school boards in the province.
Speaking before the vote on the financial plan, McKinley, who sits on the special education advisory committee, said she "cannot ... agree to do the ministry's dirty work for them."
"I most certainly will not gut our services or the programs that support our students while (Premier Doug Ford) makes announcements to send $200 cheques to all Ontario residents before an expected early election announcement," McKinley said.
"There seems to be over $3 billion at his disposal that our education system and health care system could desperately use to both balance budget deficits and increase support to school boards, as well as hospitals."
Last month, Ford announced a $200 rebate to Ontario taxpayers and $200 for each eligible child, estimated to cost about $3 billion. The provincial government said the funds are from an unexpected budget revenue windfall.
Ontario's Ministry of Education has said 31 school boards are in a deficit situation this year.
"Other boards were given longer timelines to comply, but because our board's problems are systemic, we were given much shorter timelines," board chair Hatfield said. The board was given a Nov. 30 deadline to submit its financial plan to the Education Ministry.
Trustees "don't have an option" to comply, Hatfield said. "Trustees have a legal obligation to comply with the ministry."
Delegate Antonella Ciampa, vice-president of the Greater Essex Elementary Teachers'Federation of Ontario, lauded trustees for their "courageous decision" in June to pass a budget with a deficit of almost $6.4 million in an attempt to save special education programs and other resources.
But the financial plan approved Tuesday reverses that decision.
"We are all aware of the mental health crisis afflicting our society, and yet we are sacrificing special education staff at a time when they are needed," Ciampa said.
She urged trustees to "defy both the Ministry of Education and the provincial government."
Ilana Mizel, who has a child supported by the RISE program, told trustees the program has been "a beacon of hope for many families, including my own."
Approving the financial recovery plan would mean "we're sending a message to our community that our school board will turn its backs on some of the most vulnerable members," Mizel said. "We are essentially telling these children and their families that they are not worth the investment."
Jacqueline Andresch, whose child is also in the RISE program, said "special education services are not a luxury or an extra line item to be adjusted at will."
"Imagine being a child who struggles with reading or communicating, and knowing that there's less support coming your way. Imagine being a parent who knows their child's potential but sees that potential hindered by a lack of resources."
Francine Salinitri, who has a child in the international baccalaureate (IB) program at Riverside Secondary School, said many students transferred to the school for that program.
Parents were not told of the program's potential cancellation and had to discover it for themselves in the agenda posted in advance of this week's board meeting, she said.
"This deficit is not the IB students'burden to bear," she said in urging trustees to allow Grade 9 and 10 students to graduate from the IB program.
"Cuts to mental health and special education services are not sustainable and will not resolve the complex budget issues before you," Shelley Deane, local president of the Professional Student Services Personnel bargaining unit of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers'Association, told trustees. Her unit stands to lose seven positions.
Shannon Hazel, who retired from the board in 2023 after spending her last six years as a special education and learning support teacher, said the board was "always on the cutting edge, forward-thinking ahead of other school districts."
Hazel said it was disheartening to tell parents what their child needs then not provide the resources.
"We give them the list of supports and then we have to follow that up with things like, 'There's a two-year wait-list,'or 'We don't have a spot,'or 'They've aged out of that service.'" Trustees voting in favour of the financial plan were: Julia Burgess, Nancy Armstrong, Cathy Cooke and Buckler. McKinley and Christie Nelson opposed. bamacleod@postmedia.com

Subject: Families & family life; Special education; Students; Mental health; Secondary schools; Trustees; Teachers; Budget deficits; School boards; School districts

Business indexing term: Subject: Trustees Budget deficits

Identifier / keyword: citing; fears; province; painful; budget; public

Title: School board approves plan to make deep cuts; Province could take control if $6.4m deficit not eliminated

Publication title: The Windsor Star; Windsor, Ont.

First page: A.1

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 7, 2024

Document 251 of 500

Guest column: Trump's win will bring boost to floundering Justin Trudeau

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-trumps-win-will-bring-boost/docview/3125389296/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+Trump%27s+win+will+bring+boost+to+floundering+Justin+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Warren Kinsella
The happiest Canadian on Wednesday was Justin Trudeau.
Some conservatives will be happy, of course. There's always been some Trump fans on Canada's right.
But the happiest guy of all? It is the leader of the Liberal Party of Canada. Here's why:
For months now, Trudeau has been very, very unpopular. The gap has been as much as 20 points, for more than a year.
As such, he has thrown everything at the wall to see what would stick. Abortion, hidden agenda, foreign interference, you name it. But none of it has worked.
Until now. Until Donald Trump came "roaring back," as the New York Times put it, with a big, big win in the electoral college. Trump becomes the first Republican presidential candidate to win the popular vote in 20 years.
But that's American politics, which the commentariat will be endlessly debating for the next two years, until the 2026 midterms. Or, at least until JD Vance figures out a way to drive an aging Trump out using the 25th amendment.
This writer helped win a few major majority governments up here in Canada. Along the way, I learned that Canadian voters have a very different set of priorities. And, with Wednesday's announcement of Trump's win, I guarantee you - absolutely guarantee you - that many, many of them are full-on freaking out.
Not Justin Trudeau.
Trudeau has been thrown a lifeline by millions of American voters who grabbed the steering wheel and yanked it to the right. At some point, the prime minister will come out looking somber and serious. He will stand before a gaggle of microphones.
He will say three things. One, he will say that he has reached out to Donald Trump to offer his congratulations (I doubt he got through). Two, he will say that his government will continue to put the priorities of Canadians first, and continue to work closely with our most important ally and trading partner.
And then, third - in response to a question from somebody at CBC or the Toronto Star - he will say that it is now more important than ever before that Canada has a progressive team to protect Canada's interests. He will say that part with the appropriate level of drama and passion. He will say "progressive" 100 times, if he can.
And you know what? Many Canadians - who to this point have deeply disliked Trudeau - will agree with that. And, soon enough, the polls will reflect that.
Will it be enough to bridge a 20-point gap? Not right away.
Related
CHARLEBOIS: What a Trump 2.0 presidency could mean for Canada's agri-food sector
LILLEY: American voters pick Trump to fix the economy
Donald Trump elected 47th president in defeat of Kamala Harris
But Trump's MAGA party now controls the U.S. Senate and soon will control the House of Representatives. He will have total dominance. In the coming months, Ukraine will slip under the waves, having been abandoned by the United States. Trump will look the other way as the Chinese Communist Party finally makes its move on Taiwan. In the coming months, Europe will turn inward and NATO will be on its way to becoming a Wikipedia entry, and not much else.
It's at that point that Canadians will really and truly start to freak out. And they will start considering who they should be voting for.
I do not believe, not for a moment, that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is indifferent to the fate of Ukraine, Taiwan, NATO or global stability. I think he has grown in his job. I think it would be unfair to call him a Trumper.
But politics isn't fair. And Justin Trudeau is going to be working very hard to give Pierre Poilievre a shiny new MAGA tattoo. It may even work.
Sometime during Tuesday night everything changed. For the United States, for Europe, and even for little old Canada. It's about to get really bouncy.
And, if you look closely enough, you will see Justin Trudeau suppressing a smile.

Subject: Presidential elections; Voters

Location: United States--US; Canada; Ukraine; Taiwan; Europe

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: North Atlantic Treaty Organization--NATO; NAICS: 928120

Identifier / keyword: warren; kinsella; happiest; canadian; justin; trudeau

Title: Guest column: Trump's win will bring boost to floundering Justin Trudeau

Publication title: The Windsor Star (Online); Windsor

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Nov 6, 2024

Section: Columnists

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Windsor

Country of publication: Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3125389296

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websi tes/guest-column-trumps-win-will-bring-boost/docview/3125389296/se-2?accountid=15090

UPDATED: Windsor-Essex school board approves plan to make deep cuts

Ilana Mizel, a parent of a child in the RISE program, speaks to trustees of the Greater Essex County District School Board during a meeting on cuts to the budget Tuesday, November 5, 2024. (Brian MacLeod/Windsor Star)
RISE provides elementary school half-day instruction in English and math outside the regular classroom for students who are more than two years behind in those areas.
Students in the program will be integrated into regular classrooms, which is the case at other school boards in the province.
Speaking before the vote on the financial plan, McKinley, who sits on the special education advisory committee, said she "cannot ... agree to do the ministry's dirty work for them."
"I most certainly will not gut our services or the programs that support our students while (Premier Doug Ford) makes announcements to send $200 cheques to all Ontario residents before an expected early election announcement," said McKinley.
Antonella Ciampa, vice-president of the Greater Essex Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario, speaks during a meeting on cuts to the budget Tuesday, November 5, 2024 while the public gallery looks on.
"There seems to be over $3 billion at his disposal that our education system and health care system could desperately use to both balance budget deficits and increase support to school boards, as well as hospitals."
In October Ford announced a $200 rebate to Ontario taxpayers and $200 for each eligible child, estimated to cost about $3 billion. The provincial government said the funds are from an unexpected budget revenue windfall.
Ontario's Ministry of Education has said 31 school boards are in a deficit situation this year.
"Other boards were given longer timelines to comply, but because our board's problems are systemic, we were given much shorter timelines," board chair Hatfield said. The board was given a Nov. 30 deadline to submit its financial plan to the education ministry.
Trustees "don't have an option" to comply, Hatfield said. "Trustees have a legal obligation to comply with the ministry."
Delegate Antonella Ciampa, vice-president of the Greater Essex Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario, lauded trustees for their "courageous decision" in June to pass a budget with a deficit of almost $6.4 million in an attempt to save special education programs and other resources.
But the financial plan approved Tuesday night reverses that decision.
"We are all aware of the mental health crisis afflicting our society, and yet we are sacrificing special education staff at a time when they are needed," Ciampi said.
She urged trustees to "defy both the ministry of education and the provincial government."
Ilana Mizel, who has a child supported by the RISE program, told trustees the program has been "a beacon of hope for many families, including my own."
Approving the financial recovery plan would mean "we're sending a message to our community that our school board will turn its backs on some of the most vulnerable members," Mizel said. "We are essentially telling these children and their families that they are not worth the investment."
Jacqueline Andresch, whose child is also in the RISE program, said "special education services are not a luxury or an extra line item to be adjusted at will."
"Imagine being a child who struggles with reading or communicating and knowing that there's less support coming your way. Imagine being a parent who knows their child's potential but sees that potential hindered by a lack of resources."
Francine Salinitri, who has a child in the international baccalaureate (IB) program at Riverside Secondary School, said many students transferred to the school for that program. Many of them will now have to transfer back to their community schools, she said.
Parents were not told of the program's potential cancellation and had to discover it for themselves in the agenda posted in advance of this week's board meeting, she said.
"This deficit is not the IB students' burden to bear," she said in urging trustees to allow Grade 9 and 10 students to graduate from the IB program.
"Cuts to mental health and special education services are not sustainable and will not resolve the complex budget issues before you," Shelley Deane, local president of the Professional Student Services Personnel bargaining unit of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Association, told trustees. Her unit stands to lose seven positions.
Shannon Hazel, who retired from the board in 2023 after spending her last six years as a special education and learning support teacher, said the board was "always on the cutting edge, forward-thinking ahead of other school districts."
Hazel said it was disheartening to tell parents what their child needs then not provide the resources. "We give them the list of supports and then we have to follow that up with things like, 'There's a two-year wait list,' or 'We don't have a spot,' or 'They've aged out of that service.'"
Trustees voting in favour of the financial plan were: Julia Burgess, Nancy Armstrong, Connie Buckler and Cathy Cooke. Kim McKinley and Christie Nelson opposed.
bamacleod@postmedia.com

Subject: Families & family life; Special education; Students; Children & youth; Mental health; Secondary schools; Trustees; Teachers; Budget deficits; School boards; School districts

Business indexing term: Subject: Trustees Budget deficits

Document 254 of 500

Stellantis revenues plunge as global vehicle shipments slide 20%

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stellantis-revenues-plunge-as-global-vehicle/docview/3123452265/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stellantis+revenues+plunge+as+global+vehicle+shipments+slide+20%25&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stellantis reported its third-quarter net revenues plunged 27 per cent to $49.9 billion compared to the same quarter in 2023 as it shipped 20 per cent fewer vehicles worldwide.
The automaker, which delivered 1.1 million vehicles in the quarter, is struggling with slow sales, bloated inventories, a push on incentives and gaps in its product mix.
New chief financial officer Doug Osterman confirmed Thursday in a webcast the company would hit its revised financial forecast of an operating income margin of 5.5 to seven per cent. Stellantis had posted double-digit operating income margins in each of the past two years en route to posting a record $27-billion profit in 2023.
"While Q3 2024 performance is below our potential, I'm pleased with our progress addressing operational issues," Osterman said.
"In Europe, stringent quality requirements delayed the start of certain high-volume products, but with progress resolving challenges we will soon benefit from the significantly expanded reach our generational new product wave brings to 2025 and beyond."
Much of the company's focus in 2024 has turned to bolstering its performance in North America, which traditionally generates the company's largest profits but has suffered the biggest decline this year.
Shipments in North America were down 36 per cent in the quarter while net revenues fell by 42 per cent or $13.7 billion to $18.8 billion. There were 299,000 vehicle shipments in the third quarter, down 171,000 compared to the same quarter in 2023.
Ostermann said the company expects October sales for North America to be 10 per cent higher compared to September's numbers.
Stellantis reduced its U.S. inventory by 51,000 in the third quarter, to about 380,000 vehicles, and expects to have reduced that by another 30,000 units in October. Osterman said he expects the company will have a U.S. inventory of 330,000 by the end of November.
North America wasn't the only trouble spot for Stellantis in the quarter as revenues were down across the board.
European revenue slipped 12 per cent, Asia declined by 40 per cent, South American decreased two per cent and the Middle East and Africa plummeted 37 per cent.
Stellantis said production and profits in North America were down in comparison due to an extra 50,000 units of production in 2023 of nameplates on hiatus and lower sales volume and an enlarged incentive costs.
"In particular U.S. inventories, which have been reduced meaningfully and are on track for year-end targets, as well as stabilization of U.S. market share," Osterman said.
Stellantis reduced its U.S. inventory by 80,000 vehicles in the quarter and expects to reach a reduction of 100,000 by the end of November.
The company's worldwide inventory as of Sept. 30 was 1.33 million vehicles, which is down 129,000 units versus 12 months ago.
With the company expecting to dip into its cash holdings for $15.1 billion this year, its cost cutting measures have created turbulence with suppliers and the United Auto Workers.
The company recently filed a lawsuit against the German supplier Brose in a dispute over price increases for parts and components. The suit is the fifth one filed this year by Stellantis against a supplier for similar reasons.
The United Auto Workers are also currently holding strike authorization votes because of what the union claims is Stellantis not living up to a commitment to reopen a plant in Illinois and plans to move production out of the U.S.
Stellantis also recently announced 1,100 layoffs at its Warren, Mich., truck plant and temporarily paused production this week at its Detroit Assembly Complex and Toledo Jeep plants.
Ford Motor Company announced its third-quarter results earlier this week reporting a 25 per cent drop in net income despite increased revenues of 5.5 per cent to $63.9 billion. The company's bottom line absorbed a $1.39-billion hit for a charge for discontinuing a three-row electric SUV program.
Ford also announced this week it would cease building the electric Ford Lightning pickup truck for the remainder of the year.
The news was more positive for General Motors last week, with revenues rising 10.5 per cent to $68.1 billion.
The better-than-expected results prompted the automaker to up its annual forecast for adjusted earnings to between $19.4 billion and $20.8 billion. Dwaddell@postmedia.com Twitter.com/winstarwaddell

Document 255 of 500

Slogans are OK in politics, but where's the policy beef?

Author: Brown-John, Lloyd

Publication info: Brown-John, Lloyd. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/slogans-are-ok-politics-wheres-policy-beef/docview/3123449643/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Slogans+are+OK+in+politics%2C+but+where%27s+the+policy+beef%3F&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Brown-John%2C+Lloyd&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the 1958 federal election, John Diefenbaker won the largest parliamentary majority in Canadian history. He defeated Nobel Peace Prize winner Lester Pearson.
Dief tapped into populist discontent and rode to victory on a simple slogan - Follow John.
Slogans are the bread and margarine of political parties and their promoters. The word slogan may have originated in Gaelic slaughghairm - a battle cry. Former New York Times columnist William Safire defined slogan as "a catchphrase ... to thrill, exhort and inspire ... (with) the intent of sparking emotions and open the door to persuasion."
Pierre Poilievre has been hammering away with his Axe the Tax slogan for painfully long. The Conservative leader seems to prefer slogans, including having picked up former Ontario premier Mike Harris's "common sense" theme.
The problem with preaching populist slogans on the path into political office is straightforward.
He seems more adept at playing simple critic rather than constructive critic.
Somewhat like hockey. When you are plunked on your butt watching a hockey game, it is easy to criticize on-ice player failure. After all, armchair wannabes often presume themselves better players than those on ice.
And so it is in politics, as well. Standing in opposition and tossing allegations and slogans is so much easier than actually occupying and managing a government. Diefenbaker was a remarkable critic of the Liberals and Pearson.
Dief's ability to appear and behave as an outraged citizen was classic theatre. Yet when he was elected prime minister, his performance rhetoric ultimately collapsed.
Of necessity, an aspiring prime minister must understand that theatrical slogans may work in Opposition but once in office responsibility demands dramatic change.
Some may recall a fast food outlet's commercial of years ago when an elderly lady screamed, "Where's the beef?" Undogmatic voters may wish to see what plan for Canada's future would be laid out by Poilievre.
Political parties need a much more substantive outline of what they propose to do if elected. Some term that need a project. What is your post-victory project? So far, Poilievre has been rather indifferent about how his axing the carbon tax would be replaced by any viable policy alternative which might achieve the same or better results in respect to reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
Poilievre often sounds like a pale echo of a blustery American presidential buffoon, but more subdued and perhaps much less self-infatuated.
While his approach to communication may serve him well in Opposition, it is a perilous strategy for governing.
A political party and leader salivating for power and the mantle of prime minister needs to reach beyond frivolous slogans into policy substance. As Ontarians discovered with Mike Harris's common sense revolution, you really must be cautious about buying a political leader's half-formed slogan policy promises.
Like his mentor Stephen Harper, if Poilievre is determined to shift Canada more to the political right, he may need to understand that Canadians - concerned about housing, medical care and climate - may still prefer greater government intervention than hard-right Conservatives might otherwise wish.
This is not America, and the political culture which infuses Canada is much less aligned to an inherent libertarianism so often witnessed in American politics. Much of this country's history has involved government intervention to build railways, highways and social welfare structures that are often anathema to fundamentalists and far-right politicians such as Harper and now, it would appear, Poilievre.
There are serious public policy issues in this country, ranging from immigration to climate change. Poilievre needs to offer Canadians solid public policy alternatives and not slogans or cutesy TV ads of him and his wife.
Axe the Tax is a vacuous slogan. So, Mr. Poilievre, what do you have as a tangible alternative to deal with climate change? Or housing? Or immigration? Health care? Offering us dry burger buns is one thing - but where's the beef? Lloyd Brown-John is a University of Windsor professor emeritus of political science and director of Canterbury ElderCollege. He can be reached at lbj@uwindsor.ca.

Subject: Populism; Prime ministers; Climate change; Immigration; Public policy; Political parties

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: federal; election; diefenbaker; largest; parliamentary; majority

Document 256 of 500

Brown-John: Slogans are OK in politics, but where's the policy beef?

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/brown-john-slogans-are-ok-politics-wheres-policy/docview/3123373335/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Brown-John%3A+Slogans+are+OK+in+politics%2C+but+where%27s+the+policy+beef%3F&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Lloyd Brown-John
In the 1958 federal election, John Diefenbaker won the largest parliamentary majority in Canadian history. He defeated Nobel Peace Prize winner Lester Pearson.
'Dief' tapped into populist discontent and rode to victory on a simple slogan - 'Follow John.'
Slogans are the bread and margarine of political parties and their promoters.
The word 'slogan' may have originated in Gaelic 'slaugh-ghairm' - a battle cry.
Former New York Times columnist William Safire defined slogan as "a catch phrase ... to thrill, exhort and inspire ... (with) the intent of sparking emotions and open the door to persuasion."
Pierre Poilievre has been hammering away with his "Axe the Tax" slogan for painfully long. The Conservative leader seems to prefer slogans, including having picked up former Ontario premier Mike Harris's 'Common Sense' theme.
The problem with preaching populist slogans on the path into political office is straightforward. He seems more adept at playing simple critic rather than constructive critic.
Somewhat like hockey. When you are plunked on your butt, munching peanuts with your beer watching a hockey game, it is easy to criticize on-ice player failure. After all, armchair wannabes often presume themselves better players than those on ice.
And so it is in politics as well. Standing in opposition and tossing allegations and slogans is so much easier than actually occupying and managing a government. Returning to Diefenbaker, he was a remarkable critic of the Liberals and Pearson.
Dief's ability to appear and behave as an outraged citizen was classic theatre. Yet, when he was elected prime minister his performance rhetoric ultimately collapsed.
Of necessity, an aspiring prime minister must understand that theatrical slogans may work in opposition but once in office responsibility demands dramatic change.
Some may recall a fast food outlet's commercial of years ago when an elderly lady screamed, "Where's the beef?" Undogmatic voters may wish to see what plan for Canada's future would be laid out by Poilievre.
Political parties need a much more substantive outline of what they propose to do if elected. Some term that need a project. What is your post-victory project? So far, Poilievre has been rather indifferent about how his axing the carbon tax would be replaced by any viable policy alternative which might achieve the same or better results in respect to reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
Poilievre often sounds like a pale echo of a blustery American presidential buffoon, but more subdued and perhaps much less self-infatuated. While his approach to communication with the public may serve him well in opposition, however, it is a perilous strategy for governing.
A political party and leader salivating for power and the mantle of prime minister needs to reach beyond frivolous slogans into policy substance. As Ontarians discovered with Mike Harris's Common Sense revolution, you really must be cautious about buying a political leader's half-formed slogan policy promises.
Like his mentor Stephen Harper, if Poilievre is determined to shift Canada more to the political right he may need to understand that Canadians - concerned about housing, medical care and climate - may still prefer greater government intervention than hard-right Conservatives might otherwise wish.
Related
Brown-John: Who, or what, is the 'middle class'?
Brown-John: Big contrast in choosing U.S. presidents or Canadian prime ministers
Brown-John: News media don't exist to please politicians
This is not America, and the political culture which infuses Canada is much less aligned to an inherent libertarianism so often witnessed in American politics. Much of this country's history has involved government intervention to build railways, highways and social welfare structures that are often anathema to fundamentalists and far-right politicians such as Harper and now, it would appear, Poilievre.
There are serious public policy issues in this country, ranging from immigration to housing to health care and climate change. Poilievre needs to offer Canadians solid public policy alternatives and not slogans or cutesy TV ads of him and his wife wandering through glistening fields of joy.
Axe the Tax is a vacuous slogan. So, Mr. Poilievre, what do you have as a tangible alternative to deal with climate change? Or housing? Or immigration? Health care?
Offering us dry burger buns is one thing - but where's the beef?
Lloyd Brown-John is a University of Windsor professor emeritus of political science and director of Canterbury ElderCollege. He can be reached at lbj@uwindsor.ca( mailto:lbj@uwindsor.ca ).

Stellantis revenues plunge 27 per cent in third quarter

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/stellantis-revenues-plunge-27-per-cent-third/docview/3123358935/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stellantis+revenues+plunge+27+per+cent+in+third+quarter&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stellantis reported its third-quarter net revenues plunged 27 per cent to $49.9 billion (33 billion euros) compared to the same quarter in 2023 as it shipped 20 per cent fewer vehicles worldwide.
The automaker, which delivered 1.1 million vehicles in the quarter, is struggling with slow sales, bloated inventories, a push on incentives and gaps in its product mix.
New chief financial officer Doug Osterman confirmed Thursday in a webcast the company would hit its revised financial forecast of an operating income margin of 5.5 to seven per cent. Stellantis had posted double-digit operating income margins in each of the past two years en route to posting a record $27-billion profit in 2023.
"While Q3 2024 performance is below our potential, I'm pleased with our progress addressing operational issues," Osterman said.
"In Europe, stringent quality requirements delayed the start of certain high-volume products, but with progress resolving challenges we will soon benefit from the significantly expanded reach our generational new product wave brings to 2025 and beyond."
Much of the company's focus in 2024 has turned to bolstering its performance in North America, which traditionally generates the company's largest profits but has suffered the biggest decline this year.
Shipments in North America were down 36 per cent in the quarter while net revenues fell by 42 per cent or $13.7 billion to $18.8 billion. There were 299,000 vehicle shipments in the third quarter, down 171,000 compared to the same quarter in 2023.
Ostermann said the company expects October sales for North America to be 10 per cent higher compared to September's numbers.
Stellantis reduced its U.S. inventory by 51,000 in the third quarter, to about 380,000 vehicles, and expects to have reduced that by another 30,000 units in October. Osterman said he expects the company will have a US inventory of 330,000 by the end of November.
North America wasn't the only trouble spot for Stellantis in the quarter as revenues were down across the board.
European revenue slipped 12 per cent, Asia declined by 40 per cent, South American decreased two per cent and the Middle East and Africa plummeted 37 per cent.
Stellantis said production and profits in North America were down in comparison due to an extra 50,000 units of production in 2023 of nameplates on hiatus and lower sales volume and an enlarged incentive costs.
"In particular U.S. inventories, which have been reduced meaningfully and are on track for year-end targets, as well as stabilization of U.S. market share," Osterman said.
Stellantis reduced its U.S. inventory by 80,000 vehicles in the quarter and expects to reach a reduction of 100,000 by the end of November.
The company's worldwide inventory as of Sept. 30 was 1.33 million vehicles, which is down 129,000 units versus 12 months ago.
With the company expecting to dip into its cash holdings for $15.1 billion this year, its cost cutting measures have created turbulence with suppliers and the United Auto Workers.
The company recently filed a lawsuit against the German supplier Brose in a dispute over price increases for parts and components. The suit is the fifth one filed this year by Stellantis against a supplier for similar reasons.
The United Auto Workers are also currently holding strike authorization votes because of what the union claims is Stellantis not living up to a commitment to re-open a plant in Illinois and plans to move production out of the U.S.
Stellantis also recently announced 1,100 layoffs at its Warren, Mich., truck plant and temporarily paused production this week at its Detroit Assembly Complex and Toledo Jeep plants.
Ford Motor Company announced its third-quarter results earlier this week reporting a 25 per cent drop in net income despite increased revenues of 5.5 per cent to $63.9 billion. The company's bottom line absorbed a $1.39-billion hit for a charge for discontinuing a three-row electric SUV program.
Ford also announced this week it would cease building the electric Ford Lightning pickup truck for the remainder of the year.
The news was more positive for General Motors last week, with revenues rising 10.5 per cent to $68.1 billion. The better-than-expected results, prompted the automaker to up its annual forecast for adjusted earnings to between $19.4 billion and $20.8 billion.
Dwaddell@postmedia.com

Document 258 of 500

Ford hopes dougie dollars will keep taxpayers happy; Premier says inflation gave province sales-tax windfall, Brian Lilley writes

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-hopes-dougie-dollars-will-keep-taxpayers/docview/3123169792/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+hopes+dougie+dollars+will+keep+taxpayers+happy%3B+Premier+says+inflation+gave+province+sales-tax+windfall%2C+Brian+Lilley+writes&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Why is Doug Ford promising to send $200 to every Ontarian? To put it bluntly, because he can.
Ford made official what had been reported weeks ago that the fall economic statement would include a tax rebate - Dougie Dollars if you will.
"We're able to provide the relief because inflation has increased provincial sales tax revenues and because of the federal government's recent changes to their capital gains tax," Ford said.
To give you an example of how inflation has increased the government's sales tax revenues, in fiscal year 2019-20, the first full year of the Ford government, sales tax revenue stood at $28.6 billion. In 2021-22, before inflation took off, that sales tax revenue had grown to $30.4 billion, but by the end of March the government was taking in $39.9 billion. That's an increase of 39.5 per cent in four years.
Part of this is driven by inflation, part of this is also driven by Ontario's incredible population growth. Ford said rather than hang onto the extra cash, he wants to give it back to taxpayers.
Leaders of all three opposition parties at Queen's Park said that rather than give the money back to taxpayers, Ford should be investing in health care or education. Ford said that his government can do both.
"It's not either/or keep costs down or invest in social programs; it's and. We can and are doing both," he said.
Since Ford took office, health spending has increased 34.2 per cent from $63.7 billion in 2019-20 to $85.5 billion in the last fiscal year and you can bet there will be increases announced. Basically, the Ford government has more money than it can spend and rather than invent new programs, it's sending money back to you.
A family of two parents and two kids will get $800 in Dougie Dollars by the end of January, just as Christmas credit card bills are coming due. You can call this crass electioneering, you can claim Ford is bribing taxpayers with their own money, but people like getting money.
"I think a lot of people are struggling right now, but a one-time payment right before an election isn't going to solve the affordability crisis for Ontarians," said NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
The leader of the official Opposition said that while few people would say no to getting an extra $200, it won't help them pay rent, find a family doctor or deal with tent cities.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said these payments are all about the next election.
"The premier is attempting to bribe Ontarians with their own money. This is taxpayers' money. What Liberals would do was we would create a middle-income tax cut," Crombie said.
So why isn't the Ford government doing an acrossthe-board income tax cut like they promised instead of this one-off payment? The answer appears to be twofold.
"Tax cuts don't sell," said one senior Tory when asked why they aren't just cutting taxes.
There are plenty of past governments that could attest to that idea: You don't get any political benefit from a tax cut in the second year.
The second reason is more practical. We are in an uncertain economic time and while this windfall is here this year, it may not be here next year. If the economy sours and the government has just cut tax rates, that could hurt provincial coffers long term.
Ford is taking the gamble that voters won't mind an extra $200 for every person in their household and that any complaints by the opposition will soon be forgotten once the cheques clear. Brian Lilley is a Toronto Sun columnist.

Subject: Tax rates; Tax revenues; Tax cuts; Sales taxes; Income taxes

Ontario plots $191B, 10-year infrastructure spending plan

Author: Passifiume, Bryan

Publication info: Passifiume, Bryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-plots-191b-10-year-infrastructure/docview/3123169759/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+plots+%24191B%2C+10-year+infrastructure+spending+plan&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-11-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Passifiume%2C+Bryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: While most of the surprises in this year's fall surprises in this year's fall economic statement were strategically leaked to reporters earlier this week, the Doug Ford Progressive Conservatives put forward a plan Wednesday they say will continue the province's path of strategic investment and make life a little more affordable.
The statement calls for wiping out the province's deficit entirely by 2026-27, projecting a nearly $1 billion surplus within three years, after a $6.6-billion 2024-25 deficit.
The government aims to balance the province's books by the 2026-27 fiscal year, and projects growth in both employment and Ontario's real GDP in the coming year.
Among programs already announced includes the $200 rebate, where cheques will be sent to eligible adult Ontarians sometime early next year. Eligible families will also see an additional $200 per child.
Queen's Park will also provide another extension to the temporary nine cents per litre fuel tax cut, until June 30, 2025 - well past the projected increase of the federal carbon tax set for April 1. That, the province claims, will save households an average of $380 since the cut was introduced in 2022.
Municipalities will also get a boost, with the province increasing spending through the Ontario Municipal Partnership Fund by $100 million over two years.
The province expects to continue funding infrastructure, with $191 billion planned over the next decade to fund roads, highways, public transit, housing, schools, hospitals and long-term care.
Ontario will spend $150 million more over two years to fund fertility treatments, tripling the number of patients able to receive in-vitro fertilization, and shrink wait-lists at fertility clinics in the province.

Guest column: Ford hopes 'Dougie Dollars' will keep taxpayers happy

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-ford-hopes-dougie-dollars-will-keep/docview/3123059723/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+Ford+hopes+%27Dougie+Dollars%27+will+keep+taxpayers+happy&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Brian Lilley
Why is Doug Ford promising to send a $200 payment to every Ontarian? To put it bluntly, because he can.
Ford made official what had been reported weeks ago that the fall economic statement would include a tax rebate - "Dougie Dollars" if you will.
"We're able to provide the relief because inflation has increased provincial sales tax revenues and because of the federal government's recent changes to their capital gains tax," Ford said.
To give you an example of how inflation has increased the government's sales tax revenues, in fiscal year 2019-20, the first full year of the Ford government, sales tax revenue stood at $28.6 billion. In 2021-22, before inflation took off, that sales tax revenue had grown to $30.4 billion, but by the end of March the government was taking in $39.9 billion.
That's an increase of 39.5% in four years just on the sales tax.

Ontario government moves to extend 5.7 cent gas tax cut to June 2025
LILLEY: Ford puts Ontario students first for medical school
Part of this is driven by inflation, part of this is also driven by Ontario's incredible population growth. Ford said rather than hang onto the extra cash, he wants to give it back to taxpayers.
Leaders of all three opposition parties at Queen's Park said that rather than give the money back to taxpayers, Ford should be investing in health care or education. Ford said that his government can do both.
"It's not either/or keep costs down or invest in social programs; it's and. We can and are doing both," Ford said.
Since Ford took office, health spending has increased 34.2% from $63.7 billion in 2019-20 to $85.5 billion in the last fiscal year and you can bet there will be increases announced on Wednesday. Basically, the Ford government has more money than they can spend and rather than invent new programs, they're sending money back to you.
A family of two parents and two kids will get $800 in Dougie Dollars by the end of January, just as Christmas credit card bills are coming due. You can call this crass electioneering, you can claim Ford is bribing taxpayers with their own money, but people like getting money.
"I think a lot of people are struggling right now, but a one-time payment right before an election isn't going to solve the affordability crisis for Ontarians," said NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
The leader of the Official Opposition said that while few people would say no to getting an extra $200, it won't help them pay rent, find a family doctor or deal with the tent cities.
"This is a government that is out of ideas, out of gas and, I think, out of time," Stiles said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said that she will be donating her cheque to the Ontario Liberal Party to gear up for the next election. In fact, Crombie said these payments are all about the next election.
"The premier is attempting to bribe Ontarians with their own money. This is taxpayers' money. What Liberals would do was we would create a middle-income tax cut," Crombie said.
So why isn't the Ford government doing an across-the-board income tax cut like they promised instead of this one-off payment? The answer appears to be two-fold, with one part relating to politics and the other part relating to the economy.
"Tax cuts don't sell," said one senior Tory when asked why they aren't just cutting taxes.
There are plenty of past governments that could attest to that idea: You don't get any political benefit from a tax cut in the second year.
The second reason is more practical.
We are in an uncertain economic time and while this windfall is here this year, it may not be here next year. If the economy sours and the government has just cut tax rates, that could hurt provincial coffers long-term.
Ford is taking the gamble that voters won't mind an extra $200 for every person in their household and that any complaints by the opposition will soon be forgotten once the cheques clear.
Brian Lilley is a Toronto Sun columnist.

Document 261 of 500

Few surprises, but big investments, in Ontario fall economic statement

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/few-surprises-big-investments-ontario-fall/docview/3123012334/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Few+surprises%2C+but+big+investments%2C+in+Ontario+fall+economic+statement&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Bryan Passifiume
TORONTO - While most of the surprises in this year's fall economic statement were strategically leaked to reporters earlier this week, the Doug Ford Conservatives put forward a plan Wednesday they say will continue the province's path of strategic investment and make life a little more affordable.
The statement also hints at plans to wipe the province's deficit entirely by 2026-27, projecting a nearly $1 billion surplus within three years.
Ontario's Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy tabled the province's 2024 Fall Economic Statement in the legislature on Wednesday, saying they're well equipped to weather the coming uncertainty in the months and years ahead.
"Our government's responsible approach has resulted in an improved fiscal position since the 2024 Budget, allowing us to keep taxes low, invest in infrastructure like roads, highways, hospitals and schools, and provide immediate relief to Ontario families as part of our plan to keep costs down," said Bethlenfalvy in a statement released Wednesday.
"We're going to continue investing responsibly to support Ontario's growth and rebuild Ontario's economy to make our province the best place to live, work and raise a family, all while reducing the debt burden for future generations."
The government, Bethlenfalvy said, aims to balance the province's books by the 2026-27 fiscal year, and projects growth in both employment and Ontario's real GDP in the coming year.
Among programs already announced includes the $200 rebate, where cheques will be sent to eligible adult Ontarians sometime early next year.
Eligible families will also see an additional $200 per child.
Queen's Park will also provide another extension to the temporary nine cents per litre fuel tax cut, until June 30, 2025 - well past the projected increase of the federal carbon tax set for April 1.
That, the province claims, will save households an average of $380 since the cut was introduced in 2022.
Municipalities will also get a boost, with the province increasing investments in the Ontario Municipal Partnership Fund (OMPF) by $100 million over two years.
The province expects to continue funding major infrastructure projects, with $191 billion planned over the next decade to fund roads, highways, public transit, housing, schools, hospitals and long-term care.
That funding includes $27.8 billion to build and repair roads, including the Bradford bypass, the contentious Hwy. 413 project, new interchanges in Windsor and Ottawa, widening the eastern stretches of Hwy. 401, and twinning Hwy. 7 through Durham.
Ontario will invest an extra $150 million over two years to fund fertility treatments, tripling the number of patients able to receive in-vitro fertilization, and shrink waitlists at fertility clinics across the province.
The government also plans on spending $88 million over three years, starting in 2026, to fund the Learn and Stay grant program, which covers the cost of education for nursing, paramedicine and medical tech students who intend on staying and working in the region.
That program will expand to include around 1,360 eligible undergrads willing to commit to practice family medicine, and according to the government, will cover tuition, books, supplies and equipment in exchange for opening a family medicine practice in the province.
Related
Ontario government moves to extend 5.7 cent gas tax cut to June 2025
Ford says $200 rebate will be sent to each Ontario taxpayer early next year
Ontario is now projecting a $6.6 billion deficit in fiscal year 2024-25, $1.5 billion in 2025-26, and a $900 million surplus by 2026-27.
The net-to-GDP ratio is expected to reach 37.8 per cent by next fiscal year.

Document 262 of 500

Premier says $200 for each taxpayer unrelated to potential spring election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-says-200-each-taxpayer-unrelated/docview/3122810743/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+says+%24200+for+each+taxpayer+unrelated+to+potential+spring+election&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario taxpayers are set to receive $200 cheques in the mail early next year, a $3-billion plan the premier suggests is not tied to a possible early election - though opposition leaders have their doubts.
Premier Doug Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy made the announcement Tuesday, a day ahead of the province's fall economic statement, which serves as a mini budget.
Everyone who filed 2023 tax returns should expect to receive a $200 cheque, plus an additional $200 per child, Ford said.
"These $200 rebates will make a big difference for families across the province with cheques arriving early in the new year," he said.
"That's money that families can use to pay off their credit card after the holidays, to buy a winter coat for their kids or to cover the cost of gassing up the family car to make up for the cost of the Liberal carbon tax."
The announcement comes as Ford has refused to rule out the possibility of an election sooner than the next fixed date of June 2026 and while he has said he will not call one this year, opposition parties are preparing for a possible spring contest.
The two are not related, Ford suggested, saying his government has been looking for ways to save Ontarians money all along, including on transit fares, licence plate renewal fees and a gas tax cut. He then went on to attack the Liberals.
"They'd increase taxes, take money out of your pockets," he said, concluding his answer by saying the Liberals and NDP are "basically the same party."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said she will donate her $200 cheque to the party as it gears up for a possible early election. She did not mince words on what she called a money-giving "gimmick."
"The premier is attempting to bribe Ontarians with their own money," she said.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles said the cheques do not provide long-lasting help.
"I think a lot of people are struggling right now, but a one-time payment right before an election isn't going to solve the affordability crisis for Ontarians," she said.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said it doesn't make sense to dole out this money without any means testing.
"It is ridiculous that millionaires and billionaires like Galen Weston are getting a $200 cheque when so many Ontarians can't afford a place to live tonight," he said.
Many homeless people will not get the cheques because they haven't filed tax returns, said Lorraine Lam, an outreach worker in downtown Toronto.
"Once again Ford does not consider the needs of some of the most vulnerable," Lam said. "There are people who are unhoused and precariously housed who do not have access to the supports they need to get annual taxes done on time."
Ford defended sending the cheques to millionaires and even billionaires.
"They're taxpayers," he said. "These are tax dollars going back to the taxpayers and we're going to give it back to every single person in Ontario. And if they have a child, they're getting an additional $200 because life's too expensive right now."
Ford and Bethlenfalvy said the province can afford to send out $3 billion worth of cheques now because of higher-than-expected revenues, including due to the impact of inflation on provincial sales tax money in government coffers.
