Document 263 of 500

Reading the voter tea leaves; Elections In N.B.,Saskatchewan show poilievre should keep laser focus on affordability, avoid culture wars

Author: Rahim, Mohamed

Publication info: Rahim, Mohamed. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reading-voter-tea-leaves-elections-n-b/docview/3121783941/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reading+the+voter+tea+leaves%3B+Elections+In+N.B.%2CSaskatchewan+show+poilievre+should+keep+laser+focus+on+affordability%2C+avoid+culture+wars&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Rahim%2C+Mohamed&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conser vative Leader Pierre Poilievre has likely kept a close eye on this month's provincial election races in New Brunswick and Saskatchewan, where hot-button culture war issues like gender identity in schools haven't appeared to move the needle with voters.
But political strategists say it's incumbent fatigue, not disagreements over pronouns and single-sex spaces, that has weighed down conservative campaigns in both provinces.
"It's tough for incumbents right across Canada right now," said Conservative strategist Fred DeLorey.
DeLorey, who ran the last federal Conservative campaign in 2021, said he doesn't think that the decisive loss of the Blaine Higgs-led New Brunswick Progressive Conservatives last Monday will slow Poilievre's growing momentum in Atlantic Canada.
"We're comparing apples and pineapples," said DeLorey.
"Poilievre is hitting it out of the park right now, and his main message is affordability ... There's no cautionary tale for him (from Higgs's loss) other than that he's on the right track and shouldn't leave that track."
Voters in Saskatchewan seemed more interested in change than culture wars, with 55 per cent saying it's time for a new provincial government last week.
They went to the polls on Monday; the results were not in by press time.
The governing Saskatchewan Party has been in power for 17 years, by far the longest tenure of any government in Canada.
Saskatchewan Party Leader Scott Moe spent the campaign's home stretch on defence after stumbling into a messy scandal involving the "outing" of two children of an NDP candidate following a changeroom incident in greater Regina.
He brought up the incident during an impromptu mid-campaign announcement in which he said he'd immediately issue a directive banning "biological boys" from girls'school change rooms if re-elected - a promise that wasn't included in the Saskatchewan Party's platform. Moe says he wasn't aware of the children's identities at the time.
Saskatoon-based pollster Lang Mc-Gilp said it wasn't a good use of Moe's time heading into the closer-than-usual election.
"The top four issues of the election have stayed pretty consistent: health care, affordability, taxes and education," said McGilp.
"You can debate whether (gender identity issues) fit in any of these boxes, but there's a lot of other stuff that's more top of mind with voters."
New Brunswick and Saskatchewan are the only two provinces with formal parental notification and consent policies on the books. Alberta is expected to become the third later this year.
Higgs sparked a national debate last year with his resolute defence of controversial revisions to New Brunswick's Sexual Orientation and Gender Identity policy, popularly known as Policy 713. Higgs said last summer that he was willing to call an early election over the changes, which established the first provincewide parental notification and consent requirements in Canada for student name and pronoun changes in K-12 schools.
Polling at the time showed strong support, in Atlantic Canada and beyond, for parental notification requirements, with Canadians more ambivalent on the question of parental consent.
Before the year was out, Moe would adopt similar guidelines, upping the ante by pre-emptively using the notwithstanding clause to push through his so-called Parents'Bill of Rights.
Poilievre himself trod lightly on the matter, declining to comment directly on Policy 713, but later told an ethnic media outlet that he believed "parental rights (should) come before the government's rights."
Jon Robinson, a progressive strategist based in greater Saint John, N.B., said that the split in the Progressive Conservative caucus over the changes to Policy 713 likely hurt Higgs more than the changes themselves.
"This became a very personal issue for (Higgs)," said Robinson, noting that Higgs stayed highly involved in his local church while premier and was largely aligned with the province's Christian right on the issue.
Robinson said Higgs's unwillingness to compromise on the contentious revisions reinforced already negative perceptions of his "autocratic" leadership style.
"Higgs was cast as someone where it's his way or the highway," said Robinson. "And this stuck with him."
Two members of Higgs's cabinet resigned, and two more were demoted, amid party infighting.
Robinson added that controversy surrounding the nomination of Christian TV host Faytene Grasseschi to run for the Progressive Conservatives in a safe Saint John area riding exacerbated internal divisions heading into the election.
(Grasseschi narrowly lost to ex-Progressive Conservatives MLA John Herron, who ran as a Liberal.)
DeLorey said that a wave of resignations and retirements among incumbent Progressive Conservative MLAs further stacked the deck against Higgs.
"We have much smaller seats in the Maritimes than you do in the rest of the country," said DeLorey, who grew up in neighbouring Nova Scotia. "People get to know their local MLAs quite well.
"So when you have so many incumbents who don't re-offer, that's a huge blow to the campaign from the very start."
DeLorey said it would be unfair to pin the high turnover on Higgs's leadership style, noting that the veteran premier was going for an almost unheard of third term in a province with a long history of political swings.
"It's a major achievement to win two terms in New Brunswick, let alone three."
He added Liberal leader and premier-designate Susan Holt's lack of a personal connection to Justin Trudeau gave her a boost by allowing her to credibly distance herself from the toxic Trudeau brand, something her counterpart Zach Churchill will have a harder time doing when Nova Scotians head to the polls next month.
National Post rmohamed@postmedia.com

Subject: Parental rights; Provincial elections; Conservatism; Leadership; Election results; Negative campaigning; Incumbency; Voters; Gender identity; Consent

Dowie acclaimed Windsor- Tecumseh PC provincial candidate

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dowie-acclaimed-windsor-tecumseh-pc-provincial/docview/3121783048/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dowie+acclaimed+Windsor-+Tecumseh+PC+provincial+candidate&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After being acclaimed for the second time, MPP Andrew Dowie will once again campaign to represent Windsor-Tecumseh in the next provincial election.
Dowie secured the nomination unopposed during the Windsor-Tecumseh Progressive Conservative Association meeting held at the Riverside Public Library on Sunday, which coincided with the 10th anniversary of his service in elected office.
"I feel very honoured to have been acclaimed," Dowie told the Star.
"It shows support for the work that I've been doing, so to have that mandate from the members of the party has been tremendous."
Among those attending the local association meeting to celebrate Dowie's nomination were Agriculture, Food and Agribusiness Minister Robert Flack, MPP Anthony Leardi(PC - Essex), Tecumseh Mayor Gary McNamara and Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens.
For Dowie, the event was an opportunity to look back on his accomplishments over the past decade.
Dowie was first elected as the Ward 1 councillor for the Town of Tecumseh exactly 10 years ago on Oct. 27, 2014. Eight years later, on June 2, 2022, he became the MPP for the Windsor-Tecumseh riding.
Dowie said his top accomplishment has been helping to fast-track progress on the new regional acute care hospital.
"The new regional acute care hospital was originally scheduled for 2027," he said.
"Through working jointly with MPP Leardi, we were able to entice the government to speed it up. Shovels are expected in the ground at the very latest, by 2026. Ideally, we'll see something sooner. That is the thing I'm truly most proud of, that we got action to accelerate the hospital project."
As Dowie campaigns for re-election, he said he plans to continue ensuring the voices of Windsor and Tecumseh residents are represented at the provincial level.
"That is actually the beauty of being our representative, I ensure that Windsor and Tecumseh are part of the dialogue and the discussion every step away," he said.
"Our views are considered and our perspectives are considered in the drafting of government legislation and its impact on the community." mmazak@postmedia.com

Subject: Nominations

Document 266 of 500

Guest column: Ford government's 'car-first' agenda kicks cyclists to the curb

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-ford-governments-car-first-agenda/docview/3121183154/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+Ford+government%27s+%27car-first%27+agenda+kicks+cyclists+to+the+curb&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Lori Newton
This past week saw troubling news coming out of Premier Doug Ford's office and from his Progressive Conservative government.
Blaming Toronto's traffic congestion on bike lanes, the car-loving premier says three of that city's downtown bike lanes are coming out. His proposed Bill-212, Reducing Gridlock, Saving You Time Act, would amend the Highway Traffic Act to require that Ontario municipalities first get approval from the Ministry of Transportation before building any bike lanes that would "reduce the number of marked lanes available for travel by motor vehicle traffic."
While congestion in Toronto and other large cities is a serious issue, targeting bike lanes as a strategy to relieve traffic is perpetuating a myth and a lie.
Cities that install bike lanes - including Toronto, Montreal, Edmonton, Calgary, Hamilton, Waterloo, Ottawa - see dramatic increases in cycling and an equally dramatic reduction in injuries and deaths of all road users.
We must not allow the province to take us backwards to a time when streets were blatantly hostile to pedestrians and bike riders, a time when cars unquestionably ruled the road. If you're looking for evidence-based solutions, it's clear: you can't widen your way out of automobile congestion, and every bike on the road is one less car contributing to traffic congestion and greenhouse gas.
Minister of Transportation Prabmeet Sarkaria is on record admitting that he drives his single-occupancy car, like so many others, from his home in Brampton to his job in Toronto at Queen's Park. No GO Train for this guy.
Doesn't that make him part of the problem? He is traffic. He is congestion. He is why roads in the GTA are filled with cars.
Electric and self-driving cars aren't going to reduce traffic. Widening highways isn't going to reduce traffic. Allowing people to drive faster isn't going to reduce traffic. And removing a few kilometres of bike lanes certainly won't reduce traffic.
We're in Windsor-Essex so why should we care?
This move is a massive overreach into municipal decision-making about how to build roads in their communities. It sends a false message that pesky bike lanes are the root cause of chaos (congestion) on our streets, when in fact the opposite is true.
Toronto is experiencing unreal levels of traffic congestion, but taking out a few kilometres of bike lane is not going to fix it. Blame it on the province's failure to deliver good public transit and safe active-transportation options.
Since Windsor has rarely removed a car lane to accommodate active transportation facilities, has not installed a single protected bike lane, and makes decisions that prioritize car drivers, this city will likely not be impacted by this legislation.
However, the propagandistic nature of this false information will reverberate across the province and make it harder for advocates pushing for more safer streets.
The evidence-based reality:
. Bike lanes are far more efficient at moving people quickly than urban lanes moving cars;
. Bike lanes are cheap to install and maintain, especially when you consider the billions spent on car lanes;
. Bike lanes help reduce greenhouse gas emissions from car exhaust;
. Bike lanes increase traffic and sales to local businesses;
. Bike lanes encourage cycling which is great for public and personal health;
. Bike lanes deliver what car ads promise: freedom and mobility.
And, most importantly, bike lanes save lives and improve the safety of ALL road users.
Let's call this attack on bicyclists what it really is: a distraction from a number of very disturbing issues facing the province.
Lori Newton is executive director of Bike Windsor Essex.

Subject: Transportation planning; Automobiles; Greenhouse gases; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways; Bicycling

Identifier / keyword: newton; troubling; coming; premier; office; progressive

Document 267 of 500

'Car-first'agenda kicks cyclists to the curb

Author: Newton, Lori

Publication info: Newton, Lori. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/car-firstagenda-kicks-cyclists-curb/docview/3120935179/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Car-first%27agenda+kicks+cyclists+to+the+curb&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Newton%2C+Lori&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 268 of 500

$1.24M boost for women's skills training program

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/1-24m-boost-womens-skills-training-program/docview/3120201149/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%241.24M+boost+for+women%27s+skills+training+program&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=SR.8&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Women's Enterprise and Skills Training (WEST) of Windsor will receive $1.24 million in funding over three years from the provincial government to aid its program for training and supporting low-income women trying to enter the skilled trades.
The six-year-old program has provided pre-apprenticeship CNC machinist and mechanical millwright training for 400 women to date.
"It's great use of the funding because there are real career opportunities being developed right here at St. Clair College through WEST's great work," said local MPP Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh) during the funding announcementrecently at the college. "There are careers waiting for each of these students.
"The data is showing by partnering with many organizations and non-traditional ways of getting people to the workforce, it's actually paying dividends."
The funding will be spread over three years.
It will help train 20 applicants each year with St. Clair College delivering the educational portion of the program and helping assess the candidates. Unifor also is a partner with WEST.
To apply for the program's next intake or to learn more visit WEST's website at: www.westofwindsor.com.
"This year we have $300,000 to run the program," said WEST executive director Rose Anguiano Hurst.
"A lot of it goes to the training and personal protection equipment and tools needed. Part of it goes to the wage subsidies we pay employers and then part of it goes to staffing."
Hurst said WEST also has funding to help women with emergency housing, healthcare and even transportation needs. The wraparound approach has helped the program achieve an 80 per cent graduation rate over its six years.
"Without this funding it would not be possible (to run the program) and there would be 400 women who would not have had this life-changing opportunity," Hurst said.
For Nora Enaami and Miranda Campbell, who began the 42-week pre-apprenticeship program in September, the unique focus on women is the reason they enrolled.
"For me, men typically have knowledge on mechanics and I didn't have that background," said Enaami, who was laid off from a woodworking job prior to enrolling.
"A women's class, dipping their toes into the water, I thought that would be a good way (to transition to becoming a CNC machinist). They help with the funding, which is also very much a plus.
"WEST is very supportive because women have different needs. Some have children and different responsibilities. WEST has been very kind providing emotional support, mental support and financial support."
Campbell said she wanted to find something more stable than her previous factory job. It also fulfilled her desire to find a career working with her hands, something she acquired when she was young, hanging around her father's automotive garage business.
She also finds the all-female class a more comfortable learning environment.
"I wanted to have some certification that would propel my future and be able to provide for not only myself, but my daughter, being a single mother," Campbell said.
"I was in industry in a steel factory, but I wasn't properly trained, such as with this program.
"This program provides an equal playing field. This program allows an opportunity for a lot of women who don't get that otherwise." Dwaddell@postmedia.com Twitter.com/winstarwaddell

Subject: Skill development; Women; Apprenticeship; Funding

Business indexing term: Subject: Skill development

Document 269 of 500

UWindsor protest camp deals stand; Attempt to get board to pause controversial agreements fails

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/uwindsor-protest-camp-deals-stand-attempt-get/docview/3120200936/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=UWindsor+protest+camp+deals+stand%3B+Attempt+to+get+board+to+pause+controversial+agreements+fails&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's now up to the community - or the province - to push for any changes to deals the University of Windsor struck with pro-Palestinian protesters earlier this year, said a board of governors member who is speaking out against the agreements.
As previously reported by the Windsor Star, Daniel Ableser's request to have his motion to "pause" implementation of the agreements added to Tuesday's agenda was deemed "outside the purview of the board," according to board chair Helga Reidel. That decision was based on an outside legal opinion, she said, but Reidel wouldn't confirm the details or say who provided the legal assessment.
Ableser's challenge of the chair's ruling, and his proposed motion, led to a 90-minute discussion behind closed doors at the board's regular meeting on Tuesday. His motion to pause implementation of the agreements that university administration struck with pro-Palestinian groups in July to end a two-month on-campus protest camp was ruled out of order by fellow board members.
"There has been a decision made on whether my motion was proper," Albeser later told the Star. "I guess I'll digest that and see if there is other direction the board could give.
"Ultimately, I think the board has made its decision. I think at the board table, this is the end."
Ableser's motion sought to direct administration to pause implementation of the terms of the encampment agreements signed July 10 pending further direction from the board of governors. It also called for a report to the next board meeting in November on options and recommendations to address the fallout caused by the agreements.
The agreements included the university's recognition of 'the illegal Israeli settlement'and a promise not to pursue academic agreements with Israeli universities unless supported by its senate. Windsor's university also agreed to consider 'responsible investing'in recognition that Israel is an 'occupying power.'Other board members declined to talk to the Star about the decision. "The motion of the chair is upheld," Reidel said in announcing the result of the in-camera discussion before moving on to the rest of the agenda's business.
Ableser asked University of Windsor president Robert Gordon in the public session whether he would do anything differently if he had the chance to address potential encampment deals again. "It's premature to ask a question of that nature," said Gordon, who presented a report on the university but otherwise did not address the encampment issue.
Albeser, a local lawyer, was less sure of whether this was the end of potential challenges to the university's encampment deals. He said he feels he has completed his duties as a board member and it's now up to the community and others to comment if necessary.
"We're governed by the province and the province can have its say if they don't like what we did," Albeser told the Star. "There may be other community avenues.
"There may be opportunities to look at further supports and other things we can do. I'll listen to the community for that."
The debate over whether university officials followed the University of Windsor Act in making those deals without the board of governors'involvement may not be over.
Ableser presented board members with a contrary legal opinion on the board's role Tuesday.
The Star obtained a copy of that opinion, written by Melvyn L. Solmon, a partner in Toronto law firm Solmon Rothbart Tourgis Slodovnick.
Based on the University of Windsor Act and the fact the deals were a result of the encampments on campus, the matter should've been brought before the board of governors, Solomon opined.
"The President has no jurisdiction over the property of the UWindsor, nor over the investment committee established by the Board," Solomon wrote.
"These powers related to "general administration" cannot be interpreted to limit or interfere with the powers and obligations vested in the Board related to 'the government, conduct, management and control of the University and of its property, revenues, business and affairs.'" Given the importance to the university community and the media attention the deals have generated, Solomon added the issues should have been given a more public airing.
The fallout for the university to this point has included losing financial support from a number of donors, including a recently announced $1 million gift, and future donations, from local businessman Barry Zekelman; condemnation from Jewish and human rights groups; and a 2,000-plus-name petition to the board of governors challenging the agreements with the protesters and the students alliance, signed by academics and others across Canada.
An 800-name petition by academics supporting the agreements was previously presented to the university.
Former University of Windsor board of governors'chair and former Ontario NDP education minister Dave Cooke, along with local MPPs Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex) and Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh), have also been critical of the deals.
"Our MPPs have made their comments," Ableser said. "What the ministry (of colleges and universities) should do is well above my pay grade.
"I'm going to digest what happened today, but at this point I have no further plans." Dwaddell@postmedia.com Twitter.com/winstarwaddell

Document 270 of 500

UWindsor board of governors rejects motion opposing protest camp deals

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/uwindsor-board-governors-rejects-motion-opposing/docview/3119955357/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=UWindsor+board+of+governors+rejects+motion+opposing+protest+camp+deals&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's now up to the community - or the province - to push for any changes to deals the University of Windsor struck with pro-Palestinian protesters earlier this year, said a board of governors member who is speaking out against the agreements.
As previously reported by the Windsor Star, Daniel Ableser's request to have his motion to "pause" implementation of the agreements added to Tuesday's agenda was deemed "outside the purview of the board," according to board chair Helga Reidel. That decision was based on an outside legal opinion, she said, but Reidel wouldn't confirm the details or say who provided the legal assessment.
Ableser's challenge of the chair's ruling, and his proposed motion, led to a 90-minute discussion behind closed doors at the board's regular meeting on Tuesday. His motion to pause implementation of the agreements that university administration struck with pro-Palestinian groups in July to end a two-month on-campus protest camp was ruled out of order by fellow board members.
"There has been a decision made on whether my motion was proper," Albeser later told the Star. "I guess I'll digest that and see if there is other direction the board could give.
"Ultimately, I think the board has made its decision. I think at the board table, this is the end."
Ableser's motion sought to direct administration to pause implementation of the terms of the encampment agreements signed July 10 pending further direction from the board of governors. It also called for a report to the next board meeting in November on options and recommendations to address the fallout caused by the agreements.
Daniel Ableser, a member of the University of Windsor's board of governors, speaks during a board meeting at Assumption Hall on Tuesday, Oct. 22, 2024.
The agreements included the university's recognition of 'the illegal Israeli settlement' and a promise not to pursue academic agreements with Israeli universities unless supported by its senate. Windsor's university also agreed to consider 'responsible investing' in recognition that Israel is an 'occupying power.'
Other board members declined to talk to the Star about the decision. "The motion of the chair is upheld," Reidel said in announcing the result of the in-camera discussion before moving on to the rest of the agenda's business.
Ableser asked University of Windsor president Robert Gordon in the public session whether he would do anything differently if he had the chance to address potential encampment deals again. "It's premature to ask a question of that nature," said Gordon, who presented a report on the university but otherwise did not address the encampment issue.
Albeser, a local lawyer, was less sure of whether this was the end of potential challenges to the university's encampment deals. He said he feels he has completed his duties as a board member and it's now up to the community and others to comment if necessary.
"We're governed by the province and the province can have its say if they don't like what we did," Albeser told the Star. "There may be other community avenues.
"There may be opportunities to look at further supports and other things we can do. I'll listen to the community for that."
The debate over whether university officials followed the University of Windsor Act in making those deals without the board of governors' involvement might not be over.
Members of the University of Windsor board of governors are shown at a regular meeting at Assumption Hall on Tuesday, Oct. 22, 2024.
Ableser presented board members with a contrary legal opinion on the board's role Tuesday.
The Star obtained a copy of that opinion, written by Melvyn L. Solmon, a partner in Toronto law firm Solmon Rothbart Tourgis Slodovnick.
Based on the University of Windsor Act and the fact the deals were a result of the encampments on campus, the matter should've been brought before the board of governors, Solomon opined.
"The President has no jurisdiction over the property of the UWindsor, nor over the investment committee established by the Board," Solomon wrote.
"These powers related to "general administration" cannot be interpreted to limit or interfere with the powers and obligations vested in the Board related to 'the government, conduct, management and control of the University and of its property, revenues, business and affairs.'"
Given the importance to the university community and the media attention the deals have generated, Solomon added the issues should have been given a more public airing.
Related
Pro-Palestinian group denounces attempt to pause deal with UWindsor
More UWindsor donors pull funding over deal with pro-Palestinian protesters
The fallout for the university to this point has included losing financial support from a number of donors, including a recently announced $1 million gift, and future donations, from local businessman Barry Zekelman; condemnation from Jewish and human rights groups; and a 2,000-plus-name petition to the board of governors challenging the agreements with the protesters and the students alliance, signed by academics and others across Canada.
An 800-name petition by academics supporting the agreements was previously presented to the university.
Former University of Windsor board of governors' chair and former Ontario NDP education minister Dave Cooke, along with local MPPs Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex) and Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh), have also been critical of the deals.
"Our MPPs have made their comments," Ableser said. "What the ministry (of colleges and universities) should do is well above my pay grade.
"I'm going to digest what happened today, but at this point I have no further plans."
Dwaddell@postmedia.com
Twitter.com/winstarwaddell

Document 271 of 500

Reader letter: 'Reprehensible' how politicians handling Windsor's growth

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/reader-letter-reprehensible-how-politicians/docview/3119918459/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reader+letter%3A+%27Reprehensible%27+how+politicians+handling+Windsor%27s+growth&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: With housing development, is Windsor 'still respecting homeowners'? (Letters, Oct. 2)
Ms. De Froy is right - any courage councillors appeared to show when they said they would protect residents' rights was apparently just window dressing. Residents are essentially being told to "shut up" and take in stride whatever developers decide to do.
Windsor and Essex County already have the lowest percentage of forest coverage in Ontario, and it's getting "greyer." Turning more of Windsor into an urban wasteland is the wrong kind of "progress."
In order to meet some "target" set by politicians in Toronto and Ottawa, Windsor residents are supposed to put up with overcrowding and even less open space while developers line their pockets and politicians throw up their hands, pretending to have no say.
Encroaching on neighbourhoods under the guise of building "affordable housing" is disingenuous - these new builds are expensive and out of reach to most.
I understand that in the current political climate, the environment and rights and well-being of residents are not paramount - see Doug Ford's attempted Green Belt land grab - but that does not make it acceptable.
To add insult to injury, we are now expected to pay higher property taxes for the privilege of watching neighbourhoods being encroached upon.
I've laboured under the misconception that elected officials are supposed to protect and advance the wishes of residents, only to find they don't even bother listening to their constituents.
Everyone believes in building a healthy and vibrant community, but the way this city and council are handling this is quite reprehensible.
Siggi Mosmann
Windsor
Share Your Views
Send letters to the editor to letters@windsorstar.com. (Don't send them as attachments; put them in the body of the emails). Letters must include your full name, address and phone number. (We will only publish your name and the municipality where you live). Letters must be fewer than 300 words. The Star reserves the right to edit, condense and reject letters.

Reader letter: Disturbing to see rich donors 'control' university policy

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/reader-letter-disturbing-see-rich-donors-control/docview/3119537138/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reader+letter%3A+Disturbing+to+see+rich+donors+%27control%27+university+policy&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: More UWindsor donors pull funding over deal with pro-Palestinian protesters (WS Sept. 17)
I find it disturbing that donors can try to control the policies of our public universities and hospitals by threatening to withhold donations.
These donations usually amount to a very small amount of the actual budget. Perhaps the Doug Ford government can fund these institutions sufficiently so that these demands are not an issue.
I'd hate to have rich donors deciding that doctors or students can't support international law or the International Court of Justice without any repercussions.
Tim Bilida
Windsor

Document 273 of 500

Reader letter: Our schools should stick to teaching 'the basics'

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/reader-letter-our-schools-should-stick-teaching/docview/3118693406/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reader+letter%3A+Our+schools+should+stick+to+teaching+%27the+basics%27&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: Brown-John: Don't let political 'fringers' dominate school board work (Opinion, Oct. 11)
It's interesting that columnist Lloyd Brown-John refers to some of the members of the public who show up at school board meetings as "fringers."
I recall a well-known Canadian using this same language to describe truck drivers, who showed up in Ottawa to protest the violation of their Charter rights. They and their supporters numbered in the thousands.
These 'fringers', as you call them, are simply asking for a return to teaching our children the basics (math, science, history, geography, language, etc.). These are the tools they need to compete with the rest of the world. Social engineering does not belong in the classroom.
The solution to winning this battle is to create competition. This is already happening in Alberta with the introduction of charter schools. Using the same blueprint of those in conservative states in the U.S., parents are pulling their children out of public schools.
These children now get a better education, without the radical ideologies, in a school system that better aligns with their moral values. Are you listening, Doug Ford?
Rick Mailloux
Windsor
Share Your Views

(We will only publish your name and the municipality where you live). Letters must be fewer than 300 words. The Star reserves the right to edit, condense and reject letters.

'Reprehensible' handling of growth; Letters to the editor

Author: Mosmann, Siggi

Publication info: Mosmann, Siggi. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reprehensible-handling-growth-letters-editor/docview/3118390849/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Reprehensible%27+handling+of+growth%3B+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=Mosmann%2C+Siggi&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: With housing development, is Windsor 'still respecting homeowners'? (Letters, Oct. 2) Ms. De Froy is right - any courage councillors appeared to show when they said they would protect residents'rights was apparently just window dressing. Residents are essentially being told to "shut up" and take in stride whatever developers decide to do.
Windsor and Essex County already have the lowest percentage of forest coverage in Ontario, and it's getting "greyer." Turning more of Windsor into an urban wasteland is the wrong kind of "progress."

I understand that in the current political climate, the environment and rights and well-being of residents are not paramount - see Doug Ford's attempted Green Belt land grab - but that does not make it acceptable.
To add insult to injury, we are now expected to pay higher property taxes for the privilege of watching neighbourhoods being encroached upon.
I've laboured under the misconception that elected officials are supposed to protect and advance the wishes of residents, only to find they don't even bother listening to their constituents.
Everyone believes in building a healthy and vibrant community, but the way this city and council are handling this is quite reprehensible.

Identifier / keyword: housing; development; windsor; respecting; right; courage

Document 277 of 500

Stiles fights involuntary treatment; NDP leader takes a stand opposite from mayor, calls for more supports

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stiles-fights-involuntary-treatment-ndp-leader/docview/3118390771/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stiles+fights+involuntary+treatment%3B+NDP+leader+takes+a+stand+opposite+from+mayor%2C+calls+for+more+supports&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government has fallen short in building affordable housing and funding mental health and addiction programs, said the leader of Ontario's NDP during a visit to Windsor this week.
Speaking to reporters outside Marlborough Public School in the city's west-end on Thursday, Marit Stiles warned against involuntary treatment, an idea publicly supported in a Facebook post by Windsor's mayor last week. Instead, she urged the province to fund evidence-based services for those experiencing mental health or addiction crises.
"It is destroying our communities," said Stiles. "There's no question, not just in Windsor, but all across Ontario. I see it every day, but particularly here.
"We know the evidence shows that when we put money into mental health and addiction services and we connect with people where they're at, we have the most successful outcomes," she said.
"But what I'm seeing right now is a lot of talk from this government, a lot of reversals, but not actually a lot of resources coming in, and nothing much based on the evidence of what actually is successful."
MPP Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh) told the Star that, contrary to what Stiles argues, the Ford government has implemented several programs that help provide affordable housing.
In a social media post to Facebook about two weeks ago, Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens voiced his support of Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown for considering involuntary treatment to address the city's ballooning addiction and mental health crisis.
This follows British Columbia's Premier David Eby, who is moving ahead with plans to open regional facilities that will provide involuntary care under the Mental Health Act.
"Before this government or anyone else really looks at involuntary treatment," MPP Lisa Gretzky (NDP - Windsor West) told reporters Thursday, "what needs to happen is a transformation of the mental health and addiction system."
Gretzky called the lack of funding, coupled with insufficient collaboration with frontline workers and individuals with lived experience, an "abject failure" on the part of the Ford government.
In August, the province introduced a new plan to help municipalities tackle a worsening addiction crisis.
The Ford government has earmarked $378-million for 19 new Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hubs. If the city's application is successful, Windsor's hub will offer primary care, mental health services, addiction support, social services, transition beds, supportive housing and other services. One day after the announcement, SafePoint's pending application for provincial approval was denied.
At Thursday's media event, Stiles also expressed serious concern about Ontario's affordable housing supply, criticizing the Ford government for failing to sufficiently address the shortage.
"We have to start actually addressing this crisis, or more people are going to end up homeless and without hope," said Stiles.
"To do that, we need the government to get back in the business of building truly affordable, not-forprofit homes. That means things like rent geared-to-income and co-op housing."
In response to the criticism, Dowie told the Star on Thursday afternoon that the Ontario government has launched several programs, including the Building Faster Fund, to increase housing supply.
Earlier this month, after exceeding its housing targets for the year, Windsor pocketed millions in provincial grant funding to accelerate development. The city will receive $3.48-million from Ontario's Building Faster Fund.
"Right now, young people especially, cannot find any place in the market for their needs, because they just don't have enough builtup capital," said Dowie.
"They don't have enough builtup investments and they're working with an arm tied behind their back."
Though the programs lack specific requirements for gearedto-income rent, Dowie said that increasing the housing supply will help lower market prices for renters and buyers.
"It means that you can be more choosy as a buyer, and gives you options to have the home of your dreams," he said.
The Town of Tecumseh is also getting a $15.1-million grant from the Ontario Housing Enabling Systems Fund to unlock undeveloped land that could support more than 4,000 new homes.
Gretzky, however, said the programs fall short.
She urged Ford's Progressive Conservatives to adopt a model similar to the post-Second World War era, when the federal government helped fund wartime homes for returning soldiers.
"To the question of whether or not the premier is doing enough to make life affordable for people, the government is actually going in the complete opposite direction," Gretzky said.
"In our community alone, we are seeing an increase in people experiencing homelessness. We're seeing an increase in people relying on food banks, people who are working two, sometimes three jobs, who are accessing food banks." mmazak@postmedia.com

Subject: Food programs; Mental health; Addictions; Affordable housing; Funding; Homeless people; Housing needs; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing Social networks

Ontario NDP leader demands more affordable housing, addiction supports from Ford government

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-ndp-leader-demands-more-affordable/docview/3118284713/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 279 of 500

Timing around India affair raises flags

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/timing-around-india-affair-raises-flags/docview/3118113435/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Timing+around+India+affair+raises+flags&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Monday, Canada announced the expulsion of six Indian diplomats over allegations of foreign interference. In a news conference, RCMP Commissioner Michael Duheme warned of widespread violence, homicides and a public security threat linked to agents of the Indian government. New Delhi has rejected the allegations, and sent six Canadian diplomats packing in return.
The accusations are appalling, and no country should be targeting the citizens of another on foreign soil. But why are these revelations coming out now? On the surface, they provide a great distraction from the weekend's attempted internal coup on the prime minister. The story of a revolt among Liberal MPs, 30 of which apparently signed a letter asking the PM to quit, hit the news cycle on Saturday. Then, presto: on Monday, the RCMP dropped its bombshell, allowing the PM to take to the airwaves and sound all grave and solemn and prime-minister like, defending Canadian sovereignty.
But that would be too obvious. So let's look at what else is going on in Ottawa, namely the Hogue commission on foreign interference. On Friday, the commission heard from Public Safety Minister Bill Blair that his office sat for 54 days on a warrant to investigate Ontario Liberal MPP Michael Chan, accused of doing China's bidding in Canada. Blair offered "no explanation" for the delay, and neither did his chief of staff.
Three days later, boom: India stands accused of being the major agent of transnational repression in Canada and the focus switches from the government's failings to the murderous machinations of New Delhi. And who is responsible for ensuring that the RCMP is "effective, accountable and addresses the government's priorities?" You guessed it: Bill Blair.
According to Chapter 5 of the 2023 NSICOP report on the federal policing mandate of the RCMP, while there exists a core concept of police independence, the minister provides direction on "national security investigations in sensitive sectors," including issuing directives to the RCMP "to implement special measures when investigating terrorist offences or activities which have an impact on fundamental institutions in Canadian society, including academia, politics, religion, the media and trade unions."
I'm not saying India isn't engaged in transnational repression, but the timing of this exposure is highly convenient for both the minister and the government. It's also highly convenient for China, which relishes any chance to bash India, its chief rival for dominance of the Indo-Pacific.
For whom is it inconvenient? Not just India.
It's also inconvenient for Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives. Left-wing media outlets made noise last month about alleged Indian efforts to build a "war chest" of funds to disparage Trudeau and help elect Poilievre. The same month, the Hindustan Times reported that Poilievre told Canadian Hindu media that, "Anti-Hindu and Hinduphobia agendas have no place (in Canada). I detest how the prime minister has divided our people. We were getting along in this country. Now look, everybody is fighting."
And then there's NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, who, until August, was Trudeau's loyal coalition supporter. When he was deputy leader of the Ontario NDP in 2015, Singh spoke at a Sikh "sovereignty rally" in San Francisco that venerated violent Khalistani leader Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale, and accused India of "trying to wipe us out."
In an interview on a Punjabi station in Canada in 2018, when asked to comment on Khalistani separatist referendums, Singh said, "In any place if people seek freedom, it is their right to seek freedom."
Why is India apparently willing to kill to crush the Khalistani movement? Sikhs have been demanding a separate state in the Punjab since India was partitioned in 1947. In the 1980s, the Khalistanis engaged in major acts of terrorism, including the bombing of Air India Flight 182 in 1985, which killed 329 people and was attributed to Sikh extremists in Canada. Today, Khalistanis regularly hold referendums in the U.S. and Canada on creating a separate state, with the goal holding an independence vote in the Punjab in 2025.
None of this is an excuse for murdering Canadian citizens. But nor should it be an excuse for this government to play diaspora politics and shield its own misdeeds. Postmedia News Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Referendums; Murders & murder attempts; Politics; Prime ministers; Transnationalism; Sovereignty

Location: China; Canada; India

Identifier / keyword: canada; announced; expulsion; indian; diplomats; allegations

Document 280 of 500

Liberals losing four ministers; Trudeau to shuffle cabinet amid ongoing loss of leaders, growing caucus revolt

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-losing-four-ministers-trudeau-shuffle/docview/3118113340/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+losing+four+ministers%3B+Trudeau+to+shuffle+cabinet+amid+ongoing+loss+of+leaders%2C+growing+caucus+revolt&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four more Liberal ministers will not be seeking re-election in the next federal election, sources say, which will force Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to proceed with an imminent cabinet shuffle at a time when an internal caucus revolt is brewing.
As first reported by Radio-Canada, Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau, Sport and Physical Activity Minister Carla Qualtrough, Northern Affairs Minister Dan Vandal and the minister responsible for the Federal Economic Development Agency for Southern Ontario, Filomena Tassi, recently told Trudeau they will not be running again.
All four of them were first elected in 2015, and both Bibeau and Qualtrough have been in cabinet since then.
In a statement shared on X, Tassi said the reason for making this decision is "deeply personal" and that it is time for her to be closer to home with family. She had requested a lighter workload in 2022 after her husband had suffered two strokes.
Tassi called her decision to leave politics "bittersweet" and said she is "deeply grateful" to Trudeau for entrusting her with a variety of portfolios in cabinet.
"I am honoured that he believed in me and thank him for his understanding and empathy. I wish him the very best now and in the future. I believed in him in 2015 and I believe in him now," she added in her statement.
Government sources confirmed that Vandal and Qualtrough will not be seeking re-election, and that they would be publishing statements later in the day.
Bibeau's office said she was out of the country until next week, but a government source confirmed that she is not running again.
Bibeau had previously hinted that she might be interested in running for municipal politics in Sherbrooke but that she would first finish her mandate as an MP.
The source said that the four ministers each have "highly personal reasons" for not running again but they have nothing to do with Trudeau's leadership.
CTV and Globe and Mail have already reported that Liberal MPs will be asking Trudeau to step down at their caucus meeting next week, but it is unclear if the prospect of a cabinet shuffle might discourage some from coming forward.
On Tuesday, Sean Casey, the MP for Charlottetown, became the first Liberal MP to publicly say he wants Trudeau to resign since news broke this past weekend that some members of the party's caucus have been organizing behind the scenes to collect signatures and force the leader to step down.
Speaking in Winnipeg on Wednesday, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault says Trudeau continues to have his support and suggested the same is true for most other Liberal MPs.
"I would argue that he has the support of the vast majority of the caucus," he said.
Over the weekend, Liberal MP Chandra Arya wrote an email to his colleagues urging them to not precipitate a leadership race with no clear successor waiting in the wings.
"I foresee a vicious battle for leadership which will fracture the fragile party structure with vastly different ideas and personalities - the results will be disastrous."
Trudeau had already lost two senior ministers in recent months.
Seamus O'Regan resigned from cabinet for personal reasons and was replaced as minister of Labour by Steven MacKinnon this summer. And Pablo Rodriguez resigned to run for the Quebec Liberal Party leadership. His position as Transport minister was taken on by Anita Anand.
National Post, with reporting from Stephanie Taylor calevesque@postmedia.com

Subject: Leadership

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Document 281 of 500

Pro-Palestinian deals not on agenda; UWindsor contracts not under board's purview, says legal adviser

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pro-palestinian-deals-not-on-agenda-uwindsor/docview/3118113298/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pro-Palestinian+deals+not+on+agenda%3B+UWindsor+contracts+not+under+board%27s+purview%2C+says+legal+adviser&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A motion to cease divisive contracts the University of Windsor struck with pro-Palestinian protesters has been rejected before the school's board of governors could even discuss it.
"I will confirm that I have sent an email to Daniel Ableser, the board member who brought the motion forward, advising that based on legal advice, which tells me that the motions are outside the purview of the board, they will not be on the agenda for the Tuesday meeting," board chair Helga Reidel told the Star on Wednesday.
"A comprehensive legal review has determined that the subject matter of your motion is outside the board of governors'authority," Reidel said in an email to board members about Ableser's motion.
Reidel would not elaborate on the legal review. She said the legal opinion will be discussed at the board's Oct. 22 meeting, though it will be in camera.
The university said Wednesday that president Robert Gordon has returned from an out-of-the-country business trip but was unavailable to comment.
"A legal review has determined that the subject matter of the motion is outside the board of governors'authority, and therefore, the motion will not be brought forward to the board," university administration said in an email.
The Star reported last week that Ableser brought forward a motion to pause the wide-reaching deals the university signed in July. Gordon's administration inked separate but similar deals with the University of Windsor Students'Alliance and a protest group called Windsor Divest that set up an encampment on campus.
The deals include UWindsor's recognition of "the illegal Israeli settlement," and a promise not to pursue academic agreements with Israeli universities unless supported by the university senate. UWindsor also pledged to make more "responsible investing" in recognition that Israel is an "occupying power."
Ableser's motion was to be addressed at next Tuesday's meeting.
"I believe there will still be discussion about identity-based oppression and perhaps the agreements in general through Dr. Gordon's update to the board, which he does at every meeting," said Reidel.
Ableser said he has requested a more detailed explanation of the legal opinions ahead of the meeting.
"The university has told me that my motion is outside of the Board of Governor's authority," he said. "I have sought clarification and the basis for that opinion, but have not received it."
Reidel's email to board members states that Ableser's motion addresses operational matters that "fall squarely within the authority of University Administration and are outside the role and responsibility of the Board."
She wrote that a legal review found the board does not have jurisdiction over matters that "fall within the purview of the Administration, nor can it simply overturn operational decisions made by the University Administration."
Ableser said he believes the University of Windsor Act, which states "the management and control of the University shall be vested in a non-denominational board of governors," provides the authority to vote on the motion.
"Accordingly, even if university administration had authority to enter into the encampment agreements, which some have questioned, I am quite interested to hear how the Board of Governors does not have authority pursuant to its statutory mandate to give any direction regarding the conduct, management and control of the university's business and affairs," said Ableser.
He told the Star he introduced the motion over concerns that "fallout" from the deals has "caused harm" to students, UWindsor's reputation, and the community at large. That fallout included condemnation from Jewish and human rights groups and multiple large donors withdrawing support.
Local MPPs Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex) and Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh) are among the detractors. Dowie told the Star on Wednesday he also believes the University of Windsor Act empowers the board of governors to consider the motion.
"I'm very hopeful that we'll have a debate on the motion on the 22nd of October, and shenanigans should not be used to derail it," he said. "I think it's a legitimate topic of conversation for the board of governors."
He questioned the university taking a stance on "complex world matters" and its engagement with protesters who occupied part of campus, engaging in "civil disobedience."
"I am also concerned that groups that don't have democratic legitimacy can affect change for the entire population of the school," said Dowie.
"The Palestinian Liberation Zone - I'm not sure what it represents, who it represents, who is a member, how those members are chosen. It's not clear how that is organized and whether it includes members of the university community as a whole.
"It's never been defined. It's never been determined. The university hasn't shared who exactly it negotiated with for reaching that agreement."
But the deals have their supporters.
On Wednesday, members of UWindsor's law faculty released a statement of support signed by 43 organizations and 803 individuals, including community members and scholars at universities in Canada, the U.S., the Middle East, and Europe.
The statement urged the board of governors to reject Ableser's push to pause the deals, adding the undersigned "unequivocally denounce the brazen attempts to undermine and rescind the landmark 10 July 2024 agreement."
Organizers stated in a media release the deals resulted from "good faith engagement and negotiations with student groups."
They said the deal "addresses anti-Palestinian racism; brings university investment and academic relationships in line with international law and human rights legal principles; condemns political interference in academic freedom of universities; protects student rights to freedom of speech and freedom of assembly; and takes a stand in the face of genocidal violence in Palestine."
"It is disheartening that there is now a motion to undermine the agreement as it has immensely enhanced UWindsor's reputation across the world and has put UWindsor on the map," said Vasanthi Venkatesh, one of three faculty of law members who released the petition.
Protests similar to the one at UWindsor appeared at universities across Canada this summer as the war in Gaza between Israel and Hamas escalated.
Members of Hamas, the Palestinian political and militant group governing Gaza, attacked Israel on Oct. 7. The group, which Canada considers a terrorist organization, killed 1,139 people and took 252 hostages. More than 40,000 Palestinians have been killed since Israel responded with a military invasion, according to Gaza health authorities. twilhelm@postmedia.com

Subject: Civil disobedience; Agreements; University administration; College campuses; Community; Governors; Demonstrations & protests; Colleges & universities; Human rights

Document 282 of 500

UWindsor board will no longer consider motion to cease pro-Palestinian deals

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/uwindsor-board-will-no-longer-consider-motion/docview/3117930191/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=UWindsor+board+will+no+longer+consider+motion+to+cease+pro-Palestinian+deals&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A motion to cease divisive contracts the University of Windsor struck with pro-Palestinian protesters has been rejected before the school's board of governors could even discuss it.
"I will confirm that I have sent an email to Daniel Ableser, the board member who brought the motion forward, advising that based on legal advice, which tells me that the motions are outside the purview of the board, they will not be on the agenda for the Tuesday meeting," board chair Helga Reidel told the Star on Wednesday.
"A comprehensive legal review has determined that the subject matter of your motion is outside the Board of Governors' authority," Reidel said in an email to board members about Ableser's initiative.
Reidel would not elaborate on the legal review. She said the legal opinion will be discussed at the board's Oct. 22 meeting, though it will be in-camera.
The university said Wednesday that president Robert Gordon has returned from an out-of-the-country business trip but was unavailable to comment.
"A legal review has determined that the subject matter of the motion is outside the Board of Governors' authority, and therefore, the motion will not be brought forward to the Board," university administration said in an email.
The Star reported last week that Daniel Ableser brought forward a motion to pause the wide-reaching deals the university signed in July. Gordon's administration inked separate but similar deals with the University of Windsor Students' Alliance and a protest group called Windsor Divest that set up an encampment on campus.
The deals include UWindsor's recognition of "the illegal Israeli settlement," and a promise not to pursue academic agreements with Israeli universities unless supported by the university senate. UWindsor also pledged to make more "responsible investing" in recognition that Israel is an "occupying power."
Ableser's motion was to be addressed at next Tuesday's meeting.
"I believe there will still be discussion about identity-based oppression and perhaps the agreements in general through Dr. Gordon's update to the board, which he does at every meeting," said Reidel.
Ableser said he has requested a more detailed explanation of the legal opinions ahead of the meeting.
"The university has told me that my motion is outside of the Board of Governor's authority," he said. "I have sought clarification and the basis for that opinion, but have not received it."
Reidel's email to board members states that Ableser's motion addresses operational matters that "fall squarely within the authority of University Administration and are outside the role and responsibility of the Board."
She wrote that a legal review found the board does not have jurisdiction over matters that "fall within the purview of the Administration, nor can it simply overturn operational decisions made by the University Administration."
Ableser said he believes the University of Windsor Act, which states "the management and control of the University shall be vested in a non-denominational board of governors," provides the authority to vote on the motion.
"Accordingly, even if university administration had authority to enter into the encampment agreements, which some have questioned, I am quite interested to hear how the Board of Governors does not have authority pursuant to its statutory mandate to give any direction regarding the conduct, management and control of the university's business and affairs," said Ableser.
He told the Star he introduced the motion over concerns that "fallout" from the deals has "caused harm" to students, UWindsor's reputation, and the community at large. That fallout included condemnation from Jewish and human rights groups and multiple large donors withdrawing support.
Local MPPs Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex) and Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh) are among the detractors. Dowie told the Star on Wednesday he also believes the University of Windsor Act empowers the board of governors to consider the motion.
"I'm very hopeful that we'll have a debate on the motion on the 22nd of October, and shenanigans should not be used to derail it," he said. "I think it's a legitimate topic of conversation for the board of governors."
He questioned the university taking a stance on "complex world matters" and its engagement with protesters who occupied part of campus, engaging in "civil disobedience."
"I am also concerned that groups that don't have democratic legitimacy can affect change for the entire population of the school," said Dowie.
"The Palestinian Liberation Zone - I'm not sure what it represents, who it represents, who is a member, how those members are chosen. It's not clear how that is organized and whether it includes members of the university community as a whole.
"It's never been defined. It's never been determined. The university hasn't shared who exactly it negotiated with for reaching that agreement."
But the deals have their supporters.
On Wednesday, members of UWindsor's law faculty released a statement of support signed by 43 organizations and 803 individuals, including community members and scholars at universities in Canada, the U.S., the Middle East, and Europe.
The statement urged the board of governors to reject Ableser's push to pause the deals, adding the undersigned "unequivocally denounce the brazen attempts to undermine and rescind the landmark 10 July 2024 agreement."
Organizers stated in a media release the deals resulted from "good faith engagement and negotiations with student groups."
They said the deal "addresses anti-Palestinian racism; brings university investment and academic relationships in line with international law and human rights legal principles; condemns political interference in academic freedom of universities; protects student rights to freedom of speech and freedom of assembly; and takes a stand in the face of genocidal violence in Palestine."
"It is disheartening that there is now a motion to undermine the agreement as it has immensely enhanced UWindsor's reputation across the world and has put UWindsor on the map," said Vasanthi Venkatesh, one of three faculty of law members who released the petition.
Related
'Caused some harm' - UWindsor governors to discuss pausing controversial encampment deal
More UWindsor donors pull funding over deal with pro-Palestinian protesters
$1M gift gone - Big donors desert UWindsor in wake of controversial deal with pro-Palestinian protesters
Protests similar to the one at UWindsor appeared at universities across Canada this summer as the war in Gaza between Israel and Hamas escalated.
Members of Hamas, the Palestinian political and militant group governing Gaza, attacked Israel on Oct. 7. The group, which Canada considers a terrorist organization, killed 1,139 people and took 252 hostages. More than 40,000 Palestinians have been killed since Israel responded with a military invasion, according to Gaza health authorities.
twilhelm@postmedia.com

UWindsor board won't consider motion to cease pro-Palestinian deals, says chair

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/uwindsor-board-wont-consider-motion-cease-pro/docview/3117904847/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 285 of 500

Windsor gets one of three car innovation hubs; Mercedes-Benz, ovin partnering on developing ai, software, batteries

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/windsor-gets-one-three-car-innovation-hubs/docview/3117220111/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Windsor+gets+one+of+three+car+innovation+hubs%3B+Mercedes-Benz%2C+ovin+partnering+on+developing+ai%2C+software%2C+batteries&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Windsor will be home to one of three incubators in Ontario created by a partnership between German automaker Mercedes-Benz and the Ontario Vehicle Innovation Network, the province has announced.
The partnership will focus on startup creation and developing talent in North America to aid the commercialization of automotive innovation.
"Windsor is known as a town where we make things, but I think this is a vote of confidence that Windsor is a town where ideas start and they turn into technology," said OVIN head Raed Kadri.
"That technology turns into companies and those companies become the suppliers of the future."
The OVIN incubators, which Premier Doug Ford helped announce on Oct. 8, will focus on the areas of software, artificial intelligence, battery development, vehicle components and electric drive.
Kadri said last week that Windsor was one of the three sites selected to launch the program because of its obvious strengths in automotive manufacturing, the advanced state of the new EV supply chain being built and because it can serve as a lighthouse to project the province's status as player in the future of automotive across the border.
"Innovation is part of Mercedes-Benz DNA," Markus Schäfer, Mercedes-Benz Group AG's chief technology officer, development and procurement, said in a statement.
"In our global (research and development) strategy, open innovation gives us rapid and direct access to the latest ideas and developments around the world.
"In a fast-paced environment, it is another important channel for developing exciting future products and elevating our customer experience through new technologies."
Mercedes will support identified startups and projects through its specialist expertise and use cases. Selected projects will also gain access to the international Mercedes-Benz Startup Autobahn network.
Startup Autobahn, founded in 2016, is an open innovation platform for startups in the field of mobility. It already has programs in the United States, China, India and South Korea.
"I think this is a vote of confidence in the innovation that lives within Windsor," said Kadri, a Windsor native. "It's a vote of confidence in the talent that lives in Windsor and a vote of confidence that Windsor is a part of the global ecosystem."
Kadri added Mercedes has committed to having people come through each of the communities to help launch the incubators and to connect them back within the Mercedes hierarchy.
"This is just the start of bigger things to come," Kadri said. "There are more big names and companies that are going to be involved that we will be announcing soon.
"This is an opportunity to create the platform to create and commercialize the leading edge of intellectual property, turn it into startups and lift them into the global supply chain."
This latest partnership adds another piece to the plan to make the local area Canada's automobility capital, said Ed Dawson, executive director of automobility and innovation at Invest WindsorEssex.
"This fits perfectly and provides more opportunity to build on the investments the province (and Ottawa have) made here in Windsor-Essex," said Dawson, who oversees one of seven OVIN regional technology centres.
"It's helping us build an end-toend supply for zero-emission vehicles. They (Mercedes and OVIN) see this as a great region to do that.
"It puts a flag in the ground that we are Canada's automobility capital and it adds research and development opportunities to go along with manufacturing."
In addition to Windsor, Mercedes-OVIN will have incubators in Kitchener-Waterloo and Toronto.
In its pilot phase, the OVIN incubators program will identify opportunities relevant to Mercedes-Benz research.
The company will also have a research collaboration with the University of Waterloo focused on the development of algorithms for advanced driving assistance systems.
Dawson said OVIN will have boots on the ground locally to oversee the program and expects there'll be a lot of collaboration with post-secondary institutions and local organizations such as WEtech Alliance and Invest WindsorEssex to funnel potential companies and projects to Mercedes.
"It's essentially trying to bridge the gap from doing research or trying to build a startup and getting in front of someone who can help you commercialize it and create intellectual property," Dawson said.
"What we can do, as a regional OVIN technology site, is make those connections and introductions for the people we're working with." Dwaddell@postmedia.com Twitter.com/winstarwaddell
