Mercedes-Benz targets Windsor for automotive innovation centre

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mercedes-benz-targets-windsor-automotive/docview/3123775835/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mercedes-Benz+targets+Windsor+for+automotive+innovation+centre&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Ontario Premier Doug Ford is shown Oct. 8, 2024, in Toronto, announcing a new automotive partnership with Mercedes-Benz that includes an innovation incubator in Windsor.

Document 287 of 500

Guest column: Ford's highway tunnel plan a way to change channel

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-fords-highway-tunnel-plan-way-change/docview/3116978329/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+Ford%27s+highway+tunnel+plan+a+way+to+change+channel&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Robin Baranyai
Changing the channel is a pretty familiar gambit. Predictable, even.
When a public figure lands in the hot seat, expect them to schedule a funding announcement, unveil a big new plan or identify a threat; anything to change focus.
Remember when Donald Trump tried to "buy" Greenland? Six days before that story broke, a whistleblower filed a complaint detailing how the former U.S. president illegally pressured Ukraine to investigate his political rival, Joe Biden. By Christmas, the U.S Congress would vote to impeach Trump.
Nevertheless, news outlets diligently reported on Greenland's geopolitical importance, the potential for mineral extraction beneath its melting ice, and the consternation of the Danish prime minister, who seemed astonished to have to say: "Greenland is not for sale."
By now, you'd think we'd be wise to this timeworn tactic. Yet here we are, reporting on a mega-tunnel under Highway 401.
Not that we have much choice. Premier Doug Ford has proposed - indeed, promised - to tunnel under the busiest highway in North America. Like it or not, it's news. Ford suggested new vehicle lanes and transit could stretch from Mississauga to Markham. That's about 55 km.
In quick succession, the premier announced both a feasibility study, and a determination to forge ahead regardless of what the study might say. This is a man desperate to change the channel.
Still, it must be reported. Journalists are diligently putting Ford's "visionary" promise in perspective. They've plumbed comparisons to Boston's Big Dig, which delivered 12 km of underground highway for $14.8 billion. They've reported the reactions of other party leaders, from "silly" to "a complete joke." They've called upon experts to test Ford's assumption that adding vehicle lanes will, in fact, relieve traffic congestion.
The argument for more and bigger highways is an enduring zombie of public policy, repeatedly shot down but very hard to kill. Former New York City traffic commissioner Sam Schwartz told CBC News what study after study has shown: "the more lanes you introduce, the more people are likely to drive."
Schwartz described proven alternatives, such as transit investments and high-occupancy toll lanes, which relieve congestion by reducing traffic. Even building transit down the centre of the 401 would make more sense than what Ford's proposing.
Meanwhile, away from the cameras, the RCMP continues its criminal investigation into the Greenbelt scandal.
Two provincial watchdogs already sharply criticized the government's improperly managed site selection process, which stood to enrich a handful of developers to the tune of $8.28 billion. The premier's office has denied any involvement in choosing specific sites for development. Ultimately, Ford reversed course, leaving the Greenbelt intact.
During the summer, this government commanded a striking number of headlines, including funding announcements, alcohol in corner stores, a retreat on supervised consumption sites, and shuttering the Ontario Science Centre. Some of these actions are just the business of governing, and some may be unnecessary and ill-advised, but the premier kept that remote firmly in his grasp.
Through it all, the media reported on hints of an early election, perhaps next spring. Ontario could go to the polls ahead of the results of what is a criminal investigation. This government has no business contemplating an election before voters have answers.
A mega-tunnel under the 401 may strike voters as decisive, out-of-the-box thinking - or a boondoggle in the making. It's actually something else.
This is our Greenland, folks.
Robin Baranyai's columns are regularly featured in Postmedia News publications. She can be reached at write.robin@baranyai.ca( mailto:write.robin@baranyai.ca ).

Subject: Voters; Roads & highways

Location: Greenland

Identifier / keyword: robin; baranyai; changing; channel; pretty; familiar

Document 288 of 500

Women's skills training program gets $1.24M boost

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/womens-skills-training-program-gets-1-24m-boost/docview/3116752954/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Women%27s+skills+training+program+gets+%241.24M+boost&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Women's Enterprise and Skills Training (WEST) of Windsor will receive $1.24 million in funding over three years from the provincial government to aid its program for training and supporting low-income women trying to enter the skilled trades.
The six-year-old program has provided pre-apprenticeship CNC machinist and mechanical millwright training for 400 women to date.
"It's great use of the funding because there are real career opportunities being developed right here at St. Clair College through WEST's great work," said local MPP Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh) during the funding announcement Friday at the college. "There are careers waiting for each of these students.
"The data is showing by partnering with many organizations and non-traditional ways of getting people to the workforce, it's actually paying dividends."
The funding will be spread over three years.
It will help train 20 applicants each year with St. Clair College delivering the educational portion of the program and helping assess the candidates. Unifor also is a partner with WEST.
To apply for the program's next intake or to learn more visit WEST's website at: www.westofwindsor.com.
"This year we have $300,000 to run the program," said WEST executive director Rose Anguiano Hurst.
"A lot of it goes to the training and personal protection equipment and tools needed. Part of it goes to the wage subsidies we pay employers and then part of it goes to staffing."
Hurst said WEST also has funding to help women with emergency housing, healthcare and even transportation needs. The wraparound approach has helped the program achieve an 80 per cent graduation rate over its six years.
"Without this funding it would not be possible (to run the program) and there would be 400 women who would not have had this life-changing opportunity," Hurst said.
For Nora Enaami and Miranda Campbell, who began the 42-week pre-apprenticeship program in September, the unique focus on women is the reason they enrolled.
"For me, men typically have knowledge on mechanics and I didn't have that background," said Enaami, who was laid off from a woodworking job prior to enrolling.
"A women's class, dipping their toes into the water, I thought that would be a good way (to transition to becoming a CNC machinist). They help with the funding, which is also very much a plus.
"WEST is very supportive because women have different needs. Some have children and different responsibilities. WEST has been very kind providing emotional support, mental support and financial support."
Campbell said she wanted to find something more stable than her previous factory job. It also fulfilled her desire to find a career working with her hands, something she acquired when she was young, hanging around her father's automotive garage business.
She also finds the all-female class a more comfortable learning environment.
"I wanted to have some certification that would propel my future and be able to provide for not only myself, but my daughter, being a single mother," Campbell said.
"I was in industry in a steel factory, but I wasn't properly trained, such as with this program.
"This program provides an equal playing field. This program allows an opportunity for a lot of women who don't get that otherwise." Dwaddell@postmedia.com Twitter.com/winstarwaddell

Subject: Skill development; Women; Apprenticeship; Funding

Document 289 of 500

Window smasher must pay: judge; No jail time for 55-year-old who pleaded guilty to four counts of mischief

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/window-smasher-must-pay-judge-no-jail-time-55/docview/3116752310/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Window+smasher+must+pay%3A+judge%3B+No+jail+time+for+55-year-old+who+pleaded+guilty+to+four+counts+of+mischief&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Windsor man who went on a downtown window-smashing spree, targeting multiple business owners with whom he was angry, has been ordered to compensate them for all the damage.
The 55-year-old pleaded guilty on Friday to four of 14 mischief offences for which he was charged by Windsor police.
But in a joint sentencing submission by prosecution and defence that was accepted by the judge, he avoided jail by seeking out rehab and agreeing to pay restitution to eight businesses who saw their windows and glass doors smashed on 14 occasions over a six-week period between Nov. 23, 2022, and Jan. 9, 2023.
"Why did all this happen?" Ontario Court Justice Shannon Pollock asked.
There was no clear answer.
Brenell Dean, a paralegal with Sway Law PC representing the offender at his Friday court appearance, described him as "a person with a disability ... he has issues with substances." SECURITY FOOTAGE Assistant Crown attorney Logan Annisette told the court the offender explained he was having "some personal disagreements ... with the owners of these businesses."
The glass-smashing spree - not all the attacks were in the downtown core - ended after Windsor police investigators arrested a suspect on Feb. 16, 2023. Security camera video showed a silver-coloured vehicle driving by the targeted businesses at about the same times as windows were smashed. In each case, metal bolts were found on the ground.
A trial had been set to commence last Thursday, but the Crown and defence approached the judge with a proposed resolution. The prosecution's case was not cut-and-dry, and the focus became looking at getting compensation for the property damage - there were no reported injuries in the attacks - as well as getting counselling and rehabilitation for the offender.
Dean told the judge her client has "made great strides" and was "clean and sober" after graduating in June from a five-month program at Brentwood Recovery Home.
"He is (now) in a very clear state of mind and taking responsibility," said Dean.
Annisette said the offender has a (mostly dated) criminal record, including convictions for arson and assault, but that he was "upfront (with police) ... and took a lot of the right steps" after his arrest. He has since learned that smashing others'property "is not the best way to address one's problems," Annisette added.
The judge handed a suspended sentence on one of the counts and a conditional sentence for the three other counts of mischief under $5,000 after guilty pleas were entered. He'll be on probation for the next three years and was ordered not to communicate with any of the business owners nor to visit their premises.
Justice Pollock gave the offender until Oct. 11, 2025, to pay restitution orders totalling nearly $13,000 in window and door repair claims.
Collecting the money owed for the damages, however, might be challenging - the court heard the offender is retired and lives on a fixed disability pension. Neither the Crown nor the defence could say what happens if he doesn't pay.
Contacted after the judgment, the owner of one of the targeted downtown businesses said they didn't like the message the seemingly light sentence sends - that "you can do this and get away with it."
But the business community's outrage sparked at the time by the seemingly random attacks of vandalism likely helped focus new attention and action by city hall. The then-head of the Downtown Windsor BIA pointed an accusatory finger at city council and the mayor, and claimed Mayor Drew Dilkens was "not giving a lot of attention to the downtown the last eight years."
Brian Yeomans said at the time that he agreed with police Chief Jason Bellaire, stating there had to be something more than simply having more police officers on the streets: "This is not just about broken windows, this is systemic."
A big part of city hall's response came this past spring, when Dilkens unveiled the $4.2-million 'Strengthen the Core'strategy, with one of its pillars being a dozen new police officers walking the downtown streets.
With homelessness, drug addiction and mental illness all seen as root causes of what's fuelling the petty crimes that hurt the downtown's rebound efforts, local business leaders stated that steppedup law enforcement and throwing offenders behind bars can't be the only answer.
When it comes to dealing with those root causes, "Windsor is in really bad shape," Dean told the Star following Justice Pollock's decision on Friday.
She said her client's sentence is recognition by the justice system that "rehabilitation of offenders is extremely important."
The problem, Dean added, is that the local wait lists for necessary treatment and rehabilitative services are too long. It wasn't until more than a year after his arrest that Dean's client was able to get into Brentwood.
"We need to get these people help, and these problems will solve themselves," Yeomans told the Star in January 2023 in the midst of that window-smashing crime spree.
Dean's client didn't speak during Friday's court appearance.
"You should be commended (for attending Brentwood) and getting your life back on track," Justice Pollock told him.
"Good luck."
dschmidt@postmedia.com twitter.com/schmidtcity

Subject: Criminal investigations; Criminal pleas

Document 290 of 500

Windsor's WEST gets $1.24M from province to help women enter skilled trades

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/windsors-west-gets-1-24m-province-help-women/docview/3116648771/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Windsor%27s+WEST+gets+%241.24M+from+province+to+help+women+enter+skilled+trades&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Women's Enterprise and Skills Training of Windsor will receive $1.24 million in funding over three years from the provincial government to aid its program for training and supporting low-income women trying to enter the skilled trades.
The six-year-old program has provided pre-apprenticeship CNC machinist and mechanical millwright training for 400 women to date.
"It's great use of the funding because there are real career opportunities being developed right here at St. Clair College through WEST's great work," said local MPP Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh) during the funding announcement Friday at the college. "There are careers waiting for each of these students.
"The data is showing by partnering with many organizations and non-traditional ways of getting people to the workforce, it's actually paying dividends."
The funding will be spread over three years.
It will help train 20 applicants each year with St. Clair College delivering the educational portion of the program and helping assess the candidates. Unifor also is a partner with WEST.
To apply for the program's next intake or to learn more visit WEST's website at: www.westofwindsor.com.
"This year we have $300,000 to run the program," said WEST executive director Rose Anguiano Hurst.
"A lot of it goes to the training and personal protection equipment and tools needed. Part of it goes to the wage subsidies we pay employers and then part of it goes to staffing."
Hurst said WEST also has funding to help women with emergency housing, healthcare and even transportation needs. The wraparound approach has helped the program achieve an 80 per cent graduation rate over its six years.
"Without this funding it would not be possible (to run the program) and there would be 400 women who would not have had this life-changing opportunity," Hurst said.
For Nora Enaami and Miranda Campbell, who began the 42-week pre-apprenticeship program in September, the unique focus on women is the reason they enrolled.
"For me, men typically have knowledge on mechanics and I didn't have that background," said Enaami, who was laid off from a woodworking job prior to enrolling.
"A women's class, dipping their toes into the water, I thought that would be a good way (to transition to becoming a CNC machinist). They help with the funding, which is also very much a plus.
"WEST is very supportive because women have different needs. Some have children and different responsibilities. WEST has been very kind providing emotional support, mental support and financial support."
Campbell added she wanted to find something more stable than her previous factory job. It also fulfilled her desire to find a career working with her hands, something she acquired young, hanging around her father's automotive garage business.
She also finds the all-female class a more comfortable learning environment.
"I wanted to have some certification that would propel my future and be able to provide for not only myself, but my daughter being a single mother," Campbell said.
"I was in industry in a steel factory, but I wasn't properly trained, such as with this program.
"This program provides an equal playing field. This program allows an opportunity for a lot of women who don't get that otherwise."
Dwaddell@postmedia.com
Twitter.com/winstarwaddell

Document 291 of 500

No jail, but Windsor judge orders restitution for downtown window-smashing spree

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-jail-windsor-judge-orders-restitution-downtown/docview/3116567825/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+jail%2C+but+Windsor+judge+orders+restitution+for+downtown+window-smashing+spree&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Windsor man who went on a downtown window-smashing spree, targeting multiple business owners with whom he was angry, has been ordered to compensate them for all the damage.
The 55-year-old pleaded guilty on Friday to four of 14 mischief offences for which he was charged by Windsor police.
But in a joint sentencing submission by prosecution and defence that was accepted by the judge, he avoided jail by seeking out rehab and agreeing to pay restitution to eight businesses who saw their windows and glass doors smashed on 14 occasions over a six-week period between Nov. 23, 2022, and Jan. 9, 2023.
"Why did all this happen?" Ontario Court Justice Shannon Pollock asked.
There was no clear answer. Brenell Dean, a paralegal with Sway Law PC representing the offender at his Friday court appearance, described him as "a person with a disability ... he has issues with substances."
Assistant Crown attorney Logan Annisette told the court the offender explained he was having "some personal disagreements ... with the owners of these businesses."
The glass-smashing spree - not all the attacks were in the downtown core - ended after Windsor police investigators arrested a suspect on Feb. 16, 2023. Security camera video showed a silver-coloured vehicle driving by the targeted businesses at about the same times as windows were smashed. In each case, metal bolts were found on the ground.
A trial had been set to commence last Thursday, but the Crown and defence approached the judge with a proposed resolution. The prosecution's case was not cut-and-dry, and the focus became looking at getting compensation for the property damage - there were no reported injuries in the attacks - as well as getting counselling and rehabilitation for the offender.
Dean told the judge her client has "made great strides" and was "clean and sober" after graduating in June from a five-month program at Brentwood Recovery Home
"He is (now) in a very clear state of mind and taking responsibility," said Dean.
Annisette said the offender has a (mostly dated) criminal record, including for arson and assault, but that he was "upfront (with police) ... and took a lot of the right steps" after his arrest. He's since learned that smashing others' property "is not the best way to address one's problems," Annisette added.
The judge handed a suspended sentence on one of the counts and a conditional sentence for the three other counts of mischief under $5,000 for which guilty pleas were entered. He'll be on probation for the next three years and was ordered not to communicate with any of the business owners nor to step into their premises.
Justice Pollock gave the offender until Oct. 11, 2025, to pay restitution orders totalling nearly $13,000 in window and door repair claims.
Collecting the money owed for the damages, however, might be challenging - the court heard the offender is retired and lives on a fixed disability pension. Neither the Crown nor the defence could later tell the Star what happens if he doesn't pay.
Contacted after the judgment, the owner of one of the targeted downtown businesses told the Star they didn't like the message the seemingly light sentence sends - that "you can do this and get away with it."
But the business community's outrage sparked at the time by the seemingly random attacks of vandalism likely helped focus new attention and action by city hall. The then-head of the Downtown Windsor BIA pointed an accusatory finger at city council and the mayor, and claimed Mayor Drew Dilkens of "not giving a lot of attention to the downtown the last eight years."
Brian Yeomans told the Star at the time that he agreed with police Chief Jason Bellaire stating there had to be something more than simply having more police officers on the streets: "This is not just about broken windows, this is systemic."
A big part of city hall's response came this past spring, when Dilkens unveiled the $4.2-million 'Strengthen the Core' strategy, with one of its pillars being a dozen new police officers walking the downtown streets.
Windsor is in really bad shape
With homelessness, drug addiction and mental illness all seen as root causes of what's fuelling the petty crimes that hurt the downtown's rebound efforts, local business leaders stated that stepped-up law enforcement and throwing offenders behind bars can't be the only answer.
When it comes to dealing with those root causes, "Windsor is in really bad shape," Dean told the Star following Justice Pollock's decision on Friday.
She said her client's sentence is recognition by the justice system that "rehabilitation of offenders is extremely important."
The problem, Dean added, is that the local wait lists for necessary treatment and rehabilitative services are too long. It wasn't until more than a year after his arrest that Dean's client was able to get into Brentwood.
Related
Downtown calls on mayor, police for more help against crime
'Easy prey' - Windsor robber gets 20 months jail for downtown attacks
What you need to know about Windsor's $4.2M downtown revitalization plan
Latest target of smashed windows calls for more support in downtown Windsor
"We need to get these people help, and these problems will solve themselves," Yeomans told the Star in January 2023 in the midst of that window-smashing crime spree.
Dean's client didn't speak at Friday's court appearance.
"You should be commended (for attending Brentwood) and getting your life back on track," Justice Pollock told him.
"Good luck."
dschmidt@postmedia.com
twitter.com/schmidtcity

Document 292 of 500

Improve access before forcing addicts into treatment: minister; 'Scaled to need'

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/improve-access-before-forcing-addicts-into/docview/3115868722/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Improve+access+before+forcing+addicts+into+treatment%3A+minister%3B+%27Scaled+to+need%27&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Provincial leaders ought to focus on improving access to treatment before deciding whether those struggling with addiction should decide to go on their own or involuntarily, Mental Health and Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks said Friday.
Saks appeared before reporters to announce the launch of a $150-million fund that cities and First Nations can apply to access to fund programs that help them respond to the overdose crisis, which public health experts say is driven by an increasingly toxic drug supply.
Asked about growing calls for involuntary treatment on Friday, Saks said jurisdictions first need to ensure they have treatment services in place, "which currently they don't have."
"Before we contemplate voluntary or involuntary treatment, I would like to see provinces and territories ensuring that they actually have treatment access scaled to need," she said. "They are not there yet. They have the resources and tools to do it."
Leaders such as B.C. NDP Leader David Eby, who is campaigning for re-election in the province known as ground zero for the crisis, has promised to open facilities that offer involuntary care for those with serious addictions and mental illness.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith has also promised her United Conservative Party government would introduce a law allowing family members, police or physicians to seek involuntary treatment on behalf of someone else.
Federal Opposition Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre also told reporters on Thursday he supports involuntary treatment for minors and those in prisons struggling with mental illness and addictions, but is still studying the issue when it comes to adults.
Saks said the role of the federal government is to support the provinces when it comes to health care, which she underscored that provinces and territories are responsible for administering.
"Before we talk about involuntary or voluntary treatment, I would like to see them utilize and access the robust $200-billion worth of health-care agreements that have been signed across the country," she said, referring to the deal announced with jurisdictions last year.
"This is about political will and allocating the resources that they have to providing treatment access to those who need it in a way that is timely and accessible."
Poilievre has been leading the charge in criticizing Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's handling of the overdose crisis, deaths from which the federal health agency began tracking in 2016.
More than 47,000 people have died from opioid-related poisonings from January 2016 until March 2024, according to the Public Health Agency of Canada.
Many public health, addictions experts and other advocates say the staggering death toll comes from an increasingly toxic supply of drugs.
Poilievre says the federal government has failed to offer enough treatment options to help Canadians recover. He has slammed its support of supervised consumption sites, which he calls "drug dens," as fuelling addiction, and its funding of "safer supply" programs that provide users with pharmaceutical alternatives like hydromorphone to prevent them from using street drugs.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has pledged to close 10 supervised consumption sites, which are facilities that allow people to bring their own drugs to use under the watch of someone else in the event they may overdose.
Ford cited public safety concerns in his decision to shutter the sites.
On Friday, Saks defended these facilities as having reversed around 58,000 overdoses since 2016, saying that when access to them are closed, it endangers people's lives.
She added there are examples of jurisdictions where such sites operate well within communities and that concerns around public safety are properly addressed.
"Jurisdictions are responsible for the health services that they provide," Saks said Friday.
"We continue to provide guidelines and standards. We provide the regulatory structures for safe consumption sites that are adhered to by the operators, but yes, provinces have a key part to play in this."
National Post staylor@postmedia.com

Subject: Provinces; Public health; Drug overdose; Addictions; Public safety; Pharmacists; Substance abuse treatment; Mental disorders

UWindsor governors to consider pausing deal with protesters

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/uwindsor-governors-consider-pausing-deal-with/docview/3115867298/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=UWindsor+governors+to+consider+pausing+deal+with+protesters&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The University of Windsor's board of governors will discuss a motion this month to cease the controversial deals that school administration struck with pro-Palestinian protesters, including a group that occupied part of campus for two months.
The motion would also direct administration to report back with recommendations to address the "challenges" caused by the agreements, the Windsor Star has learned.
"I think the fallout has shown that this has caused some harm to the university," said Daniel Ableser, a provincial appointee to the board who brought the motion forward.
"And I think it's caused harm to our students, our reputation, the community at large. Both the Jewish and the wider community. So, I think that the board of governors, who ultimately is statutorily tasked with management of the university, needs to have a role to play in this."
The notice of motion, obtained by the Windsor Star, is scheduled to be addressed at the board of governors meeting on Oct. 22.
It will address separate but similar deals that president Robert Gordon's administration signed in July, agreeing to multiple demands from the University of Windsor Students'Alliance and a group of protesters who set up a campus encampment.
The wide-ranging deals include UWindsor's recognition of "the illegal Israeli settlement," and a promise not to pursue academic agreements with Israeli universities unless supported by senate. UWindsor also pledged to make more "responsible investing" in recognition that Israel is an "occupying power."
UWindsor administration responded to a request for comment about Ableser's motion with a one-sentence email.
"The university is aware of a board member's notice of motion request for the October board meeting."
Board chair Helga Reidel declined to comment until the motion is dealt with.
"Members are allowed to bring motions forward," she said.
"They have to be within the purview of the board. So all of that is being reviewed right now in advance of the meeting. Then it will be discussed at the meeting."
If moved and approved, the motion would dictate that administration "pause all implementation of the terms of the encampment agreements signed July 10, 2024 pending further direction from the board of governors."
The motion calls for administration to report back on any elements of the agreements it recommends the university proceed with, so the board can provide further direction.
It would also direct administration to come to the board's November meeting with options and recommendations to address the challenges caused by the agreements.
Ableser said the deals created a "governance challenge" because he sees the deals as a "strategic direction" for which the board of governors should have had oversight.
"I think there are reputational challenges," said Ableser.
"I think there are academic challenges to the importance of universities as institutions being neutral. And then encouraging views from all sides. I think this is causing challenges and harms to all students, frankly."
With some donors abandoning the university, he said the deals also created financial challenges.
"I think the financial challenges are the least of my concern," said Ableser. "But it's important when people who have had long relationships with the university are saying, 'We don't want to be associated with that university anymore.'" The Star previously reported many large donors have stopped supporting UWindsor because of the agreements.
Among them were law school alumni groups including the Windsor Law Class of 1981 Scholarship, which is the largest class-funded endowment of any UWindsor faculty.
Business mogul and philanthropist Barry Zekelman withdrew a $1-million pledge along with future financial support.
Several organizations also condemned the deals, including B'nai Brith Canada, the Friends of Simon Wiesenthal Center, the Centre for Israel and Jewish Affairs, Hillel Ontario and the Windsor Jewish Federation.
B'nai Brith, one of Canada's largest Jewish human rights organizations, launched an "official letter of complaint," alleging violations of Ontario's Discriminatory Business Practice Act.
MPP Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex), who said the university "set a dangerous precedent by negotiating with trespassers," also condemned the deals.
"I think that the trespassers should have been treated like trespassers," Leardi told the Star.
"Every other university in Canada ejected them and treated them like trespassers by ejecting them from the property they were trespassing on. The University of Windsor should have done the same thing."
Ableser said it's time the board of governors get involved.
"So I've tried to craft a motion that does that, that doesn't criticize the past, that doesn't criticize how we got here, but really focuses on where we go from here" he said.
Protests similar to the one at UWindsor appeared at universities across Canada last summer as the war in Gaza between Israel and Hamas escalated.
Hamas, the Palestinian political and militant group governing Gaza, attacked Israel on Oct. 7. The group, which Canada considers a terrorist organization, killed 1,139 people and took 252 hostages.
More than 40,000 Palestinians have been killed since Israel responded with an invasion, according to Gaza health authorities.
"It's obviously a national movement," Jamiel Nasser, with the Windsor Divest group that set up the encampment, said in July.
"We're looking at how some of the other schools ended it. Unfortunately, some of them did not end peacefully.
"We are very grateful that we are able to come to a peaceful agreement with the university, and also that the agreement is very good." twilhelm@postmedia.com

Document 294 of 500

'Find a way' to stop hazardous materials, MP says

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/find-way-stop-hazardous-materials-mp-says/docview/3115867294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Find+a+way%27+to+stop+hazardous+materials%2C+MP+says&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Local MP Irek Kusmierczyk is urging his own Liberal government to "get creative" and stop Michigan's controversial plan to allow transportation of hazardous materials across the Ambassador Bridge.
"The community is opposed to having hazmat on the bridge," Kusmierczyk (L - Windsor-Tecumseh) told the Star. "I am opposed to having hazmat on the bridge. That's the community's message.
"It's loud and clear. And that's the message I have conveyed both to the minister and to transport officials. Find a way, find a lever that we can pull."
Regulations in Canada have not changed, which means the transportation of hazardous materials over the bridge and onto city streets was already permitted here. But the federal, provincial, and municipal governments are now all pointing to each other to stop it.
Effective Oct. 29, the Michigan Department of Transportation (MDOT) will lift restrictions on the transportation of Class 3 flammable liquids and Class 8 corrosive substances over the bridge.
The Ambassador Bridge company, which requested the change, said it would "dramatically increase" safety across Michigan because the dangerous substances are travelling shorter distances within the state before entering Ontario. The only current border crossing option for truckers hauling dangerous materials is the Bluewater Bridge in Sarnia.
The main concern in Windsor has been the 95-year-old Ambassador Bridge's ability to handle an incident such as a fire or spill, and the timing of the decision given the Gordie Howe International Bridge will open next year. The Gordie has safety features such as truck lanes and fire-suppression systems built into the design.
MP Brian Masse (NDP - Windsor West) was the first to raise concerns about the change in September, calling it the "wrong decision, wrong time."
Windsor Fire Chief Stephen Laforet told the Star in September that his department filed a formal opposition to the plan during Michigan's consultation process.
Windsor city council voted unanimously to support Masse's efforts to keep hazardous materials off the Ambassador Bridge. The city sent letters to Masse and federal Transport Minister Anita Anand expressing its concerns.
The federal and provincial governments both have legislation covering the conveyance of hazardous materials. For Transport Canada, it's the Transportation of Dangerous Goods Act and Regulations. At the provincial level, it's the Dangerous Goods Transportation Act.
But both levels of government say the Ambassador Bridge issue is the other's responsibility, and also the City of Windsor's.
Transport Canada told the Star it regulates importation, transportation, and handling of hazardous materials, "but not the roads or bridges over which they travel, including international border crossings."
The federal agency said in an email that provinces and municipalities are responsible for restrictions on which roads dangerous goods can travel on in their jurisdictions.
Kusmierczyk said he reached out to Transport Canada and was told the same thing. He said the province regulates what can travel on roads and bridges, and the federal government dictates how it must be transported.
"I know the community doesn't want to see hazmat on the Ambassador Bridge, so I continue to push Transport Canada officials to find what levers we do have on this issue," he said. "But as it stands now, this is a jurisdiction responsibility of the provinces and municipalities."
In a one-sentence response to several questions about the issue, Ontario's Ministry of Transportation told the Star it's a federal responsibility.
"The transportation of hazardous Materials are regulated Canada-wide by Transport Canada under the federal Transportation of Dangerous Goods Act and regulations," the ministry said in an email without further explanation.
Local MPP Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh) was unavailable for an interview. Over several emails, he would not state whether he opposes the upcoming change. He stated that hazardous materials on the Ambassador Bridge are under federal authority.
"Once again, we are witness to the federal government abdicating its clear responsibilities to the people of Windsor and deflecting its responsibilities to others," Dowie stated.
"The government of Canada has been empowered by the parliament of Canada to determine whether hazardous materials can be transported on the Ambassador Bridge."
Dowie also wrote that the city could enact bylaws to restrict hazardous materials on municipal roads.
The city said last month it is exploring that option. But officials this week did not provide the Star with an update on that plan.
Mayor Drew Dilkens also did not respond to a request for comment, but he recently told the Star that Transport Canada's claim it has no control over dangerous goods crossing the bridge is "really hard to believe" and "unconscionable."
In an email to the Star, the city said Thursday it acknowledges that transporting hazardous materials on local roads is nothing new, and necessary to support economic supply chains.
"However, a significant incident on the bridge may create additional challenges," the city said. "The federal government has authority over the bridge, and while Windsor Fire will always respond to an incident to the best of its ability, it will be incumbent upon the federal government to ensure a proper response." twilhelm@postmedia.com

Subject: Municipal government; Federal government; Fires; Hazardous materials

Location: United States--US; Canada; Michigan

Company / organization: Name: Transport Canada; NAICS: 926120

Document 295 of 500

'Caused some harm' - UWindsor governors to discuss pausing controversial encampment deal

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/caused-some-harm-uwindsor-governors-discuss/docview/3115758590/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Caused+some+harm%27+-+UWindsor+governors+to+discuss+pausing+controversial+encampment+deal&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The University of Windsor's board of governors will discuss a motion this month to cease the controversial deals that school administration struck with pro-Palestinian protesters, including a group that occupied part of campus for two months.
The motion would also direct administration to report back with recommendations to address the "challenges" caused by the agreements, the Windsor Star has learned.
"I think the fallout has shown that this has caused some harm to the university," said Daniel Ableser, a provincial appointee to the board who brought the motion forward. "And I think it's caused harm to our students, our reputation, the community at large. Both the Jewish and the wider community. So, I think that the board of governors, who ultimately is statutorily tasked with management of the university, needs to have a role to play in this."
The notice of motion, obtained by the Windsor Star, is scheduled to be addressed at the board of governors meeting on Oct. 22.
It will address separate but similar deals that president Robert Gordon's administration signed in July, agreeing to multiple demands from the University of Windsor Students' Alliance and a group of protesters who set up a campus encampment.
The wide-ranging deals include UWindsor's recognition of "the illegal Israeli settlement," and a promise not to pursue academic agreements with Israeli universities unless supported by the senate. UWindsor also pledged to make more "responsible investing" in recognition that Israel is an "occupying power."
UWindsor administration responded to a request for comment about Ableser's motion with a one-sentence email:
"The university is aware of a board member's notice of motion request for the October board meeting."
Board chair Helga Reidel declined to comment until the motion is dealt with.
"Members are allowed to bring motions forward," she said. "They have to be within the purview of the board. So all of that is being reviewed right now in advance of the meeting. Then it will be discussed at the meeting."
If moved and approved, the motion would dictate that administration "pause all implementation of the terms of the Encampment Agreements signed July 10, 2024 pending further direction from the Board of Governors."
The motion calls for administration to report back on any elements of the agreements it recommends the university proceed with, so the board can provide further direction.
It would also direct administration to come to the board's November meeting with options and recommendations to address the challenges caused by the agreements.
Ableser said the deals created a "governance challenge" because he sees the deals as a "strategic direction" for which the board of governors should have had oversight.
"I think there are reputational challenges," said Ableser. "I think there are academic challenges to the importance of universities as institutions being neutral. And then encouraging views from all sides. I think this is causing challenges and harms to all students, frankly."
With some donors abandoning the university, he said the deals also created financial challenges.
"I think the financial challenges are the least of my concern," said Ableser. "But it's important when people who have had long relationships with the university are saying, 'We don't want to be associated with that university anymore.'"
The Star previously reported that multiple large donors have stopped supporting UWindsor because of the agreements. Among them were law school alumni groups including the Windsor Law Class of 1981 Scholarship, the largest class-funded endowment of any UWindsor faculty.
Business mogul and philanthropist Barry Zekelman withdrew a $1-million pledge along with future financial support.
Several organizations also condemned the deals, including B'nai Brith Canada, the Friends of Simon Wiesenthal Center, the Centre for Israel and Jewish Affairs, Hillel Ontario, and the Windsor Jewish Federation.
B'nai Brith, one of Canada's largest Jewish human rights organizations, launched an "official letter of complaint," alleging violations of Ontario's Discriminatory Business Practice Act (DBPA).
MPP Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex), who said the university "set a dangerous precedent by negotiating with trespassers," also condemned the deals.
"I think that the trespassers should have been treated like trespassers," Leardi told the Star. "Every other university in Canada ejected them and treated them like trespassers by ejecting them from the property they were trespassing on. The University of Windsor should have done the same thing."
Ableser said it's time the board of governors get involved.
"So I've tried to craft a motion that does that, that doesn't criticize the past, that doesn't criticize how we got here, but really focuses on where we go from here," he said.
Protests similar to the one at UWindsor appeared at universities across Canada last summer as the war in Gaza between Israel and Hamas escalated.
Related
$1M gift gone - Big donors desert UWindsor in wake of controversial deal with pro-Palestinian protesters
More UWindsor donors pull funding over deal with pro-Palestinian protesters
Hamas, the Palestinian political and militant group governing Gaza, attacked Israel on Oct. 7. The group, which Canada considers a terrorist organization, killed 1,139 people and took 252 hostages. More than 40,000 Palestinians have been killed since Israel responded with an invasion, according to Gaza health authorities.
"It's obviously a national movement," Jamiel Nasser, with the Windsor Divest group that set up the encampment, said in July. "We're looking at how some of the other schools ended it. Unfortunately, some of them did not end peacefully.
"We are very grateful that we are able to come to a peaceful agreement with the university, and also that the agreement is very good."
twilhelm@postmedia.com

Document 296 of 500

'Find a way' - Windsor Liberal MP pushes Transport Canada to stop Michigan plan for hazmat on Ambassador Bridge

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/find-way-windsor-liberal-mp-pushes-transport/docview/3115750852/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Find+a+way%27+-+Windsor+Liberal+MP+pushes+Transport+Canada+to+stop+Michigan+plan+for+hazmat+on+Ambassador+Bridge&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Local MP Irek Kusmierczyk is urging his own Liberal government to "get creative" and stop Michigan's controversial plan to allow transportation of hazardous materials across the Ambassador Bridge.
"The community is opposed to having hazmat on the bridge," Kusmierczyk (L - Windsor-Tecumseh) told the Star. "I am opposed to having hazmat on the bridge. That's the community's message.
"It's loud and clear. And that's the message I have conveyed both to the minister and to transport officials. Find a way, find a lever that we can pull."
Regulations in Canada have not changed, which means the transportation of hazardous materials over the bridge and onto city streets was already permitted here. But the federal, provincial, and municipal governments are now all pointing to each other to stop it.
Effective Oct. 29, the Michigan Department of Transportation (MDOT) will lift restrictions on the transportation of Class 3 flammable liquids and Class 8 corrosive substances over the bridge.
The Ambassador Bridge company, which requested the change, said it would "dramatically increase" safety across Michigan because the dangerous substances are travelling shorter distances within the state before entering Ontario. The only current border crossing option for truckers hauling dangerous materials is the Bluewater Bridge in Sarnia.
The main concern in Windsor has been the 95-year-old Ambassador Bridge's ability to handle an incident such as a fire or spill, and the timing of the decision given the Gordie Howe International Bridge will open next year. The Gordie has safety features such as truck lanes and fire suppression systems built into the design.
MP Brian Masse (NDP - Windsor West) was the first to raise concerns about the change in September, calling it the "wrong decision, wrong time."
Windsor Fire Chief Stephen Laforet told the Star in September that his department filed a formal opposition to the plan during Michigan's consultation process.
Windsor city council voted unanimously to support Masse's efforts to keep hazardous materials off the Ambassador Bridge. The city sent letters to Masse and federal Transport Minister Anita Anand expressing its concerns.
The federal and provincial governments both have legislation covering the conveyance of hazardous materials. For Transport Canada, it's the Transportation of Dangerous Goods Act and Regulations. At the provincial level, it's the Dangerous Goods Transportation Act.
But both levels of government say the Ambassador Bridge issue is the other's responsibility, and also the City of Windsor's.
Transport Canada told the Star it regulates importation, transportation, and handling of hazardous materials, "but not the roads or bridges over which they travel, including international border crossings."
The federal agency said in an email that provinces and municipalities are responsible for restrictions on which roads dangerous goods can travel on in their jurisdictions.
Kusmierczyk said he reached out to Transport Canada and was told the same thing. He said the province regulates what can travel on roads and bridges, and the federal government dictates how it must be transported.
"I know the community doesn't want to see hazmat on the Ambassador Bridge, so I continue to push Transport Canada officials to find what levers we do have on this issue," he said. "But as it stands now, this is a jurisdiction responsibility of the provinces and municipalities."
In a one-sentence response to several questions about the issue, Ontario's Ministry of Transportation told the Star it's a federal responsibility.
"The transportation of hazardous Materials are regulated Canada-wide by Transport Canada under the federal Transportation of Dangerous Goods Act and regulations," the ministry said in an email without further explanation.
Local MPP Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh) was unavailable for an interview. Over several emails, he would not state whether he opposes the upcoming change. He stated that hazardous materials on the Ambassador Bridge are under federal authority.
"Once again, we are witness to the federal government abdicating its clear responsibilities to the people of Windsor and deflecting its responsibilities to others," Dowie stated.
"The government of Canada has been empowered by the parliament of Canada to determine whether hazardous materials can be transported on the Ambassador Bridge."
Dowie also wrote that the city could enact bylaws to restrict hazardous materials on municipal roads.
The city said last month it is exploring that option. But officials this week did not provide the Star with an update on that plan.
Related
'Wrong decision, wrong time' - Windsor safety concerns as Michigan allows hazmat to cross Ambassador Bridge
'Disheartened' - Windsor mayor, fire chief oppose hazardous materials on Ambassador Bridge
Mayor Drew Dilkens also did not respond to a request for comment, but he recently told the Star that Transport Canada's claim it has no control over dangerous goods crossing the bridge is "really hard to believe" and "unconscionable."
In an email to the Star, the city said Thursday it acknowledges that transporting hazardous materials on local roads is nothing new, and necessary to support economic supply chains.
"However, a significant incident on the bridge may create additional challenges," the city said. "The federal government has authority over the bridge, and while Windsor Fire will always respond to an incident to the best of its ability, it will be incumbent upon the federal government to ensure a proper response."
twilhelm@postmedia.com

Subject: Municipal government; Fires; Federal government; Hazardous materials

Location: United States--US; Michigan; Canada

Company / organization: Name: Transport Canada; NAICS: 926120

Document 297 of 500

Ford's tunnel plan a desperate way to change channel

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-tunnel-plan-desperate-way-change-channel/docview/3115605651/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+tunnel+plan+a+desperate+way+to+change+channel&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Changing the channel is a pretty familiar gambit. Predictable, even.
When a public figure lands in the hot seat, expect them to schedule a funding announcement, unveil a big new plan or identify a threat; anything to change focus.
Remember when Donald Trump tried to "buy" Greenland? Six days before that story broke, a whistleblower filed a complaint detailing how the former U.S. president illegally pressured Ukraine to investigate his political rival, Joe Biden. By Christmas, the U.S. Congress would vote to impeach Trump.
News outlets reported on Greenland's geopolitical importance, the potential for mineral extraction beneath its melting ice and the consternation of the Danish prime minister, who seemed astonished to have to say: "Greenland is not for sale."
By now, you'd think we'd be wise to this time-worn tactic.
Yet here we are, reporting on a mega-tunnel under the 401.
Not that we have much choice. Premier Doug Ford has proposed - indeed, promised - to tunnel under the busiest highway in North America. Like it or not, it's news. Ford suggested new vehicle lanes and transit could stretch from Mississauga to Scarborough, about 55 kilometres.
In quick succession, the premier announced both a feasibility study, and a determination to forge ahead regardless of what the study might say. This is a man desperate to change the channel.
Still, it must be reported.
Journalists are diligently putting Ford's "visionary" promise in perspective. They've plumbed comparisons to Boston's Big Dig, which delivered 12 km of underground highway for $14.8 billion. They've reported the reactions of other party leaders, from "silly" to "a complete joke."
They've called upon experts to test Ford's assumption that adding vehicle lanes will, in fact, relieve traffic congestion.
The argument for more and bigger highways is an enduring zombie of public policy, repeatedly shot down but very hard to kill. Former New York City traffic commissioner Sam Schwartz told CBC News what study after study has shown: "the more lanes you introduce, the more people are likely to drive."
Schwartz described proven alternatives, such as transit investments and high-occupancy toll lanes, which relieve congestion by reducing traffic. Even building transit down the centre of the 401 would make more sense than what Ford's proposing.
Meanwhile, away from the cameras, the RCMP continues its criminal investigation into the Greenbelt scandal.
Two provincial watchdogs already criticized the government's improperly managed site selection process, which stood to enrich a handful of developers to the tune of $8.28 billion. The premier's office has denied any involvement in choosing specific sites for development. Ultimately, Ford reversed course, leaving the Greenbelt intact.
Over the summer, the government made headlines, including funding announcements, alcohol in corner stores, a retreat on supervised consumption sites and shuttering the Ontario Science Centre. Through it all, the media reported on hints of an early election, perhaps next spring. Ontario could go to the polls ahead of the results of what is a criminal investigation. This government has no business contemplating an election before voters have answers.
A mega-tunnel under the 401 may strike voters as decisive, out-of-the-box thinking - or a boondoggle in the making. It's actually something else.
This is our Greenland, folks.
Robin Baranyai's columns are regularly featured in Postmedia News publications. She can be reached at write. robin@baranyai.ca.

Subject: Voters; Roads & highways

Location: United States--US; Greenland

Identifier / keyword: changing; channel; pretty; familiar; gambit; predictable

Document 298 of 500

Mendicino warns of 'kangaroo court'; It's time to 'clear the air'on allegations of traitorous mps, former minister tells inquiry

Author: Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mendicino-warns-kangaroo-court-time-clear-airon/docview/3115605327/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mendicino+warns+of+%27kangaroo+court%27%3B+It%27s+time+to+%27clear+the+air%27on+allegations+of+traitorous+mps%2C+former+minister+tells+inquiry&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former public safety minister Marco Mendicino says there is a need to "clear the air" about allegations some MPs are wittingly participating in foreign interference in Canadian elections because the discourse about the claims is devolving into a "kangaroo court."
Testifying at the Public Inquiry into Foreign Interference (PIFI) Thursday, the MP and former Liberal minister said he was concerned about the discourse around the alleged presence of treasonous current or former MPs, as detailed by an intelligence review agency report earlier this year.
In June, the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP) reported that some MPs were "wittingly assisting" foreign state actors.
The report noted unnamed MPs had provided "privileged information" to foreign influence actors or had responded to "requests or direction of foreign officials to improperly influence" Parliament to the benefit of a foreign state.
The shocking revelations have since sparked calls for the government to reveal the identity of the alleged "treasonous" parliamentarians, which the Liberals have so far resisted.
During his testimony, Mendicino was told by counsel for NDP MP Jenny Kwan that she has since been labelled a traitor by some individuals simply for being a parliamentarian of Chinese descent. That, despite the fact she is a frequent critic of the Chinese government.
Mendicino responded that the NSICOP report had gone "further" than what CSIS or the prime minister's national security and intelligence adviser had assessed when reading the intelligence the review body's report was based on.
Earlier this week, current national security and intelligence adviser Nathalie Drouin said she had seen no evidence there are "traitors" in Parliament and that some of the conclusions in the NSICOP report made her "very uncomfortable."
Mendicino said he believes there is a need to "clear the air" about NSICOP's findings.
"I am very worried that the entire conversation around foreign interference and parliamentarians is being transformed into a kangaroo court with very little regard for the process of understanding how we assess intelligence," he told the inquiry.
"I think it is extremely important that we heed the opinion and the evidence that has been given to this commission, from Ms. Drouin, from CSIS, around the fact that ... this NSICOP report has gone further than where they are at in the assessment of the intelligence," he added.
During his testimony, Mendicino also brushed aside a claim by Conservative MP Michael Chong that Canada has become a "playground" for foreign interference.
"I fundamentally disagree with that characterization," Mendicino told the inquiry.
Canada is "an obstacle course for foreign interference" since the government enacted a series of new measures and laws to fight it in the last year, he added.
Mendicino was public safety minister from 2021 to 2023.
The inquiry has also spent considerable time discussing the fact that a CSIS warrant authorization allegedly targeting Liberal power broker and former Ontario cabinet minister Michael Chan sat in the office of Bill Blair - Mendicino's predecessor in the public safety portfolio - for 54 days before it was signed.
Without speaking to that warrant directly, Mendicino told the inquiry that he reviewed and decided on warrant authorization requests quickly every time one was brought to his attention.
"There were no undue delays in the approvals of warrants during my tenure as public safety minister," Mendicino said.
CSIS officials have previously testified that the average turnaround for a warrant authorization to be signed after it was sent to minister's office for approval was four to 10 days.
National Post cnardi@postmedia.com

Subject: Public safety; National security

Location: Canada

Document 299 of 500

Guest column: Questionable fairness when expropriating prime farms for industry

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-questionable-fairness-when/docview/3112767778/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+Questionable+fairness+when+expropriating+prime+farms+for+industry&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Robin Baranyai
Expropriation is a drastic measure. In acquiring land for public benefit, even when property owners are well-compensated, it should be a tool of last resort.
When negotiations do break down and governments forge ahead, we expect them to demonstrate their acquisitions are driven by reasonable necessity, and not unreasonable haste.
Municipal authority to expropriate land is granted by the province. People grudgingly abide such power when exercised with restraint. Most folks can understand the necessity of siting a new hospital in a central location, or the commitment to reroute rail lines away from Lac-Megantic.
It's a different story when governments threaten to expropriate protected farmland, paving over crops and bulldozing barns, not for critical infrastructure or public safety, but to attract industry.
This is the threat facing Wilmot Township, on the outskirts of Kitchener, where the Region of Waterloo is moving to acquire about 312 hectares (770 acres) of prime agricultural farmland to rezone for industrial use. There is no particular buyer waiting in the wings, nor any clue what type of industry might situate itself amid the local cheese makers and rolling farmlands striped with corn and cabbage.
The initiative is a response to Premier Doug Ford's call for "shovel-ready" land to attract investors, who prize quick building approvals and easy access to services such as water and gas.
In March, several landowners were approached with an offer through a private-sector land consultant. They say they were told if they did not negotiate, their land would be expropriated, and they should not bother planting crops because the deal would be done by August. (It was not.)
Efforts in Wilmot have proceeded with a singular lack of transparency. The province says it is providing funding but not involved in negotiations. Even Ford's own ministers have criticized the threat of expropriation so early in discussions.
The premier has made faster approvals for housing and infrastructure a signature policy initiative, with legislation to build transit faster (ditching expropriation hearings), build more homes (eliminating third-party appeals), and the Get It Done Act, which allows expropriation of land before environmental assessments are completed.
"We are fully aware the province is driving the bus," says Alfred Lowrick, a retired food executive who now leads the grassroots group Fight for Farmland In a recent interview, he relayed concerns about the lack of public engagement, and development beyond Countryside Line, contrary to the region's own official plan.
"The Countryside Line is basically our Greenbelt in this area," he says. "You don't have encroachment of developers buying these lands for future use."
Lowrick also questions the basic fairness of purchasing land at dramatically lower agricultural prices for the benefit of a for-profit industry, a difference of hundreds of thousands per hectare. No expropriation notices have been served to date.
In April, Ford said the province was looking for willing participants. "A lot of smaller towns, like for instance Wilmot, they need money. So what better way than clear some land and create some development?"
Lowrick says town councillors don't feel they have enough information about potential land use to declare the township an unwilling host. By the time an investor is identified, he worries, it may be too late.
Industry may indeed be good for small towns expecting population growth. However, it's not clear factories need to sit on prime agricultural farmland for any better reason than the premier is in a hurry.
"The Ford government is going to do what the Ford government does," Lowrick says with some resignation.
"We're looking at this as ground zero. If it can happen here, it can happen anywhere."
Robin Baranyai's columns are regularly featured in Postmedia News publications. She can be reached at write.robin@baranyai.ca( mailto:write.robin@baranyai.ca ).
