Document 300 of 500

Focus on adoption instead: poilievre; Abortion issue; new position in written response to activist group

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/focus-on-adoption-instead-poilievre-abortion/docview/3112645999/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Focus+on+adoption+instead%3A+poilievre%3B+Abortion+issue%3B+new+position+in+written+response+to+activist+group&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Focusing on legislation that promotes adoption would do "greater good" to help pregnant women than restricting access to abortion, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says in a new letter.
His office outlined the leader's position in correspondence sent to an anti-abortion supporter, who wrote the leader in early September calling on him to ban the procedure, suggesting Poilievre has changed his stance from being a "pro-lifer" over his 20-year career as a member of Parliament.
Campaign Life Coalition, the anti-abortion organization that shared the exchange with the National Post, points to how Poilievre voted against gay marriage in 2005, but, by 2020, said he felt it had been a success.
"While my government will not introduce or pass a law banning abortions, there are many other ways to support women who want to bring a child into the world. I do not believe that abortion should be the only option available to women faced with an unexpected pregnancy," Poilievre's letter from Sept. 11 reads.
"We can ease the challenges of putting a child up for adoption by supporting women through all nine months of pregnancy ... I believe focusing on bills that promote adoption and help pregnant women through crises would do greater good."
Asked how a Poilievre government would make adoption easier, spokesman Sebastian Skamski did not provide details but instead slammed how the Liberals voted against a private member's bill proposed by Saskatchewan MP Rosemarie Falk, which sought to create a 15-week employment insurance benefit for adoptive parents.
The Liberals ultimately introduced their own measure, which Falk herself told the House of Commons back in April provided "cause for cautious optimism."
Poilievre had supported her proposal and released a video where he reflected on his own adoption back in 1979, calling it "the greatest gift I've ever been given."
Abortion has been among the thorniest social issues Poilievre has had to navigate since becoming leader, one that has also dogged leaders before him.
When Poilie vre considered running for the Conservative leadership the first time in 2020, he said any future government of his would not reopen the issue, but would maintain free votes for MPs on matters of conscience.
Poilievre also confirmed during the party's 2022 leadership race that he considers himself to be "prochoice."
During that race, a leaked draft of a yet-to-be-made public U.S. Supreme Court decision overturning Roe v. Wade thrust the issue into the spotlight, which Poilievre responded to by saying that a future government of his would not introduce or pass any laws restricting abortion.
His wife, Anaida Poilievre, who is also campaigning for him, said during a French-language interview aired last December that the couple was "pro-choice."
The Liberals still point to the voting record and statements made by Poilievre's MPs, as the party has long been home to a well-mobilized social conservative wing, who believe the country must restrict access to abortion.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals have long attacked the Conservatives for having such views, including after a report last week that Saskatchewan MP Jeremy Patzer visited a U.S. church with anti-abortion views.
Poilievre's office was also quick to distance itself from statements made by Alberta Conservative MP Arnold Viersen, who appeared on a Liberal MP's podcast to say he hopes for the day abortion is "unthinkable," and if given the chance again would vote down gay marriage.
The Liberals also attacked a private member's bill proposed by Saskatchewan MP Cathy Wagantall, which was defeated last year, that proposed to make it an aggravating offence for someone to knowingly harm a pregnant women - a measure that proponents of abortion access warned opened the door for legislating more protections to a fetus, though Conservatives argued the bill didn't even mention abortion.
In his September letter to the anti-abortion supporter, Poilievre said those who oppose the procedure have a home with the Conservatives.
"Pro-life Canadians are welcome in our party. The Conservative Party of Canada is a big tent with a strong tradition of free votes of conscience, and I intend to maintain this status quo," it read.
The letter also added that Conservatives oppose the Liberals'pledge to strip crisis pregnancy centres of their charitable status. Advocates of abortion access say many of them spread false and misleading information about the procedure.
Poilievre pledged to "stand up" to government attacks on "organizations that help pregnant women."
Skamski said that for Poilievre, as the country's possible next prime minister, "no laws or rules will be passed that restrict women's reproductive choices. Period."
"Common-sense Conservatives support the value of adoption and supporting parents who provide a loving home to adopted children," he said. "Mr. Poilievre understands the power of adoption firsthand, having been born to a 16-year-old mother and adopted by two school teachers as a child."
He added that Canadians can expect a future Poilievre-led government to be one that "minds its own business," including when it comes to personal decisions about "their bodies."
Campaign Life Coalition president Jeff Gunnarson said in an interview he was "disappointed" by Poilievre's position on abortion, but said he felt his stance on adoption "useful."
The group came out against Poilievre during the 2022 leadership election, which he won with almost 70 per cent of the votes. The coalition instead asked supporters to vote for Ontario MP Leslyn Lewis, who was the only one of the five candidates to promise restrictions on abortion. She finished a distant third.
RightNow, another anti-abortion group, had encouraged the same. It also criticized the party for disqualifying a social conservative candidate during the nomination race for the safe Conservative riding of Oxford, Ont., last year, which was ultimately won by Arpan Khanna, now an MP who served as Poilievre's former national outreach coordinator.
Gunnarson questions whether Poilievre would make promoting adoption a high priority should he form the next government and whether he would consider dedicating money to the issue.
Despite supporters'disappointment with Poilievre, Gunnarson said many social conservatives are likely to still cast a ballot for the party on election day.
"If you don't promote (abortion) and you don't increase access and you don't put more money towards it, that's a positive," he said.
"The bar is so low in Canada, that's considered a good thing."
Poilievre's office did not answer when asked what the leader would do about federal funding for sexual health centres and services that promote education and access to abortion, as well as similar services offered overseas.
National Post staylor@postmedia.com

Subject: Conservatism; Pregnancy; Leadership; Adoption; Abortion; Same sex marriage; Prime ministers; Elections

Document 301 of 500

Workplace violence concerns grow: OFL; Affordability, health care, housing have 'folks on edge,'labour federation says

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/workplace-violence-concerns-grow-ofl/docview/3112645681/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Workplace+violence+concerns+grow%3A+OFL%3B+Affordability%2C+health+care%2C+housing+have+%27folks+on+edge%2C%27labour+federation+says&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The daily threat of violence at work is a growing fear for many Windsorites as frustrations over affordability, health care, and other issues increasingly plague the community, according to Canada's largest labour federation.
"These are really tough times for folks," said Laura Walton, president of the Ontario Federation of Labour (OFL). "We're hearing about affordability. We're hearing that with the concerns about housing.
"People are on edge right now.
And, unfortunately, when folks are on edge, it often plays out in violent behaviour."
The OFL is in the midst of an 11-city town hall tour to gauge the concerns and priorities of people across Ontario, including unionized and non-unionized workers, retirees, and community activists.
The OFL made a stop Tuesday night at the Caribbean Centre in Windsor. The tour is partly about seeking what people want ahead of a possible snap election. Premier Doug Ford has hinted at an early election call in 2025.
But Walton said it's also an effort to see how people are getting by, and what they need, after the hardest years of COVID-19.
"We recognized it's been four years of the pandemic," she said. "Folks are feeling a little bit more disconnected than we have traditionally seen. So we wanted to get out into communities and be talking to workers."
Overcrowded classrooms, health care privatization, hospital wait times, the cost of living and housing affordability are causing anxieties across Ontario, including Windsor, according to the OFL.
"We know people are not OK, and they're not happy," said OFL executive vice-president Jackie Taylor. "They want to see change. They want to see the Ontario that they know. And they want to be empowered on how to do that."
Walton said another recurring concern has been the sudden closure and prohibition of safe consumption sites, including Windsor's SafePoint.
Temporarily funded by the Windsor-Essex County Health Unit, SafePoint opened in April 2023 without provincial approvals. It closed Jan. 1 after the province continued to refuse funding.
Any hope of reviving SafePoint died in August when the province announced it would impose strict rules on existing safe consumption sites and prohibit the opening of new ones.
"People are very concerned about how that is being dealt with and how it impacts some of the most vulnerable in our communities," said Walton.
OFL representatives said they're hearing at town halls that the snowballing collection of fears and frustrations is manifesting in violent outbursts and safety concerns.
"Workplace violence is a huge issue because when people leave their homes and their families, they expect to return back home," said Taylor. "Our teachers have been under a lot of pressure because of the amount of violence happening in the classrooms and that's not OK."
Walton said it's spreading beyond classrooms to jobs where such concerns were once rare, if not non-existent.
"I'm an education worker, so I expect that I am going to hear about violence in schools," she said. "But what we're starting to hear is violence is now creeping into so many other places, including health care, retail, social services.
"It's something that I think we need to turn our minds to. How are we keeping folks safe?" twilhelm@postmedia.com

Subject: Classrooms; Workplace violence

Business indexing term: Subject: Workplace violence

'People on edge' - Windsor workplace violence a growing concern, says Ontario Labour Federation

Document 303 of 500

Walpole Island partners on $180M battery storage facility near Tilbury

Author: Shreve, Ellwood

Publication info: Shreve, Ellwood. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/walpole-island-partners-on-180m-battery-storage/docview/3112310170/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Walpole+Island+partners+on+%24180M+battery+storage+facility+near+Tilbury&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Shreve%2C+Ellwood&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Walpole Island First Nation is teaming up with renewable energy giant Boralex to build a new battery storage facility in Lakeshore expected to create up to 150 local construction jobs.
The Tilbury battery storage project, which will store 80 megawatts of power - enough to power 80,000 homes - is estimated to be completed late next year at a cost of $180 million to $200 million.
The battery storage project was announced by company, First Nation and provincial government representatives at a Friday news conference just outside Tilbury in Lakeshore.
Construction will create as many as 150 jobs, but when the facility is operational, it will only require three to four people to operate, a Boralex official said.
"Battery storage is a critical component to support the responsible expansion of Ontario's clean and reliable grid that will provide affordable energy to Ontario families and businesses," said Stephen Lecce, minister of energy and electrification.
The Independent Electricity System Operator (IESO) has indicated Ontario will need at least 60 per cent more energy in the next 25 years.
To support that demand, battery storage facilities will enable generated clean power to be stored and deployed when needed, advancing Ontario's clean energy advantage.
Lecce congratulated Boralex and the Walpole Island community "in reaching this milestone achievement which, once connected to the grid, will attract new investments and reduce energy costs for Ontario's ratepayers."
Leela Thomas, the First Nation's newly elected chief, said, "It's nice that we're able to partner on an initiative like this."
She added it's great to see a company like Boralex commit to work with Walpole Island.
With hundreds of wind turbines in Chatham-Kent, Thomas said Walpole is "always open to more discussions on more projects down the road."
There will be more battery storage projects, Lecce said. The province just launched the largest competitive procurement in Canadian history for 5,000 megawatts of power, which will include commitments for additional storage capability.
But future commitments for clean energy projects will be with willing host communities, willing community partners and with the participation and support of local Indigenous partnerships.
Noting the former Liberal government imposed wind turbine projects on communities, willing or not, Lecce vowed: "We will never impose a project without a municipal support resolution."
Willing partnerships create winwin outcomes that provide local energy capacity when needed, but also support a cleaner and more affordable power grid for Ontario families and farmers, he said.
"It's cheaper to save energy than it is to generate it," Lecce said.
The former Liberal government that procured contracts at 10 times above the market rate, "which we're still paying for today, we're procuring on price," Lecce said.
MPP Trevor Jones (PC-Chatham-Kent-Leamington) said, "Our government is working hard to ensure communities have reliable, affordable, clean power to support families and our critical farming, food processing and manufacturing sectors here."
This important investment by Boralex and Walpole Island "will improve reliability and strengthen our supply chains, particularly during peak demand periods like our harvest season," he added.
Boralex is "ecstatic" to have reached this milestone for the Tilbury Battery Storage project, said Adam Rosso, senior vice-president of development in North America.
It's a "testament to our team's skill, dedication, and hard work," he added.
With equity partner Walpole Island, Boralex is "proud to contribute to Ontario's clean energy future by providing reliable and stable capacity," Rosso said.
"This project, alongside our other battery storage initiatives, solidifies Boralex's leadership in utility-scale energy storage," he added.
Boralex has a 300-megawatt battery storage facility being built in Hagersville, near Hamilton, expected to be operational next year.
The company announced in May it is building a 125-megawatt facility in South-West Oxford projected to be up and running by 2027 or 2028. It's a 50 per cent partnership with Six Nations of the Grand River Development Corp.
The Quebec-based company, which has been operating in Ontario for nearly 15 years, also has wind farms in the Niagara and Chatham-Kent regions.
Rosso thanked Lecce and the government for their "commitment to Ontario's growing energy needs and look forward to further collaborations." With files from Brian Williams, Local Journalism Initiative eshreve@postmedia.com
BY THE NUMBERS - Tilbury Battery Storage will store 80 megawatts of power. Boralex is building 380 MW of energy storage facilities in Ontario - The IESO indicated 60 per cent more power is needed by 2050. - In spring 2024, Ontario secured nearly 3,000 MW of battery storage under the largest battery procurement framework in the nation's history Source: Ontario Ministry of Energy and Electrification

Subject: Energy storage; Electricity distribution; Clean technology; Initiatives

Location: Walpole Island

Identifier / keyword: walpole; island; first; nation; teaming; renewable

Document 304 of 500

Walpole Island partners on $180M battery storage facility in Lakeshore

Author: Shreve, Ellwood

Publication info: Shreve, Ellwood. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/walpole-island-partners-on-180m-battery-storage/docview/3112044161/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Walpole+Island+partners+on+%24180M+battery+storage+facility+in+Lakeshore&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Shreve%2C+Ellwood&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Walpole Island First Nation is teaming with renewable energy giant Boralex to build a new battery storage facility in Lakeshore expected to create up to 150 local construction jobs.
The Tilbury Battery Storage project, which will store 80 megawatts of power, enough to power 80,000 homes, is estimated to be completed late next year at a cost of $180 million to $200 million.
The project was announced by company, First Nation and provincial government representatives at a Friday news conference just outside Tilbury in Lakeshore.

The Independent Electricity System Operator (IESO) has indicated Ontario will need at least 60 per cent more energy in the next 25 years. To support that demand, battery storage facilities will enable generated clean power to be stored and deployed when needed, advancing Ontario's clean energy advantage.
Lecce congratulated Boralex and the Walpole Island community "in reaching this milestone achievement which, once connected to the grid, will attract new investments and reduce energy costs for Ontario's ratepayers."

With hundreds of wind turbines in Chatham-Kent, Thomas said Walpole is "always open to more discussions on more projects down the road."
There will be more battery storage projects in future, Lecce said. The province just launched the largest competitive procurement in Canadian history for 5,000 megawatts of power, which will include commitments for additional storage capability.
But future commitments for clean energy projects will be with willing host communities, willing community partners and with the participation and support of local Indigenous partnerships.
Noting the former Liberal government imposed wind turbine projects on communities, willing or not, Lecce vowed: "We will never impose a project without a municipal support resolution."
Willing partnerships create win-win outcomes that provide local energy capacity when needed, but also support a cleaner and more affordable power grid for Ontario families and farmers, he said.
"It's cheaper to save energy than it is to generate it," Lecce said.
The former Liberal government that procured contracts at 10 times above the market rate, "which we're still paying for today, we're procuring on price," Lecce said.
MPP Trevor Jones (PC-Chatham-Kent-Leamington) said, "Our government is working hard to ensure communities have reliable, affordable, clean power to support families and our critical farming, food processing and manufacturing sectors here."
This important investment by Boralex and Walpole Island "will improve reliability and strengthen our supply chains, particularly during peak demand periods like our harvest season," he added.
Boralex is "ecstatic" to have reached this milestone for the Tilbury Battery Storage project, said Adam Rosso, senior vice-president of development in North America.
It's a "testament to our team's skill, dedication, and hard work," he added.
With equity partner Walpole Island, Boralex is "proud to contribute to Ontario's clean energy future by providing reliable and stable capacity," Rosso said.
"This project, alongside our other battery storage initiatives, solidifies Boralex's leadership in utility-scale energy storage," he added.
Boralex has a 300-megawatt battery storage project underway in Hagersville, near Hamilton, expected to be operational next year.
The company announced in May it is building a 125-megawatt facility in South-West Oxford projected to be up and running by 2027 or 2028. It's a 50 per cent partnership with Six Nations of the Grand River Development Corp.
The Quebec-based company, which has been operating in Ontario for nearly 15 years, also has wind farms in the Niagara and Chatham-Kent regions.
Rosso thanked Lecce and the government for their "commitment to Ontario's growing energy needs and look forward to further collaborations."
With files from Brian Williams, Local Journalism Initiative
eshreve@postmedia.com
BY THE NUMBERS
Tilbury Battery Storage will store 80 megawatts of power.
Boralex is building 380 MW of energy storage facilities in Ontario
The IESO indicated 60 per cent more power is needed by 2050.
In spring 2024, Ontario secured nearly 3,000 MW of battery storage under the largest battery procurement framework in the nation's history
Source: Ontario Ministry of Energy and Electrification

Document 305 of 500

Reconciliation events aim to help healing process; National day also addresses legacy of residential schools on victims

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reconciliation-events-aim-help-healing-process/docview/3111718807/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reconciliation+events+aim+to+help+healing+process%3B+National+day+also+addresses+legacy+of+residential+schools+on+victims&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At the University of Windsor campus on Monday, Walpole Island First Nation artist Teresa Altiman showed that National Day for Truth and Reconciliation is about reflection - and also creativity.
Altiman gave a presentation to a crowd of about 75 people before observing the raising of an Orange Shirt Day flag outside Chrysler Hall Tower.
The focus was her recent art installation - an eight-and-a-halffoot snapping turtle sculpture.
"My heart is just pounding and so happy to see this sculpture," said Altiman. "All of you have creativity within you."
Altiman guided the crowd to place their right hand on top of their left, demonstrating what inspired the imagery of a snapping turtle gently resting in a palm. The towering installation is a symbol of Turtle Island, central to the Anishnaabe people's creation story.
Altiman's design was executed by Windsor-based artist and sculptor, Zeke Moores.
"The University of Windsor is grateful to our Indigenous and non-Indigenous colleagues who have made this art installation possible," said University of Windsor vice-president and provost Robert Aguirre, who attended Monday morning's event.
Undeterred by the rain on Saturday at King's Navy Yard Park in Amherstburg, about 50 people huddled under umbrellas enjoyed a morning of Indigenous storytelling, drum ceremonies and song.
The gathering was one of several events held in Windsor-Essex County over the weekend to mark National Day for Truth and Reconciliation.
"This rain is about healing," Faith Hale, executive director of Ska:na Family Learning Centre, broadcasted through a microphone to the group as raindrops continued to fall around them.
National Day for Truth and Reconciliation, observed every year in Canada on Sept. 30, is not only an opportunity to heal, but to also remember the dark legacy of residential schools and their lasting impact on Indigenous communities.
From the late 1800s to 1996, roughly 150,000 Indigenous children attended these institutions across Canada, suffering cultural erasure and sometimes abuse.
With help from ground-penetrating radar, more than 1,800 possible unmarked graves have been found at the sites of former residential schools since 2021. The discovery fuelled calls for the Canadian government and the churches that operated the schools to take accountability.
"We have a long way to go," said Hale. "But I have to believe small communities and citizens of Canada really want to take up this space in a good way, they just don't know how. So we're here to teach and to show."
Theresa Sims, traditional knowledge keeper and Windsor's inaugural Indigenous storyteller, said she often cried when her mother, a survivor of the Mohawk Institute, recounted her time in the institution from age five.
For Sims, storytelling, a key part of knowledge sharing in Indigenous culture, has allowed her to give voice to her mother's experience.
"She said she would try to make herself look as ugly as possible, because the teachers and the ministers would walk through the wards looking for the pretty ones," said Sims.
"But the next day, they'd be in tears, crying, but they'd have a lollipop, because they would abuse them, sexually assault them, then put them back to bed with a lollipop."
Amherstburg deputy mayor Chris Gibb and councillor Donald McArthur also joined the event.
"I think everybody plays their small part," McArthur told the Star. "If enough of us play that part and do what we can to help reconciliation, all those small contributions add up, and you have an entire country that works with our Indigenous people."
While Windsor-Essex County was not the site of a residential school, the closest was Brantford's Mohawk Institute, one of the oldest and longest-running institutions in Canada.
Premier Doug Ford and Greg Rickford, minister of Indigenous Affairs, released an emailed statement Monday morning in recognition of the National Day for Truth and Reconciliation.
"The painful legacy of Indian Residential Schools has resulted in ongoing, intergenerational trauma for survivors, their families and Indigenous communities," the statement read.
"As Ontarians, it is important that we continue to deepen our collective understanding, to honour those who survived, and remember the thousands of children who did not make it home."
The weekend offered many more opportunities to learn about the region's Indigenous history, as well as the ongoing fight for missing and murdered Indigenous women.
As the original stewards of the land, and key players in events such as the War of 1812, Indigenous communities have contributed to, and continue to shape the area and the country. mmazak@postmedia.com

Subject: Installation art; Schools; Murders & murder attempts; Reconciliation

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: University of Windsor; NAICS: 611310

Identifier / keyword: university; windsor; campus; walpole; island; first

Reader letter: Premier Ford - MRI, CT scan wait lists no joking matter

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/reader-letter-premier-ford-mri-ct-scan-wait-lists/docview/3111429894/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reader+letter%3A+Premier+Ford+-+MRI%2C+CT+scan+wait+lists+no+joking+matter&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's distasteful joke about sending patients waiting for an MRI or CT scan to a new animal hospital - sad to say is a reality in Ontario due to a shortage of machines.
However, due to Ford's position as Ontario premier, he'll definitely receive special hospital treatment, if needed, while a good friend of mine has waited over eight months for an MRI to diagnose a painful back problem.
It's not a joke, Doug Ford, when you spend millions putting alcohol in convenience stores but short-change our hospitals' budgets and Ontario's health care system.
Peter J Middlemore Sr.
Windsor

Document 308 of 500

Farmland should be prioritized over industry; Bulldozing barns to attract investors short-sighted, writes Robin Baranyai

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/farmland-should-be-prioritized-over-industry/docview/3110861343/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Farmland+should+be+prioritized+over+industry%3B+Bulldozing+barns+to+attract+investors+short-sighted%2C+writes+Robin+Baranyai&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Expropriation is a drastic measure. In acquiring land for public benefit, even when property owners are well-compensated, it should be a tool of last resort.
When negotiations do break down and governments forge ahead, we expect them to demonstrate their acquisitions are driven by reasonable necessity, and not unreasonable haste.
Municipal authority to expropriate land is granted by the province. People grudgingly abide such power when exercised with restraint. Most folks can understand the necessity of siting a new hospital in a central location, or the commitment to reroute rail lines away from Lac-Megantic.
It's a different story when governments threaten to expropriate protected farmland, paving over crops and bulldozing barns, not for critical infrastructure or public safety, but to attract industry.
This is the threat facing Wilmot Township, on the outskirts of Kitchener, where the Region of Waterloo is moving to acquire about 312 hectares (770 acres) of prime agricultural farmland to rezone for industrial use. There is no particular buyer waiting in the wings, nor any clue what type of industry might situate itself amid the local cheese makers and rolling farmlands striped with corn and cabbage.
The initiative is a response to Premier Doug Ford's call for "shovel-ready" land to attract investors, who prize quick building approvals and easy access to services such as water and gas.
In March, several landowners were approached with an offer through a private-sector land consultant. They say they were told if they did not negotiate, their land would be expropriated, and they should not bother planting crops because the deal would be done by August. (It was not.) Efforts in Wilmot have proceeded with a singular lack of transparency. The province says it is providing funding but not involved in negotiations. Even Ford's own ministers have criticized the threat of expropriation so early in discussions.
The premier has made faster approvals for housing and infrastructure a signature policy initiative, with legislation to build transit faster (ditching expropriation hearings), build more homes (eliminating third-party appeals), and the Get It Done Act, which allows expropriation of land before environmental assessments are completed.
"We are fully aware the province is driving the bus," says Alfred Lowrick, a retired food executive who now leads the grassroots group Fight for Farmland. In a recent interview, he relayed concerns about the lack of public engagement, and development beyond Countryside Line, contrary to the region's own official plan.
"The Countryside Line is basically our Greenbelt in this area," he says. "You don't have encroachment of developers buying these lands for future use."
Lowrick also questions the basic fairness of purchasing land at dramatically lower agricultural prices for the benefit of a for-profit industry, a difference of hundreds of thousands per hectare. No expropriation notices have been served to date.
In April, Ford said the province was looking for willing participants. "A lot of smaller towns, like for instance Wilmot, they need money. So what better way than clear some land and create some development?" Lowrick says town councillors don't feel they have enough information about potential land use to declare the township an unwilling host. By the time an investor is identified, he worries, it may be too late.
Industry may indeed be good for small towns expecting population growth. However, it's not clear factories need to sit on prime agricultural farmland for any better reason than the premier is in a hurry.
"The Ford government is going to do what the Ford government does," Lowrick says with some resignation.
"We're looking at this as ground zero. If it can happen here, it can happen anywhere." Robin Baranyai's columns are regularly featured in Postmedia News publications. She can be reached at write.robin@baranyai.ca.

Identifier / keyword: expropriation; drastic; measure; acquiring; public; benefit

Title: Farmland should be prioritized over industry; Bulldozing barns to attract investors short-sighted, writes Robin Baranyai

Publication title: The Windsor Star; Windsor, Ont.

First page: A.8

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 28, 2024

Section: Opinion

Document 309 of 500

'Vital role:' Ontario earmarks $1.5M to attract, retain international agri-food workers

Author: Holmes-Hill, Millar

Publication info: Holmes-Hill, Millar. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/vital-role-ontario-earmarks-1-5m-attract-retain/docview/3110749027/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Vital+role%3A%27+Ontario+earmarks+%241.5M+to+attract%2C+retain+international+agri-food+workers&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Holmes-Hill%2C+Millar&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal and provincial governments are investing $1.5-million over four years to enhance support for international agri-food workers in Ontario.
The funding, announced Friday at the South Essex Community Council in Kingsville, is aimed at creating a more welcoming environment by providing translation supports, expanding or introducing transportation services, and supporting cultural and recreational activities.
"International agri-food workers play a vital role in Ontario's agri-food sector, and we want them to feel engaged and supported," Minister of Agriculture, Food and Agribusiness Rob Flack said. "As the next cohort of workers arrives this growing season, our government is ensuring Ontario continues to attract and retain the skills and talent our growing agri-food sector will need."
Migrant workers are shown on a farm in Kingsville in this June 17, 2020, file photo.
MPP Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex) speaks about a provincial agri-worker program at the South Essex Community Council in Kingsville on Friday, Sept. 27, 2024.
The IAW Welcoming Communities Initiative builds on the Virtual Welcome Centre launched in 2023, which is an online resource for IAWs available in English, Spanish and French. It includes information about worker rights and responsibilities, adjusting to life in Canada, as well as living and working in the community.
"This funding will help us continue offering translation and transportation services and cultural and recreational activities - all of which provide essential services for the international agri-food workers," said Martin Varela, chair of the Migrant Worker Community Program in Leamington.
"These services are critical in ensuring that the agri-food workers feel understood and will also help them navigate through the daily challenges of being in a new environment.
"This service enriches the community and creates a more inclusive and compassionate society, especially for these workers who are vital to the economy of our region."
Related
Essex County farmers denounce as 'extreme' United Nations 'slavery' report
Kingsville migrant worker housing bylaw stays in place - for now
As migrant workers return, calls for improved farm, living conditions
The IAW Welcoming Communities Initiative intake will be open from October 8 to November 19, 2024. Applicants can receive up to 75 per cent in cost-share funding per application.
Eligible organizations and municipalities can apply to work with other organizations - faith-based, for-profit, and community health centres - that support the attraction and retention of international workers, improve their access to community services, and promote their independence.
mholmeshill@postmedia.com
South Essex Community Council executive director Carolyn Warkentin (left) talks with Ontario Minister of Agriculture, Food, and Agribusiness Rob Flack in Kingsville on Friday, Sept. 27, 2024.

Subject: Migrant workers; Transportation services; Food; Councils; Funding; Agribusiness

Business indexing term: Subject: Migrant workers Transportation services Agribusiness

Title: 'Vital role:' Ontario earmarks $1.5M to attract, retain international agri-food workers

Document 310 of 500

As victim count climbs, Windsor police unveil speedier intimate partner violence response plan

Author: MacLeod, Brian

Publication info: MacLeod, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/as-victim-count-climbs-windsor-police-unveil/docview/3110687318/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=As+victim+count+climbs%2C+Windsor+police+unveil+speedier+intimate+partner+violence+response+plan&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=MacLeod%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Windsor police are partnering with local social service agencies to provide faster support to victims of intimate partner violence.
Police will work with Windsor Regional Hospital, Hiatus House and Family Services of Windsor-Essex in a pilot initiative to "provide immediate and comprehensive support to victims of intimate partner violence," Windsor police Chief Jason Bellaire said at a press conference at police headquarters Thursday.
The initiative comes after a 7.1-per-cent increase in intimate partner violence through August of this year, Bellaire said.
Windsor police have responded to more than 2,500 intimate partner violence-related incidents so far this year, he said. Last year, city police responded to 3,400 incidents.
"We know that intimate partner violence remains widely an underreported problem," Bellaire said. "We also know that despite our best intentions, current efforts to address intimate partner violence across all sectors are simply not working."
The chief cited the tragic cases of Sahra Bulle, 36, whose body was found in a field in Windsor's west end in June 2023, and Janice Madison, 67, who was stabbed to death in Remington Park in November. Bulle's estranged husband, Michigan resident Brian Aaron, and Madison's husband, Wellington Holland, have both been charged with first-degree murder. Their cases are still before the courts.
In June, police announced that a new IPV Early Intervention Program launched a month earlier had referred 22 people for additional support. The new program is an extension of that initiative, Bellaire said.
Officers will work in real time with social workers to determine the best course of action as they respond to reports of intimate partner violence.
The idea is to ensure physical safety of victims, to separate them from immediate danger and to establish connections with victims to encourage them to seek help from social services.
"A lot can happen during that brief time between the actual incident of violence and the access to supports," Bellaire said. "Victims can be pressured to change their minds. Abusers can reoffend and children can be placed in harm's way."
Social workers dedicated to the program will work from 3 to 11 p.m., Wednesdays to Sundays, when about 50 per cent of IPV incidents occur. The program will be evaluated after three months.
Victims, however, have access to round-the-clock help through virtual services provided by Hiatus House, which provides crisis support and shelter for women and children fleeing violence and abuse.
As local social workers look on, Windsor police Chief Jason Bellaire speaks during a press conference on Thursday, September 26, 2024, announcing a pilot project to boost support to victims of intimate partner violence.
The initiative is a "significant strategic shift for the Windsor Police Service in our community on how we're responding to intimate partner violence," said Staff Sgt. Richard Sieberer, who is with the Windsor police Special Victims Unit and leading the pilot project.
The social workers who will respond to incidents with police have a "specialized skillset that is able to connect with these individuals and provide trauma support that ... our officers don't have the ability to do," Sieberer said.
Clara Holmes, director of mental health and counselling programs at Family Services Windsor-Essex, said social workers "will deliver psychological first aid while on scene with survivors."
That will help establish connections with victims to support services and assist with long-term recovery and resilience, she said.
Jonathan Foster, vice-president of emergency and mental health services at Windsor Regional Hospital, said the new initiative is an "innovate way to bring our services from the hospital to the community ... to provide services during the hours where the greatest need is."
Sylvie Guenther, executive director of Hiatus House, said after the tragic murder-suicide deaths of Carly Stannard-Walsh, 41, and her children Madison, 13, and Hunter, 8, from gunshots by Carly's husband Steven Walsh, 42, in June, many people came forward to report instances of intimate partner violence.
Windsor city council declared intimate partner violence a local epidemic in July 2023.
Related
Mom of slain Windsor woman starts foundation to fight domestic violence
NDP leader demands more Ontario funding for Windsor women's shelters
Mom of slain Windsor woman starts foundation to fight domestic violence
Guenther said the new initiative means "we're going to do something differently to help women and children and families throughout Windsor-Essex County.
"It's not always easy for people to talk about the experience of violence that's happening in their household," she said.
"Real-time intervention can play a role in helping someone, maybe for the first time with IPV."
In Ontario last year, at least 58 women were killed by an intimate partner who was either charged, convicted, or found responsible.
bamacleod@postmedia.com
With files by Madeline Mazak

Document 311 of 500

Candlelight tours return this fall to Fort Malden in A'Burg

Author: Kotsis, Julie

Publication info: Kotsis, Julie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/candlelight-tours-return-this-fall-fort-malden/docview/3110113372/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Candlelight+tours+return+this+fall+to+Fort+Malden+in+A%27Burg&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=SR.1&amp;au=Kotsis%2C+Julie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An opportunity to explore Fort Malden National Historic Site by candlelight on sunset tours is returning this fall for a limited time.
Costumed interpreters will lead guests through the waterfront grounds where soldiers once stood, through the barracks and along the Detroit River in Amherstburg.
Gripping accounts of early military history, legendary battles and untold stories from within the fort's walls will be shared.
Fort Malden was the second fort built in Amherstburg to defend Great Britain's hold against potential threat of American invasion.
The first fort on the site, Fort Amherstburg, was established near the mouth of the Detroit River in 1796.
It was the headquarters for the Right Division of the British Army in Upper Canada during the War of 1812 and was destroyed by the British when they were forced to retreat in September 1813.
Today, there are no visible remains of that earlier fortification.
The Americans occupied the ruins of Fort Amherstburg from October 1813 until July 1815 and began rebuilding the fort almost immediately, but their efforts were hampered by severe shortages of labour and supplies.
Some of the earthworks that can be seen today are remnants of the American occupation.
When the British reoccupied the site in the summer of 1815, they inherited a smaller, semi-completed earthen fortification with a few roughly built structures.
Fort Malden was also a British stronghold on the Detroit frontier during the Rebellions of 1837-1838, when soldiers fought off rebels and American sympathizers.
The end of the rebellion and subsequent improved relations with the Americans meant less need for a large military presence in the Detroit River area.
Plans for permanent improvements to the fort were shelved and the last regulars left Fort Malden in 1851. Retired soldiers were brought in to maintain the fort and farm the surrounding military reserve.
The Friends of Fort Malden and Parks Canada are co-sponsoring the candlelight tours.
Tours are being offered on Friday and Saturday evenings, Sept. 27 and 28, Oct. 4, 5, 18, 19, 25 and 26, at 7 and 8 p.m.
The cost is $20 by advance registration at fortmalden@pc.gc.ca.
The event is weather dependent and cancellations will be posted on Fort Malden's Facebook page.
Fort Malden is also open until Oct. 13, from 1 to 5 p.m., Wednesdays to Fridays and 10 a.m. to 5 p.m. on Saturdays and Sundays. jkotsis@postmedia.com twitter.com/KotsisStar

Subject: Military personnel; Military history

Location: Detroit River

Identifier / keyword: opportunity; explore; malden; national; historic; candlelight

Document 312 of 500

Guest column: The carbon tax and Canada's 'Hunger Games'

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-carbon-tax-canadas-hunger-games/docview/3109655664/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+The+carbon+tax+and+Canada%27s+%27Hunger+Games%27&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Sylvain Charlebois
Feed Ontario has just disclosed that over a million residents in Ontario, Canada's wealthiest province, sought assistance from food banks over the past year.
A million people.
That's essentially Nova Scotia's entire population as a province. This revelation presents a stark contrast when juxtaposed with the situation south of the border in the U. S.
In 2023, while 13.5 per cent of Americans households grappled with food insecurity - characterized by low or very low food security (USDA-ERS, 2024) - the rate in Canada was significantly higher at 22.9 per cent (Proof Toronto, 2024).
This suggests that food insecurity in Canada is a staggering 70 per cent more prevalent than in the U. S. - deeply unsettling.
The challenge of affording food in Canada is exacerbated by anemic food sales, particularly stark when compared to the United States, where grocery store sales increased by 1.8 per cent in the last 12 months, according to the Federal Reserve Economic Data.
In stark contrast, Canadian grocery store sales have plummeted by a worrying 3.2 per cent, according to Statistics Canada.
One plausible explanation for this disparity lies in the higher interest rates in Canada, which likely impose a heavier burden on Canadian households than on their American counterparts, given that the average debt per household is considerably higher in Canada.
The Bank of Canada's pathway to achieving a more stable inflation rate without detrimentally affecting Canadians appears much narrower than that of the U.S. Federal Reserve, evidenced by the harsh reality of 10 consecutive rate hikes between March 2022 and July 2024, compelling Canadians to economize, particularly on food expenditures.
It is becoming increasingly apparent that Canada's per capita economy is contracting, devoid of the wealth creation observed in the U. S. Notably, according to World Bank Data, in 2002, the United States' GDP per capita was 56.6 per cent higher than Canada's - a record high during the tenure of another Liberal prime minister, Jean Chrétien. The current gap, at 53 per cent, is perilously close to this historical peak.
The primary drivers of GDP growth in Canada are currently immigration and public spending, with the government undertaking the bulk of economic heavy lifting.
The situation is indeed dire.
Moreover, the enthusiasm once held for the economic greening championed by the Trudeau administration is showing signs of significant fatigue. The carbon tax, Trudeau's principal policy for fostering an eco-friendly economy, is increasingly losing traction due to the prevailing economic challenges. Dissenting voices from within the federal NDP and B.C.'s NDP government now challenge the carbon tax as the optimal path forward.
The failures of the carbon tax are emblematic of a broader trend in recent policymaking endeavours - the implementation of populist policies devoid of rigorous metrics for evaluating their success over time, all while constructing an expansive communications strategy designed to convince Canadians of the policy's merits.
The federal carbon tax is set to escalate to $95 per tonne by April 2025, marching inexorably towards the 2030 goal of $170 per tonne. Despite the mounting pressure, the Trudeau government has neglected to assess whether the policy meaningfully impacts our climate and emissions or to evaluate how the policy might influence the economy over time, with a particular focus on the ramifications for the agri-food sector - from farm to plate.
The design of the rebate system ostensibly allows Canadians to overlook the real costs of this poorly conceived policy.
However, administering this massive program is not only costly, but it also fails to leverage the Canadian economy effectively. It is imperative to devise policies that genuinely foster both economic and environmental sustainability for the nation.
Sylvain Charlebois is a professor and senior director of the Agri-Food Analytics Lab at Dalhousie University and co-host of The Food Professor podcast.

Subject: Food security; Canadians; Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Carbon; Environmental tax; Grocery stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Food security Gross Domestic Product--GDP Environmental tax Grocery stores

Location: United States--US; Canada

Identifier / keyword: sylvain; charlebois; ontario; disclosed; million; residents

Title: Guest column: The carbon tax and Canada's 'Hunger Games'

Publication date: Sep 25, 2024

Document 313 of 500

City puts heat on Ottawa to help with hazmat issue; Transport Canada says it does not have authority over bridge rules

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-puts-heat-on-ottawa-help-with-hazmat-issue/docview/3109554691/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+puts+heat+on+Ottawa+to+help+with+hazmat+issue%3B+Transport+Canada+says+it+does+not+have+authority+over+bridge+rules&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Michigan's decision to permit hazardous material shipments across the Ambassador Bridge - and Canada's apparent inaction on it - is causing a stir in the City of Windsor.
City council on Monday voted unanimously to support local MP Brian Masse's (NDP - Windsor West) efforts to keep hazmat materials off the 95-year-old bridge between Windsor and Detroit.
"If there was an accident on the bridge with a transport truck that was transporting caustic goods and it went into the water - come on," Ward 6 Coun. Jo-Anne Gignac said during city council debate on the issue.
"We're talking about our drinking water."
At Gignac's request, the city is sending a letter to both Masse and federal Transport Minister Anita Anand expressing concern about the Ambassador Bridge company's ability to transport hazardous goods without approval from Transport Canada.
Starting Oct. 29, the Michigan Department of Transportation (MDOT) will lift restrictions on the transportation of Class 3 and Class 8 substances across the Ambassador Bridge.
The Windsor-Detroit truck ferry had been the only legally approved border crossing for hazardous or oversized goods in the area - but after 33 years in business, financial losses forced its closure last September.
The Ambassador Bridge company requested the change. It touted how it would "dramatically increase" safety across Michigan and enhance the flow of commerce in the Detroit-Windsor corridor.
Transport Canada oversees the transportation of hazardous materials at the federal level through the Dangerous Goods Act.
In an email to the Star earlier this month, the department would not say whether it opposed MDOT's approval.
Transport Canada said it does not have authority over MDOT's decision to lift restrictions on certain dangerous goods in its jurisdiction, "but will closely monitor any developments stemming from this should they fall under the scope of its mandate or impact Canadian safety and security regulations."
Mayor Drew Dilkens called Transport Canada's claim that it has no control over dangerous goods crossing the bridge "really hard to believe" and "unconscionable."
Transport Canada, Dilkens said, "can issue orders to the Ambassador Bridge - they've done it before when there was falling concrete, where there have been other issues that have been deemed safety issues.
"At the end of the day, what we really want is a continuance of the restriction that is in place," Dilkens said.
"There haven't been any spills because they (dangerous goods) haven't been able to cross, and we think that's quite sensible when you're talking about an important and vital waterway like the Detroit River."
Shawna Boakes, Windsor's executive director of operations, said the city is looking into restricting the transportation of hazardous materials on municipal roadways through bylaws.
"I've already reached out to contacts at MTO (Ontario's Ministry of Transportation), as well as some other municipalities that have similar crossing accesses," Boakes said. "As soon as I have that information, administration will be co-ordinating with our legal team to provide additional information back to council."
In a letter to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and then-Transport Minister Rodriguez earlier this month, Masse - who is the NDP's critic for Canada-U.S. border issues - said he will continue to object to MDOT's hazmat allowance until the Government of Canada proves the change won't pose a threat to public safety, the environment and the economy.
"This decision appears to be rather suspicious given we are mere months away from the Gordie Howe International Bridge being open, and that will have the highest standards to deal with these substances," Masse wrote.
The Windsor MP also brought it up during question period in the House of Commons last week, accusing the Liberals of trying to "pass the buck to the province" on the matter.
"This is bad for the environment, bad for the economy and an unnecessary risk for public safety," Masse told MPs. - With files from Trevor Wilhelm. tcampbell@postmedia.com twitter.com/wstarcampbell

Subject: Hazardous materials; Councils; Public safety

Location: United States--US; Canada; Michigan; Detroit Michigan

Document 315 of 500

Trudeau Liberals following same path as Harper Conservatives

Author: Brown-John, Lloyd

Publication info: Brown-John, Lloyd. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-liberals-following-same-path-as-harper/docview/3107610810/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+Liberals+following+same+path+as+Harper+Conservatives&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=Brown-John%2C+Lloyd&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sometimes the rhetoric of Canadian politics reminds me of our dog. Tip was his name.
With ease, this German shepherd could be convinced to chase his own tail in circles. I've thought about my old canine friend as I reflect on the last 20 years of Canadian politics.
As Pierre Poilievre presses the other opposition leaders to support his Conservatives in a non-confidence vote in the House of Commons to bring down Justin Trudeau's Liberal government, it might prove useful to remember that Paul Martin's Liberal government fell on such a confidence vote in late 2005. Just 19 years ago we were indulging in the same style of recriminations we are hearing today. In case you have forgotten, Martin was replaced by Conservative Stephen Harper in 2006. By 2015, only nine years later, Harper was struggling to survive. He was replaced by Justin Trudeau. Now, almost nine years, later the political knives are out for Trudeau. In the past we might have benefited from term limits on prime ministers akin to America's two-term presidencies. However, recent federal electoral history suggests that rule wouldn't be necessary as we've tended to toss out prime ministers rather regularly.
Having arrived at the waning months of 2024, we have Poilievre salivating for an election and the coveted Prime Minister's Office, a bewildered NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, who may well be beyond his best-before date, and a single-purpose Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-Francois Blanchet causing Albertans enormous indigestion because Blanchet represents only one province and it 'ain't Alberta!'or even Ontario. Despite a capacity to effect a minority national government such as Trudeau's Liberals, the Bloc Québécois has little more purpose in Parliament than as an impediment to national governance or now as a Liberal prop.
Much of this could either be humorous or scary for younger voters. But for those of us who have kicked about elections for generations, often it is repetitive. Ottawa journalist Les Whittington, a former Toronto Star national reporter who now writes weekly political columns for Ottawa's Hill Times, published a delightful book in 2015. In Spinning History: A Witness to Harper's Canada and 21st Century Choices, he discusses, from proximity, the collapse of Stephen Harper's government.
What makes Whittington's book so timely again is that many of the noises leading up to Harper's Conservative collapse are the same sounds now tearing at Justin Trudeau's Liberals. At both federal and provincial government levels in Canada power to govern has been aggregated in the office of prime minister or premier. The democratic autocracies into which our parliamentary system has decayed suggest that the lifespan of any incumbent government is but a few years.
Real power in today's Canadian politics rests firmly in the office of prime minister or premier, usually populated by non-elected and non-accountable dedicated loyalists.
As Whittington notes, by summer 2014 "Canadians were having second thoughts about Harper." Byelection losses were imminent.
Much of Harper's centralized PMO style of super-control finds parallels in Trudeau's PMO. Harper was thrown out of office and Trudeau was tumbled into office. Trudeau seems destined to a similar fate as Harper and for the same reasons. The top-down Prime Minister's Office and the minions in control are not parliamentarians but loyal, appointed and often fanatical hangers-about or shiny-shoe lickers.
The PMO costs Canadians amazing amounts of money. In fiscal year 2019-20 the PMO budget exceeded $9 million. By 2023-24 it had risen to more than $11 million. Exercising political power costs money.
Despite his nonsensical "axe the tax" bleatings, Poilievre would have no reason to return political power back to elected parliamentarians. Odds are that if Poilievre is successful in his salivating passion for the PMO, within a few years he'll be looking for another job. So the aggregation of consequential political power will continue to accumulate in the PMO to the detriment of elected MPs and certainly to the detriment of Canadians.
The circles of politics always return.
Lloyd Brown-John is a University of Windsor professor emeritus of political science and director of Canterbury ElderCollege. He can be reached at lbj@uwindsor.ca.

Document 316 of 500

The carbon tax and Canada's 'Hunger Games'; Sylvain Charlebois asks: What are the real costs of this program?

Author: Charlebois, Sylvain

Publication info: Charlebois, Sylvain. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carbon-tax-canadas-hunger-games-sylvain/docview/3107610761/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+carbon+tax+and+Canada%27s+%27Hunger+Games%27%3B+Sylvain+Charlebois+asks%3A+What+are+the+real+costs+of+this+program%3F&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=Charlebois%2C+Sylvain&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Feed Ontario has just disclosed that more than a million residents in Ontario, Canada's wealthiest province, sought assistance from food banks over the past year.
A million people. That's essentially Nova Scotia's entire population. This revelation presents a stark contrast when juxtaposed with the situation south of the border in the U. S. In 2023, while 13.5 per cent of Americans households grappled with food insecurity - characterized by low or very low food security (USDA-ERS, 2024) - the rate in Canada was significantly higher at 22.9 per cent (Proof Toronto, 2024). This suggests that food insecurity in Canada is a staggering 70 per cent more prevalent than in the U. S. - deeply unsettling.
The challenge of affording food in Canada is exacerbated by anemic food sales, particularly stark when compared to the United States, where grocery store sales increased by 1.8 per cent in the last 12 months, according to the Federal Reserve Economic Data.
In stark contrast, Canadian grocery store sales have plummeted by a worrying 3.2 per cent, according to Statistics Canada. One plausible explanation for this disparity lies in the higher interest rates in Canada, which likely impose a heavier burden on Canadian households than on their American counterparts, given that the average debt per household is considerably higher in Canada.
The Bank of Canada's pathway to achieving a more stable inflation rate without detrimentally affecting Canadians appears much narrower than that of the U.S. Federal Reserve, evidenced by the harsh reality of 10 consecutive rate hikes between March 2022 and July 2024, compelling Canadians to economize particularly on food expenditures.
It is becoming increasingly apparent that Canada's per capita economy is contracting, devoid of the wealth creation observed in the U. S. Notably, according to World Bank Data, in 2002, the United States'GDP per capita was 56.6 per cent higher than Canada's - a record high during the tenure of another Liberal prime minister, Jean Chrétien. The current gap, at 53 per cent, is perilously close to this historical peak.
The primary drivers of GDP growth in Canada are currently immigration and public spending. The situation is indeed dire. The carbon tax, Trudeau's principal policy for fostering an eco-friendly economy, is increasingly losing traction due to the prevailing economic challenges. Dissenting voices from within the federal NDP and B.C.'s NDP government now challenge the carbon tax as the optimal path forward.
The failures of the carbon tax are emblematic of a broader trend in recent policy-making endeavours - the implementation of populist policies devoid of rigorous metrics for evaluating their success over time, all while constructing an expansive communications strategy designed to convince Canadians of the policy's merits.
Despite the mounting pressure, the Trudeau government has neglected to assess whether the policy meaningfully affects our climate and emissions or to evaluate how the policy might influence the economy over time, with a particular focus on the ramifications for the agri-food sector - from farm to plate.
The design of the rebate system ostensibly allows Canadians to overlook the real costs of this poorly conceived policy.
However, administering this massive program is not only costly, but it also fails to leverage the Canadian economy effectively. It is imperative to devise policies that genuinely foster both economic and environmental sustainability for the nation. Sylvain Charlebois is a professor and senior director of the Agri-Food Analytics Lab at Dalhousie University and co-host of The Food Professor podcast.

Subject: Food security; Canadians; Gross Domestic Product--GDP; Carbon; Environmental tax; Grocery stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Food security Gross Domestic Product--GDP Environmental tax Grocery stores

Brown-John: Trudeau Liberals following same path as Harper Conservatives

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/brown-john-trudeau-liberals-following-same-path/docview/3107486950/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Brown-John%3A+Trudeau+Liberals+following+same+path+as+Harper+Conservatives&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Lloyd Brown-John is a University of Windsor professor emeritus of political science and director of Canterbury ElderCollege. He can be reached at lbj@uwindsor.ca( mailto:lbj@uwindsor.ca ).

Document 318 of 500

Guest column: Byelection losses show Trudeau, Liberals courting general election extinction

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-byelection-losses-show-trudeau/docview/3107405574/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+Byelection+losses+show+Trudeau%2C+Liberals+courting+general+election+extinction&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Don Braid
Canada edged closer to an early election Monday night - two ridings closer. The Liberals showed once again that they can't win an urban seat.
Under Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, this party is on the verge of electoral obliteration. The Liberals prove they can lose not just anywhere, but everywhere.
Impossible as this might seem for Canada's so called national governing party, it can happen. Old parties do die.
The most vivid example is the great Progressive Conservative wipeout of 1993, when the party Brian Mulroney built dropped from a majority to two seats in Parliament.
That led to the end of the PCs as a party. Such a thing had seemed impossible. The same fate awaits the party Trudeau refuses to release from his bondage.
Worried Liberals now recall the 2011 election, when they won only 34 seats. No, it's worse than that. The Liberals are about to become the woolly mammoths of politics: memorable, but extinct.
The NDP won Winnipeg's Elmwood-Transcona riding in a squeaker with the surging Conservatives. The Liberals got a pathetic 4.8 per cent of the vote in that riding.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh comes out re-energized and looking strategically adept. It's quite likely that New Democrat Leila Dance would have lost if Singh hadn't ditched the toxic supply and confidence deal with the Liberals.
In the 1980 election the Liberals won only two western seats, both in Winnipeg. Ontario and Quebec still delivered them a majority.
It's now possible they will win no seats from Manitoba through to British Columbia. And the old central Canadian bastions are dead to them.
Mondays real test was Montreal's LaSalle-Emard-Verdun riding. The Bloc Quebecois won with 28 per cent of the vote. The Liberals got 27.2 per cent and the NDP 26.1 per cent.
Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives were nowhere in that race, with 11.6 per cent, despite their commanding lead in national polls.
You'd think that with the leading party virtually absent, the Liberals could have pulled this one off. But the result showed that any combination of vote splits will defeat them.
Trudeau's response? Canadian's need to be "more engaged" so we understand them better.
This follows the Liberals' shocking loss to the Conservatives three months ago in Toronto-St Paul's, a riding right next door to Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland's.
She was desperately flailing away on the eve of these byelections, promising 30-year mortgage amortization. This she hailed as a boon to first-time buyers, when it just means they'll end up paying even more for a house.
Singh's NDP and Yves-Francois Blanchet's Bloc Quebecois now have even more leverage over Trudeau and his Liberal minority. The survival of the government from day-to-day is entirely up to them.
They must be sorely tempted to trigger an election. The Bloc could sweep Quebec and the NDP has every prospect of winning more seats than the Liberals.
But the colder calculation, once the thrill fades away, is that they'll have no parliamentary leverage at all if the Conservatives win the expected majority.
Nonetheless, it's hard to say what might happen in a Parliament as emotional and fractious as this one.
Singh might actually imagine he can replace the Liberals in government. Blanchet could like the idea of the Bloc becoming the official Opposition, as it was from 1993-97.
And what of Trudeau? His resistance to the obvious is almost pathological. He seems to think he can win again as the champion of the carbon tax, the only leader who cares about climate change.
Mulroney brought in the wildly unpopular GST in 1991. We know what happened to his party in 1993.
The trajectory of the carbon tax in public opinion is eerily similar. So is the course of Trudeau's popularity.
Extinction awaits.
Don Braid's column appears regularly in the Calgary Herald

Subject: Prime ministers; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Company / organization: Name: Bloc Quebecois; NAICS: 813940

Identifier / keyword: braid; canada; edged; closer; early; election

Title: Guest column: Byelection losses show Trudeau, Liberals courting general election extinction

Publication date: Sep 20, 2024
