"We've laid out the plan, invited the community to participate, and the result is now we have the requirement in dollars to get the infrastructure in the ground for them (developers) to lay it out," McNamara said.
"The development community is excited about that."
McNamara said the new subdivision has been meticulously planned and will feature a mixture of housing types, commercial nodes and parks to create the type of community residents have told the town they desire.

Document 359 of 500

Measures, action urged to prevent overdose deaths; 'We need to lead with support, not stigma'

Author: Kotsis, Julie

Publication info: Kotsis, Julie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/measures-action-urged-prevent-overdose-deaths-we/docview/3100670859/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Measures%2C+action+urged+to+prevent+overdose+deaths%3B+%27We+need+to+lead+with+support%2C+not+stigma%27&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Kotsis%2C+Julie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Community members gathered Saturday at All Saints Anglican Church for the third year to mark International Overdose Awareness Day and remember loved ones lost.
Bruce and Brenda Brownlie know first hand the loss of life associated with the opioid drug crisis, having watched a son, a niece and a nephew succumb.
Bruce, an executive board member with Reaching for the Stars Optimist Club that sponsors the event, said the annual memorial helps deal with grief.
"I feel a little better every year," Bruce said.
He said he got involved three years ago following the death of his stepson Jeremy.
Ninety wooden crosses were set up outside All Saints Church, commemorating those who lost their lives this year and during the last two years.
"We honour the memories of those who have left us too soon, and we hold onto the hope that the actions we take today will help save lives tomorrow," said Mallory Dickenson, president of Reaching for the Stars Optimist Club.
"This event is a testament to both our sorrow and our solidarity," Dickenson said. "We want those who are struggling to know they are not alone - our community stands ready to support them and their families."
The evening of reflection and remembrance was held on a day dedicated to commemorating people lost to the drug crisis, but also a day devoted to inspiring change "for a brighter future."
Organizers said the gathering provided a platform for information sharing, encouraged conversations around prevention, recovery and the importance of community support.
MPP Lisa Gretzky (Windsor West), NDP critic for mental health and addictions, issued a statement to mark the day, saying, "Addictions can impact absolutely anyone.
"When it comes to making lives better and easier, we need to lead with support, not stigma," Gretzky said.
"Unfortunately, we are seeing an unprecedented humanitarian crisis in communities of every size across Ontario, with rising rates of overdoses and an epidemic of opioid addiction."
Gretzky said communities and emergency services are asking for resources to ensure "everyone in our community is safe and cared for."
"On International Overdose Awareness Day, we need to look out for one another."
You Are Not Alone resonated throughout the ceremony as participants acknowledged the impact of the overdose epidemic on the Windsor/Essex County community.
Organizers said the crosses were planted in memory of loved ones lost - to honour their lives but also as a show of commitment to prevent future tragedies.
The evening ended with a candlelight vigil at sunset and a moment of quiet reflection. jkotsis@postmedia.com twitter.com/KotsisStar

Document 360 of 500

Nakedly partisan, despite the fig leaf; Trudeau's reforms did little to alter our cushy Senate

Author: Ivison, John

Publication info: Ivison, John. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nakedly-partisan-despite-fig-leaf-trudeaus/docview/3100669884/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nakedly+partisan%2C+despite+the+fig+leaf%3B+Trudeau%27s+reforms+did+little+to+alter+our+cushy+Senate&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Ivison%2C+John&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Senate expenses affair in the run-up to the 2015 election was a very Canadian scandal - there was no sex and no one was found guilty of doing anything wrong.
Then-senator Mike Duffy was charged with 31 offences by the RCMP over expense claims he made but was exonerated by the Ontario Superior Court.
Charges against two other senators over allegedly questionable expenses were subsequently dropped.
However, the Sen -ate's reputation as an ermine-lined soft landing for party hacks was established and the Red Chamber's credibility was further impaired by a broader investigation by then-auditor general Michael Ferguson, who found 30 senators had claimed expenses that were not in accordance with Senate rules.
Stephen Harper's government had already tried to reform the Senate by introducing term limits for senators but had been foiled by a Supreme Court ruling that said Ottawa could not act without provincial consent.
As a result, Harper all but washed his hands of the Senate, leaving 22 vacancies in the upper house by the time he left office in 2015.
Justin Trudeau's response to the discredited Senate's lack of accountability and transparency had more to commend it - at least on paper.
Without warning on the morning of Jan. 29, 2014, he gathered all 32 Liberal senators - many of whom were friends and contemporaries of his father - and told them they were out, expelled from the caucus.
At the same time, he said that if elected prime minister, he would inaugurate a non-partisan appointment process.
In the run-up to the 2015 election, he unveiled a 32-point plan to "restore democracy in Canada" that included introducing electoral reform (2015 would be the last election under the first-past-the-post system), overhauling the access to information regime and creating a nonpartisan Senate.
In government, he instituted a Senate advisory board to nominate new senators that has since made 86 "independent" appointments to the 105-seat chamber.
The dynamics of the Red Chamber were altered irrevocably from the adversarial structure of government and opposition typical of Westminster institutions. There remains a small core of Conservatives and three other loose parliamentary groups that may or may not vote together.
Critics have long made the claim that the new senators are Liberal in all but name. As former Harper deputy chief of staff Howard Anglin and Cardus cofounder Ray Pennings wrote recently: "Without casting aspersions on the integrity of any senator, Trudeau's appointments are not representative of a cross-section of 'independent'political perspectives. They are a relatively cohesive ideological conformity that is mostly aligned with the current Liberal party."
Yet, at least initially, you didn't need to be a card-carrying party member to be considered, as now seems to be the case for the majority of new senators.
There have been occasions over the past 81/2 years when the independent Senate has bitten back. In 2017, the Red Chamber refused to pass the government's budget as written, with some senators unhappy that the Trudeau Liberals had stuffed the plan with legislation for its new infrastructure bank and included an escalator tax on beer that would have seen prices rise in perpetuity.
The Liberal-dominated House vetoed Senate amendments and the senators were forced to back down, but not before reiterating their powers under the Constitution to amend legislation.
The problem is that the Senate's attempts to assert the independence to which Trudeau paid lip service has encouraged the prime minister to make a mockery of his promise to "restore democracy."
This summer has seen the appointment of five new senators - Victor Boudreau in New Brunswick, who is a former interim leader of the province's Liberal party; Tracy Muggli in Saskatchewan, who is a former federal Liberal candidate; Charles Adler in Manitoba, a former conservative broadcaster who professes his dislike of Pierre Poilievre; and last weekend in Alberta, Daryl Fridhandler, a corporate lawyer and businessman who was once a regional fundraiser for the federal Liberals, and Kristopher Wells, a professor who has been a vocal critic of Premier Danielle Smith's gender policies.
The last two are perhaps the most egregious appointments, given that Alberta held Senate elections in 2021 and chose three Conservatives, with one candidate, Pam Davidson, garnering 382,000 votes.
The government's fig leaf response is that all new senators were recommended by an advisory board chaired by former University of Ottawa chancellor Huguette Labelle. But the appointments are ultimately made by the prime minister. We have no idea how many recommendations he passed over before agreeing to candidates who fit his partisan demands.
Milton Friedman's assertion that it is a great mistake to judge policies by their intentions, rather than their results, is a criticism that could be applied to this government in almost every circumstance, but it is particularly apt in this case.
If Trudeau had in good faith named a bipartisan advisory board and chosen senators who were representative of a cross-section of Canadian society, he could have fudged an acceptable compromise.
But he seems determined to stack the Red Chamber with ideological fellow travellers, with the intent of frustrating an incoming Conservative government.
Anglin and Pennings were concerned that this could foreshadow a Constitutional crisis, where senators breach the convention that they can oppose the government "vocally, but not politically."
That seems to me to be an unlikely development. Senators know their legitimacy depends on them backing down.
Canada's Constitution is judged the world's most difficult democratic constitution to change by formal amendment - major reforms are virtually impossible and killing the Senate requires unanimous consent of all the provinces.
But if a "Liberal" Senate tried to impede a Conservative-dominated House, Poilievre could go to the people and call a Senate-abolition referendum, as the late senator Hugh Segal proposed twice when he served in the Red Chamber.
If Canadians were suitably outraged, a 50-per-centplus-one vote for abolition in every province, if unlikely, may not be an impossible outcome.
There remain four vacancies in the Senate. If there are more overtly partisan appointments, we really will be back to the days when, at roll-call, senators don't know whether to shout "present" or "not guilty."
National Post jivison@criffel.ca

Subject: Conservatism; Electoral reform; Reforms; Term limitations; Prime ministers; Elections

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Senate-US; NAICS: 921120

Identifier / keyword: senate; expenses; affair; election; canadian; scandal

Title: Nakedly partisan, despite the fig leaf; Trudeau's reforms did little to alter our cushy Senate

First page: NP.1

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 4, 2024

Document 361 of 500

Tories target NDP with new ads; Labour day; 'Ordinary people' focus of bid for orange ridings

Author: Thomson, Stuart

Publication info: Thomson, Stuart. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-target-ndp-with-new-ads-labour-day/docview/3100355894/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+target+NDP+with+new+ads%3B+Labour+day%3B+%27Ordinary+people%27+focus+of+bid+for+orange+ridings&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Thomson%2C+Stuart&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conservatives are doubling down on the party's appeals to "ordinary people" with a multi-pronged advertising campaign designed to woo workers.
A 60-second television ad launched on Labour Day celebrates "the people who rise when it is still dark" and describes a litany of problems that plague them, including crime, inflation and unaffordable housing.
The ad ends on a hopeful note, with a voice-over from Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre describing a "new dawn rising" where "everyone gets a fair shot at a good life."
That advertisement will be accompanied by a similar 30-second TV commercial and a 30-second radio ad calling NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh a "sellout" for continuing to prop up the minority Liberal government.
By now, it's become common to see Canada's conservative parties making overt appeals to unions and blue-collar workers, and Poilievre has been hammering home his message to the "common people" for two years.
"I think it is smart politics.
The union movement is not that big in the private sector. What they're signalling, though, is that they share the concerns of those who are working hard daily for their money, who don't have it easy, who don't have connections," said Brian Dijkema, who is the president of the faith-based Cardus think tank and a former union organizer.
Although previous Conservative leaders have tried to court blue collar workers, Poilievre has backed it up recently with some votes in the House of Commons that he has described as "pro-worker."
It surprised supporters earlier this year when Poilievre's party voted in favour of a ban on the use of replacement workers at federally regulated workplaces - commonly referred to as "anti-scab" legislation.
The Conservatives also took a low profile last week when a rail stoppage threatened to grind Canada's economy to a halt, until the Liberal government sent the dispute to binding arbitration.
The pro-worker turn from Poilievre has made some of his free-market friends in the conservative coalition chafe.
"It will certainly cost him some support among free-market conservatives, but he has evidently calculated this will be more than offset by the support he gets from other voters," said Matthew Lau, an adjunct scholar at the Fraser Institute, a free market think tank, and regular columnist for the Financial Post.
Poilievre's strategy follows moves made by Ontario Premier Doug Ford to appeal to working-class voters, which paid off in the last Ontario election when Ford poached some longtime NDP seats.
"This shift in policy to get votes is normal politics from a mainstream politician - similarly to Ford in Ontario, a conservative politician shifting away from conservative policies to get votes," said Lau.
Monte McNaughton, Ford's former labour minister, imagines a new kind of conservatism that cares more about clean washrooms for truckers than cutting corporate taxes.
"I would argue that everything I did from a policy perspective was conservative," said McNaughton, who believes that a fundamental shift has happened among voters.
With downtown dwellers and corporate-class voters shifting to the centre-left, it leaves an opening for conservative parties with working people.
"The parties on the left abandoned working-class people a long time ago. They are more concerned about boutique social issues instead of what matters to workers - good jobs with pensions and benefits, like those in skilled trades," said McNaughton.
Federally, the Conservative strategy is designed to appeal to average Canadian workers on a grand scale. On a smaller scale, it's targeted at garnering seats from the NDP, which has traditionally styled itself as the party of blue-collar workers.
Staffers in Poilievre's office are particularly enticed by the prospect of winning the riding of Elmwood- Transcona, Man., in a federal byelection on Sept. 16, which would turn an NDP stronghold blue, and they've been eyeing the Timmins-James Bay, Ont., seat held by longtime NDP MP Charlie Angus, who is retiring.
A radio ad that accompanies the Conservative television advertisements takes direct aim at Singh, describing him as a champagne socialist who likes "BMWs, Rolex watches, Versace bags."
The ad picks up on a line of attack that Poilievre debuted last week, urging Singh to stop supporting the Liberals in the House of Commons and provoke a "carbon tax election" in October.
The Conservatives have accused Singh of keeping the Liberals in power just long enough that the NDP leader will qualify for a "taxpayer-funded pension."
National Post stthomson@postmedia.com

Subject: Conservatism; Workers; Think tanks; Working class; Voters; Political parties; Tax elections

Document 362 of 500

Overdose Awareness Day marked by crosses, evening of reflection at All Saints' Church

Author: Kotsis, Julie

Publication info: Kotsis, Julie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/overdose-awareness-day-marked-crosses-evening/docview/3099752799/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Overdose+Awareness+Day+marked+by+crosses%2C+evening+of+reflection+at+All+Saints%27+Church&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Kotsis%2C+Julie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Community members gathered Saturday at All Saints' Church for the third year to mark International Overdose Awareness Day and remember loved ones lost.
Bruce and Brenda Brownlie know first hand the loss of life associated with the opioid drug crisis, having watched a son, a niece and a nephew succumb.
Bruce, an executive board member with Reaching for the Stars Optimist Club that sponsors the event, said the annual memorial helps deal with grief.
"I feel a little better every year," Bruce said.
He said he got involved three years ago following the death of his stepson Jeremy.
Rows of wooden crosses bearing the names of those lost to the opioid crisis in Windsor-Essex were placed outside All Saints Church Saturday, Aug. 31, 2024.
Ninety wooden crosses were set up outside All Saints Church, commemorating those who lost their lives this year and and during the last two years.
"We honour the memories of those who have left us too soon, and we hold onto the hope that the actions we take today will help save lives tomorrow," said Mallory Dickenson, president of Reaching for the Stars Optimist Club.
"This event is a testament to both our sorrow and our solidarity," Dickenson said. "We want those who are struggling to know they are not alone - our community stands ready to support them and their families."
The evening of reflection and remembrance was held on a day dedicated to commemorating people lost to the drug crisis, but also a day devoted to inspiring change "for a brighter future."
Organizers said the gathering provided a platform for information sharing, encouraged conversations around prevention, recovery and the importance of community support.
Messages about those lost to the opioid crisis were displayed on wooden crosses placed Saturday, Aug. 31, 2024 outside All Saints' Church in Windsor.
MPP Lisa Gretzky (Windsor West), NDP critic for mental health and addictions, issued a statement to mark the day, saying, "Addictions can impact absolutely anyone.
"When it comes to making lives better and easier, we need to lead with support, not stigma," Gretzky said.
"Unfortunately, we are seeing an unprecedented humanitarian crisis in communities of every size across Ontario, with rising rates of overdoses and an epidemic of opioid addiction."
Gretzky said communities and emergency services are asking for resources to ensure "everyone in our community is safe and cared for.
"On International Overdose Awareness Day, we need to look out for one another."
You Are Not Alone resonated throughout the ceremony as participants acknowledged the impact of the overdose epidemic on the Windsor/Essex County community.
Related
People lost to overdoses remembered with white crosses downtown
Opioid crisis: Ford government urged to speed up review of Windsor's SafePoint, other treatment centres
Windsor's SafePoint application denied one day after province changes rules for drug consumption sites
Organizers said the crosses were planted in memory of loved ones lost - to honour their lives but also as a show of commitment to prevent future tragedies.
The evening ended with a candlelight vigil at sunset and a moment of quiet reflection.
jkotsis@postmedia.com
twitter.com/KotsisStar
Messages about the addiction crisis were displayed on wooden crosses placed outside All Saints Church Saturday, Aug. 31, 2024.

Document 363 of 500

Provinces lead on cannabis reform while feds dither

Author: Khan, Omar

Publication info: Khan, Omar. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provinces-lead-on-cannabis-reform-while-feds/docview/3097590249/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provinces+lead+on+cannabis+reform+while+feds+dither&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Khan%2C+Omar&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When the Cannabis Act became law back in 2018, it was hailed by the federal Liberals as a bold move that would keep cannabis out of the hands of youth, protect public health and safety, and reduce criminal activity. The idea was that by creating a regulated industry, illicit operators would either jump on the bandwagon and go legal or be pushed out of the market by a newly created legal sector. Some progress has been made on this front.
A 2022 study by Deloitte estimated that between 2018 (legalization) and 2021, the industry contributed $43.5 billion toward Canada's GDP while sustaining over 150,000 jobs nationally. Despite this, almost six years on, the industry continues to face serious challenges, with many feeling that the federal Liberals have washed their hands of the industry they helped to create.
According to Statistics Canada, the illicit market still accounts for about 35 per cent of total sales nationally. Another recent study by Deloitte found that the average price in the illicit market remains 20 per cent lower than in licensed stores, which deters price-conscious consumers from going legal. This poses real risks to Canadians as revenues generated by the sale of illicit market cannabis often find their way into the pockets of organized crime.
Despite this, the federal government continues to ignore the advice of the House of Commons Standing Committee on Finance, which in February recommended adjustments to the excise tax structure aimed at improving the ability of the legal cannabis sector to compete with an entrenched illicit market.
And product from the illicit market is risky. A 2022 study by the National Research Council on behalf of the Ontario Provincial Police found that a random sampling of seized illegal cannabis contained high levels of unauthorized pesticides.
Add to this, the fact that many of the products they sell are packaged to look like popular junk food brands to appeal to youth, and it's clear that more work is needed.
After the creation of a Cannabis Industry Forum within the Department of Innovation, Science and Economic Development this past February, some industry players expressed cautious optimism about increased federal engagement. But the minister has yet to attend a meeting, signalling political indifference to an already skeptical stakeholder group.
The good news is that Canadian provinces, many with Conservative or NDP governments, have decided to fill the void created by federal inaction and have begun taking decisive steps to help ensure the long-term viability of their legal operators.
In September 2023, for instance, Ontario reduced the average wholesale markup it charges licensed producers. It then doubled the number of stores a licensed retailer can operate and allocated $31 million over three years to fund a Provincial Joint Forces Cannabis Enforcement Team targeting illicit market stores and websites across the province.
The Alberta government has boldly allowed licensed retailers to sell products at festivals or trade shows closed to minors - opening up another opportunity for responsible consumer engagement. In June of this year, the province announced that certain legally licensed retailers could sell private label products, usually sold at lower prices to appeal to a more cost-conscious consumer.
In 2023, Manitoba eliminated a six-per-cent levy on legal cannabis sales, which had driven prices up unnecessarily.
As helpful as these provincial changes are, they cannot fully compensate for a lack of federal action.
In March of this year, the recommendations of a statutory review of the Cannabis Act were released (more than a year late), with the federal government's expert panel confirming that "... the illicit cannabis market remains entrenched, and too many illicit retailers continue to operate both online and physical stores." Legitimate cannabis retailers, who are on the front lines of the fight against illegal operators, know this all too well with more and more cannabis companies filing for bankruptcy protection in 2024.
Cannabis legalization remains a cornerstone achievement of the Trudeau Liberals, but the lack of meaningful reform and engagement with industry since legalization risks tarnishing this legacy. With a federal election just over a year away, there is still time for federal action to help ensure the sustainability and success of an industry they helped to create. Only time will tell if they follow the lead of their provincial counterparts.
Omar Khan is chief communications and public affairs officer at Calgary-based High Tide Inc., the second-largest retailer of legal cannabis in the world by store count. He previously served as chief of staff to Ontario's minister of Health and Long-Term Care and minister of Economic Development and Trade, and senior adviser to Ontario's attorney general.

Subject: Retail stores; Licenses; Economic development; Regulated industries; Marijuana

Business indexing term: Subject: Retail stores Economic development Regulated industries

Document 364 of 500

Approval for safe injection site denied after provincial rule change

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/approval-safe-injection-site-denied-after/docview/3096595972/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Approval+for+safe+injection+site+denied+after+provincial+rule+change&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Windsor-Essex County Health Unit has confirmed that SafePoint's application for provincial approval has been denied following a two-year review period, closing the door on any possibility of the site reopening.
Calling the province's decision "disappointing" in an emailed statement on Wednesday, the local health unit said it received a letter earlier that morning denying its pending application for Windsor's safe consumption and treatment site.
During its eight months in operation, SafePoint saw more than 1,200 visits from 249 people, and reversed five on-site overdoses.
"I cried, then I got angry," said Kathy Moreland, a local nurse who became a vocal advocate for SafePoint after her son died from an opioid overdose in 2020.
"There's going to be more mothers like me out than there need to be," said Moreland, who is also a member of the Windsor CTS Advocacy Coalition and Moms Stop the Harm.
The letter arrived a day after Health Minister Sylvia Jones made an announcement at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference in Ottawa on Tuesday, banning the establishment of new supervised consumption sites and prohibiting facilities from operating within 200 metres of schools and childcare centres.
The health unit had been awaiting provincial approval for SafePoint for more than two years since submitting an application to the Ministry of Health in July 2022.
"I was pretty confident that we were moving forward to a potential approval," said Pozitive Pathways Community Services executive director Michael Brennan, who told the Star on Thursday he was surprised to learn Windsor's application had been rejected.
The site opened in April last year under the expectation that provincial funding would kick in once Windsor's application was approved.
However, a provincewide review of consumption and treatment services (CTS) sites launched after a fatal shooting outside a Toronto location last summer put all new applications on hold, including Windsor's.
When temporary funding from the local health unit ran out, SafePoint was forced to shutter Jan. 1.
Moving away from the safe consumption model, the provincial government, instead, has now revealed plans to commit $378-million to create 19 Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) Hubs.
The HART Hubs will provide primary care, mental-health services, addiction support, social services, addiction support, transition beds, supportive housing, and other essential services.
The move received vocal support from Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens, who, in a media release on Tuesday, called the announcement "a real game-changer."
But Brennan feels that the province's decision to do away with CTS sites is a "misguided move that comes at a time when the drug poisoning crisis is worsening."
"Our agency denounces this catastrophic plan, and we urge the Government of Ontario to preserve and scale up CTS funding alongside voluntary treatment services, and reject further restrictions on safe supply and decriminalization," he said.
"The Ministry of Health can move at a glacial pace, particularly when we're talking about treatment and recovery. I have some concerns that they will not be able to scale up these services to match the ongoing overdoses and emergency room visits."
Moreland believes the HART Hubs would benefit from keeping CTS Sites, which provide support before individuals experiencing addiction are prepared to seek treatment.
"There will be the additional deaths as a result because not everybody is ready for treatment," she said.
Locally, Windsor-Essex has recorded a surge in reported opioid overdoses over the last few months.
The latest alert from the health unit revealed 22 overdoses in a single week - the highest this year.
Since the start of June, the Windsor-Essex Community Opioid and Substance Strategy has issued five overdose alerts, with most cases involving fentanyl.
"The government is ignoring the evidence of how safe consumption sites save lives," said Moreland.
"I don't see why they can't continue to have safe consumption sites and the HART Hubs."
During its eight months of operation, the health unit said SafePoint staff were able to make 237 referrals to mental health and addiction treatment.
However, "the site's true impact," according to the health unit, "can best be described through the stories shared by those who accessed services, such as those who were able to reconnect with family or obtain employment and housing as a result of the support they received at SafePoint."
Brennan said SafePoint's safe needle exchange program helped reduce the spread of blood-borne infections like HIV and hepatitis C.
"People have to stay alive in order to access treatment," he said.
"Removing the CTS is really moving a very important tool out of the toolbox for a comprehensive overdose response by the community."
The province's new strict rules for supervised drug consumption sites means that 10 locations will close in Ottawa, Guelph, Hamilton, Thunder Bay, Kitchener and Toronto by March 31.
The Star sought comment from MPP Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh) but was directed to MPP Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex) as Dowie is currently out of the country.
Leardi said Tuesday nine existing sites are permitted to stay open, but the province will encourage them to transition to HART Hubs.
Meanwhile, the health unit said Wednesday it plans to "continue to work collaboratively and engage our community in terms of next steps."
"While this announcement is disappointing to us, we have begun exploring what opportunities may be in place for the site and will be reaching out to our partnering agencies to determine the best path forward for our community."
Brennan said Pozitive Pathways will keep working with the health unit to educate the community about the toxic drug supply alert systems, distribute more naloxone kits and share best practices for safe drug use. mmazak@postmedia.com

Subject: Drug overdose; Addictions; Funding; Narcotics

Identifier / keyword: county; health; confirmed; application; provincial; approval

Title: Approval for safe injection site denied after provincial rule change

Publication title: The Windsor Star; Windsor, Ont.

First page: A.2

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Aug 24, 2024

Section: City+Region

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Windsor, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Windsor's SafePoint application denied one day after province changes rules for drug consumption sites

Document 366 of 500

Consumption site ban ends hope of SafePoint revival; Move 'a death sentence for people that use substances': nurses CEO

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/consumption-site-ban-ends-hope-safepoint-revival/docview/3095803511/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Consumption+site+ban+ends+hope+of+SafePoint+revival%3B+Move+%27a+death+sentence+for+people+that+use+substances%27%3A+nurses+CEO&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province's move Tuesday to impose strict rules on supervised drug consumption sites - and ban any more from opening - means any hopes of reviving Windsor's SafePoint are officially dead.
Harm reduction experts immediately condemned the controversial plan, also aimed at preventing cities and organizations from funding their own sites, warning that "people will die."
"You will see a disaster in Windsor," said Doris Grinspun, CEO of the Registered Nurses'Association of Ontario. "This will get a lot worse. It's a death sentence for people that use substances."
Health Minister Sylvia Jones announced Tuesday, to repeated applause at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference in Ottawa, that the province will prohibit the opening of any new supervised consumption sites. It is also banning facilities within 200 metres of schools and childcare centres, which means 10 facilities now open will be forced to close.
"Our first priority must always be protecting our communities," said Jones. "Especially when it comes to some of our most innocent and vulnerable: our children."
The government said in a media release that the ban on sites near school zones means sites in Ottawa, Guelph, Hamilton, Thunder Bay, Kitchener and Toronto must close by March 31.
The province will also introduce legislation in the fall that, if passed, would make it virtually impossible for a community or organization to apply for federal exemption to operate sites independent of Queen's Park. The legislation would prohibit any municipality or organization "from standing up new consumption sites or participating in federal socalled 'safer'supply initiatives."
It would also prohibit municipalities from requesting the decriminalization of illegal drugs from the federal government.
The province is abandoning the safe consumption model in favour of spending $378-million on 19 new Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) Hubs.
"We've got a different solution" MPP Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex) told the Star. "It's called the HART Hub. We think that's the right way to go. We think that's going to put people back on the path to recovery. We want people to get addictions treatment. We don't want them to get drugs."
The HART Hub will offer primary care, mental health services, addiction support, social services, transition beds, supportive housing, and other services.
"HART Hubs are not going to provide any drugs at all," said Leardi. "They're not going to provide any drug supervision. They're not going to give you needles to inject yourself with. None of that is going to happen at a HART Hub."
In the media release that followed Jones's address, Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens called the announcement "a real game changer."
"This major investment will truly support people to get their lives back on track through needed treatment and recovery, while ensuring that neighbourhoods in Windsor and across Ontario remain safe," he said.
In her remarks, Jones did not specifically address the Windsor site, which closed Jan. 1 due to lack of provincial funding. But Leardi told the Star that SafePoint will not reopen.
He said there are nine existing sites in the province that can remain open, but the province will be "encouraging" them to transition to HART Hubs.
Those sites will also face tougher rules, including new requirements for safety and security plans, policies to discourage loitering, and the promotion of conflict de-escalation and community engagement.
"I have a lot of concern that instead of leading with compassion and helping to de-stigmatize addiction even further, what the government is doing is going in the opposite direction, which is going to further stigmatize those who use drugs, who struggle with addiction," MPP Lisa Gretzky (NDP - Windsor West) told the Star.
SafePoint opened in April 2023 without provincial approvals under a federal exemption from Health Canada. The Windsor-Essex County Health Unit initially funded the site, expecting the province would step in by last summer.
But the Ontario government refused to provide funding during a provincewide review of all 17 drug consumption and treatment services sites. The province said it was doing a critical incident review following a fatal shooting outside a Toronto CTS site last summer.
When provincial funding never materialized, the health unit's board of directors closed SafePoint on Jan. 1.
The health unit declined to comment, stating it had not received any direct communication from the Ministry of Health about Tuesday's announcement, and was waiting for more information.
Gretzky said the government's plan is based on ideology, not evidence.
"They're moving forward based on their ideology rather than going forward with the compassion and the understanding and the openness that is needed to actually address the crisis," said Gretzky, who lost a brother to an overdose.
"We're going to continue to see more people die at the height of this crisis."
Grinspun also called the plan a political one that "has nothing to do with evidence."
"Many will die," she said. "And you will see more needles in the streets. You will see more toxicity in the streets. You cannot help a dead person."
twilhelm@postmedia.com - with files from Madeline Mazak

Subject: Drugs; Bans; Addictions; Capital punishment; Funding; Stigma

'People will die' - Province's controversial move to restrict supervised consumption sites means Windsor's SafePoint will not reopen

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor.

Abstract: None available.

Document 368 of 500

Guest column: Time to act against social media harms, even imperfectly

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-time-act-against-social-media-harms/docview/3095054016/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+Time+to+act+against+social+media+harms%2C+even+imperfectly&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Robin Baranyai
Is it me, or do kids these days have it way harder? Obviously their challenges are not more difficult than those of children living in war zones, or with the Bubonic plague.
Broadly speaking, however, the struggles of today's youth are rooted in some pretty hard realities.
They may have been raised with every modern comfort, but this generation is saddled with the price tag for every previous generation's unchecked waste. Climate anxiety is a hallmark of their childhood experience, among other anxieties.
We are all survivors of pandemic self-isolation, but the long months of reduced social contact contributed significantly to the youth mental health crisis.
Online connection offered kids a lifeline during isolation. It was especially important for minorities seeking community. However, the social platforms that kept youth tethered to the world outside were booby-trapped.
Social media turned out to be a minefield of battered self-esteem, relentless marketing to minors, dopamine-inducing rewards for constant engagement, and a crippling fear of missing out.
We know platforms such as Instagram contribute to negative body image and eating disorders, particularly among girls. An environment of constant comparison makes them feel bad about their bodies, and Sephora product lines cash in.
Very few escape the pull. This generation of digital natives is hyperconnected, whether they're immersed in doomscrolling, makeup routines, podcasts, or 100 flavours of misinformation delivered in varying hues of rage.
Could a surgeon general's warning - like the graphic warnings on cigarettes - change the way young people use social media? U.S. surgeon general Vivek Murthy argues it's time to try.
"In an emergency, you don't have the luxury to wait for perfect information," he wrote in a recent New York Times op-ed. "You assess the available facts, you use your best judgment, and you act quickly."
Related
Baranyai: Doug Ford is all about 'sorry, not sorry'
Baranyai: Let's hope justice system doesn't fail Grassy Narrows too
The available facts are damning. A 2019 study found teens who spend more than three hours a day on social media are twice as likely to experience anxiety and depression. Social media can expose users to bullying, harassment, hateful and violent content, and online predators. Online habits can lead to sleep disruption, reduced self-esteem and self-harm.
A Centre for Addictions and Mental Health (CAMH) study found more than a third of Ontario high school students - 35 per cent - spend five or more hours a day of their free time on electronic devices. Beginning this fall, Ontario will ban cellphones in classrooms provincewide. Quebec, B.C. and Alberta are following suit with cellphone and social media restrictions at school.
Cutting social media exposure at school - without making parents the bad guys - is a clear win for parents, who get a lot of pushback on curbing screen time at home. Nevertheless, mental health experts urge families to create "phone-free zones" around bedtime, meals and other times of social connection.
Some researchers contend a surgeon general's warning is an overly broad remedy, fearing it could demonize regular adolescent behaviour. They argue harms lurk in the type of online content consumed, rather than the medium in which it's delivered.
On the other hand, the medium is hardly neutral. Last fall, 41 U.S. states launched a lawsuit against Instagram owner Meta, alleging its platforms are designed to be addictive, at the expense of teens' mental health. The suit identifies features such as likes, autoplay and endless scrolling as dopamine manipulators, along with the algorithms that determine what content users see.
We don't have conclusive proof social media is dangerous to kids, but as Murthy emphasizes, we also don't have reliable evidence it's safe.
In an emergency, we need to ask: Do we risk more harm by stigmatizing social media, or by doing nothing?
Robin Baranyai's columns are regularly featured in Postmedia News publications. She can be reached at write.robin@baranyai.ca.( mailto:write.robin@baranyai.ca )

Subject: User behavior; Mental health; Dopamine; Surgeons General; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: User behavior Social networks

Location: United States--US

Identifier / keyword: robin; baranyai; harder; obviously; challenges; difficult

Document 369 of 500

'I want to stop the suffering'; Group launches Charter challenge over mental illness exclusion from assalisted dying

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/i-want-stop-suffering-group-launches-charter/docview/3094928897/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I+want+to+stop+the+suffering%27%3B+Group+launches+Charter+challenge+over+mental+illness+exclusion+from+assalisted+dying&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Charter challenge has been launched against the federal government over its decision not to allow those suffering solely from mental illness the ability to access an assisted-death.
On Monday, a statement of claim was issued at the Ontario Superior Court of Justice by Dying with Dignity Canada, a national charity that advocates for people's right to access the procedure, along with two individuals diagnosed with mental disorders who say it has filled their life with suffering.
"I just want to be free," said 47-yearold Claire Elyse Brosseau, an actress and comedian who has lived with bipolar disorder among other mental illnesses and substance abuse disorder since she was a teenager.
She said she hopes those opposed to patients like her having the option of accessing medical assistance in dying (MAID) never need it themselves, because, "they cannot possibly know what it feels like to be on your knees begging for your life."
Brosseau said she only leaves her home when absolutely necessary and has cut herself off from the world, outside of doctors and lawyers.
"They think it's begging for death, which it is, but it's begging for my life, to please, please, please, just let me be free and take it away. Make it stop."
The challenge comes almost six months after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government legislated another delay in expanding the eligibility to include those whose only reason for seeking assisted-death is from an intolerable mental illness.
The expansion has been pushed back until 2027, which falls after the next scheduled federal election, which must happen no later than October 2025.
The challenge filed on Monday asks the court to declare that the exclusion infringes on sections 7 and 15 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, which states every person is guaranteed to "the right to life, liberty and security" and equal protection under the law without discrimination, respectively.
The federal government "admits that mental illness can cause enduring and intolerable suffering," according to the document, which outlines how the "blanket exclusion" for those with mental disorders perpetuates stigma against them.
"There is no constitutional justification for the prolongation of the enduring and intolerable suffering of those Canadians who are eligible for MAID but for the mental illness exclusion," it reads.
Medical assistance in death has been legal in Canada since 2016 after the Supreme Court of Canada struck down Criminal Code provisions against it in a historic 2015 ruling.
Only adults with incurable physical illness whose death had "reasonable foreseeability" were able to access it until March 2021, when Parliament passed an updated version of the law following a decision by the Superior Court of Quebec, which ruled such a provision to be unconstitutional.
The Liberals also accepted a Senate amendment made to the bill at the time that sought to end the exclusion for those whose suffering came solely from a mental disorder. Senators who argued for it said the exclusion violated their Charter rights.
The change was initially set to take effect in March 2023, two years after the updated law was passed.
However, ministers at the time legislated an additional one-year delay to provide medical professionals more time to prepare, as concerns mounted about the need for appropriate safeguards to protect a vulnerable population.
By February 2024, the federal health and justice ministers announced the government was seeking an additional three-year delay, given concerns it heard from provinces and territories about not being ready in terms of the number of professionals trained and having a received a recommendation from a special joint committee that called for a delay.
That group of members of Parliament and senators heard from psychiatrists who raised concerns about the difficulty of determining whether someone suffering from mental illness could improve.
Critics also questioned what role factors like poverty and a lack of medical access would play in leading individuals suffering from mental illness to seek assisted-death.
Proponents of expanding access, including assisted-dying assessors and other practitioners, said the system was ready and voiced concern that those opposed to the change would never be satisfied that the country was ready.
Opposition Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has promised to scrap plans to expand eligibility to those with mental illness, should his party form the next government.
John Scully, an 83-yearold former journalist and war correspondent who is an applicant in the case, said after years of treatments, including six stays at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health, he continues to suffer from post-traumatic stress disorder, depression and anxiety.
He said he lives with violent nightmares that leave him sleep-deprived, and spends his days dealing with after-effects of medication.
"It gets worse by the day, life does," he said in a recent interview. "I want to stop the suffering."
Both he and Brosseau, who is also an applicant in the case, say they want a death that is an alternative to suicide, not only to spare themselves from a potentially violent death, but from causing trauma to their loved ones and even strangers.
But in absence of giving her the option of an assisted-death, Brosseau said she doesn't feel there are other options. "If the government has to justify violence, then I would like them to justify that."
While Brosseau said she has found relief over periods of her life and has hope for future treatments, her mental illnesses have not disappeared.
"I cannot be a part of this anymore," she said.
Court documents say assessors have deemed both Brosseau and Scully eligible under existing requirements.
"I still am reluctant to concede that suicide is the one way out," Scully said. "But MAID for the mentally ill is the option of choice."
National Post staylor@postmedia.com

Subject: Mental disorders

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: charter; challenge; launched; against; federal; government

Title: 'I want to stop the suffering'; Group launches Charter challenge over mental illness exclusion from assalisted dying

Document 370 of 500

UWindsor rejects union, community proposals to save University Players

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/uwindsor-rejects-union-community-proposals-save/docview/3091266508/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=UWindsor+rejects+union%2C+community+proposals+to+save+University+Players&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Despite attempts by the union and local theatre supporters to save a 65-year-old tradition, the University of Windsor says it won't back down from shuttering the University Players to help address budgetary pressures.
Of the union and community proposals, a university spokesperson said "none will allow for the long-term sustainability of the theatre."
The spokesperson said in an email to the Star on Thursday that the proposals - made by CUPE Local 1393 executive committee members and other concerned community groups to save the longtime theatre company - "were considered in detail and costed out by the budget office."
A separate statement from the union, however, said it was "profoundly disappointed" that the university "refused to reverse its decision to close University Players, despite being given the opportunity this week to consider a range of viable options presented to them by the union.
"CUPE 1393 maintains that the University Players should have and could have been sustained if there had been the desire from University administration to support the arts at University of Windsor, the wider Windsor-Essex arts community, and the industry as a whole," the union's statement read.
"It would have cost them nothing to implement creative solutions and preserve both these positions and the reputation of the university."
Last week, the union announced that the university would - at least temporarily - reverse its decision to terminate six staff members. This reversal was due to a clause in the collective agreement requiring the employer to consider alternative solutions before issuing layoff notices - a step that had been overlooked.
At the time, Kristen Siapas, both an affected staff member and acting-president of CUPE Local 1393, told the Star that the union had prepared six alternatives for administration to consider.
However, a university spokesperson on Thursday said the academic institution would "proceed with its plan to close University Players."
In a separate meeting that took place on July 26, longtime University Players supporters Peter and Denise Hrastovec, and former UWindsor board of governors member and education minister Dave Cooke, met with university vice-president and provost Robert Aguirre, interim president Bruce Tucker, and board chair Helga Reidel.
Peter Hrastovec told the Star that he proposed a one-year delay on the University Players closure.
Peter Hrastovec is shown in front of the University of Windsor's Essex Hall on Wednesday, August 7, 2024. He's among members of the local community advocating for the reversal, or at least a delay, in UWindsor's decision to kill the University Players, a theatrical production entity formed 65 years ago.
"A one-year moratorium on the decision to close up will give the opportunity for the students who were already slated to do their roles, to have their opportunities to perform on the University Players stage," said Hrastovec.
"This is essentially their laboratory. They should be allowed to have that opportunity.
"And with this additional year, we believe that we will create enough community support to help this go forward and to allow University Players to have a continuing existence."
However, a university spokesperson said the delay "did not meet the necessary financial sustainability. Even with optimistic assumptions, the proposals resulted in a significant annual loss."
But despite community members rallying behind University Players in the weeks following the shutdown announcement in June, the academic institution said the "financial reality" of the theatre has not changed.
Attendance has steadily declined over the past decade, according to the university, and revenues now cover less than 10 per cent of the $1.3-million annual cost.
A university spokesperson told the Star that closing the theatre production company would result in annual savings of about $580,000.
During an in-person interview with the Star, Aguirre said the cut was part of a 1.5-per-cent budget cut levied across all departments to help address this year's $5.6-million shortfall.
However, the union claimed in Thursday's statement that the university is "using false numbers to construct a narrative that justifies the cuts they are determined to make."
Siapas told the Star separately that ticket revenue generated by University Players was never intended to cover the operational costs of the program.
In fact, she said the theatre recently recorded one of its most successful seasons to date, with all performances in Essex Hall, which seats 320, selling out.
"The message that the university has for the the arts at the post-secondary level, is that the support is just not there, that they don't see the value in it," said Siapas.
"That is disappointing more than anything else, and it's disappointing not just for our staff and the professionals that work here, but for our students who are studying to go into these fields.
"What does it say to them when the university says we don't value theatre, we don't value the degree program that you are enrolled in?"
The university said it has a plan for fourth-year bachelor of fine arts students to earn their two required performance credits, and is also developing an experiential learning opportunity with the Stratford Festival.
In the fall 2024 academic calendar, a "laboratory assignment supporting the production schedule of University Players" is a requirement for two courses in the BFA program.
But the university said that it can "change program requirements as deemed appropriate to fulfill its role and mission or to accommodate circumstances beyond its control."
An agreement with the province outlined in a strategic mandate agreement (SMA) requires the University of Windsor to provide experiential learning opportunities to students.
The university said it had met its target for the 2023-2024 academic year.
According to the university, the theatre is a "non-academic unit with the School of Dramatic Art," therefore, not requiring the decision to go before its senate.
The senate is a governing body, which president Robert Gordon sits on, that oversees matters related to policy, admissions, program regulations, and curriculum development.
The matter also did not go before the board of directors.
The Star sought comment from Reidel regarding University Players and the July 26 meeting, which she participated in, but on Aug. 1 received an email from UWindsor's public affairs and communications department stating that all media inquiries should be directed to their office.
"When I was minister of education, when we were redoing curriculum, it was a two-year project," said Cooke, who served as Ontario's education minister under NDP leader Bob Rae.
"You certainly didn't do it in eight or nine weeks over the summer," said the former city councillor and Windsor MPP, who sat on the university board of directors for nine years from 2000 to 2009.
"If I was a student or if I was a parent of a student who was paying this tuition, I'd be furious that this is so incompetent and so wrong.
"This is a university. We're expecting excellence."
Related
UWindsor backs down on University Players layoffs for now
'Extremely difficult' - UWindsor VP explains decision to kill University Players
Guest column: UWindsor decision to kill University Players must be overturned
In Thursday's statement, the union said its priority now is to support members as the layoff process proceeds.
The six theatre-related layoffs are part of a total of 12 affected CUPE 1393 members across various departments and faculties. The layoffs include 20 full-time and part-time positions with Unifor Local 2458, and four non-union workers.
"The one beautiful thing that has come out of this is the outpouring of support that we've received from the community," said Siapas, who calls the university's decision "personally devastating."
"It's been incredibly heartening to know that the work that our staff does, the work that our students do in putting together these productions, doesn't go unnoticed."
mmazak@postmedia.com

Subject: Presidents; Students; Theater; Curricula; Cost control; Experiential learning; Community support; Layoffs; Education; Boards of directors

Document 371 of 500

Opioid crisis: Ford government urged to speed up review of Windsor's SafePoint, other treatment centres

Author: Kotsis, Julie

Publication info: Kotsis, Julie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/opioid-crisis-ford-government-urged-speed-up/docview/3091106013/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opioid+crisis%3A+Ford+government+urged+to+speed+up+review+of+Windsor%27s+SafePoint%2C+other+treatment+centres&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Kotsis%2C+Julie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the death toll from the opioid crisis continues to mount, a coalition of more than 50 organizations is urging the Ontario government to provide emergency funding to reopen supervised consumption sites, including Windsor's SafePoint.
As a provincial review continues with no end in sight, the Canadian Drug Policy Coalition has written a second letter to Health Minister Sylvia Jones and associate minister Michael Tibollo.
The organizations are urging officials to introduce transparency and expedited timelines in the application process and immediately scale up and reopen supervised consumption services sites across the province.
Downtown Windsor's supervised consumption and treatment site was shuttered at the end of last December. An application for permanent funding from the province was submitted 21 months ago, but the local health unit had provided interim operation funding which ran out at the end of the year.
"I think it's necessary and it's unfortunate that we had to write the second letter because 10 people a day are dying in the province, and Windsor itself lost 125 people last year," said Kathy Moreland, incoming president of the Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario Windsor-Essex chapter.
"Those are the confirmed deaths with the coroner," Moreland, who is also a member of Moms Stop the Harm and the Windsor CTS Advocacy Coalition, told the Star.
The coalition is calling on the Government of Ontario to immediately support communities desperate for "these life-saving services."
Its written communications have thus far been met with silence, the advocates say, despite the fact that one person in Ontario dies every 2.5 hours due to toxic unregulated drugs. Applications to the province for funding for community-based supervised consumption services have "languished for years in an opaque process that has left site operators and local municipalities frustrated and dangerously under-resourced."
"This crisis requires a full range of supports to save lives, including harm reduction, withdrawal management and evidence-based, voluntary treatment," said Nicholas Boyce, policy director with the Canadian Drug Policy Coalition.
"We have seen emergency services become overwhelmed due to overdoses and poisonings from the unregulated drug supply," Boyce added. "We have seen communities declare states of emergency.
"We desperately need to get in front of this, and supervised consumption services are a key part of a humane and fiscally responsible response."
Local MPP Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh) said the province's review continues but that supporting the mental health and wellbeing of the people of Ontario is a "key priority for this government.
"It's why we are investing more than any government before us to create a system of mental health and addictions care in Ontario that prioritizes giving people their lives back through treatment and recovery," he said.
"Through the Roadmap to Wellness, we've increased annual funding for mental health and addictions supports by over $500 (million), and in our 2024 budget added an additional $396 (million)."
Dowie said an additional 400 detox, treatment and withdrawal management beds have been added in communities across the province through the renewed and expanded Addictions Recovery Fund.
"We're also funding critical community and wrap-around supports like Mobile Crisis Response Teams with Windsor Police, the OPP, Windsor Regional Hospital and Hotel Dieu-Grace Healthcare, Brentwood Recovery Home, House of Sophrosyne, and supportive housing," he added.
"And we've ensured that naloxone is free and easily accessible everywhere in Ontario."
Dowie said applications for new Consumption and Treatment Sites including SafePoint remain on pause in response to a tragic incident that occurred outside a Toronto CTS site.
"Windsor had not been previously given an approval nor funding by the province as it was operating with a federal exemption," Dowie said. "It was receiving funding from the Windsor-Essex County Health Unit, whose funding came to an end.
"The operations of the province's 17 (previously approved) CTS sites remain ongoing throughout the review process."
Moreland, whose son died from a fentanyl overdose in 2020, said safe consumption sites are only part of the answer - but a big part for most marginalized people.
"Harm reduction involves many pillars," she said. "Addressing homelessness, addressing mental health issues and trauma, like prevention issues, are important.
"Treatment is only a part of the continuum," Moreland added. "There's not good access to treatment in Windsor. And it's not for everybody."
She also said decriminalization of simple possession would go a long way to help because "people who have a record can't get housing, can't get jobs.
"Therefore it leads to more despair, therefore it leads to more drug use," Moreland said. "So even if you're going to put more money into treatment, you have to make sure there's a place for them to live after they finish treatment."
The province froze all new funding for supervised consumption services across Ontario a year ago following a woman's killing near a Toronto site.
Related
Christmas a sombre day at Windsor's drug consumption site as funding ends
Protesters rally amid battle to reopen Windsor's SafePoint drug consumption site
"The waiting game is over": advocates push MPP Dowie on SafePoint's future
"The government is wasting taxpayers dollars (due to) the acquired hepatitis and HIV (cases) that's happening as a result of no needle exchanges," Moreland said.
"Not just the deaths, but that they're putting more stress on paramedics, more stress on emergency rooms and first responders.
"If the government can't do this (re-open supervised consumption sites) because it saves lives, then they should be doing it because it saves taxpayers dollars."
jkotsis@postmedia.com
twitter.com/KotsisStar

Subject: Emergency medical care; Drug overdose; Drug withdrawal; Mental health; Addictions; Drug policy; Funding; Narcotics; Crisis intervention; Harm reduction

Document 372 of 500

Pressure mounts on government to reopen drug consumption sites

Author: Kotsis, Julie

Publication info: Kotsis, Julie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pressure-mounts-on-government-reopen-drug/docview/3091049074/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pressure+mounts+on+government+to+reopen+drug+consumption+sites&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Kotsis%2C+Julie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the death toll from the opioid crisis continues to mount, a coalition of more than 50 organizations is urging the Ontario government to provide emergency funding to reopen supervised consumption sites, including Windsor's SafePoint.

Document 373 of 500

'I see my party winning'election; Steven Mackinnon; New minister sees Liberal turnaround

Author: Trépanier, Antoine

Publication info: Trépanier, Antoine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/i-see-my-party-winningelection-steven-mackinnon/docview/3090026085/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I+see+my+party+winning%27election%3B+Steven+Mackinnon%3B+New+minister+sees+Liberal+turnaround&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Tr%C3%A9panier%2C+Antoine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's new minister of labour and seniors thinks his party is fully capable of pulling off an upset in next year's election.
The blue-Grit Paul Martin-era political wonk doesn't like labels. But Steven MacKinnon is either delusional or a soothsayer.
"People want Justin Trudeau to succeed. They want Canada to succeed. And I think at the end of the day, that will be an essential part of the ingredients for victory," said MacKinnon in an exclusive interview with the National Post last week.
MacKinnon, who was recently sworn in as a fulltime cabinet member after serving as interim House leader and chief government whip, isn't kidding.
"Of course, I see my party winning the next election," he said.
But Canadians are fed up with Trudeau, whose approval rating stands at only 29 per cent while the Conservatives enjoy an 18-point lead in the polls.
The Liberals have not generated a single surplus since coming to power in 2015. The deficit has skyrocketed to $40 billion this year, and they have implemented a carbon tax and raised taxes on the wealthiest.
For months, the Trudeau Liberals have been hammering the idea that Canada is in a better financial spot than any other country in the world. Of course, economists might take issue with that claim.
Twelve years ago, so would MacKinnon.
"Like Paul Martin, today's Liberals must also rededicate themselves to eliminating the scourge of deficit and debt," he wrote in a 2012 op-ed.
Now he says "society has evolved."
"In the intervening time period, we have had a decade of quite revolutionary changes around the world. And with the rise of technology in particular, we can, for example, more cost effectively deliver the kinds of programs like dental care," said MacKinnon.
"The Liberal party is the party of fiscal prudence, of making careful choices."
The polling suggests voters aren't buying the message. But MacKinnon seems to expect Canadians will have some sort of epiphany in the upcoming year.
That doesn't make him crazy, just loyal, says longtime friend David Herle.
"He is very level. Very level.
At a time when people are getting so busy, frazzled by the circumstances, he's got a really strong basis of bringing everything back to ground," said Herle, a former political adviser to Paul Martin.
Herle worked with MacKinnon in 2004 to help the Liberals win a fourth consecutive mandate. The 57-year-old MacKinnon, who is originally from Prince Edward Island and worked in New Brunswick for Frank McKenna's government in the 1990s, was the "Atlantic guy" before playing a bigger role nationally.
"He's just a master of political organization. He's a real student of how you structure campaigns and how you motivate workers," said Herle, who described MacKinnon as "tough," "experienced" and "not intimidated."
But even good friend Herle, who is now a political consultant, said that a miraculous comeback by the Liberals is almost impossible at this point.
MacKinnon cuts short any comparisons between the Liberal party of 2024 and that of 2004, mainly because the issues at stake and the leaders are "very different."
Back then, the Liberals were bickering among themselves publicly. This time, they mostly remain disciplined, although there has been simmering discontent since the party's surprising loss in the June byelection in Toronto-St. Paul's.
MacKinnon has had a lot to do with these internal debates getting mostly swept under the rug. His colleagues describe him as a "team player" who wants to keep dirty laundry in the locker room. He gets results: only one MP has called on the prime minister to resign since the byelection debacle.
With a byelection scheduled for the Liberal stronghold of LaSalle-Émard- Verdun on Sept. 16, Liberals could find out if the party's troubles in Ontario are seeping into Quebec, which has traditionally been its last bastion of support. While the Conservatives have been threatening Liberal ridings across the rest of Canada, they're still polling in third place in Quebec.
Trudeau tapped MacKinnon, one of his most experienced Liberals, to replace his old friend Seamus O'Regan as labour minister after O'Regan announced he was quitting politics. He is the 12th cabinet member from Quebec.
MacKinnon, who also wrote in that 2012 op-ed that "Liberals are not New Democrats" because there is a "lack of common DNA," and they have an "abject disinterest in social democratic experiments" and a "lack of umbilical attachment to big labour," must now work hand in hand with the latter.
He's tasked with steering the implementation of legislation to ban federal employers from using replacement workers.
Bill C-58 was adopted with unanimous consent in Parliament and was a long-standing demand by unions and New Democrats. It's also part of the supply and confidence agreement the Liberals struck with the NDP in order to keep their minority government in power.
"It has to be implemented within 12 months, and we think it's too long. Mr. MacKinnon needs to show a lot of leadership in that file to make sure it gets to the finish line," said Magali Picard, the president of the Quebec Federation of Labour, one of Quebec's largest unions.
"He already called me twice. He wants to have clear communication channels, and I'm encouraged with that," said Picard, who expects to meet him soon.
Frédérik Larouche, who worked for MacKinnon as a political adviser, describes his former boss as a "true Liberal" who "believes in the Liberal machine."
"He is very pragmatic. He wants to find solutions and the Liberals need him there," said Larouche, who is a director with StrategyCorp.
But he admits that his former boss sometimes likes to get his hands dirty.
In May, MacKinnon publicly criticized the Quebec government for its management of the health-care system in his riding of Gatineau and the Outaouais region.
Historically, the region has suffered from a lack of resources. Doctors and nurses have often crossed the river to work in Ontario, where salaries are much better. And the provincial government relies heavily on Ontario to provide services to its population.
"People are going to die," MacKinnon said at the time, calling the situation in his region a "national catastrophe" that should preoccupy the "highest level of the Quebec government."
In Gatineau and Quebec City, members of the National Assembly were flabbergasted by his approach.
The provincial minister responsible for the Outaouais region, Mathieu Lacombe, called his remarks "irresponsible."
"We must work together, not scare citizens by taking shortcuts. If the federal government wants to help us, rather than lecturing us, it could write us a check to increase health transfers," Lacombe wrote on social media at the time.
"I have no regrets about doing that. And frankly, I think that it has helped Quebec maybe train a little bit more focus on the healthcare system in this region," said MacKinnon last week.
National Post atrepanier@postmedia.com

Subject: Prime ministers; Political parties

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: National Post; NAICS: 513110

Identifier / keyword: prime; minister; justin; labour; seniors; thinks

Title: 'I see my party winning'election; Steven Mackinnon; New minister sees Liberal turnaround
