Document 384 of 500

'Neighbourhoods are going to change' - Windsor speeding up home builds

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/neighbourhoods-are-going-change-windsor-speeding/docview/3084353345/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Neighbourhoods+are+going+to+change%27+-+Windsor+speeding+up+home+builds&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In hot pursuit of new housing for its fast-growing population, Windsor has taken steps to streamline the development process for residential growth.
But city hall and municipal politicians concede the latest effort to build more homes faster will cause friction in local neighbourhoods.
"The change is going to be difficult for some - there's no doubt about it," Mayor Drew Dilkens said during council discussion on the matter this week.
Just last month, Windsor's development committee recommended roughly 900 new residential units for approval.
"There's going to be points of friction here, just because of the amount of growth," said Dilkens.
Council on Monday approved procedural changes that will see city staff guide developers earlier in the construction application process, ensuring projects are viable before investors commit significant time and money.
In some cases, developers will have to hold a public open house and gather residents' feedback before filing a development application. That's an effort by city hall to have neighbourhood concerns addressed by developers ahead of plans being presented to council.
With tense exchanges between residents and politicians at recent development committee meetings in mind, councillors praised the push for more public consultation and better communication with surrounding property owners.
Getting to the point where we need security - it's really sad.
If councillors want to support residents and oppose a development, "your support is of no value if it can't be backed up by the planning principles you're objecting to," Dilkens told council.
"I think we're in a transitionary state where the city is growing at rates we've never seen before," said Ward 2 Coun. Fabio Costante.
"There needs to be a wide recognition, not just at this table, but in the community, that neighbourhoods are going to change.
"Concerns are valid. Concerns sometimes lead to good solutions. But those solutions always have to be rooted in planning principles."
Since 2019, Doug Ford's government has introduced several legislative changes to support more home construction in Ontario and push municipalities to shorten the development approval process and increase the supply of new housing units.
Windsor city staff have been reporting those changes to council and, with council's direction, implementing corresponding zoning bylaw and official plan amendments and altering the development application process.
But it's "unreasonable" to expect that residents can keep up with ever-changing provincial planning rules, said Ward 6 Coun. Jo-Anne Gignac. Like her council colleagues, she has expressed a desire to better communicate those changes to the community.
'People are going to be concerned.' Ward 6 Coun. Jo-Anne Gignac speaks during a meeting of Windsor city council at city hall on Monday, July 22, 2024.
"I think we all can agree that people are going to be concerned," said Gignac. "They move into neighbourhoods and they expect that the neighbourhood is not going to ever change."
Ward 10 Coun. Jim Morrison, who chairs the development committee, noted heightened tension at recent committee meetings. Numerous residents have spoken out, sometimes emotionally, against adding denser housing development in their existing single-family-home neighbourhoods.
"It's getting to the point where we need security," said Morrison. "It's really sad.
"It's not fair to the councillors - we're trapped dealing with the legislation in front of us."
Monday's official plan amendment was the final of a three-phase project at city hall paid for in part by Ontario's Streamline Development Approval Fund. In 2022, Phase 1 saw council designate and pre-zone land to allow mixed-use development along many transit corridors and major intersections.
Under Phase 2, council last year approved procedural changes that shortened the time between a development application's completion and a city council decision.
Among other things, Phase 3, which council approved this week, gives developers heightened guidance from city staff earlier in the application process. That way, developers know sooner whether a project is worth pursuing, whether they should change a proposal, and whether they should consider an alternate site before spending significant amounts of time or money on a project that likely won't be approved.
The recent changes also require that developers, in some instances, hold an open house and gather neighbourhood feedback even before submitting a development application.
"It'll provide awareness to the neighbours," said Jelena Payne, commissioner of economic development and innovation. "It will allow them to voice their opinions, their concerns, and share their ideas, but it's also going to help our development community."
Early community consultation, Payne said, will stop developers from undertaking lengthy and expensive technical studies for projects that the development committee might not support.
"In my opinion, as somebody who hears a lot of these complaints, I think it's a win-win for both sides," Payne said.
The entire country is facing a housing crisis
In the past, affected residents would only learn about a nearby development proposal if they received an invitation in the mail to attend a meeting of council's development committee. By that stage, the developer's plans had already undergone an "extensive" administrative review, a staff report said.
If city planners deem a proposal appropriate under current provincial legislation, the development committee - and eventually city council - usually approves it, regardless of how many residents speak out in opposition.
That's because developers whose applications are denied can appeal council's decision to the Ontario Land Tribunal. The costly and lengthy appeal process forces the city to defend its position and explain why council decided against its own planners' recommendation.
In April, the province introduced Bill 185, the Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act, which, in part, limits third-party appeals to municipal planning decisions. That means individuals and resident groups can no longer fight developments outside city hall.
Ward 9 Coun. Kieran McKenzie said it's "crucial" that basic planning principles be "made clear" to the community. He said he hopes the city can build trust with residents, some of whom have been vocally suspicious of city officials during recent development committee meetings.
"Housing is the issue of our time," McKenzie said. "It's not just the City of Windsor. It's Canada.
"The entire country is facing a housing crisis."
Related
Windsor area sees 'shocking' big jump in new home construction
Windsor council approves Banwell Road apartments, offices, care home
Roseland resistance: plan for high-end condos on Windsor golf course land met with criticism
City staff are coming up with ways to better explain development proposals to the public, said Neil Robertson, manager of growth and deputy city planner.
That includes creating a summary page of each proposed development that "touches on the main issues" around the project and including that as part of often lengthy and complicated public planning reports.
tcampbell@postmedia.com( tcampbell@postmedia.com )
twitter.com/wstarcampbell

Subject: Meetings; Councils; Appeals; Neighborhoods; Planning; Cities

Premier bullish on LCBO's future

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-bullish-on-lcbos-future/docview/3084143562/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+bullish+on+LCBO%27s+future&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "I'm glad they're back."
Following a two-week strike, Windsorite Mark Mulligan celebrated his local LCBO's reopening on Tuesday with the purchase of a single can of beer.
"It impacted me, having to go to the supermarket for alcohol - I totally cut down on my drinking," Mulligan, 66, told the Star after visiting the downtown LCBO store on Church Street and University Avenue West.
"They should have job security," he said. "The service there is excellent. They're very friendly. They recognized me right away and asked me how I'm doing."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford says he believes the province's main liquor retailer has a bright future as thousands of workers returned to work Monday after a two-week strike. Liquor Control Board of Ontario stores reopened for business on Tuesday.
Ford says he has great confidence in the LCBO's future, despite concerns raised by the Ontario Public Service Employees Union during the strike.
The union representing the LCBO workers had said it believed Ford's plan to expand alcohol sales to convenience and grocery stores would threaten union jobs and the public revenue the LCBO provides to the province.
Ford sped up those plans after the strike began on July 5, allowing grocery stores already licensed to sell beer and wine to also sell ready-to-drink cocktail beverages as of Thursday last week.
The union ratified the proposed deal over the weekend.
About 9,000 employees across the province had hit the picket lines for the first strike in the LC-BO's 97-year history. The Canadian Press - With files by Taylor Campbell

Document 386 of 500

Does Ford's plan really threaten many LCBO jobs?

Author: Brown-John, Lloyd

Publication info: Brown-John, Lloyd. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/does-fords-plan-really-threaten-many-lcbo-jobs/docview/3082885152/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Does+Ford%27s+plan+really+threaten+many+LCBO+jobs%3F&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Brown-John%2C+Lloyd&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Belatedly, I have become a bit of a fan of Ontario's LCBO alcohol quasi-monopoly sales system.
Many may remember those strange days when you entered an LCBO store, found the appropriate form, filled out your order and passed it across a counter to be filled. It was primitive and premised upon an assumption - prevalent in Ontario until the late 1970s - that consuming alcoholic beverages was in some manner sinful.
For years, temperance wars raged across Ontario and were fought in the pages of local community newspapers. Morality for various reasons - often religious - continues to be an issue of public debate and even violence.
Frequently, support for temperance was spurred by women and usually for good reason. Women laboured at home and bore children.
Men laboured in mines, forests and factories at low wages. Drink was a form of escape, so cash pay packets might be spent on booze, leaving pittances for families.
In 1916, Ontario's male-dominated middle-class legislative reformers approved Ontario's "Temperance Act." Within two years the federal government, under the peace, order and government provision of the Constitution of Canada, issued an order prohibiting production, importation and transportation of alcohol into Ontario as well as other provinces where purchasing booze became illegal.
It was a time of war, and arguments for controlling alcohol were premised on a notion that drinking could constitute a distraction from fighting that war.
Patriotic and holier-than-thou politicians jumped on temperance bandwagons. Many, of course, also indulged in private celebrations of their patriotic and even religious fervour with a good hearty drink.
After several referendums, Ontario's flirtation with temperance was relegated to history.
Today's LCBO was launched via The Liquor Control Act of 1927. The legislation created a liquor monopoly authorized to "control the sale, transportation and delivery" of alcoholic beverages in Ontario.
Until 1962, the LCBO required customers to first obtain an Individual Liquor Permit. Valid for a year, customers had to present their permit when seeking to purchase alcohol. LCBO clerks recorded information about individual purchases.
It took more years before Ontarians were presumed to be adults and separate men and ladies pub entrances were abandoned. Apparently, two entrances protected the moral integrity of females.
LCBO stores have changed dramatically in recent years. I order many wines online and always enjoy fine service at LCBO stores.
I have never purchased alcohol from our local supermarket and most certainly will not purchase anything from local convenience shops, including ready-to-drink alcoholic Kool-Aids.
If Premier Doug Ford is determined to have soda pop-style alcoholic beverages sold at corner stores, the long-term consequence will be easier access for youths.
LCBO employees with whom I've often had informative contact over the years have served satisfactorily with well-intentioned knowledge. I'm not convinced junk-drink sales at convenience shops will threaten many LCBO jobs.
Naturally, I shan't spill wine about the occasion my wife and I were joined in classes at the German Wine Academy at Kloster Eberbach, Germany, by "students" from Ontario's LCBO. I won't spill grapes about how after a week of wine lessons and German Sekt tastings my wife took top honours and a trophy at the final comprehensive examination as that session's most knowledgeable German wine specialist.
We have explored wines and other alcoholic delicacies in more than 20 countries worldwide.
LCBO staff have improved from those days when we made special trips to Ann Arbor, Mich., to load up on quality wines.
Those heady long-ago days when moving a few cases of wine across the border was routine - largely because Ontario's LCBO was so limited in selection - are now gone. All has changed and today we never cross the border to buy wines.
So, Premier Ford, proceed with your sodapop booze being available wherever. We'll stick with the competence and helpful staff we so often experience at our local LCBO stores. Lloyd Brown-John is a University of Windsor professor emeritus of political science and director of Canterbury ElderCollege. He can be reached at lbj@uwindsor.ca.

Subject: Alcohol; Beverages; Liquor; Morality; Alcoholic beverages; Patriotism

Business indexing term: Subject: Alcoholic beverages

Premier helps Enbridge break ground on $358M gas pipeline; 19-km line will run next to Panhandle pipeline and feed into greenhouses

Author: Millar Holmes- Hill

Publication info: Millar Holmes- Hill. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-helps-enbridge-break-ground-on-358m-gas/docview/3082884980/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+helps+Enbridge+break+ground+on+%24358M+gas+pipeline%3B+19-km+line+will+run+next+to+Panhandle+pipeline+and+feed+into+greenhouses&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Millar+Holmes-+Hill&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Helping address an increasing demand for natural gas, Enbridge Gas on Friday broke ground at the site of a new $358-million pipeline in Leamington.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce joined Enbridge Gas officials to mark the beginning of construction on the Panhandle Regional Expansion Project.
"Our government is continuing to secure the reliable and affordable energy that is critical to our plan to rebuild Ontario's economy, keep costs down for families and build homes across the province," said Ford. "This project will help deliver that reliable energy supply and, in the process, create good-paying jobs, support our world-leading greenhouse sector and power Ontario's economic growth for many years to come."
The project is expected to generate around 7,000 jobs and $4.5 billion in new investment opportunities, according to the province.
"Southwestern Ontario is a great place to both live and work, and it is seeing huge economic and population growth," said Energy-Intensive Industries Associate Minister Sam Oosterhoff.
"This new investment in natural gas energy expansion will enable the rapid continued growth of our world-leading greenhouse industry in Southwestern Ontario and strengthen the province's agrifood sector."
The ceremony marked the start of construction of 19 kilometres of new pipeline that will parallel the existing Panhandle Pipeline.
The new gas pipeline will be 36 inches in diameter and be located adjacent to an existing pipeline corridor between Richardson Side Road in Lakeshore and Enbridge Gas's existing Dover Transmission Station in Chatham-Kent.
The expansion project has a targeted in-service date of Nov. 1 with more construction related to the project to be completed in 2025.
The transmission system serves residential, commercial, industrial and greenhouse customers across Windsor-Essex and Chatham-Kent. mholmeshill@postmedia.com

Premier Ford helps Enbridge break ground on $358M gas pipeline expansion in Leamington

Author: Holmes-Hill, Millar

Publication info: Holmes-Hill, Millar. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/premier-ford-helps-enbridge-break-ground-on-358m/docview/3082824260/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+Ford+helps+Enbridge+break+ground+on+%24358M+gas+pipeline+expansion+in+Leamington&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Holmes-Hill%2C+Millar&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Helping address an increasing demand for affordable and reliable natural gas, Enbridge Gas on Friday broke ground at the site of a new $358-million pipeline in Leamington.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce joined Enbridge Gas officials to mark the beginning of construction on the Panhandle Regional Expansion Project.
"Our government is continuing to secure the reliable and affordable energy that is critical to our plan to rebuild Ontario's economy, keep costs down for families and build homes across the province," said Ford.
"This project will help deliver that reliable energy supply and, in the process, create good-paying jobs, support our world-leading greenhouse sector and power Ontario's economic growth for many years to come."
Premier Doug Ford speaks during a press conference in Leamington on Friday, July 19, 2024. (MILLAR HOLMES-HILL /Windsor Star)
The project is expected to generate around 7,000 jobs and $4.5 billion in new investment opportunities, according to the province.
"Southwestern Ontario is a great place to both live and work, and it is seeing huge economic and population growth," said Energy-Intensive Industries Associate Minister Sam Oosterhoff.
"With that growth comes the need for more energy of all types to power the success of industries in the region," he said. "This new investment in natural gas energy expansion will enable the rapid continued growth of our world-leading greenhouse industry in Southwestern Ontario and strengthen the province's agri-food sector."
The ceremony marked the start of construction of 19 kilometres of new pipeline that will parallel the existing Panhandle Pipeline.
Premier Doug Ford (left) is pictured with Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce at a press conference in Leamington on Friday, July 19, 2024. MILLAR HOLMES-HILL /Windsor Star

Document 389 of 500

With supplies dwindling, local bars, restaurants feel pain of LCBO strike

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/with-supplies-dwindling-local-bars-restaurants/docview/3082710688/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+supplies+dwindling%2C+local+bars%2C+restaurants+feel+pain+of+LCBO+strike&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It could be an increasingly sobering summer for local bar owners if the ongoing LCBO strike - which is already drying up supplies in peak patio season - turns into a prolonged dispute.
"It is a challenging time for restaurants and bars all over Ontario that are seeing this effect," said Andrew Corbett, a bar owner and vice-chair of the Downtown Windsor Business Improvement Association (DWBIA).
"Hopefully, our members have prepared themselves and are stocked up and are working with the LCBO to get their products as quickly as possible."
About 9,000 employees across the province hit the picket lines on July 5 for the first strike in the 97-year history of the Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO).
The unsettled issues in the contract dispute include union demands for wage increases, more full-time jobs instead of part-time positions, and keeping the sale of ready-to-drink cocktails strictly in LCBO stores.
The expansion of alcohol sales has been a controversial sticking point.
The Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) argues it could lead to a decline of government revenues, LCBO store closures, and job losses among its ranks.
The provincial government is undeterred. On Monday, it announced it is accelerating the expansion of sales at supermarkets.
Starting Thursday, licensed grocery stores can sell ready-to-drink cocktails and large beer packs. That change was initially scheduled to happen in August. Another major change will come Sept. 5, when Premier Doug Ford's government plans to allow convenience stores to start selling alcohol.
Terry Yaldo, chair of the Ontario Convenience Store Association, said about 80 local stores have already applied to sell alcohol. He said the LCBO will be the wholesaler to such stores, so the government will continue making money on that level.
Yaldo, who owns Midway Convenience on Wyandotte Street East, added that stores like his will "complement" the LCBO locations. It will be about convenience, he said, like running into the corner store if you just need milk instead of going to the grocery store.
"We're not going to be able to carry spirits," said Yaldo. "We don't have the space or the means to carry the selection they have. They have a lot of specialty products, a lot of world-class products that our business won't necessarily be able to sell."
CUPE and the LCBO returned to the bargaining table on Wednesday for the first time since before the strike began.
A member survey by the Tourism Industry Association of Ontario (TIAO) after the first week of the strike revealed that 60 per cent of food and beverage businesses and 50 per cent of culinary tourism operators were already hurting.
The report found that common challenges for businesses included limited product availability, slow fulfilment and delivery times, restocking capacity, and impacts to the visitor experience.
"The disruption to the supply chain is impacting a large percentage of tourism businesses that sell alcohol as part of the visitor experience - from restaurants and bars to culinary tourism, to hunting and fishing lodges in Northern Ontario," said association president and CEO Andrew Siegwart.
"Tourism businesses are not able to put forward the best visitor experience during the height of the season."
During peak tourism season, TIAO said it is "concerned about worsening impacts to the small businesses that make up 90 per cent of the industry."
"Supply chain disruptions to beverage alcohol are adding to challenges that many businesses are already facing around high operational costs and labour shortages," the association said in a media release.
The LCBO has allowed small businesses to order from the same website open to individual customers during the strike, but there are quantity limits and some products are in short supply.
Corbett told the Star bars can also order directly from local distilleries such as Copper Rose and Wolfhead. Some distilleries, such as Windsor's J.P. Wiser's, are offering free delivery on minimum orders.
"Making sure we have our wells is the main priority," said Corbett. "Making sure that we're able to serve our base vodka, whisky, rum, tequila, gin - that people can come in and get those."
But despite an "uptick" in direct retail sales for some smaller distilleries, Siegwart said many of them are also suffering because they are losing "substantial sales through their LCBO channels."
"Those lost sales ultimately affect their bottom line and potentially their current and future capacity to offer compelling on-site visitor experiences," he said.
Corbett, a partner in the group that owns The Bull & Barrel Urban Saloon, Disco Inferno, Wild Child Nightlife, and the three GOAT Tap and Eatery spots, said they stocked up in anticipation of the strike. But supplies are dwindling.
"It's definitely affecting us," said Corbett. "We're hoping for a quick resolution. We're in full support of the LCBO.
"So hopefully it has a quick resolution. But we are working toward a long-term strategy just in case it is extended." twilhelm@postmedia.com

Document 390 of 500

Supplies dwindling - Windsor bars, restaurants feel LCBO strike pain

Author: Wilhelm, Trevor

Publication info: Wilhelm, Trevor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/supplies-dwindling-windsor-bars-restaurants-feel/docview/3082560679/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Supplies+dwindling+-+Windsor+bars%2C+restaurants+feel+LCBO+strike+pain&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Wilhelm%2C+Trevor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It could be an increasingly sobering summer for local bar owners if the ongoing LCBO strike - which is already drying up supplies in peak patio season - turns into a prolonged dispute.
"It is a challenging time for restaurants and bars all over Ontario that are seeing this effect," said Andrew Corbett, a bar owner and vice-chair of the Downtown Windsor Business Improvement Association (DWBIA).
"Hopefully our members have prepared themselves and are stocked up and are working with the LCBO to get their products as quickly as possible."
About 9,000 employees across the province hit the picket lines on July 5 for the first strike in the 97-year history of the Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO).
The unsettled issues include union demands for wage increases, more full-time jobs instead of part-time positions, and keeping the sale of ready-to-drink cocktails strictly in LCBO stores.
The expansion of alcohol sales has been a controversial sticking point. The Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) argues it could lead to a decline of government revenues, LCBO store closures, and job losses among its ranks.
The provincial government is undeterred. On Monday, it announced it's accelerating the expansion of sales at supermarkets.
Starting Thursday, licensed grocery stores can sell ready-to-drink cocktails and large beer packs. That change was initially scheduled to happen in August. Another major change will come Sept. 5, when Premier Doug Ford's government plans to allow convenience stores to start selling alcohol.
Terry Yaldo, chair of the Ontario Convenience Store Association, said about 80 local stores have already applied to sell alcohol. He said the LCBO will be the wholesaler to such stores, so the government will continue making money on that level.
Yaldo, who owns Midway Convenience on Wyandotte Street East, added that stores like his will "complement" the LCBO locations. It will be about convenience, he said, like running into the corner store if you just need milk instead of going to the grocery store.
"We're not going to be able to carry spirits," said Yaldo. "We don't have the space or the means to carry the selection they have. They have a lot of specialty products, a lot of world-class products that our business won't necessarily be able to sell."
CUPE and the LCBO returned to the bargaining table on Wednesday for the first time since before the strike began.
A member survey by the Tourism Industry Association of Ontario (TIAO) after the first week of the strike revealed that 60 per cent of food and beverage businesses and 50 per cent of culinary tourism operators were already hurting.
The report found that common challenges for businesses included limited product availability, slow fulfillment and delivery times, restocking capacity, and impacts to the visitor experience.
"The disruption to the supply chain is impacting a large percentage of tourism businesses that sell alcohol as part of the visitor experience - from restaurants and bars to culinary tourism, to hunting and fishing lodges in Northern Ontario," said association president and CEO Andrew Siegwart.
"Tourism businesses are not able to put forward the best visitor experience during the height of the season."
During peak tourism season, TIAO said it is "concerned about worsening impacts to the small businesses that make up 90% of the industry."
"Supply chain disruptions to beverage alcohol are adding to challenges that many businesses are already facing around high operational costs and labour shortages," the association said in a media release.
The LCBO has allowed small businesses to order from the same website open to individual customers during the strike, but there are quantity limits and some products are in short supply.
Corbett told the Star bars can also order directly from local distilleries such as Copper Rose and Wolfhead. Some distilleries, such as Windsor's J.P. Wiser's, are offering free delivery on minimum orders.
"Making sure we have our wells is the main priority," said Corbett. "Making sure that we're able to serve our base vodka, whisky, rum, tequila, gin - that people can come in and get those."
But despite an "uptick" in direct retail sales for some smaller distilleries, Siegwart said many of them are also suffering because they are losing "substantial sales through their LCBO channels."
"Those lost sales ultimately affect their bottom line and potentially their current and future capacity to offer compelling on-site visitor experiences," he said.
Related
Ontario further speeds up alcohol expansion amid LCBO strike
Windsor-Essex LCBO stores closed in first-ever workers strike
Andrew Corbett, a partner in the group that owns The Bull & Barrel Urban Saloon, Disco Inferno, Wild Child Nightlife, and the three GOAT Tap and Eatery spots, said they stocked up in anticipation of the strike. But supplies are dwindling.
"It's definitely affecting us," said Corbett. "We're hoping for a quick resolution. We're in full support of the LCBO.
"So hopefully it has a quick resolution. But we are working toward a long-term strategy just in case it is extended."
twilhelm@postmedia.com

Document 391 of 500

Knights of Columbus volunteers build beds for needy children in Windsor and Quebec

Author: Holmes-Hill, Millar

Publication info: Holmes-Hill, Millar. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/knights-columbus-volunteers-build-beds-needy/docview/3082175342/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Knights+of+Columbus+volunteers+build+beds+for+needy+children+in+Windsor+and+Quebec&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Holmes-Hill%2C+Millar&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Knights of Columbus volunteers from across Windsor and Essex County set up shop Sunday to build hundreds of beds worth of headboards for children who need a comfortable place to sleep.
"We're making a difference," said Brian Cyncora, co-founder and president of the Windsor chapter of Sleep in Heavenly Peace. "The demand for our beds is real, continual, and growing."
Sleep in Heavenly Peace is an international donation-based organization dedicated to building beds to ensure children in need do not sleep on the floor or in unsafe conditions.
The organization said it is estimated that two to three per cent of children in the community do not have their own bed to sleep on.
"We always have a waitlist of children needing our support and as of today we have over 100 children awaiting beds," Cyncora said.
Volunteers are shown sanding pieces of wood for hundreds of bed headboards for needy children during the Sleep In Heavenly Peace build day in Windsor on Sunday.
Members from Knights of Columbus councils across Windsor and Essex County joined the effort on Sunday to help the charity prepare the materials for 200 headboards, which will be delivered to Quebec City when the Knights of Columbus hosts its Supreme Convention in August.
"We're taking material for 200 headboards to Quebec City and we're going to have them assemble those headboards so the international Knights of Columbus get exposure to building beds," Cyncora told the Star. "The Knights are paying for all of this and the material that we take out there is coming back. That will be 100 beds invested in our region."
Organizers gathered at the new Sleep In Heavenly Peace workshop on Kildare Road in Windsor and spent the day getting the materials ready for delivery.
Volunteers are shown working hard to build hundreds of beds worth of headboards for needy children during the Sleep In Heavenly Peace build day in Windsor on Sunday, July 14, 2024. PHOTO BY MILLAR HOLMES-HILL /Windsor Star
"I love build days because we get members of the community that are here and who want to build beds for kids in their communities," Luke Mickelson, founder and head of development for Sleep in Heavenly Peace, told the Star.
"To see the joy in the volunteers, although they're dusty and sweaty, it's a great time. It's great camaraderie for us and strangers to get together for a great cause."
Since 2021 the Windsor chapter of Sleep in Heavenly Peace has delivered more than 556 beds, at a cost of about $450 each, for children between the ages of three and 17.
Liam Hebert, 11, was one of dozens of local volunteers building beds for needy children.
"This is doing work with a direct benefit to people in the community," said MPP Andrew Dowie (PC - Windsor-Tecumseh).
"I can pick up a sander and know that it's going to be destined for a bed for someone who doesn't have one today. This is a phenomenal initiative.
"The feeling that someone will have a more comfortable and safe place to sleep is irresistible and that is why I keep coming back to help."
mholmeshill@postmedia.com

Document 392 of 500

Ontario's groundbreaking journalism plan; Local media needs support at all levels

Author: Taube, Michael

Publication info: Taube, Michael. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarios-groundbreaking-journalism-plan-local/docview/3082056581/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+groundbreaking+journalism+plan%3B+Local+media+needs+support+at+all+levels&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Taube%2C+Michael&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Recently, Ontario's Progressive Conservative government announced a plan to help provide financial support for newspaper publishers in the province. The best part about it: it doesn't come at any cost to the taxpayers.
Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy sent letters to several provincial Crown corporations, including the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation, Ontario Cannabis Retail Corporation and the Liquor Control Board of Ontario. These agencies were directed to allocate one-quarter of their annual advertising budget, which is reportedly over $100 million, to Ontario's news publishers by Sept. 3.
This means there will be roughly $25 million available for news organizations in Ontario, including the National Post's parent company, Postmedia Network Inc., to fund local journalism.
"These significant advertising budgets should help support Ontario-based publishers and the Ontario-based workers creating local news content for people in our province," reads Bethlenfalvy's letter. It also highlights the Ontario government's belief there is a "compelling opportunity to leverage the advertising spends of Ontario's major government agencies to support Ontario workers and content."
This is a creative and forward-thinking economic plan by Premier Doug Ford and the Ontario PCs. It will help ensure that a strong focus on local news content will be maintained, and significant job opportunities will be available for young journalists and established veterans alike. Most importantly, it will play a vital role in the survival of Ontario newspapers.
The reality we all have to face is that there's going to be some level of financial involvement by federal and provincial governments in Canada's news industry, both now and in the future.
The vast majority of our newspapers, magazines and news websites currently receive some type of public funding. That being said, the use of direct subsidies can't be the only funding lifeline for Canadian publications, including newspapers.
Ontario's directive to allocate part of the advertising budget of a number of provincial Crown corporations is, therefore, a step in the right direction.
This advertising budget is, unlike the introduction of direct subsidies, already earmarked and accounted for in annual government expenditures. It's an existing sum rather than an additional source of taxpayer money. Hence, the $25 million allocated for Ontario publications simply involves shifting one-quarter of the budget, while the remaining three-quarters can be used as the Crown corporations see fit.
The Ford government's policy will allow government agencies to continue to get their messages across to Ontarians, while investing in local journalism. The money is to be used for Ontario-based newspapers, news content and journalists working in the profession.
This funding directive isn't being handed out with no strings attached, either. A review will apparently be conducted on a quarterly basis. If the Ontario government feels that the main objectives of "promoting local content and culture and supporting Ontario jobs" aren't being met, it will consider withdrawing the funding altogether. Hence, it's up to Ontario newspapers to ensure that the money is being used effectively, wisely and, above all, properly.
Andrew MacLeod, Postmedia's president and chief executive officer, said, "I'm certainly extremely grateful to the leadership of Premier Ford and his team. My hope is this will serve as an example, a north star, not just for other provincial governments, but for the federal government and for major corporations."
I agree with MacLeod's sentiment wholeheartedly. There are ways for the public and private sectors to help and support Canadian journalism that don't involve direct subsidies.
Ford and the Ontario PCs have identified one financial path to success. There are many others still to be considered and utilized.
National Post

Subject: Journalism; Government agencies; Advertising; Newspaper industry; Funding; Subsidies

Business indexing term: Subject: Advertising Newspaper industry Subsidies

Company / organization: Name: National Post; NAICS: 513110

Identifier / keyword: recently; progressive; conservative; government; announced; provide

Group's volunteers spend Sunday building beds for children in need

Document 394 of 500

'Great day for transit'as $83M in funds roll in; Queen's Park, Ottawa pledge financial backing for buses, upgrades

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/great-day-transitas-83m-funds-roll-queens-park/docview/3079932194/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Great+day+for+transit%27as+%2483M+in+funds+roll+in%3B+Queen%27s+Park%2C+Ottawa+pledge+financial+backing+for+buses%2C+upgrades&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A big boost to Transit Windsor from Ottawa and Queen's Park is expected to help fund dozens of new buses, garage upgrades, terminal improvements and more.
In a joint media conference at Transit Windsor headquarters on Thursday, MP Irek Kusmierczyk (L - Windsor-Tecumseh) and MPPs Andrew Dowie (PC Windsor-Tecumseh) and Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex) announced more than $83 million for the city's transit system.
The funding will support 10 Transit Windsor projects outlined in the city's 10-year capital budget. The federal government is contributing roughly $45.5 million through the Investing in Canada Infrastructure Program's public transit infrastructure stream, and the province is contributing nearly $38 million.
City council previously approved $34.3 million over the next decade for the projects.
"It's a great day for transit," said Transit Windsor executive director Tyson Cragg. "To have an announcement like this - it makes my life a lot easier in terms of being able to move a lot of Transit Master Plan and continuous improvement projects along."
The projects supported by funding announced Thursday are:
Purchasing 34 standard 40-foot hybrid electric buses to replace aging fleet stock.
Acquiring 25 diesel buses over three years to replace existing diesel buses at the end of their life cycle.
Replacing and repairing components at the Transit Windsor garage.
Upgrading and expanding the garage to store more buses, and improve its maintenance capacity
and operating efficiency.
Rehabilitating and replacing garage equipment.
Upgrading office space and improving accessibility at the garage.
Upgrading the St. Clair College and Hotel-Dieu Grace Healthcare transit terminals.
Relocating the east Windsor terminal, and bus stop improvements.
Evaluating, purchasing, and implementing modern transit technology.
Purchasing and installing 60 automatic passenger counters on the remainder of the bus fleet MP Kusmierczyk said he saw Transit Windsor struggle with aging buses and infrastructure during his time on its board of directors when he was a member of city council.
"Public transit is vital to the residents of Windsor and Essex County," he said.
"This is real partnership in action. This is our federal government working hand in hand with the Government of Ontario and with the mayor and city council."
Windsor is expected to receive the funding from both the province and federal government right away.
"Investing in our public transit system to make it more dependable and efficient is essential to making sure Windsor-Essex keeps on moving," said MPP Dowie.
Ward 9 Coun. Kieran McKenzie, who sits on Transit Windsor's board, said the demand for transit service is "unprecedented," noting the region's growing population and economic development in the form of the NextStar Energy Inc. battery plant and the new regional acute care hospital planned for County Road 42. He called transit investments from senior levels of government "crucial."
"We are seeing unprecedented growth across the entire province. Our community is right at the forefront of that," McKenzie said. "We need partners in order to be able to deliver services that are going to be required - transit is at the forefront." tcampbell@postmedia.com

Subject: Public transportation; Infrastructure; Councils; Funding; Cities

Document 395 of 500

Chicken farmers treat Amherstburg seniors to lunch

Author: Millar Holmes- Hill

Publication info: Millar Holmes- Hill. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chicken-farmers-treat-amherstburg-seniors-lunch/docview/3079930926/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chicken+farmers+treat+Amherstburg+seniors+to+lunch&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Millar+Holmes-+Hill&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The seniors'congregate lunch at the Amherstburg Food Bank typically doesn't start with the chicken dance, but it certainly does when the Chicken Farmers of Ontario (CFO) are in town.
Members of Amherstburg's senior community were joined by MP Chris Lewis (PC - Essex), MPP Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex), Amherstburg Mayor Michael Prue, and Essex Mayor Sherry Bondy on Thursday for a chicken lunch provided by the CFO.
"We have about two million folks visiting a food bank per month across Canada," said Lewis. "Next year, we'll get into some three million folks visiting a food bank per month.
"For all that the Chicken Farmers of Ontario do from supporting food banks and putting food in the mouths of our seniors, our little ones, and everyone in between - thank you."
The lunch was donated through the CFO Cares: Farmers to Food Banks program, which contributes more than a million meals to food banks across the province each year.
"Proteins, like chicken, are among some of the most needed items at food banks, and we want to ensure that Ontario communities and food banks have a steady supply of safe, healthy, and locally-grown chicken," said CFO board director Brian Lewis, who represents Windsor-Essex, Chatham-Kent, and the counties of Elgin, Lambton, Middlesex, and Oxford.
"Our donation program is designed to help people in need. We have over 1,300 farmers who help donate over a million meals a year and have been since 2015."
The organization also announced its donation of nearly 1,800 kilograms of chicken throughout 2024 - which has helped more than 16,000 families or individuals through the Windsor Essex County Food Bank Association - on Thursday at the UHC - Hub of Opportunities in Windsor.
"Since May 2022, Windsor and Essex County have been the direct beneficiaries of over 32,000 meals provided to local food banks," said Prue.
"This program is a shining example of the power of community and the significant impact of local support." mholmeshill@postmedia.com

Document 396 of 500

Keep ad spending local; News industry

Author: Sharpe, Peter

Publication info: Sharpe, Peter. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/keep-ad-spending-local-news-industry/docview/3079929990/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Keep+ad+spending+local%3B+News+industry&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Sharpe%2C+Peter&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Please forgive this recitation of a long-held and hopefully not controversial belief: a strong, free press is a pillar of any strong democracy. That's certainly been my experience through my career in business, and I've had a frontrow seat to its impact in my role on the board of Postmedia, on which I've had the privilege to serve since the company's inception.
Our team, the largest group of journalists in Canada not employed by the CBC, does the same thing every day: it puts its head down and focuses on our mission to deliver ambitious, trusted and high-quality journalism.
To say the last 15 years has been transformational in our industry is to put it lightly. The business model that sustained journalism for centuries has been dramatically disrupted. In Canada, foreign tech giants like Alphabet (Google) and Meta (Facebook) currently control around 80 per cent of the online advertising market but contribute little in the way of employment or taxes. (While, in some cases, increasing access to disinformation that can actively harm our democracy.)
Disruption can happen in any industry, and businesses are responsible for sustaining their own futures. That includes Postmedia, and that's a responsibility that we take seriously. But the core of our business is journalism - and it's a business that sustains a strong democracy.
When a pillar of democracy is at stake, it's incumbent upon everyone in a position of power to ask what they can do to help and be open to new structures and paradigms. Great leaders step up.
That's why I was delighted when Ontario's Progressive Conservative government announced its plan to support journalism in the province by devoting a quarter of its advertising budget, or roughly $25 million a year, to Ontario publishers. This will not just benefit Postmedia, but all publishers that provide important coverage of local issues throughout the province.
As part of this, Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy directed several provincial Crown corporations - including Metrolinx, the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corporation, Ontario Cannabis Retail Corporation and the Liquor Control Board of Ontario - to have plans in place to implement this directive by the beginning of September.
This is an example of true leadership by Ontario Premier Doug Ford, and governments in other jurisdictions across the country should follow suit. But please forgive me for reciting another long-held belief: a strong country with strong institutions is also the foundation for success in business, and the growth of great corporations. That's why corporate Canada also has a crucial role to play by putting its own oar in the water to support Canadian media.
Canada's business leaders and companies, which form the nucleus of our private sector, have the ability and the financial wherewithal to help strengthen our domestic news media industry, while growing their own businesses through advertising that reaches their target audiences.
Research from News Media Canada shows that more than 80 per cent of Canadians read newspaper content each week, whether in print or online, and upwards of 60 per cent read it on a daily basis.
By diverting a greater portion of their ad budgets away from foreign companies and towards local publishers that for decades have been a critical part of Canada's diverse communities, corporate Canada has an opportunity to reach its customers while supporting a vibrant news industry and helping to ensure the long-term viability of Canada's free press.
It's a win-win for everyone involved.
Postmedia Network Peter Sharpe is chair of the board of Postmedia Network Inc.

Subject: Journalism; Leadership; News media; Advertising; Democracy; Newspaper industry; Advertising expenditures

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Advertising Newspaper industry Advertising expenditures

Location: Canada

Document 397 of 500

Randall Denley: The LCBO union will lose, but Ford's anti-monopoly crusade remains incomplete

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/randall-denley-lcbo-union-will-lose-fords-anti/docview/3079905603/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Randall+Denley%3A+The+LCBO+union+will+lose%2C+but+Ford%27s+anti-monopoly+crusade+remains+incomplete&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Randall Denley
The attempt by LCBO workers to stop the expansion of beer, wine and canned-cocktail sales to corner stores is a little hard to figure for those who dwell in the rational world. There is absolutely no chance whatsoever that the Doug Ford government is going to go back on the expansion, which is a deal that takes effect next month.
Ford made it abundantly clear at a
media availability
on Wednesday that he's not backing down on the plan. And yet, Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) president JP Hornick says this latest bit of "privatization" is where the union has drawn its
"line in the sand."
Hornick went so far as to suggest that the union was "making history."
If only this boneheaded move was unique. In fact, the strike is a perfect illustration of the central delusion of public sector unions, the idea that they can and should dictate government policies. It's a notion that rests on a shaky foundation of anti-business ideology and hubris. In the unions' world, it doesn't matter if a democratically elected government is acting on an explicit election promise. Ford promised the corner store alcohol sales in 2018 and now he's finally delivering.
If the union thinks its tactic will garner public support, it needs to think again. Consumers have no reason to oppose more convenient retail locations and the strike is only mildly inconveniencing them. The strike will hurt the LCBO financially, but it's not like the government relies very much on the Crown corporation's income. Ontario anticipates $205.7 billion in total revenue this fiscal year. The LCBO typically supplies about $2.5 billion of that, just over one per cent.
Related
Randall Denley: Ontario ponders another costly, McGuinty-esque electricity spending spree
Randall Denley: Doug Ford derangement syndrome comes to the Ontario Science Centre
The people being hurt, in addition to the workers themselves, are Ontario alcohol producers, who rely on the LCBO to sell their products, and the bar and restaurant industry, which has to get all of its supply from the LCBO.
The LCBO is responding to the strike with all the deftness and good management one would expect from a cumbersome government monopoly. The company promised that its wholesale operation would continue as usual, but it hasn't. Restaurateurs are
reporting delays
and product shortages. A plan to open five stores to serve industry customers directly was cancelled after the union said it would picket the sites. Who couldn't have foreseen that?
The disruption could hurt the wholesale customers that the LCBO, and its workers, ultimately rely on, but that seems to have eluded OPSEU. It has taken workers out on strike on an unwinnable issue, but the union hasn't even formally responded to the LCBO's offer on wages, benefits and job security. Those are the issues that matter most.
The union is afraid that Ford will let the LCBO wither away. Expanding retail sales opportunities might reduce LCBO retail volume, but the government has no intention of giving up an easy source of cash. "We are not privatizing the LCBO. We are not selling the LCBO," Ford said Wednesday.
The union has been particularly irked by a move to add ready-to-drink cocktails to the original corner store plan. The premier explained the government's situation in business terms, saying the addition "involves a very small percentage of overall sales," that won't hurt the LCBO. He added, "If you are a wholesaler, that's where you make money."
Ford reminded Ontarians that the LCBO will continue to be "the largest liquor wholesaler in the world." Clearly, he thinks that's something good, but looked at another way, no other jurisdiction anywhere has given itself such a large monopoly on alcohol sales. Maybe they know something Ontarians don't.
Ford is keen on a form of retail competition, although the LCBO will still be the exclusive wholesaler and set minimum liquor prices. The LCBO's wholesale empire will remain untouched. Should it?
Why is government involved in the booze business at all? Imagine a normal world where alcohol was just like any other product. There wouldn't be a single wholesaler that every retailer, restaurant and bar had to rely on. If workers at Acme Booze Wholesale went on strike, customers would just move to their competitors. That's how most of the business world works.
Instead, Ontario has a misguided union disrupting business activities, a government monopoly that can't serve its customers, and a premier who is essentially powerless to do anything more than exhort the union to come to its senses.
Ford has come part way to rationality on the alcohol industry, certainly farther than any of his predecessors. His retail expansion is the right thing to do. The only reason to hold on to the wholesale business is revenue. Why can't the government just settle for the taxes that alcohol wholesalers would pay? That's the way it treats every normal business.
It's time the premier asked himself if the booze business is worth the distraction from the government's real work.
Randall Denley is an Ottawa journalist.
randalldenley1@gmail.com

Subject: Alcohol; Privatization; Liquor; Strikes; Wholesalers

Business indexing term: Subject: Privatization Strikes Wholesalers

Identifier / keyword: randall; denley; attempt; workers; expansion; sales

'A great day for transit' - Feds, province pledge $83M for Transit Windsor upgrades

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/great-day-transit-feds-province-pledge-83m/docview/3079870720/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+great+day+for+transit%27+-+Feds%2C+province+pledge+%2483M+for+Transit+Windsor+upgrades&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A big boost to Transit Windsor from Ottawa and Queen's Park is expected to help fund dozens of new buses, garage upgrades, terminal improvements and more.
In a joint media conference at Transit Windsor headquarters on Thursday, MP Irek Kusmierczyk (L - Windsor-Tecumseh) and MPPs Andrew Dowie (PC Windsor-Tecumseh) and Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex) announced more than $83 million for the city's transit system.
The funding will support 10 Transit Windsor projects already outlined in the city's 10-year capital budget. The federal government is contributing roughly $45.5 million through the Investing in Canada Infrastructure Program's public transit infrastructure stream, and the province is contributing nearly $38 million.
City council previously approved $34.3 million over the next decade for the projects.
"It's a great day for transit," said Transit Windsor executive director Tyson Cragg. "To have an announcement like this - it makes my life a lot easier in terms of being able to move a lot of Transit Master Plan and continuous improvement projects along."
The projects supported by funding announced Thursday are:
Purchasing 34 standard 40-foot hybrid electric buses to replace aging fleet stock
Acquiring 25 diesel buses over three years to replace existing diesel buses at the end of their life cycle
Replacing and repairing components at the Transit Windsor garage
Upgrading and expanding the garage to store more buses, and improve its maintenance capacity and operating efficiency
Rehabilitating and replacing garage equipment
Upgrading office space and improving accessibility at the garage
Upgrading the St. Clair College and Hotel-Dieu Grace Healthcare transit terminals
Relocating the east Windsor terminal, and bus stop improvements
Evaluating, purchasing, and implementing modern transit technology
Purchasing and installing 60 automatic passenger counters on the remainder of the bus fleet
MP Kusmierczyk said he saw Transit Windsor struggle with aging buses and infrastructure during his time on its board of directors when he was a member of city council.
"Public transit is vital to the residents of Windsor and Essex County," he said.
"This is real partnership in action. This is our federal government working hand in hand with the Government of Ontario and with the mayor and city council."
Windsor is expected to receive the funding from both the province and federal government right away.
"Investing in our public transit system to make it more dependable and efficient is essential to making sure Windsor-Essex keeps on moving," said MPP Dowie.
Ward 9 Coun. Kieran McKenzie, who sits on Transit Windsor's board, said the demand for transit service is "unprecedented," noting the region's growing population and economic development in the form of the NextStar Energy Inc. battery plant and the new regional acute care hospital planned for County Road 42. He called transit investments from senior levels of government "crucial."
"We are seeing unprecedented growth across the entire province. Our community is right at the forefront of that," McKenzie said. "We need partners in order to be able to deliver services that are going to be required - transit is at the forefront."
tcampbell@postmedia.com( tcampbell@postmedia.com )
twitter.com/wstarcampbell

Subject: Public transportation; Infrastructure; Councils; Funding; Cities

'CFO Cares' food bank program treats Amherstburg seniors with a special lunch

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27CFO+Cares%27+food+bank+program+treats+Amherstburg+seniors+with+a+special+lunch&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Holmes-Hill%2C+Millar&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The seniors' congregate lunch at the Amherstburg Food Bank typically doesn't start with the chicken dance, but it certainly does when the Chicken Farmers of Ontario (CFO) are in town.
Members of Amherstburg's senior community were joined by MP Chris Lewis (PC - Essex), MPP Anthony Leardi (PC - Essex), Amherstburg Mayor Michael Prue, and Essex Mayor Sherry Bondy on Thursday for a chicken lunch provided by the CFO.
"We have about two million folks visiting a food bank per month across Canada," said Lewis. "Next year, we'll get into some three million folks visiting a food bank per month.
"For all that the Chicken Farmers of Ontario do from supporting food banks and putting food in the mouths of our seniors, our little ones, and everyone in between - thank you."
The lunch was donated through the CFO Cares: Farmers to Food Banks program, which contributes more than a million meals to food banks across the province each year.
"Proteins, like chicken, are among some of the most needed items at food banks and we want to ensure that Ontario communities and food banks have a steady supply of safe, healthy, and locally-grown chicken," said CFO board director Brian Lewis, who represents Windsor-Essex, Chatham-Kent, and the counties of Elgin, Lambton, Middlesex, and Oxford.
"Our donation program is designed to help people in need. We have over 1,300 farmers who help donate over a million meals a year and have been since 2015."
The organization also announced its donation of nearly 1,800 kilograms of chicken throughout 2024 - which has helped over 16,000 families or individuals through the Windsor Essex County Food Bank Association - on Thursday at the UHC - Hub of Opportunities in Windsor.

"Since May 2022, Windsor and Essex County have been the direct beneficiaries of over 32,000 meals provided to local food banks," said Prue.
"This program is a shining example of the power of community and the significant impact of local support."

LCBO union will lose, but Ford's crusade isn't finished

Author: Denley, Randall

Publication info: Denley, Randall. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-union-will-lose-fords-crusade-isnt-finished/docview/3079696212/se-2?accountid=15090

Subject: Alcohol; Privatization; Liquor; Strikes; Wholesalers

Business indexing term: Subject: Privatization Strikes Wholesalers

Document 401 of 500

Guest column: Doug Ford's Tories could use math refresher

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-doug-fords-tories-could-use-math/docview/3078329441/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+could+use+math+refresher&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Robin Baranyai
In 2020, when Ontario introduced new math curricula for grades one to eight, standardized test scores already had been going downhill for a decade.
Last year, just half of Grade 6 pupils were meeting the provincial standard in math. Ontario Conservatives' 'back-to-basics' focus on math is aimed at helping young people prepare for tech careers and basic household budgeting.
Unfortunately, a basic math refresher also seems in order for some of the folks managing our money at Queen's Park.
Take the government's decision to cancel the province's 10-year contract with The Beer Store, which by Conservatives' own account will cost $225 million. It's a steep price to ever-so-slightly expand the alcoholic offerings in grocery stores, and roll out sales in convenience stores a little sooner, fulfilling a pressing demand perceived by no one but the premier.
The Beer Store contract already was set to expire in December 2025; Premier Doug Ford's inability to wait it out (to save a lot of money) is a little like failing the marshmallow test (a study on delayed gratification). Meanwhile, corner store cashiers will become the gatekeepers of underage access to alcohol.
It gets worse. The provincial Liberals assert the true cost of cancelling the contract is closer to $1 billion. Let's frame that in math problem form: How long would it take to waste $1 billion, at a rate of $1 million a day? The answer: You would have to flush away $1 million a day, every day, for nearly three years.
This staggering abuse of funds became a sharp point of contrast this week for folks protesting the closure of the Ontario Science Centre. The iconic attraction was erected in 1969. It was abruptly shuttered on June 21, after an engineering report by Rimkus Consulting Group raised concerns parts of the roof made with aerated concrete could be at risk of collapsing under snow buildup.
"Unfortunately, we have to take the warning signs of engineers very seriously," Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma told reporters. "It was a very difficult decision."
Surma's dilemma was preceded by the premier's deeply unpopular decision, announced in April, to relocate the Science Centre downtown, and tear down the remarkable building designed by Toronto architect Raymond Moriyama. A new facility is slated to open in 2028 within a redeveloped Ontario Place, alongside a lush, private spa.
Eight months after this decision, Infrastructure Ontario belatedly released cost estimates justifying the move. The report estimated the government could build and run a new facility for $600 million less than it would cost to renovate and operate the existing facility during a 50-year period. (That's $12 million a year, for those following the math.)
Of course, Ford already gave his reason for the move back in April: "All of us grew up going to the Science Centre one time or another. It's tired."
Well, I'm tired, too. The Moriyama building is the same age as me, and though my body may sag in places, I find it hard to believe architecture of this vintage is too old to be saved.
Indeed, the Rimkus report recommends precisely how to save the roof. It recommends replacing the aerated concrete panels in stages during the next 10 years, as they come up for regularly scheduled renewal. Moreover, the most high-risk panels (which comprise less than three per cent of the roof area) can be addressed while safely keeping nearly all of the permanent exhibits open to the public.
Keeping the facility open is less a matter of health and safety than simple spending priorities.
The cost of these roof repairs is pegged at between $22 million and $40 million - a drop in the keg, as it were.
Robin Baranyai's columns are regularly featured in Postmedia News publications. She can be reached at write.robin@baranyai.ca.( mailto:write.robin@baranyai.ca )

Subject: Architecture; Core curriculum; Cost estimates; Aerated concrete

Business indexing term: Subject: Architecture Cost estimates

Identifier / keyword: robin; baranyai; ontario; introduced; curricula; grades

Ford government could use a math refresher

Author: Baranyai, Robin

Publication info: Baranyai, Robin. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-government-could-use-math-refresher/docview/3076315619/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+could+use+a+math+refresher&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Baranyai%2C+Robin&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 403 of 500

LCBO workers hit picket lines, stores close after talks dry up

Author: Millar Holmes- Hill

Publication info: Millar Holmes- Hill. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-workers-hit-picket-lines-stores-close-after/docview/3076315584/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+workers+hit+picket+lines%2C+stores+close+after+talks+dry+up&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Millar+Holmes-+Hill&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Thousands of Ontario liquor store employees are on strike after bargaining talks between their union and the LCBO broke down.
Leaders of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU), representing the workers, blamed Premier Doug Ford for the strike. Talks fell apart late Thursday evening, leading to more than 9,000 workers initiating a strike at 12:01 a.m. Friday.
"Doug Ford wants to make life better for his wealthy friends," said OPSEU/SEFPO president JP Hornick in a statement.
"It's why he's wasting upwards of a billion dollars of our money to fast-track privatized alcohol sales and hand more of the public revenues generated by the LCBO over to the CEOs and big box grocery and convenience chains like Loblaws and Circle K."
All stores are now closed for two weeks.
"We told Ford not to ruin everybody's summer, but now he's closed the Science Centre and forced a dry summer for Ontarians by refusing to offer a deal that would be good for LCBO workers and Ontario," Hornick said.
If the strike lasts longer than two weeks, the LCBO said it will open a limited number of stores three days a week on Fridays, Saturdays and Sundays with limited hours. Information on store locations and hours will be released at a later date.
"We argued that we grow the LCBO to meet demand and increase convenience by opening more stores, increasing the hours of operation, and increasing warehousing, logistics and e-commerce capacity," reads the LCBO statement. "Doing this will also expand public revenues by increasing LCBO sales which help fund our public services like health care and education as well as key infrastructure."
On its website, the LCBO said it's disappointed that the union has "initiated a strike - the first in our long history in Ontario."
"We have plans in place to help minimize disruption as much as possible and ensure that our products remain available to our valued retail and wholesale customers," the company's statement reads. "We will be operating our business, but it is not business as usual."
The LCBO website and mobile app will continue to accept orders for free home delivery during the strike.
The strike will not affect the roughly 2,300 private retail points of sale across the province, including licensed grocery stores, the Beer Store, wineries, breweries and distillery outlets, as well as bars and restaurants. mholmeshill@postmedia.com

Windsor-Essex LCBO stores closed in first-ever workers strike

Author: Holmes-Hill, Millar

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/windsor-essex-lcbo-stores-closed-first-ever/docview/3076255846/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Full text: Thousands of Ontario liquor store employees have gone on strike following the breakdown of bargaining talks between their union and the LCBO.
