Document 420 of 500

Pass intimate partner violence law now, NDP urges Ford government

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pass-intimate-partner-violence-law-now-ndp-urges/docview/3070944753/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pass+intimate+partner+violence+law+now%2C+NDP+urges+Ford+government&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government is taking far too long to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic in the province, Ontario's NDP opposition leader said during a visit to Windsor this week.
"The government could pass that legislation tomorrow," Marit Stiles told reporters ahead of a roundtable discussion on Wednesday with local partners who work with vulnerable women.
"I know that the women around the table and gender-diverse people have been telling the government what needs to happen for many years. We know what needs to be done, and the government should be taking action today.
The epidemic declaration in new legislation would be a small but "crucial" step toward protecting women from violence, she said.
"The very first and most crucial step is declaring intimate partner violence an epidemic," she said.
In April, the Ford government announced it would support Bill 173, the Intimate Partner Violence Act, after almost a year spent refusing to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic.
However, that government support was to send the bill to a legislative committee for study, rather than passing it into law immediately - a move both Stiles and local MPP Lisa Gretzky (NDP - Windsor West) have been calling for.
Declaring intimate partner violence an epidemic in Ontario is one of 86 recommendations made in the Renfrew Report - a coroner's inquest into the murders of three women in Renfrew County by a mutual ex-partner.
"The government has these recommendations," Gretzky said Wednesday. "If they're talking to survivors, if they're talking to victims'families, then they would know what to do to move forward."
In an email to the Star on Thursday, the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services said the legislative committee will "carefully assess current available programs and root causes," and that it is "committed to taking all necessary steps to ensure the safety and well-being of everyone in the province."
The email said the province is investing $1.4 billion over four years into Ontario-STANDS, a plan aimed at gender-based violence.
"When it comes to preventing violence against women and children and helping survivors, our government is focused on actions that deliver concrete, tangible results," the email from the ministry said.
Despite calls for the Ford government to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic before the summer break, that did not happen. Ontario's legislature rose early for summer recess on June 6, and the house is not expected to return until mid-October.
"I don't know where Doug Ford is right now," said Stiles. "Maybe he's at the cottage. I have no idea. But we're going to keep doing the work that's necessary to ensure that not one more woman is killed in this province."
Nearly 100 municipalities in Ontario have declared intimate partner violence an epidemic, including Windsor, Tecumseh, Essex, Lakeshore, Kingsville and Amherstburg. tcampbell@postmedia.com twitter.com/wstarcampbell

Subject: Police reports; Domestic violence

Document 421 of 500

Kids skip class to escape sweltering temperatures; NDP demands Ford government install emergency AC in schools

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/kids-skip-class-escape-sweltering-temperatures/docview/3070944711/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kids+skip+class+to+escape+sweltering+temperatures%3B+NDP+demands+Ford+government+install+emergency+AC+in+schools&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Environment Canada warns of dangerous conditions due to a heat dome casting a weeklong blanket of muggy hotness over southwestern Ontario, many students in Windsor-Essex struggle to learn in sweltering conditions.
Students are skipping classes to escape the heat, and nearly half of local public school board buildings lack air conditioning, according to Mario Spagnuolo, president of the Greater Essex Elementary Teachers Federation of Ontario.
With temperatures in some classrooms reaching highs of 30 to 35 C, he said some students are choosing to stay home. And many teachers have been using their own funds to buy fans and Popsicles to help keep their students cool.
"We're dealing with children here," Spagnuolo said outside a Windsor school on Wednesday, "and that should be a priority of this provincial government.
"One teacher said that half of her class was missing, so its affecting our kids and it's affecting the quality of the education that we can provide."
Spagnuolo said some teachers have left early to seek medical advice because they were unable to cope with the hot classroom conditions.
Ontario NDP Opposition Leader Marit Stiles, and local MPP Lisa Gretzky (NDP - Windsor West) joined Spagnuolo at Prince Edward Public School to call for immediate action by the Ford government to provide emergency air conditioning to schools.
"There's no messing around anymore," said Stiles. "Our kids should not be suffering like this."
Stiles said it's the provincial government's responsibility - not schools boards - to retrofit classrooms with air conditioning. Wednesday's temperature in Windsor hit 34.2 C, with the humidex making it feel like more than 40 C.
Stiles said that AC costs should not have to be covered by the local school board, which has faced many cuts over the last six years and is now operating at a deficit.
On Tuesday, the local public school board approved a $532-million budget for 2024 with a $6.38-million deficit. It included staff and spending cuts, but trustees signalled more job cuts could lie ahead.
The board must now present a plan to Ontario's Education Ministry on how it will eliminate that deficit in two years.
"I know Doug Ford called for a 19-week vacation - well, get back out there and solve this problem."
Earlier this month, the Ford government paused its current Queen's Park session to begin a summer recess scheduled to end in late October.
"The (school) board cannot do this on their own," said Gretzky.
"The funding flows from the province, and we've been hearing for years about funding cuts and repair backlogs.
"It's time for the government, for the premier specifically, to get back to work to provide the funding that these boards need," she said, "to ensure that we do have safe places for the kids to learn."
She added that the dangers of the high temperatures are heightened for students and staff with pre-existing medical conditions.
Spagnuolo said there is some relief to be found at Prince Edward Public School such as the library, which is air-conditioned to preserve books, but students are only allowed to enter once every four days for about 15 minutes.
The public school board did not provide a comment on the situation ahead of the Star's print deadline on Thursday.
At the Windsor-Essex Catholic District School Board, communications coordinator Stephen Fields said one-third of its elementary schools are fully air-conditioned.
The remaining two-thirds, he said, have between 60 and 96 per cent of their classrooms and office spaces cooled, he said.
Fields said each Catholic school has at least one room with a window unit where staff and students can go to cool off.
Fields said all eight of the Catholic board high schools are fully retrofitted with air conditioning, Continuing her tour through Windsor-Essex on Wednesday, Stiles also visited the Downtown Mission of Windsor and participated in a roundtable with local stakeholders to discuss intimate partner violence.
On Thursday, she met with members of Caldwell First Nation to discuss environmental protection priorities.
She also joined a roundtable with Essex Mayor Sherry Bondy and Councillor Kim Verbeek on the rising cost of leasing land for mobile homes. mmazak@postmedia.com

Subject: Public schools; Students; Classrooms; Air conditioning; Funding; Elementary schools; Teachers; School boards

Document 422 of 500

Immigration a boon to separatists

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/immigration-boon-separatists/docview/3070944699/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Immigration+a+boon+to+separatists&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If times are good, immigration does not drive politics. If times are bad, immigrants fast become a lightning rod and a political football. This predictable pattern played out in recent European Parliament elections, where the economy, migration and war were the top issues. Nationalist right-wing parties promising to crack down on immigration, including that of Marine Le Pen in France and Georgia Meloni in Italy, won the day.
In Canada, a similar drama is unfolding. In the past two years, Canada admitted 900,000 permanent residents and 1.5 million non-permanent residents. During the same period, Canadians experienced record-high interest rates, inflation and a housing shortage. Toronto and Vancouver are "impossibly unaffordable" housing markets, with Vancouver the third least affordable in the world after Hong Kong and Sydney, Australia. Toronto now has 7.9 per cent unemployment, a result of population growth outpacing job growth.
Are Canadians connecting the dots? Apparently so. Forty-four per cent of us believe immigration is having a mostly negative effect on Canada, up six points from last year, compared with 42 per cent who view it positively. Forty-six per cent want immigration to decrease.
Mario Canseco, whose firm The Research Company conducted the survey, said housing is a major factor: "(Some question) if we can't really have homes for everybody here, how are we going to deal with another influx of immigrants?" These effects were entirely predictable and mirror trends around the world. And they could produce a fresh national unity crisis.
Last week, Quebec Premier François Legault gave Ottawa a bill for one billion dollars for immigration-related expenses. He trotted out charts of Quebec Labour Ministry data showing the province received 80,151 requests for social assistance between March 22 and Nov. 21, 2023, compared with 72,221 for the same period in 2022, the largest increase in 25 years. During the same period, the monthly average of asylum seekers more than doubled, going from 19,455 adults to more than 40,000. Legault also said "100 per cent of the housing crisis problem" is a result of temporary immigration.
To assist, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau gave Quebec $750 million, which Legault qualified as "disappointing," despite it being the second such payment to Quebec in less than a year. In January, Quebec received $100 million when Legault and Ontario Premier Doug Ford complained about the cost of housing asylum seekers, and Ottawa tossed the two provinces a combined $362 million. Cue the outrage in B.C., where this week Premier David Eby complained bitterly about the latest funding to Quebec coming "at the expense" of the West.
"That announcement today with Quebec, frankly, is the straw that broke this camel's back," Eby said Monday at the Western Premiers'Conference in Whitehorse. "I'll say for British Columbia how frustrated we are to see the money being showered down on Quebec and Ontario, and us scrabbling around for what's left over. It's not acceptable."
But in Quebec, the issue isn't just money, it's also powers. This week, Legault told Radio-Canada that Quebec should have a referendum on its constitution, to give it full powers over immigration. Why? Because immigration is also driving another potential referendum, this one on Quebec separation. Parti Québécois Leader Paul St-Pierre Plamondon declared that if elected, his party would hold another referendum on Quebec separation. The reason? Immigration is fuelling population growth in the rest of Canada and thereby reducing Quebec's share.
"Either we choose to raise our immigration thresholds to between 120,000 and 150,000 per year to try to maintain Quebec's proportion (of immigration to Canada), which means the decline of the French language and an acute housing crisis ... or we choose thresholds based on our capacity to receive, which means a significant drop in Quebec's political weight in Canada, possibly below 15 per cent," Plamondon said last month.
Federalists may shrug and say yes, but the polls show he'll never win. Separatism is polling at 35 per cent, while 54 per cent of Quebecers support remaining in Canada. To which I say, yes. For now. But no one thought French voters would endorse Le Pen's party, either. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Population growth; Referendums; Immigration

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: times; immigration; drive; politics; immigrants; become

'Kids should not be suffering like this' - many Windsor area schools lack air conditioning

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kids-should-not-be-suffering-like-this-many/docview/3070714838/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Kids+should+not+be+suffering+like+this%27+-+many+Windsor+area+schools+lack+air+conditioning&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Environment Canada warns of dangerous conditions due to a 'heat dome' casting a week-long blanket of muggy hotness over southwestern Ontario, many students in Windsor-Essex struggle to learn in sweltering conditions.
Students are skipping classes to escape the heat, and nearly half of local public school board buildings lack air conditioning, according to Mario Spagnuolo, president of the Greater Essex Elementary Teachers Federation of Ontario.
With temperatures in some classrooms reaching highs of 30 to 35 degrees Celsius, he said some students are choosing to stay home. And many teachers have been using their own funds to buy fans and popsicles to help keep their students cool.
"We're dealing with children here," Spagnuolo said outside a Windsor school on Wednesday, "and that should be a priority of this provincial government.
"One teacher said that half of her class was missing, so its affecting our kids and it's affecting the quality of the education that we can provide."
Spagnuolo said some teachers have left early to seek medical advice because they were unable to cope with the hot classroom conditions.
Shelley Armstrong, the public board's treasurer and superintendent of business, confirmed that nearly half of the schools are not completely air-conditioned, and around a quarter have partial cooling.
Inside schools that are not fully air-conditioned, Armstrong said there are designated "cooling stations" where staff and students can find relief.
When asked about any medical incidents during the heatwave, Armstrong said there was "nothing reported to me that I'm aware of."
Ontario NDP opposition leader Marit Stiles, and local MPP Lisa Gretzky (NDP - Windsor West) joined Spagnuolo at Prince Edward Public School to call for immediate action by the Ford government to provide emergency air conditioning to schools.
"There's no messing around anymore," said Stiles. "Our kids should not be suffering like this."
Ontario NDP leader Marit Stiles, left, speaks with teachers outside Prince Edward Public School in Windsor on Wednesday, June 19, 2024, about the lack of air conditioning at the school.
Stiles said it's the provincial government's responsibility - not schools boards - to retrofit classrooms with air conditioning. Wednesday's temperature in Windsor hit 34.2 C, with the humidex making it feel like more than 40 C.
Stiles said that expense should not have to be covered by the local school board, which has faced many cuts over the last six years and is now operating at a deficit.
On Tuesday, the local public school board approved a $532-million budget for 2024 with a $6.38-million deficit. It included staff and spending cuts, but trustees signalled more job cuts could lie ahead. The board must now present a plan to Ontario's education ministry on how it will eliminate that deficit in two years.
"I know Doug Ford called for a 19 week vacation - well get back out there and solve this problem."
Earlier this month, the Ford government paused its current Queen's Park session to begin a summer recess scheduled to end in late-October.
"The (school) board cannot do this on their own," said Gretzky. "The funding flows from the province, and we've been hearing for years about funding cuts and repair backlogs.
"It's time for the government, for the premier specifically, to get back to work to provide the funding that these boards need," she said, "to ensure that we do have safe places for the kids to learn."
She added that the dangers of the high temperatures are heightened for students and staff with pre-existing medical conditions.
Spagnuolo said there is some relief to be found at Prince Edward Public School such as the library, which is air-conditioned to preserve books, but students are only allowed to enter once every four days for about 15 minutes.
At the Windsor-Essex Catholic District School Board, communications coordinator Stephen Fields said one-third of its elementary schools are fully air-conditioned. The remaining two-thirds, he said, have between 60 and 96 per cent of their classrooms and office spaces cooled.
Fields said each Catholic school has at least one room with a window unit where staff and students can go to cool off.
All eight of the Catholic board high schools are fully retrofitted with air conditioning, he said.

Continuing her tour through Windsor-Essex on Wednesday, Stiles also visited the Downtown Mission of Windsor and participated in a roundtable with local stakeholders to discuss intimate partner violence.
On Thursday, she met with members of Caldwell First Nation to discuss environmental protection priorities and joined a roundtable with Essex Mayor Sherry Bondy and Councillor Kim Verbeek on the rising cost of leasing land for mobile homes.

Document 424 of 500

Act now on intimate partner violence law: NDP leader calls out Ford

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/act-now-on-intimate-partner-violence-law-ndp/docview/3070708809/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Act+now+on+intimate+partner+violence+law%3A+NDP+leader+calls+out+Ford&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government is taking far too long to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic in the province, Ontario's NDP opposition leader said during a visit to Windsor this week.
"The government could pass that legislation tomorrow," Marit Stiles told reporters ahead of a roundtable discussion on Wednesday with local partners who work with vulnerable women.
"I know that the women around the table and gender-diverse people have been telling the government what needs to happen for many years. We know what needs to be done, and the government should be taking action today.
The epidemic declaration in new legislation would be a small but "crucial" step toward protecting women from violence, she said.
"The very first and most crucial step is declaring intimate partner violence an epidemic," she said.
In April, the Ford government announced it would support Bill 173, the Intimate Partner Violence Act, after almost a year spent refusing to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic.
However, that government support was to send the bill to a legislative committee for study, rather than passing it into law immediately - a move both Stiles and local MPP Lisa Gretzky (NDP - Windsor West) have been calling for.
Declaring intimate partner violence an epidemic in Ontario is one of 86 recommendations made in the Renfrew Report - a coroner's inquest into the murders of three women in Renfrew County by a mutual ex-partner.
"The government has these recommendations," Gretzky said Wednesday. "If they're talking to survivors, if they're talking to victims' families, then they would know what to do to move forward."
Participants in a roundtable discussion on intimate partner violence are shown on Wednesday, June 19, 2024, at Mackenzie Hall in Windsor.
In an email to the Star on Thursday, the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services said the legislative committee will "carefully assess current available programs and root causes," and that it is "committed to taking all necessary steps to ensure the safety and wellbeing of everyone in the province."
The email said the province is investing $1.4 billion over four years into Ontario-STANDS, a plan aimed at gender-based violence.
"When it comes to preventing violence against women and children and helping survivors, our government is focused on actions that deliver concrete, tangible results," the email from the ministry's media team said.
Related
After another woman killed, MPP Gretzky presses Ontario government to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic
Slain Windsor woman's mom urges support of intimate partner violence law
Mother of slain Windsor woman still concerned after province reverses position on intimate partner violence definition
Despite calls for the Ford government to declare intimate partner violence an epidemic before the summer break, that did not happen. Ontario's legislature rose early for summer recess on June 6, and the house is not expected to return until mid-October.
"I don't know where Doug Ford is right now," said Stiles. "Maybe he's at the cottage. I have no idea. But we're going to keep doing the work that's necessary to ensure that not one more woman is killed in this province."
Nearly 100 municipalities across the province have declared intimate partner violence an epidemic, including Windsor, Tecumseh, Essex, Lakeshore, Kingsville, and Amherstburg.
tcampbell@postmedia.com( tcampbell@postmedia.com )
twitter.com/wstarcampbell

Document 425 of 500

Catholic board approves $322.2M budget for coming school year

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/catholic-board-approves-322-2m-budget-coming/docview/3070171824/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Catholic+board+approves+%24322.2M+budget+for+coming+school+year&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trustees for the local Catholic school board on Tuesday night approved a $322.2-million budget for the 2024-25 school year.
With a projected enrolment of 22,145 students - roughly 1,000 more than the previous year - the new Windsor-Essex Catholic District School Board budget represents an 8.9 per cent increase.
"Every budget plan presents a different set of challenges, but administration has done an excellent job of considering community feedback, while balancing our spending commitments with eliminating the in-year deficit," board of trustees chair Fulvio Valentinis said in a media release Tuesday night.
The board is expected to see $319.9 million of in-year grant revenue. After the $322.2 million in expenses, the board expects an in-year deficit of $2.3 million.
The largest expenses are for increased staffing - mainly due to increased enrolment - as well as hikes in salaries due to labour agreements and forecasted wage adjustments associated with the resolution of Bill 124. The bill from the Doug Ford Conservatives that capped salary increases for broader public sector workers was ruled unconstitutional, and the government repealed it.
Staffing accounts for 79 per cent of the board's budget. Salaries and benefits are estimated at $253.6 million for the next school year, up roughly 11 per cent from the year prior.
The board expects to spend about $42 million on special education expenses, roughly $5.6 million more than its grant allocation.
The approved budget is slightly different from the draft budget presented to the board during its June 11 meeting. Based on feedback from trustees, two staff positions were re-added to the budget: an itinerant teacher for the deaf and an itinerant teacher for the blind. Without the change, the duties of both positions would have been reassigned.
The budget includes an in-year deficit elimination plan that will use about $1.6 million from the board's accumulated surplus to fund post-retirement benefit amortization expenses. The plan also includes a $660,000 reduction to the board's contingency provision.
"Developing our budget is an exhaustive process, but throughout it all, we have listened to the feedback from our trustees and our community, and have delivered a solid plan that meets the needs of our students and their families," said Emelda Byrne, the board's director of education.
"It provides stability as well as the dexterity we require to make adjustments throughout the coming school year, if necessary. This is a plan that we can all be comfortable with." tcampbell@postmedia.com twitter.com/wstarcampbell

Document 426 of 500

School board seeks funds from province to fix $6.4M budget deficit

Author: Macleod, Brian

Publication info: Macleod, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/school-board-seeks-funds-province-fix-6-4m-budget/docview/3070171795/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=School+board+seeks+funds+from+province+to+fix+%246.4M+budget+deficit&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Macleod%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The local public school board on Tuesday approved a $532-million budget containing a $6.38-million deficit, but more job cuts could be coming. Despite choosing an administration-provided option containing the least number of staffing cuts - 21 positions - the Greater Essex County District School Board might still eliminate additional personnel and services.
In approving next year's spending, trustees at the special budget meeting also directed a review of all expenses not funded by the province - much of which is the cost of special education teachers and social workers.
By approving the deficit budget, the board is now mandated by Ontario's education ministry to provide a plan to eliminate what is classified as structural deficit within two years.
The budget and that plan must be submitted to the ministry by June 28.
There are more than 800 unfunded positions in the budget in special education, consultants, para-professionals and education support workers, and they represent the major portion of the expenses to be reviewed by the board.
Unfunded spending in the budget includes $10 million for special education, $8.1 million for supply staff, $2.9 million for student transportation, $1 million for justice, inclusion, equity and diversity, and $5.6 million for federal statutory benefits such as employment insurance and pensions.
Board chair Gale Hatfield is urging the community to put pressure on the province to provide more funding to avoid additional staff cuts.
"I think our community or school communities or parents need to rise up, say we're mad as hell and we're not going to take it anymore when it comes to underfunding public education," Hatfield said. She abstained from the budget vote, explaining she didn't know what the deficit elimination plan will ultimately contain.
Trustee Julia Burgess asked administration whether a review of staffing and funding cuts would be onerous to complete.
"We already know everything that's unfunded," director of education Vicki Houston replied. "We know, and I caution that a majority of that, unfortunately, is positions."
Trustee Sarah Cipkar, who chairs the finance committee, conceded the currently approved budget may not gain approval from the ministry. "I think at this point ... this whole budget is a risk for us."
Asked for her opinion on how the ministry may view the deficit reduction plan, board superintendent of business Shelley Armstrong was blunt: "I think that it lacks specificity ... I think the ministry's going to look at this and say, 'How does this actually adjust your cost structure?'"And so I think it's vague. I think that, more likely than not, it will probably come back either to be more specific, or they may tell us to go back and do more work. But they will certainly increase the level of monitoring that we will be under."
Trustees settled on using last year's deficit elimination plan, which included reviewing program delivery and boundaries for French immersion, transportation operator agreements, and all non-salary budgeted expenses.
They also directed that a letter be sent to the ministry pleading for more money to pay for employment insurance, pensions, short-term supply teachers, transportation costs, inflationary adjustments, special education and mental health supports.
Mario Spagnuolo, president of the Greater Essex Elementary Teachers'Federation of Ontario, said after the meeting the decision by trustees to review unfunded spending means re-examining positions trustees were trying to save.
"It's very worrisome because they've already cut 21 positions in special education.
"And if they're going to start reviewing the unfunded positions, those are the positions that we were talking about last week, which was social workers and psychologists, the speech pathologist. Those are critical to the kids that need it the most."
Spagnuolo told trustees that "the provincial government is using trustees as their shield to continue to devalue our schools. If it means the ministry takes the board over, so be it.
"Let our provincial elected officials be accountable for the slashing of essential services to students in need."
Erin Roy, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers Federation District 9, cited a report from the Canadian Centre for Policy alternatives that concludes Ontario is spending $1,500 less per student than it did in 2018-19.
She commended trustees for taking a stand against underfunding.
"Let's invite (Premier) Doug Ford (and) MPPs to our budget meetings," she said. "Let them witness first-hand the impact of funding cuts on our schools."
Lindsay Nhan, a parent of three children in elementary school and a teacher with the board, told trustees students who enter Grade 9 with below-average literacy skills face a daunting future.
"These students can be more difficult to engage and become discouraged leading to poor attendance, therefore low achievement and a vicious cycle that is difficult to break," she said.
Nhan stressed the importance of the RISE special education program in elementary schools to help students with their literacy skills.
Parent Caiti Casey said her seven-year-old daughter has several neurological conditions. She told trustees the small rural school her daughter attends is losing one of its three educational assistants.
Casey fears her daughter "is going to be the one that slips through the cracks because her ability to have the support she needs to learn in an equitable way and have access to her classroom will not be there." bamacleod@postmedia.com

Subject: Workforce planning; Special education; Students; Funding cuts; Social workers; Trustees; Elementary schools; Teachers; Budget deficits; School boards; Literacy

Windsor school board risks provincial takeover with $532M budget

Author: MacLeod, Brian

Publication info: MacLeod, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/windsor-school-board-risks-provincial-takeover/docview/3070016737/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Windsor+school+board+risks+provincial+takeover+with+%24532M+budget&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=MacLeod%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The local public school board on Tuesday approved a $532-million budget containing a $6.38-million deficit, but more job cuts could be coming.
Despite choosing an administration-provided option containing the least number of staffing cuts - 21 positions - the Greater Essex County District School Board might still eliminate additional personnel and services.
In approving next year's spending, trustees at the special budget meeting also directed a review of all expenses not funded by the province - much of which is the cost of special education teachers and social workers.
By approving the deficit budget, the board is now mandated by Ontario's education ministry to provide a plan to eliminate what is classified as structural deficit within two years.
The budget and that plan must be submitted to the ministry by June 28.
There are more than 800 unfunded positions in the budget in special education, consultants, para-professionals and education support workers, and they represent the major portion of the expenses to be reviewed by the board.
Unfunded spending in the budget includes $10 million for special education, $8.1 million for supply staff, $2.9 million for student transportation, $1 million for justice, inclusion, equity and diversity, and $5.6 million for federal statutory benefits such as employment insurance and pensions.
Board chair Gale Hatfield is urging the community to help put pressure on the province to provide more funding to avoid additional staff cuts.
"I think our community or school communities or parents need to rise up, say we're mad as hell and we're not going to take it anymore when it comes to underfunding public education," Hatfield said. She abstained from the budget vote, explaining she didn't know what the deficit elimination plan will ultimately contain.
Trustee Julia Burgess asked administration whether a review of staffing and funding cuts would be onerous to complete.
"We already know everything that's unfunded," director of education Vicki Houston replied. "We know, and I caution that a majority of that, unfortunately, is positions. We've said that an abundant number of times."
This whole budget is a risk for us
Trustee Sarah Cipkar, who chairs the finance committee, conceded the currently approved budget may not gain approval from the ministry. "I think at this point ... this whole budget is a risk for us."
Asked for her opinion on how the ministry may view the deficit reduction plan, board superintendent of business Shelley Armstrong was blunt: "I think that it lacks specificity ... I think the ministry's going to look at this and say, 'How does this actually adjust your cost structure?'
"And so I think it's vague. I think that, more likely than not, it will probably come back either to be more specific, or they may tell us to go back and do more work. But they will certainly increase the level of monitoring that we will be under."
Trustees settled on using last year's deficit elimination plan, which included reviewing program delivery and boundaries for French immersion, transportation operator agreements, and all non-salary budgeted expenses.
They also directed that a letter be sent to the ministry pleading for more money to pay for employment insurance, pensions, short-term supply teachers, transportation costs, inflationary adjustments, special education and mental health supports.
Erin Roy, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers Federation District 9, speaks to Greater Essex County District School Board trustees during the budget deliberations Tuesday, June 18, 2024.
Mario Spagnuolo, president of the Greater Essex Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario, said after the meeting the decision by trustees to review unfunded spending means re-examining positions trustees were trying to save.
"It's very worrisome because they've already cut 21 positions in special education.
"And if they're going to start reviewing the unfunded positions, those are the positions that we were talking about last week, which was social workers and psychologists, the speech pathologist. Those are critical to the kids that need it the most."
Spagnuolo told trustees that "the provincial government is using trustees as their shield to continue to devalue our schools. If it means the ministry takes the board over, so be it.
"Let our provincial elected officials be accountable for the slashing of essential services to students in need."
Erin Roy, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers Federation District 9, cited a report from the Canadian Centre for Policy alternatives that concludes Ontario is spending $1,500 less per student than it did in 2018-19.
She commended trustees for taking a stand against underfunding. "Let's invite (Premier) Doug Ford (and) MPPs to our budget meetings," she said. "Let them witness first-hand the impact of funding cuts on our schools."
Lindsay Nhan, a parent of three children in elementary school and a teacher with the board, told trustees students who enter Grade 9 with below-average literacy skills face a daunting future.
"These students can be more difficult to engage and become discouraged leading to poor attendance, therefore low achievement and a vicious cycle that is difficult to break," she said.
Nhan stressed the importance of the RISE special education program in elementary schools to help students with their literacy skills.

Document 428 of 500

Team points to success in domestic violence interventions

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/team-points-success-domestic-violence/docview/3068308314/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Team+points+to+success+in+domestic+violence+interventions&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Barely over a month after launching a new program pledging to intervene earlier in cases of intimate partner violence, local police and a mental health agency have already referred 22 individuals to additional support.
The goal of the IPV Early Intervention and Prevention program - a partnership between Windsor Police Service and Family Services Windsor-Essex which kicked off May 6 - is to intervene before calls escalate into more serious threats.
So far this year, more than 600 intimate partner violence complaints, which have not resulted in criminal charges, were filed in the region.
"By intervening early, we can provide the necessary support to those in need and help stop the escalation of conflict," said Ciara Holmes, director or mental health and counselling programs at Family Services Windsor-Essex.
Both partners held a press conference Wednesday at the downtown police headquarters to tout the program's early success.
Moving forward, all reports of intimate partner violence to the Windsor Police Service - incidents that have yet to produce any arrests or criminal charges - are redirected to the IPV Early Intervention and Prevention program.
A team of representatives will then follow up with complainants upon their consent, to assess their needs and connect them with support services, including; counselling, safety planning, and more.
"This partnership provides us with an important new tool to curb this disturbing trend and empower victims to better protect themselves from harm," said Windsor police Insp. Andrew Randall.
In Ontario last year, at least 58 women were killed by an intimate partner who was either charged, convicted, or found responsible.
Windsor joined several municipalities across the province in declaring intimate partner violence an epidemic after a city council decision in July.
Following the killing of another woman by her former intimate partner in Belleville last month, local MPP Lisa Gretzky (NDP - Windsor West) renewed her call for the Ford government to support a private member's bill declaring it an epidemic in Ontario.
The Ford government has instead sent the proposed opposition party legislation, Bill 173, Intimate Partner Violence Act, which was co-sponsored by Gretzky, to committee for review.
For immediate assistance, call 911 or the Hiatus House 24/7 crisis line at 519-252-7781.
Hiatus House provides crisis services, emergency shelter, and support services to women affected by domestic violence.
All referrals to the IPV Early Intervention and Prevention program are made through Windsor Police Service. mmazak@postmedia.com

Subject: Prevention programs; Mental health; Domestic violence; Intervention

Document 430 of 500

Senate delays vote on creation of Ojibway urban park

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/senate-delays-vote-on-creation-ojibway-urban-park/docview/3067612748/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Senate+delays+vote+on+creation+of+Ojibway+urban+park&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's Senate has postponed until the fall a final vote on Windsor MP Brian Masse's bill to create and protect an Ojibway National Urban Park.
With other pending legislation deemed more urgent ahead of the current session end, senators recently decided to wait to vote on Bill C-248 until after their 12-week summer break.
Negotiations to finalize amendments to the private member's bill were taking more time than anticipated, according to Masse.
Rather than a setback, however, Masse said it was a "wise move" that will allow more time to address specifics of the bill with the City of Windsor, the province and other stakeholders.
"We want this bill as quickly as possible, but at the same time, we don't want to forego a better product at the end of the day," he told the Windsor Star.
Over the past couple of months, witnesses have testified before the Senate's standing committee on energy, the environment and natural resources, including Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens, MPP Andrew Dowie (C - Windsor-Tecumseh), Caldwell First Nation Chief Mary Duckworth, and others.
During those meetings, concerns arose concerning privately owned parcels of land located inside the proposed boundaries outlined in Bill C-248.
Masse said they are starting to receive the co-ordinates of these properties to make modifications.
Questions also surfaced regarding the City of Windsor's ability to maintain access to critical infrastructure - hydro corridors, municipal roads, sewers, water lines and gas lines - within the proposed area.
At the most recent meeting on May 23, Dowie told senators the provincial government requested that the bill be amended to exclude the Ojibway Prairie Provincial Nature Reserve from the description.
Last year, the province indicated an interest in transferring the 105-hectare parcel, currently managed by Ontario Parks, for inclusion in the proposed Ojibway National Urban Park.
Since the provincial land - a significant part of the overall park - will be automatically surrendered to the federal government if Bill C-248 passes, Dowie is urging Parks Canada to negotiate an official land transfer with the province beforehand.
Masse (NDP - Windsor West) said he hopes the extra time will help facilitate these discussions between the province and Parks Canada, which were delayed while waiting for the bill to reach the committee phase.
Although Masse said he is concerned the delay pushes the vote closer to the federal election scheduled for October 2025, he said the decision shows that "there's really a genuine interest" among senators "to help Windsor achieve this goal." mmazak@postmedia.com

Canada's senators delay vote on Windsor national urban park bill

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline.

Document 432 of 500

Windsor railway concerned over national urban park's impact on train service

Author: Holmes-Hill, Millar

Publication info: Holmes-Hill, Millar. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/windsor-railway-concerned-over-national-urban/docview/3067412338/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Windsor+railway+concerned+over+national+urban+park%27s+impact+on+train+service&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Holmes-Hill%2C+Millar&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The president of a Windsor-based railway company is concerned that MP Brian Masse's private members' bill to establish a national urban park could threaten service through a local rail corridor.
Essex Terminal Railway (ETR), which connects Windsor to Amherstburg through LaSalle, operates along tracks that fall within the coordinates of Bill C-248, which would establish the Ojibway National Urban Park.
"We have concerns about how the bill is going to impact rail access to our valued customers that are dependent on rail transportation," ETR president Tony De Thomasis told the Windsor Star.
According to Parks Canada, if the bill is passed in its current form, the targeted lands will be transferred to the federal government.
That means any private, municipal, or provincial infrastructure in the bill's boundaries, from sewer lines to roadway allowances - and in this case, part of the rail corridor - become subject to the Canada National Parks Act and its regulations.
De Thomasis said he's unaware of the bill's specifics, but said the company has operated the tracks next to Ojibway Parkway for many years.
He said ETR offers daily service to customers in the lumber, steel, agricultural, liquid petroleum gas and other industrial sectors which have access to the Detroit River and move cargo to the world.
"Without that rail access, there's potentially a significant negative impact on how they move their product to the market," said De Thomasis. "Rail plays a critical part in moving commodities and cargo that arrive in Windsor-Essex through the Great Lakes."
This December 2023 concept drawing for one of the design options for a wildlife crossing over Ojibway Parkway and Essex Terminal Railway tracks shows the train tracks in relation to parts of the proposed Ojibway National Urban Park.
While Masse (NDP - Windsor West) is looking to establish the park through his private members' bill, MP Irek Kusmierczyk (L - Windsor-Tecumseh) is trying to make it happen through a parallel process with Parks Canada.
Since 2021, the agency has been working with the City of Windsor, local First Nations, and community partners to determine park boundaries and address their concerns.
Kusmierczyk said the Senate has raised some serious issues that need answers, such as privately owned parcels of land and conflicting business interests within the bill's proposed boundaries.
"That shipping hub is an absolutely vital piece of our economy," Kusmierczyk said. "You also have Windsor Salt that has mining rights there. Through the Parks Canada process, you have those consultations and discussions before anything is set in stone."
Essex Terminal Rail president Tony De Thomasis is shown at the Ojibway Railway Yard on June 6, 2024.
The Senate's standing committee on energy, the environment and natural resources held its second hearing on Masse's bill on May 23.
Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens, Caldwell First Nation Chief Mary Duckworth, Friends of Ojibway committee chair Mike Fisher, and the University of Windsor's National Urban Park Hub co-lead Anneke Smit testified.
During the hearing, Dilkens proposed an amendment to introduce a streamlined process for boundary adjustments to accommodate the addition and removal of land.
"It is critical for the bill to address the management of existing infrastructure within the park's boundaries," said Dilkens.
"To mitigate potential complications and assure continued access to essential infrastructure - such as storm sewers, utilities, and transportation corridors - we propose amending the bill to exclude lands containing such infrastructure from the scope of the park."
Related
Dowie's Ontario urban park bill clears second reading
Province seeks to withdraw land from Windsor's national urban park bill
Senators also expressed concerns about navigating the operation of Windsor Salt - a mine producing up to three million metric tons of salt annually - which works underground within the proposed park area covered by Bill C-248.
The Windsor Star made several attempts to contact Windsor Salt for comment but did not hear back.
mholmeshill@postmedia.com

Document 433 of 500

Guest column: Beer will soon muscle aside food in Ontario corner stores

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-beer-will-soon-muscle-aside-food/docview/3067277453/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+Beer+will+soon+muscle+aside+food+in+Ontario+corner+stores&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Robert Fox
There is good reason to question the logic and motives of Doug Ford's push to fast-track beer and wine sales in Ontario convenience stores, even more so given that hundreds of millions of dollars that might better be spent on health, housing or education are being diverted to accelerate this drive.
A neglected consequence of this misguided boondoggle is its impact on ready-access to good food for the most vulnerable.
Many people depend on corner stores to meet their daily food needs - or fill in gaps when they run short. While corner store prices aren't usually the lowest in price, when you factor in access and convenience the value proposition can be compelling.
For the elderly and people with limited mobility, the corner store is often the only option at hand. Immigrant families, accustomed to shopping daily from trusted neighbourhood merchants, may feel more comfortable running up to the corner store to get their milk, eggs, noodles and rice. And for those without a car or with limited access to transit, the chains and big box stores are just too far away.
Most corner stores are already heavy on chips, sodas and cigarettes and light on fruit, vegetables or fixings for dinner. But the situation will deteriorate further given the Ford government's drive to increase access to alcohol.
In the competition for limited shelf space, cans of beer are likely to win out over cans of beans, corn or diced tomatoes. Bottles of wine will edge out bread, cereal, lentils and chickpeas. Cheese, hummus and ham, already hard to find among the iced coffees and carbonated waters, will be displaced by ciders and coolers.
Too many of the poor, frail and isolated already live in "food deserts" - where access to fresh produce, nutritious food and low-cost staples is very limited. Yet this problem is likely to be gravely aggravated in the coming years.
While Ford pitches his push to ease access to alcohol as "all about increased choice and convenience," his plan will almost certainly reduce choice, creating a dynamic where ever more neighbourhood stores get out of the food business in favour of alcohol products with their higher margins and longer shelf lives.
As a result, we'll have no choice but to become ever more reliant on the big grocery chains, consolidating their stranglehold on food. We'll also set back the walkable, bikeable city, forcing us to drive or take transit to get a loaf of bread or a dozen eggs - or more reliant on expensive, impractical and emission-spewing delivery services.
As with cannabis, we'll soon have a wine vendor on every block, but many of our parents, children and neighbours won't be able to buy what they need for breakfast, lunch and dinner in their neighbourhood, leaving many vulnerable citizens without options.
A compelling case for jump-starting ready access to alcohol has yet to be made and there are implications for public health, the provincial treasury and the environment that need to be further studied. As part of that review, it's important we consider the knock-on effect of turning our corner stores into beer stores.
Food prices are top-of-mind for most Canadians. For too many among us, they pose a daily challenge. But for the most vulnerable, accessibility can be as important as affordability. The elderly and isolated will bear the brunt of this wrong-headed policy, finding themselves unable to rely on their corner store for food basics, scrambling to find and afford food from other sources - ever more at risk.
Robert Fox is an Ottawa resident active on anti-poverty and human rights issues.

Subject: Alcohol; Liquor; Beer; Food; Neighborhoods

Document 434 of 500

Ontario adding hundreds of beds to jails; few details on new programs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-adding-hundreds-beds-jails-few-details-on/docview/3066467755/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+adding+hundreds+of+beds+to+jails%3B+few+details+on+new+programs&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Randy Richmond
LONDON, ONT. - Ontario is adding hundreds of beds and staff to the province's jail system in what the Progressive Conservative government is touting as a significant effort to improve community safety.
But there was no word on whether programs to rehabilitate inmates before they return to those communities would also be expanded.
"Our government is taking action to make one of the most significant correctional capacity increases in our province in many years," Solicitor General Michael Kerzner announced at Elgin-Middlesex Detention Centre (EMDC) in London on Monday.
More than 630 additional beds and up to 200 more staff will be added to Ontario's adult correctional centres, he said.
Those staff could include correctional, nursing and support staff, Kerzner added.
Although the announcement touted the opening of new beds, about 400 of those beds were used in the past.
The government is reopening and repurposing the 110 beds at the Regional Intermittent Centre in London and the 320 beds at the Toronto Intermittent Centre at the Toronto South Detention Centre.
The centres were designed for people serving weekend sentences for less severe crimes, but the province announced in 2021 the centres were being shut down and more inmates would be placed under GPS monitoring systems.
The centres will be re-opened by 2026 and house minimum- and medium-risk inmates, the province announced.
The province is also adding 18 new beds at the Quinte Detention Centre in Napanee, bringing the institution's capacity to more than 300 beds, and will more than double capacity at the new Brockville Correctional Complex by adding 184 new beds, Kerzner announced.
The building of the Brockville complex and a new jail in Kemptville were announced in 2020.
The expansion announced Monday will "feature spaces to support mental and health services, inmate programming, and staff training, all things that can help reduce the risk of recidivism and help break the cycle of criminal behaviour," Kerzner said.
But asked later if the province was adding programming or resources for inmates, Kerzner couldn't provide a clear answer.
"I'm happy to carry a message that public safety matters to people in our communities. To the people stealing our cars, committing criminality, and all the violent and repeat offenders, we will lock you up," he said at first.
When asked again, Kerzner said he's met with support staff across the province.
"I've met with the chaplains. I've met with the Native Inmate Liaison Officers. I've met with the program directors to ensure that we're providing the supports we need," he said.
Related
Loved ones mourn man's death at London jail - the 22nd since 2009
Every inmate death adds to sorrow, concern about London's troubled provincial jail: lawyer
Since 2009, 22 inmates at EMDC have died and coroner's inquests have revealed the prevalence of drugs and overdoses in the system.
Asked how the new resources would prevent deaths or drug smuggling, Kerzner said the Conservative government has made "record investments" in corrections, including hiring more than 2,000 correctional officers over the past few years.
"When the Liberals were in office, they brought the correctional system, they brought public safety to its knees," he said.

Subject: Prisons; Public safety

Document 435 of 500

'Why should Canadians trust you with their vote?'; Review; Author takes on challenge of humanizing Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre

Author: Portman, Jamie

Publication info: Portman, Jamie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-should-canadians-trust-you-with-their-vote/docview/3065664837/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Why+should+Canadians+trust+you+with+their+vote%3F%27%3B+Review%3B+Author+takes+on+challenge+of+humanizing+Conservative+Leader+Pierre+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=Portman%2C+Jamie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre: A Political Life Andrew Lawton Sutherland House There's something undeniably engaging about this snapshot of a scrawny Calgary teenager busily on the phone hustling up Reform Party votes.
"Hello Mrs. Smith, I'm Pierre Poilievre, calling on behalf of Jason Kenney, the tax fighter. Mrs. Smith, do you support Jean Chretien's high-tax, soft-oncrime, anti-Alberta polices? I didn't think you would ..."
It's a craftily orchestrated moment in author Andrew Lawton's rewarding exploration of the Poilievre phenomenon. It not only reveals the young Pierre's early passion and commitment, it also shows that his rhetorical skills, his ease with persuading and communicating, started early. But this book still must deal with a troublesome riddle. What is Pierre Poilievre really like? Lawton has sought to deliver a measured portrait of a presumptive prime minister in waiting, a portrait that sees him dutifully setting his own conservatism aside in conceding that, in the course of Poilievre's impressive ascent, there have been troublesome warning signals along the way and questions still to be answered.
So yes, he writes, Poilievre and the Conservative party he now leads seem certain to win the next election. But then comes this perplexed observation. "What he will do with a mandate is less clear."
It's an unexpected virtue of this book that Lawton identifies something not easily acknowledged amid all the hoopla - the enigma that is Pierre Poilievre. We're not talking here about this author's worthy effort to humanize a politician whose mean-minded, attack-dog skills (and they are skills) continue to leave many Canadians, even those with right-wing leanings, offended and hostile. The challenge here is that Poilievre himself remains a work in progress, and that ambushes may be lurking if you write about him.
This biography is subtitled "a political life" - and that's appropriate to the image of a man who has lived and breathed politics since his teenage years, who has tenaciously embraced a vision of freedom that he says currently does not exist in Canada, and whose own political advancement has revealed a discipline and determination that some would say borders on the ruthless.
Fair-minded readers will not turn away from Lawton's attempt to soften the image of a man once described by former Liberal strategist Warren Kinsella as "loathsome ... a pestilence made flesh." We're reminded by Lawton that Poilievre is not the devil incarnate but rather the "son of a teenage mother, adopted by two school teachers, husband of a Venezuelan refugee and the father of a girl with autism ..." There are numerous endearing glimpses of Poilievre the human being in his book - the night in a Manotick pub when he imitated Edith Piaf singing La Vie En Rose, his hatred of the nickname "Skippy" impishly conferred on him by his pal John Baird, the significance in his life (a great story) of a bottle of Johnnie Walker Blue Label whisky.
Yet the enigma persists.
"Poilievre has been so shrewd and calculating throughout his political career that even his friends have wondered at times what's real and what's a persona," Lawton writes, adding that some diehard Tories fear that a Poilievre in power will "dilute" his conservatism if expedient. These private concerns underline a particularly valuable aspect of a book that not only constitutes an account of a remarkable young politician's rise but also chronicles the turbulent history of Canadian conservatism in recent decades. When Brian Mulroney antagonized much of the country by rolling the dice on behalf of Quebec nationalism, he helped spark the rise of the Reform Party, the eventual disintegration of the hallowed "Progressive" Conservative Party of Canada, the transformative entry of Reform and its variations into the fold, the reign of Stephen Harper, the growing rifts within a reborn Conservative party and the internal divisiveness that led to the expulsion of Andrew Scheer and Erin O'Toole as party leaders.
Poilievre, because he started so young, has played a part in this history, and his ability to confidently navigate his way through treacherous waters is striking.
Yet, while there is no doubt about his hawkishness as a fiscal Conservative, raging against what he sees as the destructive economic follies of the Trudeau Liberals, he is more of a libertarian than a social Conservative. That makes him interesting. So that leads to anther question: what kind of party does he really lead? American-born academic Tom Flanagan, the former University of Calgary professor who masterminded Harper's 2006 campaign, has grumbled that Canada is not yet a conservative or Conservative country. That suggests that, in the governance of Canada, only the political centre may be capable of maintaining a hold. Does Poilievre privately concur? Meanwhile, he still creates unease among many. His adeptness, in Question Period and elsewhere, in issuing sulphurous sound bites about his perceived enemies may win applause from a red-meat base, but a talent for invective doesn't make him likable to many voters who want Trudeau out but are offended by Poilievre's lack of civility and penchant for simplistic pronouncements. Hence a book like this may play a useful role on the eve of an election whose outcome may depend on which party leader is less disliked.
Lawton is too sure-footed to be accused of hagiography, but he is effective in reminding Poilievre skeptics that the positives are there if you look for them.
By all accounts, Poilievre, who represents an Ottawa riding, is a model MP who will go the extra mile for his constituents. When he was still a teenager, heavily involved with Reform, Kenney called him a "genius" and praised his discipline, intellectual curiosity and principled conduct. Indeed, with respect to the latter quality, Poilievre was so uncomfortable with a Bulgari watch given him by the government of Kuwait during a parliamentary trip there that he turned it over to the federal ethics commissioner on his return. Furthermore Poilievre is not averse to bipartisanship, joining forces with the NDP's Pat Martin to ensure passage of the federal Accountability Act through a minority Liberal Parliament.
However, Lawton also reminds us of the negatives: the type of recklessness that led to ill-considered remarks about residential schools, followed by a dressing down from Harper that brought him close to tears; recurring reports from various sources that he was so insufferable as a cabinet minister in the Harper government that staff turnovers were constant; his vocal support of the infamous anti-vaccine Freedom Convoy that paralyzed Ottawa's downtown during the pandemic; a loathing for much of the media and especially the CBC.
Poilievre, a brilliant tactician, has honed his social media skills so successfully that he sees no need to communicate through traditional channels. To reach his base, he no longer relies on the media, particularly the so-called "mainstream" media, viewing it as a hotbed of leftist, anti-Conservative hostility - an odd belief considering the political inclinations of much of Canada's media ownership.
Furthermore, unscripted media encounters can place a politician on the defensive. There's every indication that loss of control is something that Poilievre cannot abide.
"I'm not interested in starting fights I can't win," he says. When unexpectedly cornered, he can turn cocky and unpleasant which is what happened a few months ago when British Columbia journalist Don Urquhart started asking him a few questions in an apple orchard and an apple-munching Poilievre proceeded to humiliate him with a succession of smug Socratic evasions.
The confrontation, which some believe was carefully orchestrated by the Conservatives, went viral on the internet and became required viewing for right-wingers. But other Canadians, horrified at what it suggested about Poilievre's personality, thought differently. A portion of the exchange appears as a dubious promotional device on the back of the book jacket. Take note, however, that the hapless journalist gets the final word. "Why should Canadians trust you with their vote?"

Subject: Conservatism; Canadians; Books; Politicians; Political parties

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Company / organization: Name: Reform Party; NAICS: 813940

Title: 'Why should Canadians trust you with their vote?'; Review; Author takes on challenge of humanizing Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre
