Document 488 of 500

Carney snubs tories' invite; Parl. committee; Speculation rises on ambition to lead Liberals

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carney-snubs-tories-invite-parl-committee/docview/3047478273/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carney+snubs+tories%27+invite%3B+Parl.+committee%3B+Speculation+rises+on+ambition+to+lead+Liberals&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conservatives are hoping to grill former central banker Mark Carney during a House of Commons committee meeting on the carbon tax and affordability, arguing that he may be a future contender for leader of the Liberal Party of Canada.
Even though Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has repeatedly said he is staying on as leader of his party, the ongoing speculation about Carney's political prospects has intensified since he delivered a speech Monday evening during which he criticized the federal budget.
In a press release, the Conservative party said its MPs will be moving a motion Tuesday to invite Carney to appear at the House of Commons committee on finance.
"It's clear that Carney is positioning for Trudeau's job and since he's attempting to get as much media attention as possible, he should welcome the Conservative invitation to appear at committee," reads the unsigned release sent Friday morning.
In an email, Carney said that he has not yet received the invitation from the committee but that "releasing it first to journalists betrays the performative nature of the request."
He added that he had already accepted a prior invitation to appear at the Senate Committee on Banking, Commerce and the Economy, on May 8, which he said "will focus on Canada's opportunities to build clean growth and new jobs."
"I'm not interested in playing games on such important issues for Canada's economic future," he wrote in the email.
"Canadians rightly expect our elected parliamentarians to produce solutions to Canada's challenges, rather than performing political stunts or mindlessly repeating simplistic slogans."
Carney delivered a speech earlier this week as part of an event held in Toronto by Canada 2020, a progressive think-tank with close ties to the Liberal party and of which he is an advisory board chair.
He made some subtle digs at the spending under the current Liberal government, saying that governments that "spend too much and invest too little will eventually pay a heavy price" while countries that "welcome and celebrate risk takers will thrive."
The federal budget, which forecasts a deficit of nearly $40 billion this year, has been under fire for its increase in taxation on capital gains, which will affect individuals who make large profits by selling a second property, or corporations selling assets.
Business groups have argued that the tax hike will stifle innovation and drive away entrepreneurship in Canada.
Carney insisted that the new economic era Canada finds itself in will "demand fiscal discipline and a relentless focus on delivery, rather than reflex spending that only treats the symptom but doesn't cure the disease."
But his most pointed attacks were directed at Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, whom he accused of using current anxieties "as an opportunity to stoke anger."
"After all, people don't demolish things when they're positive or optimistic. I know this from my time in the U.K. where for years the rallying cry of Brexiters was 'broken Britain'and their solution to 'take back control'was actually code for 'tear down your future,'" he said.
"When politicians claim our great democracies are broken, it's not because they want to fix them. It's because they want a licence to demolish."
Carney also took aim at the Conservative leader's fierce opposition to the carbon tax. "Remember that when he shouts, 'Axe the tax,'he's really whispering, 'Can the plan,'and leaving us with nothing. Demolition is not a plan. And slogans, denials, and defeatism won't make our businesses more competitive, and they won't grow jobs at home."
In their press release Friday, Conservatives make it clear that they intend to draw parallels between Trudeau and Carney should he come to the finance committee.
"Canadians deserve to know how much Carney will increase Trudeau's carbon tax, how much more debt he will add, and whether he would destroy Canada's energy sector," reads the release.
They also accused him of endorsing the federal government's Housing Accelerator Fund - dubbed the "photoop fund" - which they claim "gives billions of taxpayer dollars to the same gatekeepers who created the housing crisis in the first place."
It remains to be seen whether or not the Conservatives will be able to table their motion to invite Carney to the finance committee Tuesday, as members will be starting the clause-by-clause consideration of C-59.
National Post calevesque@postmedia.com

Subject: Conservatism; Federal budget; Carbon; Congressional committees; Tax increases; Environmental tax

Document 489 of 500

Guest column: Ford's reversals show citizens can effect change

Author: Schmidt, Doug

Publication info: Schmidt, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/guest-column-fords-reversals-show-citizens-can/docview/3047098352/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Guest+column%3A+Ford%27s+reversals+show+citizens+can+effect+change&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Schmidt%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By: Robin Baranyai
Something Premier Doug Ford probably doesn't get enough credit for: He knows how to backtrack. As compliments go, it's undeniably backhanded, yet it's meant with genuine appreciation.
The world is full of politicians who won't back down from a policy, even in the face of intense criticism. The folksy premier certainly is capable of digging in his heels with great tenacity, and his bully-boy tactics can be audacious, but when the people object loudly enough and long enough, he has shown he can listen.
After months of resistance, Ford's Progressive Conservatives have agreed to back an NDP motion declaring intimate partner violence (IPV) an epidemic.
This kind of about-face is democracy at its best.
We saw it in 2019, when the government announced an under-baked overhaul of the Ontario Autism Program. Changes meant to reduce waiting lists would have booted thousands of kids from government-funded therapy, and doled out reduced funding based on family income rather than each child's needs. The public outcry was loud, sustained, and successful: the government reversed course on most of its changes.
We saw it again in 2022 when the province imposed a four-year contract on education workers, building in the notwithstanding clause to override their right to strike. The modestly paid workers walked out, and unions flexed the threat of a general strike. Within days, Bill 28 was not merely unanimously repealed; it was "deemed for all purposes never to have been in force."
Of course, there was no bigger reversal than the flip-flip-flop-flop drama over Greenbelt development. The premier clung far too long to a developer-friendly plan to rezone protected areas for housing development. Environmentalists hollered blue murder. As details of the preferential selection process emerged through two investigations, even steadfast Conservatives joined the chorus.
Ford finally acknowledged he'd made a mistake and said he was "very, very sorry." The government not only rolled back the zoning changes, but wrote the Greenbelt's boundaries into law.
Declaring IPV an epidemic is a largely symbolic gesture, but it signals a significant multi-system response to a lethal and ongoing health crisis. The measure topped a list of 86 recommendations by an inquest into a triple femicide in Renfrew County in 2015.
The government's shamefully dismissive response, completed last June, scoffed IPV "would not be considered an epidemic as it is not an infectious or communicable disease."
Perhaps they'd forgotten about the opioid epidemic, which is neither infectious nor communicable, and nevertheless has killed more than 40,000.
Across Canada, a woman is killed by her intimate partner every six days. The Ford government has devoted significant resources to fighting IPV and supporting survivors; its refusal to act on the inquest's top recommendation has been frankly baffling. Sustained pressure has come from dozens and dozens of municipalities, from Toronto to Windsor and Sault Ste. Marie, which have passed motions declaring IPV an epidemic, urging the province to follow suit.
It's never too late to do the right thing. Reversals don't happen every day. But they happen enough to remind us that our voices have power.
This is the essence of democracy.
It's not only about voting at four- or five-year intervals. We can effect change as informed and engaged citizens. There are more platforms to raise awareness than ever before. The pre-digital standbys also still work: old-school protest, letters to the editor, and contacting your MPP's constituency office.
The premier has forged ahead with some controversial policies - carving up swaths of Ontario Place for a private spa springs to mind - but he also has proven he can hear us, when we speak loudly enough.
Robin Baranyai is a Postmedia Network Inc. columnist.

Identifier / keyword: robin; baranyai; something; premier; probably; enough

Title: Guest column: Ford's reversals show citizens can effect change

Publication title: The Windsor Star (Online); Windsor

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Apr 26, 2024

Section: Letters

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Windsor

Country of publication: Canada

Language of publication: English

Document 491 of 500

Honda invests $15B to build Ontario EV plants, create battery supply chain

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/honda-invests-15b-build-ontario-ev-plants-create/docview/3047040164/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Honda+invests+%2415B+to+build+Ontario+EV+plants%2C+create+battery+supply+chain&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Honda Motor Company announced Thursday it was investing $15 billion to expand its Ontario footprint to build electric vehicles and create a supporting supply chain.
The investment will require four new plants in Ontario, including a new production facility and a battery plant to be located at Honda's Canadian headquarters in Alliston.
The two Alliston plants alone will create 1,000 new jobs.
"Honda will establish a battery integrated and complete EV battery supply chain in Canada," said Honda's global CEO Toshihiro Mibe.
"This will involve all parts of production - from raw materials to finished products. Canada is rich in natural resources and in clean energy.
"It helps Honda pursue our three targets of net zero by 2050, use of 100 per cent green energy and 100 per cent sustainable materials in production.
"Canada has a very attractive business environment for carmakers."
The investment is the largest in Canadian automotive history, more than double the value of the Volkswagen complex and triple the size of Windsor's NextStar Energy battery plant.
Thursday's announcement was made at the company's Alliston production facilities with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford, along with several other key cabinet ministers, in attendance "This is a historic day," Trudeau said.
"This announcement is creating in Canada a comprehensive electric vehicle supply chain from start to finish."
Trudeau and Ford confirmed that each level of government would be offering financial support and tax credits in the range of $2.5 billion.
Mibe expects full production to begin at the new plants in 2028 with the capacity to build 240,000 electric vehicles annually.
The auto analyst firm AutoForecast Solutions has said the automaker will build a small electric crossover carrying the Honda badge and an electric Acura crossover at the new plant.
The investment will also require Honda to build a supply chain to provide the EV components required.
Mibe said the automaker will have partnerships to build a cathode plant and a separator facility, which prevents battery cells from short circuiting.
Honda's proposed EV supply chain will include a new cathode facility through a joint venture partnership with Korean battery materials company POSCO Future M Co., Ltd. It will partner with Japanese chemical firm Asahi Kasei Corp. to build a separator facility.
"We will have more details in the next six months," Mibe said.
Southwestern Ontario is expected to be home to some of those plants. Honda officials and their partner companies have already visited Essex County to inspect a potential site.
The Windsor area is also already home to several companies supplying Honda, including at least three that will be supplying EV and battery-related components.
Jonathan Azzopardi, president of Laval Tool International in Tecumseh, said this means more opportunities for local suppliers.
"Honda is hard to get in with for a supplier, but once you do, they're very loyal," Azzopardi said. "They're a good OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) because they're interested in building their local supply chain, quality and making sure their suppliers are healthy.
"This is a huge opportunity." To help cement the deal with Honda, the two levels of government are offering a package of tax credits and infrastructure supports.
The provincial supports will be focused on the capital side to help provide the infrastructure required.
The federal government has shifted its focus from support based on production, such as for the LG/Stellantis and Volkswagen battery plants, to tax credits for the building of plants by supply chain companies.
Last week's budget included a new 10-per-cent tax credit to support EV supply chain investment.
Honda will also be able to access the Clean Technology Investment tax credit. That offers companies a 30 per cent credit for the cost of machinery and equipment required to manufacture electric vehicles.
"This deal outlines what you can expect when two governments work together to rebuild the economy, create jobs and bigger paycheques," Ford said.
"Today's announcement is a game changer for the industry." Dwaddell@postmedia.com Twitter.com/windstarwaddell

Subject: Supply chains; Tax credits; OEM; Clean technology; Electric vehicles; Suppliers

Business indexing term: Subject: Supply chains Tax credits OEM Suppliers

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Volkswagen AG; NAICS: 336110, 336390

Document 492 of 500

Honda's $15B Ontario auto investment 'huge opportunity' for Windsor

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hondas-15b-ontario-auto-investment-huge/docview/3046820511/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Honda%27s+%2415B+Ontario+auto+investment+%27huge+opportunity%27+for+Windsor&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Honda Motor Company announced Thursday it's investing $15 billion to expand its Ontario footprint to build electric vehicles and create a supporting supply chain.
And that huge commitment by the Japanese carmaker will trigger big economic spinoff benefits for the Windsor-Essex area, auto sector experts say.
The investment will require four new plants in Ontario, including a new production facility and a 36-gigawatt hour battery plant to be located at Honda's Canadian headquarters in Alliston. The latter two plants will create 1,000 new jobs.
"Honda will establish a battery integrated and complete EV battery supply chain in Canada," said Honda's global CEO Toshihiro Mibe.
"This will involve all parts of production - from raw materials to finished products. Canada is rich in natural resources and in clean energy.
"It helps Honda pursue our three targets of net zero by 2050, use of 100 per cent green energy and 100 per cent sustainable materials in production.
"Canada has a very attractive business environment for carmakers."
The investment is the largest in Canadian automotive history, more than double the value of the Volkswagen complex being built in St. Thomas and triple the announced price tag of Windsor's NextStar Energy battery plant.
Workers are shown on the vehicle assembly line on Thursday, April 25, 2024, shortly before it was shut down for an event at the Honda of Canada Manufacturing Plant 2 in Alliston, Ont.
Honda will partner with Korean battery materials company POSCO Future M Co. to build the cathode plant. It will partner with Japanese chemical firm Asahi Kasei Corp. to build a separator facility.
The automaker said it would announce the location of the cathode plant and separator plants in the next six months.
Southwestern Ontario is expected to be in line for at least one of those plants. The Star has confirmed Honda officials and their partner companies have already visited Essex County to inspect a potential site.
"It's a great show of confidence in the auto industry in Ontario," said Laval International president Jonathan Azzopardi, whose firm is located in Maidstone in Lakeshore.
"All of us who have invested in the industry should feel more confident about making those investments, especially right now with EV facing some headwinds.
"When auto plants come in, the Tier One's and Two's follow. I believe Ontario is one of the only jurisdictions that can produce everything a plant needs.
"This is a huge opportunity."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford speaks as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau looks on during Thursday's announcement in Alliston, Ont., that Honda is making the single-biggest investment ever in Canada's automotive sector.
Azzopardi said his main worry about the rapid rebirth of Ontario's auto sector, which has seen over $43 billion of new investments announced in the past three years, is the workforce.
"My one concern as an owner is the shrinking workforce," Azzopardi said. "We're not seeing anything come to the table to solve that.
"It's not just skilled trades we need, it's just good labour in general."
The Windsor area is already home to several companies supplying Honda, including at least three that will be supplying EV and battery-related components.
Joe Goncalves, vice-president of investment attraction and strategic initiatives with Invest WindsorEssex, said Honda's investment announcement on Thursday only makes the region more attractive to companies looking at locating here.
"This stimulation to automotive in Ontario turns us into an EV powerhouse," Goncalves said. "There will be spinoffs to the supply chain companies.
"It's a great opportunity for local companies to expand their relationship with Honda and it's a great opportunity for companies coming here to supply Honda both in Alliston and the U.S.
"They can do that from here."
The fact that Honda is also building cathode and separator plants in Ontario is a huge win. Goncalves added.
"Having cathodes and anodes, battery and EV assembly plants brings us to the next level in North America as a leader in the EV sector," Goncalves said.
Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, alongside head of Honda Global, Toshihiro Mibe and Ontario Premier Doug Ford, tours the manufacturing line at the Honda of Canada Manufacturing Plant 2 in Alliston, Ont. on April 25, 2024.
Trillium Network for Advance Manufacturing managing director Brendan Sweeney expects Windsor firms to benefit in ways beyond just being suppliers to Honda.
As home to Canada's first automotive battery plant to become operational when NextStar Energy launches in 2025, any local companies that were involved in the NextStar launch will have unique experience to offer.
"Being the first movers in the sector is going to be a big advantage for Windsor companies," Sweeney said. "Whether it was making something for NextStar, automation or engineering services, that experience is something Honda will be interested in."
Sweeney added the Honda announcement further secures Canada's place at the table as a major player in the future of EVs.
It also signals a further shifting of the industry's composition in Ontario, which now counts six automakers who will be manufacturing EVs in the province.
"It really signals the U.S. automakers, though they're still important, they're not leading anymore," Sweeney said.
"With Honda, Toyota, Volkswagen and (Europe-headquartered) Stellantis, though they have Detroit connections, we're getting a lot more international and a lot less American."
The significance of Thursday's announcement was illustrated by the presence of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Ontario Premier Doug Ford and key federal and provincial cabinet ministers in attendance in Alliston.
"This is a historic day," Trudeau said.
"This announcement is creating in Canada a comprehensive electric vehicle supply chain from start to finish."
Trudeau and Ford confirmed that each level of government would be offering financial support and tax credits in the range of $2.5 billion.
Honda's Mire expects full production to begin at the new plants in 2028 with the capacity to build 240,000 electric vehicles annually.
Workers are shown along the vehicle assembly line at the Honda of Canada Manufacturing Plant 2 in Alliston, Ont., on Thursday shortly before the Japanese automaker's major automotive investment announcement for Ontario.
Auto analyst firm AutoForecast Solutions has said the automaker will build a small electric crossover carrying the Honda badge and an electric Acura crossover at the new plant.
To help cement the deal with Honda, the two levels of government are offering a package of tax credits and infrastructure supports.
The provincial supports will be focused on the capital side to help provide the infrastructure required.
Related
Auto sector in 2023 fuelled by historic investments, transformative drive
E-vehicle sales slowdown won't slow 'spectacular' Ontario investments - Fedeli
Windsor lands another big battery supply chain investment
The federal government has shifted its focus from support based on production, such as for the LG/Stellantis and Volkswagen battery plants, to tax credits for the building of plants by supply chain companies.
Last week's budget included a new 10-per-cent tax credit to support EV supply chain investment.
Honda will also be able to access the Clean Technology Investment tax credit. That offers companies a 30-per-cent credit for the cost of machinery and equipment required to manufacture electric vehicles.
"This deal outlines what you can expect when two governments work together to rebuild the economy, create jobs and bigger paycheques," Ford said.
"Today's announcement is a game changer for the industry."
Dwaddell@postmedia.com
Twitter.com/windstarwaddell
Honda chief executive Toshihiro Mibe shakes hands with Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau as Ontario Premier Doug Ford looks on after the automaker's announcement of $15 billion in new Ontario investments.
Thursday was a day of great news announcements for Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.

People: Trudeau, Justin; Mibe, Toshihiro

Document 493 of 500

Honda to build EVs in Ontario: analyst; Plan will require multiple plants, thousands of workers, expert says

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/honda-build-evs-ontario-analyst-plan-will-require/docview/3046214734/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Honda+to+build+EVs+in+Ontario%3A+analyst%3B+Plan+will+require+multiple+plants%2C+thousands+of+workers%2C+expert+says&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Honda plans to build two new electric crossover models by early 2028 in Ontario and will require multiple plants in more than one centralized location, says Auto-Forecast Solutions'Sam Fiorani.
Honda's nearly $18-billion electrification plan for Ontario is expected to be formally announced as early as Thursday.
The investment includes plants for electric vehicle and battery production, along with the supply chain plants - such as cathode and separator facilities - required to manufacture EV components.
The Windsor Star previously reported that Honda was looking at Southwestern Ontario, including Essex County, as potential sites for part of its supply chain.
"Once the investment is made, it cements Honda in Ontario," said Fiorani, who is vice-president of global vehicle forecasting for the firm that specializes in providing data on future global automotive production.
"This is not going to be one large plant. It will be multiple, large plants and thousands of employees," Fiorani told the Star.
"When this all flushes out, it's going be a big win for Ontario."
Fiorani said he expects the Japanese automaker will build the new EV production and battery plants close to its existing facilities in Alliston, Ont.
"The first vehicle will be a small electric crossover that will be built alongside the CR-V," Fiorani said.
"We expect that new EV to be produced in Alliston by late 2027. That will be a Honda model.
"That will be followed in early 2028 with an Acura model crossover. More production (models) before the end of the decade will require faster adoption of EVs."
While Ontario Premier Doug Ford didn't confirm it was Honda making what he called the largest investment in Canadian history on Monday, he did say the investment will be spread across the region with widespread benefits.
The pieces that are expected to be located outside of the immediate Alliston area are a cathode manufacturing plant and a separator, which prevents short circuits in a battery cell.
"Honda's workforce in the Alliston area is so big, it already has many of the employees needed tied up," Fiorani said. "It makes more sense to go elsewhere to focus on getting new groups of employees."
Fiorani said southern Ontario also makes sense for the company as it can easily service its Ohio facilities with components. However, batteries will be produced closer to the assembly plants because of logistics costs.
"In the current transitional phase of EVs, locating plants in places that can serve multiple plants makes sense," Fiorani said. "At this point, everyone is trying to build capacity.
"A lot of infrastructure needs to be built that Honda doesn't have in place right now. It's going to entail large investments by them just to get up to speed."
On a trip to Windsor earlier this month, Ontario Minister of Economic Development, Job Creation and Trade Vic Fedeli said some of the plants coming to form the province's EV supply chain ranged in value from $1 billion to $4 billion.
With three battery plants and five automakers making electric vehicles in the province, Fiorani said Canada has positioned itself to be a significant player in the industry.
"Ever since Canada decided to focus on the automotive industry to lure companies, the region between Windsor and Toronto has become a centre of excellence for electric vehicle production," Fiorani said.
"Between Oakville (Ford), Ingersoll (Toyota), Brampton (Stellantis), Alliston (Honda) and Windsor (Stellantis), you have one million units of potential EV production in the region."
Fiorani credited a variety of factors for differentiating Canada and making the nation so competitive in the EV arms race.
"It's a confluence of a lot of factors - quality of the products produced, quality of the workforce and a supply chain that is unique in North America," Fiorani said.
"The government incentives that have come along have ensured the future of the automotive industry in the area."
Fiorani said companies are also attracted by Canada's natural resources and the makeup of its electrical power supply.
"Canada has all kinds of critical minerals needed to make batteries that the U.S. doesn't have," Fiorani said. "Additionally, companies can be closer to the source of those minerals.
"As automakers look to go green, hydro and the other green energy available in Canada is a big win for any assembly plant looking at trying to be green through the whole chain of production.
"When you're making green vehicles, and you can be green along the way, that really makes it look better for the company." Dwaddell@postmedia.com Twitter.com/windstarwaddell

Subject: Strategic materials; Supply chains; Automobile industry; Employees; Electric vehicles; Factories

Business indexing term: Subject: Supply chains Automobile industry Employees Factories

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Stellantis NV; NAICS: 336110, 551112

Identifier / keyword: honda; plans; build; electric; crossover; models

Honda building two EV crossovers, multiple new Ontario plants - auto analyst

Author: Waddell, Dave

Publication info: Waddell, Dave. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/honda-building-two-ev-crossovers-multiple-new/docview/3045322683/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Honda+building+two+EV+crossovers%2C+multiple+new+Ontario+plants+-+auto+analyst&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Waddell%2C+Dave&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 495 of 500

'How we make a living' - musicians protest Essex's call for unpaid performers

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-we-make-living-musicians-protest-essexs-call/docview/3045066922/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27How+we+make+a+living%27+-+musicians+protest+Essex%27s+call+for+unpaid+performers&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Area musicians are upset after the Town of Essex posted a call on social media seeking unpaid performers for a summer concert series.
"Because we're having fun doing what we're doing, people think that we're only doing this as a hobby, or we're only doing this to pass time, and they don't realize that for a lot of people, this is how we make a living," said local musician Kristopher Marentette.
Since the town's arts, culture and tourism committee launched Tune Up The Parks - a popular annual showcase of local musicians in Essex's green spaces - performers have volunteered their talents without financial compensation.
However, shortly after the town put out a call last week inviting musicians to join this year's lineup, criticism ignited online urging the town to fairly pay its performers.
Marentette, who has earned a livelihood as a musician in Windsor-Essex for nearly three decades, took to Facebook Monday to voice his frustration. His post soon amassed nearly 60 comments, including other musicians who weighed in.
"I encourage all performers to reach out and ask our time and talent to be acknowledged," wrote Marentette.
"This is not strumming the guitar at a beach party or around a campfire, we are professionals doing a job and deserve to be treated as such."
Former Windsor city councillor Chris Holt also shared his disappointment on Facebook.
"I can't believe anyone is still not willing to pay for talent," wrote Holt, runner-up for city mayor in the most recent civic elections.
Marentette launched an online Change.org petition calling on the town to pay its performers, which garnered 100 signatures in little more than 24 hours.
The town launched Tune Up The Parks a decade ago, attracting hundreds of attendees who enjoy live performances in green spaces throughout Essex during the months of July and August.
This year's series features 18 performances at Heritage Gardens Park at Essex Centre and the Colchester Harbour Pavilion.
But despite the event's popularity, Essex Mayor Sherry Bondy said the town does not have the budget to pay performers.
"We as the town still only have limited budgets," Bondy told the Windsor Star on Tuesday.
"We're trying to bring programs to the community within those very limited budgets."
The Tune Up the Parks concert series "provides opportunity for new and emerging musical artists to gain experience playing to a live audience," Marc Totola, the town's manager of strategic communications, told the Star in an email Wednesday. "Similar to an 'open mic' night, performers are not paid," he said, adding the work of the arts committee helps "assist acts in honing their skills. Performances are hosted by committee members and tipping is encouraged as a form of consideration and recognition."
Although Bondy is not a member of the town's arts committee, she was a delegate on Jan. 6, 2021, when the event moved online briefly during the COVID-19 pandemic. Bondy said she encouraged the committee at the time to explore avenues for online donations to performers.
"I do share a similar concern for the musicians," she said. "I've spent many years in the service industry, so I understand nobody wants to work for free."
Asked if anything was done to address her concerns, Bondy said "nothing changed," but emphasized that musicians have the choice to volunteer their time.
Although participating musicians are not paid by the town, she said they do receive compensation through other means.
"There is a pass-the-hat component that we push hard when we introduce the musicians," said Bondy.
"Tune Up The Parks is widely attended, and I believe people give generously when they do come.
"I actually just had lunch with one [musician] who said he makes more money there than at a bar, so I think it can be very successful, as well."
Yet Marentette said expecting musicians to perform for free "in very trying financial times" is an "inappropriate ask. You're asking one of the most financially vulnerable groups in the community to forego any kind of compensation."
He provided examples of other local municipalities that run comparable events - including the Ottawa Street Pride Fest, the Town of Tecumseh's Corn Festival, and Lakeshore's Summer Parks Concert Series - and that allocate funds to pay performers.
"There is a possibility for this to happen," Marentette said, "so why is it just not happening here? That's the question.
"We all want to support the communities we live in, we all want to make our communities better. The difference is that this is a program that's designed to drive tourism dollars to the town."
The online discord has resulted in a discussion between the Town of Essex and the union representing approximately 250 to 300 musicians in Windsor-Essex County.
"What we would like to see first and foremost is that musicians get paid and get paid fairly for their work," said Chris Borshuk, president of the Windsor Federation of Musicians. "Simple as that."
Borshuk told the Star he had a meeting with committee members Tuesday to address the concerns.
"Maybe this was something that wasn't thought through by the committee or a committee member," Borshuk said.
"People forget that [musicians] are workers, as well.
"We're unionized workers, in the case of our members. This is a profession as well as it is a joyful thing for us to do."
Related
Musicians strut their stuff at open audition for Amherstburg's festival roster
LaSalle named one of Ontario's 'best festival cities'
Bondy said that the town's budget for 2024 has already been set in stone, so other sponsorships would have to be found to pay the performers.
"We don't want to cancel the series," Bondy said.
Marentette said he has signed up as a delegate at the upcoming Town of Essex council meeting on May 6.
mmazak@postmedia.com

Document 496 of 500

House leader touts federal funds aimed at elevating entrepreneurs

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/house-leader-touts-federal-funds-aimed-at/docview/3044926853/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=House+leader+touts+federal+funds+aimed+at+elevating+entrepreneurs&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government is promising to help fund hundreds of aspiring entrepreneurs in Windsor-Essex with its $60-million budget allocation supporting emerging business owners across Canada.
Touting the renewed investment announced in the Liberals'2024 budget unveiled last week, government House leader Steven MacKinnon (L - Gatineau) made a brief stop in Windsor on Monday to share Ottawa's funding topup for Futurpreneur Canada - a non-profit organization that provides loan financing and mentorship to entrepreneurs ages 18 to 39.
The funding aims to help launch around 6,250 businesses across the country over the next five years.
MacKinnon was joined by local MP Irek Kusmierczyk (L - Windsor-Tecumseh), as well as representatives from Futurpreneur Canada and Invest Windsor-Essex at Timber + Plumb Kitchens and Cabinetry design studio.
"We know that the backbone of our economy, the people who employ by far the most Canadians - but the people who are also emblematic of the spirit of a community - are those entrepreneurs and small businesses that sprout up," said MacKinnon.
"People put all their chips in the middle of the table when they choose to go into business for themselves, but need that support, need that little bit of financial assistance, and the vote of confidence that comes with an organization like Futurpreneur Canada."
For more than two decades, the non-profit organization has helped over 17,700 young entrepreneurs launch more than 13,900 businesses countrywide - including 3,800 in Ontario - backed by $161.5 million in federal funding.
The renewed investment will roll out over the next five years, matched by contributions from Futurpreneur Canada's corporate sponsors and partnerships with five provincial governments.
"What's been driving prosperity in our community has always been entrepreneurship, has always been business owners," said Kusmierczyk.
"We're seeing this young generation's dynamism that wants to take the baton, that wants to be the next job creators, that wants to be the next drivers of prosperity in our community.
"It just fits perfectly with who we are as a community. We are a community of entrepreneurs here in Windsor-Essex."
According to Janis Nixon, Futurpreneur Canada's vice-president of programs and partnerships, 76 per cent of small-business owners in Canada are expected to retire over the next decade, presenting a prime opportunity for young entrepreneurs.
"At the same time, millennials and gen Zs are becoming more and more interested in owning a business as a way to build economic stability and long-term wealth," Nixon said.
Calling it an "important step in ensuring that the next generation of entrepreneurs don't get left behind," Nixon said this renewed commitment from the federal government guarantees "young people across Canada can pursue their entrepreneurial dreams and launch successful businesses."
"With an aging population of business owners," said Nixon, "now's the moment for young people to seize the opportunity to sustain local economies and foster economic prosperity at Futurpreneur." mmazak@postmedia.com

Document 497 of 500

Liberal House leader visits Windsor to tout $60M investment in young entrepreneurs

Author: Mazak, Madeline

Publication info: Mazak, Madeline. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberal-house-leader-visits-windsor-tout-60m/docview/3044400842/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberal+House+leader+visits+Windsor+to+tout+%2460M+investment+in+young+entrepreneurs&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Mazak%2C+Madeline&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government is promising to help fund hundreds of aspiring entrepreneurs in Windsor-Essex with its $60-million budget allocation supporting emerging business owners across Canada.
Touting the renewed investment announced in the Liberals' 2024 budget unveiled last week, government House leader Steven MacKinnon (L - Gatineau) made a brief stop in Windsor on Monday to share Ottawa's funding top-up for Futurpreneur Canada - a non-profit organization that provides loan financing and mentorship to entrepreneurs ages 18 to 39.
The funding aims to help launch approximately 6,250 new businesses across the country over the next five years.
MacKinnon was joined by local MP Irek Kusmierczyk (L - Windsor-Tecumseh), as well as representatives from Futurpreneur Canada and Invest Windsor-Essex at Timber + Plumb Kitchens and Cabinetry design studio in Windsor.
"We know that the backbone of our economy, the people who employ by far the most Canadians - but the people who are also emblematic of the spirit of a community - are those entrepreneurs and small businesses that sprout up," said MacKinnon.
"People put all their chips in the middle of the table when they choose to go into business for themselves, but need that support, need that little bit of financial assistance, and the vote of confidence that comes with an organization like Futurpreneur Canada."
For more than two decades, the non-profit organization has helped over 17,700 young entrepreneurs launch over 13,900 businesses nationwide - including 3,800 in Ontario - backed by $161.5 million in federal funding.
One of the entrepreneurs who benefited was Markie Tuckett, owner of Timber + Plumb Inc., who applied to the program six years ago when she was 25.
"The most daunting task is funding," said Tuckett.
"When I was 25, I could barely get a credit card. I don't come from a family of wealth. I'm actually the first entrepreneur in my family. So, I didn't have mentorship or guidance.
"It was basically starting a business hoping to get funding from a bank. When all the banks turned me down, I was able to step in with Futurpreneur and build that out that direction."
Tuckett also lauded the two-year mentorship provided through the program, which helped her make decisions.
Without the guidance offered through Futurpreneur, Tuckett said her "business probably wouldn't have gotten off the ground."
The renewed investment will roll out over the next five years, matched by contributions from Futurpreneur Canada's corporate sponsors and partnerships with five provincial governments.
"What's been driving prosperity in our community has always been entrepreneurship, has always been business owners," said Kusmierczyk.
"We're seeing this young generation's dynamism that wants to take the baton, that wants to be the next job creators, that wants to be the next drivers of prosperity in our community.
"It just fits perfectly with who we are as a community. We are a community of entrepreneurs here in Windsor-Essex."
Member of Parliament and government House leader Steven MacKinnon speaks on Monday to Markie Tuckett, owner of Timber + Plumb Kitchens and Cabinetry in Windsor.
According to Janis Nixon, Futurpreneur Canada's vice-president of programs and partnerships, 76 per cent of small businesses owners in Canada are expected to retire over the next decade, presenting a prime opportunity for young entrepreneurs.
"At the same time, millennials and Gen Z's are becoming more and more interested in owning a business as a way to build economic stability and long-term wealth," Nixon said.
Related
Students get a look at entrepreneurship through Downtown Accelerator
Windsor area sees second-highest business activity level in Ontario
Calling it an "important step in ensuring that the next generation of entrepreneurs don't get left behind," Nixon said this renewed commitment from the federal government guarantees that "young people across Canada can pursue their entrepreneurial dreams and launch successful businesses.
"With an aging population of business owners," said Nixon, "now's the moment for young people to seize the opportunity to sustain local economies and foster economic prosperity at Futurpreneur."
mmazak@postmedia.com

CITY HALL BLOG: Chief tells Windsor council youth crime up 27%

Author: Campbell, Taylor

Publication info: Campbell, Taylor. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/city-hall-blog-chief-tells-windsor-council-youth/docview/3043902996/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=CITY+HALL+BLOG%3A+Chief+tells+Windsor+council+youth+crime+up+27%25&amp;title=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-04-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Campbell%2C+Taylor&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Windsor+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Follow Windsor Star reporter Taylor Campbell's live blog below for up-to-the-minute coverage of Windsor city council's meeting on Monday, April 22, 2024.
_____________________________________________________________________________________
1:21 p.m. - Public meeting over - Stories to come at windsorstar.com
1:02 p.m. - Some of today's council questions:
Costante asks for a report to council confirming the number of participants at free public skating session offered during the 2023-24 season.
Morrison asks admin to waive parking tickets and fees downtown for Saturday, the NFL draft party, "to show that we're a very supportive and friendly and welcoming city here, to bring as many people as we can to the downtown core."
Dilkens says the city's Lot 11 near city hall can be free from noon to 7 p.m. this Saturday. Council supports.
Ward 6 Coun. Jo-Anne Gignac asks admin to further comment on the truck route study.
Shawna Boakes, executive director operations / deputy city engineer, says the public information sessions are being held on April 29 (at Windsor Public Library John Muir branch) and 30 (at WFCU Centre). Two truck route options are being proposed. A report will be coming to council during a standing committee meeting in either June of July.
1:00 p.m. - Ward 1 Coun. Fred Francis presents a petition from residents opposed to the city's current housing proposal on the Roseland Golf and Curling Club property.
12:53 p.m. - Kieran McKenzie asks for clarification on the bylaw amendment discussed by delegate Howard Weeks.
He asks if a subsequent council could build a 20-storey municipal building on the waterfront as a result of the change.
City planner Thom Hunt says no. The bylaw is limited to municipal infrastructure, like a pumping station.
Kieran McKenzie asks about the original proposal for Festival Plaza, which extended above the crest of Riverside Drive. Would that be allowed under these changes?
Hunt says no, it would not.
"The genesis of the policy is routine types of infrastructure requirements that don't perform another part of development," Hunt says.
"It's really the bread and butter kind of work that Public Works and infrastructure undertake all the time, that it wouldn't be an impediment, we wouldn't have to go through the process of an official plan amendment," Hunt said.
12:52 p.m. - Council will receive the report on moving the location of Bright Lights for information, meaning council won't take any action on the topic.
12:43 p.m. - Ward 3 Coun. Renaldo Agostino: "Instead of talking about moving (Bright Lights), why don't we do something else that's really good, and then do something else after that that's really good? That's what's going to help build our downtown core.
"That's what we're working towards."
12:34 p.m. - Kaschak asks administration about the challenges that would come with moving Bright Lights downtown.
Michelle Staadegaard, city manager of culture and events, said it would be challenging to secure the event space and make sure the light displays would be safe downtown.
12:19 p.m. - Rhys Trenhaile, a local developer, delegates on 11.4 - Response to CQ 2-2023 - moving the location of Bright Lights.
Trenhaile: "It's cultural aspects that other great downtowns have and we have to make sure we have," he says, noting Windsor already has hospitals, schools, and hotels - the essentials - in its core.
12:14 p.m. - Council has voted unanimously to receive the employee experience project report for information and to support the implementation plan developed as part of the project with the consultant.
12:05 p.m. - Chief administrative officer Joe Mancina said salary levels and pay "continue to be something that, both from a union perspective and a management perspective, we are aware of and that we continue to work on at the end of the day.
"The objective here is we have to attract talent to the municipal sector, to be able to equip us and position us for the future. That's across all levels of the workforce. It doesn't just relate to one particular level of the workforce," Mancina said.
11:58 a.m. - Ward 7 Coun. Angelo Marignani asks Petten what the union's top area of concern.
Petten said the wage grid hasn't kept pace with non-union. That's the largest issue for his members.
"Our members are very aware of that and they're looking for that to be fixed," Petten said.
11:54 a.m. - Costante asks how the consultant envisions council's role, from a governance perspective, in rolling out employee initiatives.
Holland: "Council should be involved in many of these objectives and initiatives, especially in the short term." There are some pressing initiatives the PwC is suggesting council implement or update almost immediately.
11:45 a.m. - Mark McKenzie asks about work-life balance and if Local 543 are still advocating for a four-day work week.
Petten said it is.
"I'm sure there are probably a number of work-life balance initiatives that we feel the city can undertake that wouldn't affect services," Petten said.
"We're not saying that it's one-size-fits-all."
11:35 a.m. - In response to a question from Kaschak, Holland says work-life balance could be improved to increase retention. Employees are less concerned with pensions and benefits than they are with pay and work-life balance.
11:31 a.m. - Holland: "I know the city is advancing on its direction towards diversity, equity, inclusion - I think that would help the culture.
"I wouldn't say it's above and beyond what we've seen at other cities across Canada."
Holland says there was a balance of employees who felt included.
Parker: "What you see in the report is not exceptional relative to other cities and more broadly what we see in public sector organizations when we look across all industries."
11:27 a.m. - Kieran McKenzie asks about the report's culture and leadership section and challenges and sentiments expressed by employees.
"It's no secret that there have been some high-profile human resources-related moments that have happened," Kieran McKenzie said.
"Trying to read between the lines, a little bit - some of what you're saying, there's a culture of fear.
"I'm trying to understand ... how intense those feelings actually are and how critical is is for us, collectively, to address those issues and culture to get where we're trying to go."
11:21 a.m. - Kathy Parker, PwC partner, national workforce of the future consulting leader, said the issues at the City of Windsor are not unique. The company is seeing similar issues in other municipalities.
11:17 a.m. - Kieran McKenzie asks Michelle Holland, PwC director of consulting and deals (representing the consultant hired to undertake the review) to respond to Petten's comments.
Holland: "Given the fact that there were 890 employees interviewed during the survey, all viewpoints were brought forward. It's not meant to dilute or diminish any (viewpoint).
"I understand David's point of view and fully support the union throughout. This is obviously a very important initiative with the employee sentiment brought forward, but I think that is captured in the key findings..."
11:09 a.m. - On to presentations and delegations.
Item 11.3 - Retention and Employee Experience Project.
David Petten, president of CUPE Local 543, is a delegate. He represents more than 1,300 city workers, from lifeguards to engineers.
"As the report highlights, not all is well at the city, and shining the light on our workers' struggles was needed," Petten said.
"I was hoping to see something more tailored that Local 543 members could confidently say they were heard. Unfortunately, our feedback ended up being lumped in with other workplace parties.
"Non-union employee participation represented more than 75 per cent of the participation group, while the feedback of the union parties represented 25 per cent from their combined group, which is significant."
Petten says administration has had this document about employee experience "for months, but our relationship has largely carried on in the same way, what I would suggest is an unproductive way."
11:07 a.m. - Costante asks about adding to the consent agenda item 11.7 - 2023 Year-End Operating Budget Variance Report. Council agrees.
Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens highlights that this variance report includes the roughly $900,000 expense stemming from the city's response to the Feb 2022 Ambassador Bridge Blockade. The federal government declined to reimburse the city for that portion of the blockade costs.
"We've talked about it. You've been really passionate advocates and helped convey the message to the federal government which hasn't gotten the appropriate attention yet," Dilkens said.
"Now you're actually seeing the number on paper and the impact (on the taxpayer) being wrapped up."
10:57 a.m. - Kieran McKenzie asks about 8.17 - Community and Corporate Greenhouse Gas Emissions and Energy Monitoring Report.
"What levels do we need to his from a public transit perspective and/or an active transportation perspective to actually start to trend in a direction that would allow us to hit our (energy targets)?"
Karina Richters, supervisor, environmental sustainability and climate change: 25 per cent increase.
Richters says the city does not have a set target of how many combustion engine vehicle users would need to shift to electric to help the city reach its targets. The city will have to "recalculate" if it wants to reach net zero emissions by 2050.
"For every rider we shift to a transit bus, we're seeing about a 1/3 to 2/3 emission shift (for that person)," Richter said.
Kieran McKenzie: "Last year we declared a climate change emergency. That means that we acknowledge and take specific actions in order to address those challenges.
"The report is stating very clearly that if we continue doing what we're doing, we will not hit our target, and that goal remains important to us."
10:52 a.m. - On item 8.5. - Rent Supplement Program Expiries and Mitigation Update, Ward 2 Coun. Fabio Costante asks that city staff continue to advocate to the province to extend the rent supplement program. Council agrees.
Kieran McKenzie asks how provincial funding is being reallocated to address concerns, considering the programs included in the report are no longer being funded.
Kirk Whittal, executive director housing and children services, says the city has been able to divert individuals who relied on those "sunsetted" services to other active programs in the community.
10:49 a.m. - Council is moving on to the consent agenda, things they will approve unanimously without discussion.
Council is adding the following items to consent:
8.9. - 986 Ouellette Avenue, Masonic Temple, Community Heritage Fund & Downtown
Windsor Enhancement Strategy and Community Improvement Plan Grant Extension
11.1 - Little River Pollution Control plant (LRPCP) UV Disinfection Equipment Pre-Purchase
11.2 - Ojibway Trunk Sewer Maintenance Hole Rehabilitation - Delayed Commencement
11.5 - Declaration of a Vacant Parcel of Land Municipally Known as 0 Forest Glade Drive
Surplus and Authority to Offer Same for Sale
11.8 - Reinvestment of Special WDBL Dividend in WDTC
11.9 - Contract Amendment for Net Metering Photovoltaic Rooftop Project at 11 City Facilities
10:43 a.m. - Ward 3 Coun. Renaldo Agostino also addresses the police chief and deputy chief.
"I see a lot of supports downtown. Is it safe to say that these supports in some ways damper efforts in other parts of the city?" - Agostino
Bellaire: "We look at it holistically... We are diverting a lot of calls for services away. We're creating more calls for service because we are doing things up front, diverting more response-style calls for services because we are dealing with those issues upfront. We've been getting to 911 calls faster because of that."
10:38 a.m. - Ward 8 Coun. Gary Kaschak asks Windsor Police Chief Bellaire about the police/nurse team.
"It was a good partnership with Windsor Regional Hospital as part of an ongoing strategy, a data-driven strategy," Bellaire said.
Top repeat users of the WRH Ouellette Campus emergency department have been reduced by almost 50 per cent, Bellaire said.
The program sees emergency department nurses hit the streets with police officers to help divert folks from emergency rooms and connect them with health-care services.
10:33 a.m. - Ward 10 Coun. Jim Morrison brings up 7.1.9, correspondence from Architectural Conservancy Ontario, a letter to amend Subsection 27(16) of the Ontario
Heritage Act to request support of an extension of
the deadline in the Act for five years, to January 1,
2030.
City staff is undertaking a "monumental project" to go through hundreds of heritage properties and batch designate them, Morrison says.
Read the Star's coverage of that issue here and here
10:31 a.m. - Kieran McKenzie puts forward a motion to provide a letter of support to Windsor West MPP Lisa Gretzky (NDP) for her Bill 173: Partner Violence Epidemic Act. Council supports sending a letter.
10:28 a.m. - Kieran McKenzie brings up item 7.1.2. Notice of Regulation Proposal regarding Minister's
Permit and Review powers under the Conservation
Authorities Act. Environment Registry of Ontario.
Council supports a motion from Kieran McKenzie to have administration bring back a report that outlines the changes being brought forward to the Conservation Authorities Act by the province.
10:24 a.m. - Ward 9 Coun. Kieran McKenzie has questions about several correspondence items.
on 7.1.1. from Ontario Superior Court of Justice, Windsor Housing Providers Inc. v Windsor (City);
Re: application to quash Residential Licensing Bylaw 14-2023; Justice K. A. Gorman, Reasons for
Judgement. McKenzie applauds city administration for winning a legal battle and upholding the council-approved residential rental licensing bylaw.
You can read the Star's coverage of that court battle here, here, and here
10:15 a.m. - On 7.1.4 2023 Windsor Police Service Annual Report, Ward 4 Coun. Mark McKenzie has questions for Windsor Police Chief Jason Bellaire. He asks about an increase in youth related crime, up 26.7 per cent.
Bellaire says youth crime rates fluctuate.
"We're still addressing how to do with youth crime upstream. By the time it gets to the courts, it's almost too late - I don't mean in a permanent way. It's just the interventions should have happened already.
"The police can only do so much as part of those interventions. We need to work with schools, we need to work with partners."
Council supports a motion from Mark McKenzie to have the city send a letter to the Greater Essex County District School Board encouraging the board to work more collaboratively with Windsor police with a focus on police/youth engagement.
10:08 a.m. - Council is discussing correspondence items.
Council voted to cancel its April 8 meeting in light of the solar eclipse. The cancellation stemmed from a decision by local school boards to dismiss students early that day out of concern for student safety. Families were responsible for arranging end-of-day transportation for students, and enough city staff have children that administrators recommended council cancel its meeting that day.
You can read today's 1,323-page council agenda here and the consolidated agenda here Tune in to a city-run livestream of the meeting here
tcampbell@postmedia.com( tcampbell@postmedia.com )
twitter.com/wstarcampbell

Subject: Athletic drafts & trades; Infrastructure; Councils; Work life balance; Employees; Engineers; Workforce

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure Work life balance Employees Workforce
